## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024:**

### 📌 Core Definitions
- ✅ **Sensor**: Input device that converts a physical stimulus into an electrical signal
- ✅ **Transducer**: General term for any device that converts energy from one form to another
- ✅ **Active sensor**: Self-generating (no external power) — e.g., Thermocouple, Piezoelectric
- ✅ **Passive sensor**: Externally powered — e.g., RTD, Thermistor, Strain gauge, LVDT

### 📌 Key Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ RTD: $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha T]$ with **α = 0.00385 /°C (Platinum)**
- ✅ Thermocouple EMF: $E = \alpha \Delta T + \beta (\Delta T)^2$ (Seebeck effect)
- ✅ LVDT output: $V_{out} = S \cdot x$ (linear with displacement)
- ✅ Capacitive sensor: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$
- ✅ Piezoelectric charge: $Q = d_{ij} \cdot F$
- ✅ Wheatstone bridge balance: $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$
- ✅ Bridge sensitivity: $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/4)(\Delta R / R)$
- ✅ First-order response: $y(t) = Y_{ss}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$; at $t = \tau$, output = **63.2%**
- ✅ Second-order overshoot: $M_p = e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \times 100\%$

### 📌 Static vs Dynamic Characteristics
- ✅ **Static** (steady input): Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Resolution, Repeatability, Drift
- ✅ **Dynamic** (varying input): Rise time, Settling time, Time constant, Natural frequency, Damping ratio, Bandwidth

### 📌 Standard Values
- ✅ **PT100**: 100 Ω at 0°C, 138.5 Ω at 100°C
- ✅ **Thermocouple Type K**: −200°C to +1260°C
- ✅ **Quartz piezoelectric coefficient**: $d_{11} = 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- ✅ **Permittivity of free space**: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

### 📌 Sensor Selection Logic
- ✅ Need **no external power** → Active sensor
- ✅ Need **highest accuracy in temperature** → RTD (PT100)
- ✅ Need **very high temperatures** (>1000°C) → Thermocouple Type R/S/B
- ✅ Need **linear displacement** → LVDT
- ✅ Need **dynamic pressure/vibration** → Piezoelectric
- ✅ Need **non-contact detection** → Photoelectric / Inductive proximity

### 📌 Key Exam Triggers
- ✅ "Define sensor" → Always mention input-side + electrical output
- ✅ "Differentiate active vs passive" → Use a comparison table
- ✅ "Derive LVDT output" → Show the mutual inductance step
- ✅ "Time constant" → 63.2% response is the magic number
- ✅ "Piezoelectric applications" → Accelerometer, microphone, NDT

> 🎯 **Pro Tip for KTU 2024:** In 14-mark questions, dedicate **at least 2 marks' worth** (≈ 1 page) to a **neat labelled diagram**. Examiners explicitly reward visual aids in instrumentation papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Manufacturing Techniques?

A **Manufacturing Technique** is a systematic, scientifically engineered sequence of physical, chemical, and mechanical operations employed to convert raw materials (metals, polymers, ceramics, composites) into finished engineering components, instruments, and assemblies with the desired geometry, dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and functional properties.

In the context of **Industrial Instrumentation**, manufacturing techniques form the backbone for producing sensor bodies, transducer housings, diaphragm elements, bellows, bourdon tubes, valve bodies, flow-meter internals, and precision mechanical sub-assemblies used in measurement and control systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**  
> Manufacturing techniques refer to the established industrial processes — primary shaping (casting, forming, powder metallurgy), secondary processing (machining, joining, heat treatment, surface finishing), and modern non-conventional methods (EDM, laser, additive manufacturing) — used to fabricate the physical hardware of an industrial instrument.

## 1.2 The Big Picture: The Manufacturing Family Tree

Every manufacturing process belongs to one of the following broad families:

| S. No. | Process Family | What It Does to Material | Typical Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Casting & Molding** | Gives shape via solidification of a liquid | Pressure gauge housings |
| 2 | **Bulk Deformation (Forming)** | Reshapes solid metal using compressive forces | Forged valve bodies |
| 3 | **Sheet Metal Forming** | Reshapes thin metal sheets | Instrument cabinets, diaphragms |
| 4 | **Material Removal (Machining)** | Removes unwanted material to achieve geometry | Lathes, milling spindles |
| 5 | **Joining / Assembly** | Permanently bonds two or more parts | Welded pipes, brazed junctions |
| 6 | **Powder Processing** | Compresses and sinters metal powders | Sintered bronze filter elements |
| 7 | **Heat Treatment** | Alters internal microstructure without changing shape | Hardened sensor shafts |
| 8 | **Surface Engineering** | Modifies the outer skin properties | Plated, coated, anodized parts |
| 9 | **Non-Conventional / Advanced** | Uses thermal, chemical, or electrical energy | EDM holes, 3D-printed sensor bodies |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Clay & Sculpture" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cookie-Cutter and Clay Analogy**  
> Imagine you are a chef. You have three basic ways to make a cookie:
> 1. **Press a cutter into a rolled dough** → *Forming / Machining* (starts with excess, removes or pushes aside)
> 2. **Pour melted chocolate into a mould and let it solidify** → *Casting* (starts as a liquid, hardens in shape)
> 3. **Squish chocolate putty in your palm** → *Plastic deformation* (starts solid, gets reshaped by force)
>
> Manufacturing is the same idea applied to engineering materials. Every industrial component — be it a thermocouple sheath or a Bourdon tube — is made by repeating these three fundamental principles: **removing**, **reshaping**, or **solidifying-in-shape** the raw material.

## 1.4 Why an Instrumentation Engineer Must Study This

An instrumentation engineer frequently interacts with manufactured hardware — selecting materials, specifying tolerances, diagnosing mechanical failures, and validating performance. A clear understanding of how a part was made directly influences:

- Selection of suitable **materials** (e.g., stainless steel 316 for chemical compatibility)
- Understanding of **dimensional tolerances** achievable by a process
- Awareness of **residual stresses** introduced during manufacturing
- Capability to recommend **rework or replacement** strategies during maintenance

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (Recall for Exams):**  
> - **Dimensional Accuracy (D_a)** → measured in **micrometers (µm)** or **mm**  
> - **Surface Roughness (R_a)** → measured in **µm** or **µin**  
> - **Material Utilization Ratio (MUR)** → ratio of finished part mass to raw material mass  
> - **Production Rate** → pieces/hour or pieces/day  
> - **Tool Life (T)** → minutes (for cutting tools)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Casting Processes — Solidification-Based Shaping

Casting exploits the fluid behaviour of molten (or liquid-state) material. The liquid fills a mould cavity, cools, and solidifies into the desired shape.

**Key sub-processes:**

- **Sand Casting** — uses compacted silica sand moulds; cheapest; low dimensional accuracy (~±1.5 mm).
- **Investment Casting (Lost-Wax)** — wax pattern is coated with ceramic slurry, melted out, then metal poured. Used for **turbine blades, jewellery, intricate sensor parts**.
- **Die Casting** — molten metal (Zn, Al, Mg) injected under high pressure (10–100 MPa) into hardened steel dies. Excellent surface finish, high production rate.
- **Centrifugal Casting** — molten metal poured into a rotating mould; centrifugal force pushes metal against the mould wall. Used for **large-diameter pipes, cylinder liners**.
- **Continuous Casting** — molten metal continuously solidified into long strands (billets, slabs, blooms) for further rolling.
- **Shell Moulding** — resin-bonded sand shell, better accuracy than green-sand casting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Casting Tolerance Reference (KTU 2024):**  
> - Sand casting → **±1.0 to ±3.0 mm**  
> - Die casting → **±0.1 to ±0.5 mm**  
> - Investment casting → **±0.05 to ±0.15 mm**

**Casting Shrinkage Allowance:**

Most metals shrink during solidification. The **shrinkage allowance (S_a)** is given by:

$$S_a = \frac{L_{mould} \times \text{shrinkage percent}}{100}$$

**Typical linear shrinkage values (as a percentage):**

| Material | Shrinkage (%) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Cast Iron (Grey) | 1.0 |
| Cast Steel | 2.0 |
| Aluminium Alloys | 1.3 |
| Brass | 1.5 |
| Bronze | 1.5 |

## 2.2 Bulk Deformation (Forming) Processes

Forming reshapes solid metal plastically (without removal) using compressive, tensile, or shear stresses at temperatures below the melting point.

**Hot Working vs Cold Working:**

- **Hot Working** — performed above the recrystallization temperature. Low yield stress, high ductility, no work hardening. Used for large deformations (forging, hot rolling).
- **Cold Working** — performed below recrystallization temperature. Improves strength, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy, but reduces ductility.

**Common Forming Operations:**

| Process | Description | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Forging (Open/Closed Die)** | Compressive shaping with hammers or presses | Crankshafts, connecting rods |
| **Rolling** | Passing between rotating rollers | Plates, sheets, I-beams |
| **Extrusion (Direct/Indirect)** | Forcing metal through a die | Pipes, rods, complex profiles |
| **Drawing** | Pulling metal through a die (tensile) | Wires, tubes |
| **Stamping / Pressing** | Sheet metal cut and shaped in a press | Instrument panels, brackets |

**Forging Pressure Formula:**

For an open-die forging operation, the approximate forging force **F_f** is:

$$F_f = K_f \times A_{proj} \times \epsilon^{n}$$

where **K_f** is the material flow strength, **A_proj** is the projected area, **ε** is the true strain, and **n** is the strain-hardening exponent.

## 2.3 Sheet Metal Forming

Sheet metal operations are vital for making **instrument enclosures, chassis, brackets, diaphragms, and bellows**. Common operations include:

- **Shearing / Blanking / Punching** — cutting sheets along straight or curved lines
- **Bending** — V-bend, U-bend, edge-bend; spring-back must be compensated
- **Deep Drawing** — pulling sheet metal into a die cavity to form cups, cans, shells
- **Stretch Forming** — sheet stretched over a form block
- **Hydroforming** — uses high-pressure fluid to deform sheet against a die

**Spring-back Compensation (KTU important):**

$$R_{die} = R_{part} \times (1 + k_b)$$

where **R_die** is the radius on the die, **R_part** is the desired radius on the part, and **k_b** is the spring-back coefficient (typically 0.03 to 0.05 for low-carbon steel).

## 2.4 Machining Processes (Material Removal Family)

Machining uses a harder tool to remove softer material (chip) and create the desired geometry.

**Principal Turning Parameters:**

- **Cutting Speed (V)** — surface speed at which the tool engages the workpiece, in **m/min**.
- **Feed (f)** — distance the tool advances per revolution, in **mm/rev**.
- **Depth of Cut (d)** — radial penetration of the tool, in **mm**.

**Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Turning:**

$$\text{MRR} = V \times f \times d \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

Equivalently:

$$\text{MRR} = \pi \times D \times N \times f \times d$$

where **D** is the workpiece diameter (mm) and **N** is the spindle speed (rev/min).

**Spindle Speed (N):**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} \quad [\text{rev/min}]$$

**Cutting Power (P_c):**

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \times V}{60\,000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

where **F_c** is the tangential cutting force in Newtons.

**Taylor's Tool Life Equation (KTU favourite):**

$$V \times T^{n} = C$$

where:
- **V** = cutting speed (m/min)
- **T** = tool life (min)
- **n** = tool-life exponent (typically 0.1 to 0.4)
- **C** = Taylor's constant

**Common Machining Operations:**

| Operation | Motion | Tool Shape | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Turning** | Tool moves linearly; workpiece rotates | Single-point | Shafts, pins |
| **Milling** | Rotating multi-point cutter | End mill, face mill | Slots, pockets |
| **Drilling** | Rotating drill; axial feed | Twist drill | Bolt holes |
| **Grinding** | Abrasive wheel rotates | Grinding wheel | Hardened surfaces |
| **Boring** | Enlarges existing hole | Boring bar | Cylinder bores |

## 2.5 Joining Processes

**Welding (Fusion Welding):**

- **Arc Welding (SMAW, GTAW, GMAW)** — uses an electric arc to melt and fuse metals.
- **Resistance Welding** — uses Joule heating from high current; spot, seam, projection welding.
- **Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene)** — flame melts filler metal.
- **TIG / MIG** — tungsten inert gas / metal inert gas welding; used for stainless-steel instrument housings.

**Heat Input (HI) — a critical KTU formula:**

$$\text{HI} = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

where **V** is the arc voltage (V), **I** is the current (A), and **S** is the travel speed (mm/min).

**Soldering & Brazing:**

- **Soldering** — filler metal melts **below 450 °C** (e.g., tin-lead for electronic PCBs).
- **Brazing** — filler metal melts **above 450 °C** but **below base metal melt point** (e.g., copper-phosphorus, silver brazing for instrument tube joints).

**Adhesive Bonding** — uses epoxies, cyanoacrylates, and structural adhesives; excellent for dissimilar materials (e.g., bonding a ceramic to a metal in a sensor assembly).

**Mechanical Joining** — bolts, rivets, snap-fits, threaded fasteners.

## 2.6 Powder Metallurgy (P/M)

Produces parts by compacting fine metal powders and sintering them below the melting point.

**Process Steps:**

1. Powder preparation (atomization, chemical reduction)
2. Blending (mixing with lubricants and binders)
3. Compaction in a die (pressure 200–800 MPa)
4. Sintering in a controlled furnace (T = 0.7 to 0.9 × T_melt)
5. Secondary operations (re-pressing, machining, infiltration)

**Sintered Density Formula:**

$$\rho_{sintered} = \rho_{theoretical} \times (1 - P_{porosity})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **P/M in Instrumentation:**  
> Sintered bronze filters are used in pneumatic instrument air filters. Tungsten-copper P/M electrodes are used in EDM machines. Sintered magnets (Nd-Fe-B) power DC motors in actuators.

## 2.7 Non-Conventional / Advanced Manufacturing

These processes do **not** use a harder tool than the workpiece; they use thermal, electrical, chemical, or mechanical energy.

| Process | Energy Source | Best For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **EDM (Electric Discharge Machining)** | Electrical sparks | Hardened steels, intricate shapes |
| **ECM (Electrochemical Machining)** | Electrolytic dissolution | Thin-walled aerospace parts |
| **LBM (Laser Beam Machining)** | High-energy laser | Micro-drilling, ceramics |
| **AJM (Abrasive Jet Machining)** | High-velocity abrasive particles | Cleaning, deburring |
| **WJM (Water Jet Machining)** | High-pressure water + abrasive | Heat-sensitive materials |
| **EBM (Electron Beam Machining)** | Focused electron beam | Micro-welding, drilling |
| **AM (Additive Manufacturing / 3D Printing)** | Layer-by-layer deposition | Prototypes, custom sensor bodies |

**MRR for EDM:**

$$\text{MRR}_{EDM} = K_{EDM} \times I \times t$$

where **K_EDM** is a material-dependent constant, **I** is the discharge current, and **t** is the time of machining.

**MRR for ECM:**

$$\text{MRR}_{ECM} = \frac{A \times \rho \times \eta_{current} \times I}{Z \times F}$$

where **A** is atomic mass, **ρ** is density, **η_current** is current efficiency, **Z** is valency, and **F** is Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol).

## 2.8 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Manufacturing Formulas (Master Before Exam):**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$ | Spindle speed in turning | rev/min |
| 2 | $\text{MRR} = V \times f \times d$ | Material removal rate | mm³/min |
| 3 | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \times V}{60\,000}$ | Cutting power | kW |
| 4 | $V \times T^{n} = C$ | Taylor's tool life | m/min, min |
| 5 | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S}$ | Welding heat input | J/mm |
| 6 | $F_f = K_f \times A_{proj} \times \varepsilon^{n}$ | Forging force | N |
| 7 | $\rho_{sintered} = \rho_{theoretical}(1 - P)$ | Sintered density | kg/m³ |
| 8 | $\text{MRR}_{EDM} = K_{EDM} \times I \times t$ | EDM material removal | mm³/min |
| 9 | $\text{MUR} = \dfrac{m_{part}}{m_{raw}} \times 100$ | Material utilization ratio | % |
| 10 | $R_{die} = R_{part}(1 + k_b)$ | Spring-back compensation | mm |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Most-Used Manufacturing Process | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensor diaphragms (pressure) | Sheet-metal deep drawing + heat treatment | Thin, uniform, accurate |
| Valve bodies | Sand casting / investment casting | Complex internal passages |
| Bourdon tubes | Cold bending + stress relief | Accurate spring constant |
| Thermocouple sheaths | Tube drawing + welding | Long, leak-proof tubes |
| Instrument PCBs | Soldering (reflow / wave) | Electrical continuity |
| Process fittings | CNC turning + threading | Tight tolerances |
| Flow-meter rotors | Investment casting + CNC machining | Smooth surface, balanced |
| Turbine blades | Investment casting / 5-axis machining | High strength, heat resistance |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Spindle Speed (N) in Turning

**Given:**
- Cutting speed **V** in m/min
- Workpiece diameter **D** in mm

**Step 1 — Definition of Cutting Speed:**

Cutting speed **V** is the linear surface speed of the workpiece at the point of contact with the tool.

$$V = \pi \times D \times N_{rev\,per\,min} / 1000$$

The factor **1/1000** converts millimetres to metres.

**Step 2 — Rearranging to Solve for N:**

$$\pi \times D \times N / 1000 = V$$

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (KTU-type):**

> A mild steel shaft of **diameter 50 mm** is turned at a cutting speed of **V = 30 m/min**. Determine the spindle speed.

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 50}$$

$$N = \frac{30\,000}{157.08}$$

$$N \approx 191 \text{ rev/min}$$

> The spindle must rotate at approximately **191 rpm** to achieve the required cutting speed.

## 3.2 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Turning

**Step 1 — Geometric Reasoning:**

In one spindle revolution, the tool moves axially by the feed **f** (mm) and radially cuts to a depth **d** (mm). The unrolled chip cross-section is a rectangle of area **f × d**.

**Step 2 — Linear Travel of the Workpiece Surface per Minute:**

The circumference traced per minute = **π × D × N** mm.

**Step 3 — MRR Calculation:**

$$\text{MRR} = (\text{circumference/min}) \times (\text{feed}) \times (\text{depth})$$

$$\text{MRR} = \pi \times D \times N \times f \times d$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Form using Cutting Speed:**

Since $V = \pi D N / 1000$, we have $\pi D N = 1000 V$. Substituting:

$$\text{MRR} = 1000 \times V \times f \times d$$

For consistency in units (V in m/min → mm/min = 1000 V), the result is in **mm³/min**.

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification:**

> Given: V = 30 m/min, f = 0.2 mm/rev, d = 2 mm.

$$\text{MRR} = 1000 \times 30 \times 0.2 \times 2$$

$$\text{MRR} = 12\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 12 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Cutting Power (P_c)

**Step 1 — Definition:**

Cutting power equals the cutting force times the cutting velocity.

**Step 2 — Force × Velocity:**

$$P_c \text{ [W]} = F_c \text{ [N]} \times V_{instantaneous} \text{ [m/s]}$$

**Step 3 — Convert Cutting Speed from m/min to m/s:**

$$V_{m/s} = \frac{V_{m/min}}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$P_c = F_c \times \frac{V_{m/min}}{60}$$

**Step 5 — Convert W to kW:**

$$P_c \text{ [kW]} = \frac{F_c \times V_{m/min}}{60\,000}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example:**

> Cutting force F_c = 1500 N, V = 30 m/min.

$$P_c = \frac{1500 \times 30}{60\,000} = 0.75 \text{ kW}$$

> A **0.75 kW** motor is sufficient to drive this cut.

## 3.4 Derivation of Taylor's Tool Life Equation

**Step 1 — Empirical Observation:**

As cutting speed **V** increases, tool life **T** decreases. The relationship is non-linear, typically following a power law.

**Step 2 — Assume a Power-Law Form:**

$$V \times T^{n} = C$$

**Step 3 — Take Logarithms to Linearize:**

$$\log V = \log C - n \log T$$

This is a straight line with slope **−n** and intercept **log C** when plotted on log-log axes.

**Step 4 — Constants for Common Tool-Workpiece Pairs:**

| Tool | Workpiece | n | C (m/min) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| HSS | Mild Steel | 0.125 | 200 |
| Carbide | Mild Steel | 0.30 | 800 |
| Ceramic | Cast Iron | 0.40 | 1500 |

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**

> Using HSS tool on mild steel (n = 0.125, C = 200). What cutting speed gives a tool life of 60 min?

$$V \times 60^{0.125} = 200$$

$$V = \frac{200}{60^{0.125}} = \frac{200}{1.647} \approx 121.4 \text{ m/min}$$

> The optimum cutting speed is about **121.4 m/min** for a 60-minute tool life.

## 3.5 Welding Heat Input Derivation

**Step 1 — Heat Generated in the Arc:**

The arc acts as an electrical resistor dissipating energy as heat.

$$Q = V \times I \quad [\text{W or J/s}]$$

**Step 2 — Heat Delivered per Unit Length of Weld:**

For a given travel speed **S** (mm/min), the heat input per mm is:

$$\text{HI} = \frac{V \times I}{S/60} = \frac{60 \times V \times I}{S} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**

> Arc voltage V = 25 V, current I = 200 A, travel speed S = 5 mm/s = 300 mm/min.

$$\text{HI} = \frac{60 \times 25 \times 200}{300} = \frac{300\,000}{300} = 1000 \text{ J/mm}$$

> A heat input of **1000 J/mm** — used to check against the maximum allowed for the parent metal to avoid HAZ cracking.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for Common Instrument Parts

| Instrument Part | Material | Critical Properties | Recommended Process | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pressure gauge case | Cast iron | Dimensional stability | Sand casting | Cheap, vibration damping |
| Bourdon tube | Phosphor bronze | Elasticity, fatigue life | Cold bending + heat treatment | Precise spring constant |
| Diaphragm (pressure sensor) | SS 316L | Corrosion resistance, thinness | Deep drawing + electropolishing | Uniform thin wall |
| Thermocouple sheath | Inconel 600 | Heat resistance | Tube drawing + TIG welding | Continuous length |
| Control valve body | WCB carbon steel | Pressure containment | Sand casting + CNC machining | Complex internal cavities |
| Orifice plate | SS 304 | Sharp edge accuracy | CNC milling + lapping | Sharp-edged for flow accuracy |
| Solenoid plunger | Soft iron | Magnetic permeability | Cold heading + grinding | Smooth, accurate surface |
| Instrument PCB | Copper-clad FR4 | Conductive tracks | Soldering (wave/reflow) | Reliable joints |
| Pitot tube | SS 304 | Smooth bore, accuracy | Tube bending + brazing | Leak-proof joint |
| Flow-meter body | Aluminium alloy | Lightweight | Die casting | High volume, low cost |

## 3.7 Comparative Process-Selection Tree

When a manufacturing engineer selects a process, the following decision hierarchy applies:

**Step 1 — Material type** (metal / polymer / ceramic / composite)
- **Metals** → forming, machining, casting
- **Polymers** → injection moulding, extrusion, thermoforming
- **Ceramics** → pressing + sintering, injection moulding
- **Composites** → lay-up, filament winding, pultrusion

**Step 2 — Geometry** (simple / complex / thin-walled / prismatic)
- **Simple prismatic** → turning, milling
- **Complex 3D** → casting, additive manufacturing
- **Thin-walled** → sheet-metal forming

**Step 3 — Quantity** (prototype / batch / mass)
- **Prototype** → 3D printing, CNC machining
- **Small batch** → conventional machining
- **Mass production** → die casting, injection moulding, stamping

**Step 4 — Tolerances and Surface Finish** (coarse / fine / mirror)
- **Coarse (>0.1 mm)** → casting, forming
- **Fine (0.01–0.1 mm)** → machining
- **Mirror (<0.01 mm)** → grinding, lapping, polishing, EDM
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of Manufacturing Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturing Techniques] --> B[Primary Shaping]
    A --> C[Secondary Processing]
    A --> D[Non-Conventional / Advanced]

    B --> B1[Casting and Molding]
    B --> B2[Powder Metallurgy]
    B --> B3[Plastic Forming]
    B --> B4[Additive Manufacturing]

    B1 --> B1a[Sand Casting]
    B1 --> B1b[Investment Casting]
    B1 --> B1c[Die Casting]
    B1 --> B1d[Centrifugal Casting]
    B1 --> B1e[Shell Moulding]

    B2 --> B2a[Compaction]
    B2 --> B2b[Sintering]
    B2 --> B2c[Infiltration]

    B3 --> B3a[Forging]
    B3 --> B3b[Rolling]
    B3 --> B3c[Extrusion]
    B3 --> B3d[Drawing]
    B3 --> B3e[Sheet Metal Forming]

    B4 --> B4a[3D Printing FDM]
    B4 --> B4b[Stereolithography SLA]
    B4 --> B4c[Selective Laser Sintering SLS]

    C --> C1[Machining]
    C --> C2[Joining]
    C --> C3[Heat Treatment]
    C --> C4[Surface Finishing]

    C1 --> C1a[Turning]
    C1 --> C1b[Milling]
    C1 --> C1c[Drilling]
    C1 --> C1d[Grinding]

    C2 --> C2a[Welding]
    C2 --> C2b[Brazing]
    C2 --> C2c[Soldering]
    C2 --> C2d[Adhesive Bonding]

    C3 --> C3a[Annealing]
    C3 --> C3b[Quenching]
    C3 --> C3c[Tempering]
    C3 --> C3d[Case Hardening]

    C4 --> C4a[Plating]
    C4 --> C4b[Anodizing]
    C4 --> C4c[Polishing]
    C4 --> C4d[Coating]

    D --> D1[EDM]
    D --> D2[ECM]
    D --> D3[Laser Machining]
    D --> D4[Water Jet]
    D --> D5[Abrasive Jet]
    D --> D6[Electron Beam]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Typical CNC Turning Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Inputs]
        I1[Raw Workpiece Billet]
        I2[CAD File G-code Program]
        I3[Tool Selection]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Control Unit]
        CU1[Machine Controller MCU]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "manufacturing technique" and list any four primary shaping processes used in industrial component fabrication.**

**Model Answer:**

A manufacturing technique is a systematic sequence of industrial operations that transforms raw material into a finished component of desired shape, size, and properties.

The four primary shaping processes are:

1. **Casting** — molten metal solidified in a mould (e.g., sand casting, die casting)
2. **Forging** — plastic deformation of solid metal using compressive force
3. **Powder Metallurgy** — compacting and sintering metal powders
4. **Injection Moulding** — molten polymer injected into a mould cavity

> **Allocation (3 marks):** Definition 1 mark + four process names 0.5 mark each = **3 marks**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish clearly between soldering, brazing, and welding. State the temperature criterion for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Joining Method | Filler Melting Point | Base Metal | Heat Affected? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Soldering** | **Below 450 °C** | Not melted | No HAZ |
| **Brazing** | **Above 450 °C, below base metal MP** | Not melted | Minor HAZ |
| **Welding (Fusion)** | Equal to or above base metal MP | **Melted and fused** | Significant HAZ |

> **Allocation (3 marks):** Tabular distinction 2 marks + correct temperature statements 1 mark = **3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **investment casting process** step-by-step. Mention any **five advantages** and **two limitations** of this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical workpiece of diameter **80 mm** is turned on a lathe. The cutting speed is **V = 40 m/min**, the feed is **f = 0.25 mm/rev**, and the depth of cut is **d = 3 mm**. Calculate:
- (i) Spindle speed **N** in rpm
- (ii) Material removal rate **MRR** in mm³/min
- (iii) Cutting power **P_c** in kW, if cutting force is **1500 N** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Investment casting (lost-wax process) produces parts of high dimensional accuracy and excellent surface finish. The steps are:

**Step 1 — Pattern Creation:** A wax pattern of the desired part is injection-moulded using a metal die.

**Step 2 — Tree Assembly:** Several wax patterns are joined to a central wax sprue to form a "tree."

**Step 3 — Shell Building:** The tree is repeatedly dipped into a ceramic slurry, then coated with fine stucco (refractory sand) and dried. This is repeated 5–8 times until a strong ceramic shell (≈ 5–15 mm thick) is built.

**Step 4 — Dewaxing (Burnout):** The shell is placed in an autoclave or flash-dewax furnace at ~150 °C, where the wax melts out rapidly, leaving a hollow cavity.

**Step 5 — Sintering:** The shell is fired at high temperature (~1000 °C) to remove residual wax and strengthen the ceramic.

**Step 6 — Pouring:** Molten metal is poured into the pre-heated shell.

**Step 7 — Solidification & Cooling:** The metal solidifies in the cavity.

**Step 8 — Decasting:** The ceramic shell is broken (knocked off) using vibration or hammering.

**Step 9 — Cut-off & Finishing:** The cast parts are cut from the tree, and the gates are ground off.

**Five Advantages:**

1. Excellent dimensional accuracy (±0.1 mm or better)
2. Very smooth surface finish (Ra 1.6–3.2 µm)
3. Can cast intricate shapes with thin walls and fine details
4. No parting line, so flash-free components
5. Wide range of alloys can be cast (steels, aluminium, titanium, superalloys)

**Two Limitations:**

1. High initial cost of wax dies and ceramic slurry
2. Long process cycle (5–8 dipping/drying layers, each taking hours)

> **Mark Split (7):** Block diagram 2 + step-by-step 2 + 5 advantages 1.5 + 2 limitations 1.5 = **7 marks**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Part (i) — Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$$

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 40}{\pi \times 80}$$

$$N = \frac{40\,000}{251.33}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 159.15 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Part (ii) — Material Removal Rate:**

$$\text{MRR} = 1000 \times V \times f \times d$$

$$\text{MRR} = 1000 \times 40 \times 0.25 \times 3$$

$$\text{MRR} = 30\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = 30\,000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 30 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Part (iii) — Cutting Power:**

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \times V}{60\,000}$$

$$P_c = \frac{1500 \times 40}{60\,000}$$

$$P_c = 1.0 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_c = 1.0 \text{ kW}}$$

> **Mark Split (7):** Spindle speed 2 + MRR 2.5 + Cutting power 2.5 = **7 marks**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain **Taylors Tool Life Equation** in detail. A HSS tool has a tool-life exponent **n = 0.125** and a Taylor constant **C = 200 m/min**. Determine the cutting speed for a desired tool life of **90 minutes**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **conventional machining** and **non-conventional machining** processes. With a labelled schematic, explain the working principle of **Electrical Discharge Machining (EDM)**. List any **two applications** of EDM in instrumentation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Taylors Tool Life Equation:**

Tool life **T** is the total time in minutes for which a cutting tool can be used economically between regrinds. F. W. Taylor (1907) established an empirical relationship between cutting speed **V** and tool life **T**:

$$V \times T^{n} = C$$

**Where:**
- **V** = cutting speed in m/min
- **T** = tool life in minutes
- **n** = tool-life exponent (slope of the log-log plot, typically 0.1 to 0.4)
- **C** = Taylor's constant (cutting speed corresponding to 1-minute tool life)

**Significance:**

- Higher cutting speed → shorter tool life.
- The log-log plot of **V vs T** is a straight line of slope **−n** and intercept **log C**.
- This helps the production engineer select the optimum cutting speed that balances productivity against tool-change costs.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: n = 0.125, C = 200 m/min, T = 90 min.

$$V \times (90)^{0.125} = 200$$

$$V = \frac{200}{(90)^{0.125}}$$

$$(90)^{0.125} = 90^{1/8}$$

Let us compute step-by-step:
- log(90) = 1.9542
- 0.125 × 1.9542 = 0.2443
- 10^0.2443 = 1.7548

$$V = \frac{200}{1.7548} \approx 113.98 \text{ m/min}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 114 \text{ m/min}}$$

> **Mark Split (7):** Equation explanation 3 + significance 2 + numerical step-by-step 2 = **7 marks**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Comparison Table — Conventional vs Non-Conventional Machining:**

| Parameter | Conventional Machining | Non-Conventional Machining |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tool hardness | Tool **harder** than workpiece | Tool **not necessarily** harder |
| Contact | Physical contact between tool and workpiece | **No direct mechanical contact** |
| Energy used | Mechanical energy (cutting force) | Thermal / electrical / chemical energy |
| Material limitation | Limited to ductile materials | Can machine **hardened steels, ceramics** |
| Accuracy | ±0.01–0.05 mm | ±0.005–0.01 mm (some processes finer) |
| Surface finish | Good (Ra 0.8–1.6 µm) | Excellent (Ra 0.2–0.8 µm) |
| Examples | Turning, milling, drilling, grinding | EDM, ECM, LBM, WJM, AJM |

**Working Principle of EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining):**

- The workpiece and the tool (electrode) are both connected to a DC pulse-power source.
- A small gap (0.01–0.5 mm) called the **spark gap** is maintained between them.
- The gap is flooded with a **dielectric fluid** (kerosene or deionized water).
- When voltage is applied, the dielectric ionizes, producing a **spark** that vaporizes a tiny portion of the workpiece.
- The spark reaches a temperature of **8,000–12,000 °C**, melting and vaporizing the material.
- The dielectric then flushes away the eroded particles and cools the region.
- This cycle repeats thousands of times per second, gradually eroding the workpiece to the desired shape.
- A servo mechanism maintains a constant gap as material is removed.

**Two Applications in Instrumentation:**

1. **Machining micro-holes** in stainless-steel pressure-sensor diaphragms and fuel-injector nozzles.
2. **Fabricating intricate die cavities** used in plastic-injection-moulded instrument housings.
3. **Die-sinking for forging dies** used to mass-produce precision instrument components.

> **Mark Split (7):** Comparison table 2 + EDM principle with schematic 3 + 2 applications 2 = **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in MRR:** Always show the factor **1000 × V × f × d** when V is in m/min and feed/depth are in mm. Skipping the 1000 factor costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Soldering and Brazing Temperature:** The magic number is **450 °C** — below it is soldering, above is brazing. Students often interchange these.
> 3. **Forgetting Spring-Back Allowance:** Sheet-metal bending questions demand compensation for spring-back. Writing only the part radius **without** the die compensation is incomplete.
> 4. **Heat Input Units:** Heat input is always in **J/mm**, not J/m. Convert travel speed correctly using **S in mm/min** and a 60-multiplier.
> 5. **Tool Life Exponent Sign:** The slope of the log-log plot is **−n** (negative), not **+n**. Marks deducted for wrong sign.
> 6. **Investment Casting Steps:** Do NOT skip the dewaxing stage. The word "lost-wax" itself comes from the wax being **lost** during dewaxing.
> 7. **EDM Energy Source:** EDM uses **electrical/thermal** energy, not mechanical. Mention the spark temperature range (8,000–12,000 °C) for full credit.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Manufacturing Techniques**

- **Three fundamental material-shaping principles:** material **removal**, material **reshaping** (forming), and material **solidification-in-shape** (casting).
- **Primary shaping processes** to memorize: sand casting, investment casting, die casting, centrifugal casting, powder metallurgy, injection moulding, 3D printing.
- **Bulk forming processes:** forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing; **sheet forming:** bending, deep drawing, stretching, hydroforming.
- **Hot working** is above recrystallization temperature; **cold working** is below. Cold working improves strength but reduces ductility.
- **Machining parameters** are **V** (m/min), **f** (mm/rev), **d** (mm), **N** (rpm), and **MRR** (mm³/min).
- **Taylor's equation:** $V \times T^{n} = C$ — always memorize the logarithmic linearization: $\log V = \log C - n \log T$.
- **Cutting power formula** in kW: $P_c = F_c \times V / 60\,000$.
- **Joining temperature threshold** is **450 °C** — separates soldering from brazing. Welding fuses the base metal.
- **Welding heat input** uses a **60-multiplier** in the numerator: $\text{HI} = 60 V I / S$.
- **Non-conventional processes** use energy forms **other than mechanical**: electrical (EDM), chemical (ECM), thermal (LBM), kinetic (WJM, AJM).
- **EDM requires a dielectric fluid** and a spark gap of 0.01–0.5 mm. Spark temperature reaches 8,000–12,000 °C.
- **Spring-back** in sheet metal: $R_{die} = R_{part} (1 + k_b)$ with $k_b \approx 0.03$–$0.05$.
- **Casting shrinkage** must be added to the mould dimension: most metals shrink 1%–2% linearly.
- **Material Utilization Ratio (MUR)** is highest for near-net-shape processes (casting, forging, P/M) and lowest for machining.
- **Process selection hierarchy:** Material → Geometry → Quantity → Tolerance → Cost.
- **For instrumentation parts:** sensor diaphragms → deep drawing; Bourdon tubes → cold bending; valve bodies → casting + machining; PCBs → soldering; precision orifices → grinding/lapping.
- **Powder metallurgy** in instrumentation: sintered bronze filters, sintered magnets (Nd-Fe-B) in actuators, tungsten-copper EDM electrodes.
- **Heat treatment** never changes shape — it only alters **microstructure and mechanical properties**.
- **Surface finishing** protects against corrosion, reduces friction, and improves appearance (e.g., anodizing aluminium instrument panels).

---

**End of Module 1 — Topic: Manufacturing Techniques**

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Measuring Circuits

### Formal Definition
A **measuring circuit** is an electrical network designed to convert the output of a primary sensing element (transducer) into a form suitable for indication, recording, or control. In temperature measurement, these circuits process the small voltage, resistance, or current variations produced by sensors and translate them into accurate, calibrated readings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Measuring circuits are the *signal conditioning backbone* of all modern temperature instrumentation. Without them, even the best sensor is useless.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a measuring circuit as a **medical stethoscope for industry**. A stethoscope amplifies the tiny vibrations of a heartbeat so a doctor can hear them. Similarly, a measuring circuit amplifies the tiny electrical signal from a temperature sensor (e.g., a few millivolts from a thermocouple or a fraction of an ohm change in an RTD) so that a display, controller, or recorder can interpret it.

The most important measuring circuits in temperature instrumentation are:
1. **Wheatstone Bridge (DC)** – Used with RTDs, thermistors, and strain gauges.
2. **Potentiometric Circuit** – Used with thermocouples and linear position sensors.
3. **Current Loop (4–20 mA)** – Industry standard for noise-immune signal transmission.
4. **AC Bridges (Kelvin, Maxwell)** – Used with capacitive/inductive transducers.

---

## 1.2 Temperature and Thermometers

### Formal Definition
**Temperature** is the physical quantity that characterizes the mean kinetic energy of the microscopic degrees of freedom of a system. A **thermometer** is a device that exploits a temperature-dependent physical property (volume, pressure, resistance, EMF, radiation) to provide a measurable indication.

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Unit of Temperature:** Kelvin (K).
> **Conversion:** T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15

### Classification of Temperature Measuring Devices

| Type | Principle | Typical Range |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| Expansion Thermometers | Thermal expansion of fluid/solid | –200 °C to +1000 °C |
| Electrical Resistance Thermometers | Resistance change of metal/semiconductor | –200 °C to +850 °C |
| Thermocouples | Seebeck EMF | –270 °C to +2300 °C |
| Radiation Pyrometers | Thermal radiation intensity | 600 °C and above |

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a hot cup of coffee. A **bimetallic strip** in your kitchen oven bends because the two metals expand at different rates—similar to how a bimetallic coil in a thermostat unwinds. This bending is the "language" the sensor uses to *speak* temperature to the rest of the instrument.

---

## 1.3 Pyrometry

### Formal Definition
**Pyrometry** is the science and technology of measuring high temperatures (typically above 600 °C) using the **thermal radiation** emitted by hot bodies, without making physical contact with the body. Devices used are called **pyrometers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key advantage of pyrometers:** No contact → no contamination, no heat sink effect, can measure moving objects, can measure very high temperatures beyond the range of contact sensors.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine standing near a campfire. You can *feel* the heat on your face even before you touch the flame. Your skin is essentially acting as a radiation detector. A pyrometer does the same thing—but with extreme mathematical precision, using the laws of blackbody radiation.

---

## 1.4 Stefan-Boltzmann Law and Blackbody Radiation

### Formal Definition
A **blackbody** is an idealized physical body that absorbs **all** incident electromagnetic radiation, regardless of frequency or angle of incidence. The **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** states that the total radiant exitance (energy emitted per unit area per unit time) of a blackbody is proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.

$$E_b = \sigma \cdot T^4$$

Where:
- **σ = 5.670374419 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**.
- T is the absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
A blackbody is the **"perfect sponge"** of radiation—it absorbs everything and also emits the maximum possible radiation for its temperature. Real objects (called *gray bodies* or *non-blackbodies*) emit less, characterized by a factor called **emissivity (ε)**, where 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1.

For a **real (gray) body**:

$$E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Emissivity (ε)** is the ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a blackbody at the same temperature. ε = 1 for blackbody, ε = 0 for perfect reflector.

---

## 1.5 Overview of Pyrometer Types

| Pyrometer Type | Wavelength Used | Working Principle |
|----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Total Radiation Pyrometer** | All wavelengths (0 → ∞) | Measures total emitted radiant energy; uses Stefan-Boltzmann law |
| **Optical (Disappearing Filament) Pyrometer** | Single wavelength (~0.65 μm, red) | Compares brightness of source with calibrated internal filament |
| **Ratio (Two-Color) Pyrometer** | Two wavelengths | Measures ratio of intensities at two wavelengths |

---

## 1.6 Introduction to Expansion Thermometers

### Formal Definition
**Expansion thermometers** measure temperature by exploiting the predictable thermal expansion of materials. Sub-classes include:
- **Liquid-in-glass thermometers** (mercury, alcohol)
- **Bimetallic thermometers** (two metals with different expansion coefficients bonded together)
- **Liquid-filled (pressure) thermometers** – Class I, II, III, V
- **Gas-filled (pressure) thermometers** – Class I, II, III

> [!NOTE]
> **Liquid-filled and gas-filled thermometers** are often called **pressure thermometers** because they convert the temperature-induced pressure change of a confined fluid into a mechanical pointer deflection via a Bourdon tube.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Measuring Circuits in Detail

### 2.1.1 The Wheatstone Bridge

The **Wheatstone Bridge** is the most fundamental measuring circuit. It compares an unknown resistance with three known resistances.

**Balanced condition:**

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$$

When balanced, the galvanometer reads zero. Small changes in any resistance (e.g., RTD due to temperature) unbalance the bridge, producing an output voltage V₀ proportional to the resistance change.

For a single active arm (RTD = R₁), the output is:

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} \quad \text{(for small } \Delta R\text{)}$$

Where:
- V_s = excitation voltage
- R = nominal resistance of all four arms
- ΔR = change in resistance of the active arm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use a bridge?** It cancels out lead resistances, eliminates common-mode errors, and converts resistance changes to a measurable voltage. This is the *heart* of industrial RTD instrumentation.

### 2.1.2 The Potentiometric Circuit

Used primarily with **thermocouples**. A thermocouple produces a small DC voltage (microvolts to millivolts). The potentiometric circuit compares this unknown EMF against a known reference voltage. No current is drawn from the thermocouple during the balance condition, which **eliminates the error due to internal resistance**.

### 2.1.3 Current Loop (4–20 mA)

The **4–20 mA current loop** is the industrial standard for analog signal transmission. The "live zero" of 4 mA (instead of 0 mA) is used because:
- It distinguishes a **live signal** (4 mA) from a **broken wire** (0 mA).
- It powers the transmitter itself (two-wire system).
- It is **immune to voltage drops** along long cables (current is the same regardless of resistance, by Ohm's law).

---

## 2.2 Bimetallic Thermometers

### Construction
A **bimetallic strip** consists of two metals with **different coefficients of linear expansion** (α) bonded together throughout their length. Common pairs:
- **Invar (low α) + Brass or Nickel alloy (high α)**
- **Invar + Monel**

### Working Principle
When heated, the metal with the higher expansion coefficient expands more, causing the strip to **bend toward the side of the metal with lower α**. When cooled, it bends the other way.

**Mathematical deflection of a straight bimetallic strip:**

$$\delta = \frac{K \cdot L^2 \cdot \Delta T}{t}$$

Where:
- δ = deflection at the free end
- L = length of the strip
- t = thickness of the strip
- ΔT = temperature change
- K = constant depending on the metals used (proportional to Δα)

> [!NOTE]
> A common industrial form is the **bimetallic helix or spiral coil**. The deflection is then converted to angular rotation of a pointer via a mechanical linkage.

### Applications
- Household ovens, toasters, and irons
- Industrial temperature gauges
- Thermostat switches
- Circuit breakers (bimetallic trip mechanism)

---

## 2.3 Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers

### Principle
Based on the **apparent expansion of mercury or alcohol** in a glass capillary. The liquid expands more than the glass envelope, so the rise in the capillary indicates temperature.

$$V_t = V_0 (1 + \beta \Delta T)$$

Where β is the **coefficient of apparent expansion** of the liquid in glass.

### Limitations
- Fragile (glass)
- Cannot be read remotely
- Mercury is toxic (banned in many applications)
- Limited range: –39 °C (mercury freezes) to +357 °C (mercury boils), or –114 °C to +78 °C for alcohol

---

## 2.4 Liquid-Filled and Gas-Filled (Pressure) Thermometers

### Construction (Common to Both)
A **pressure thermometer** has three components:
1. **Bulb** – Contains the sensing fluid (liquid or gas), placed in the medium whose temperature is to be measured.
2. **Capillary tube** – A long, thin tube transmitting pressure.
3. **Bourdon tube / Helical spring** – Pressure-to-displacement converter that drives the pointer.

### 2.4.1 Liquid-Filled Thermometer (Class I)

**Sensing fluid:** Mercury, xylene, or other low-vapor-pressure liquid.

**Principle:** The liquid in the bulb expands with temperature. Because it is confined, the pressure on the Bourdon tube increases. The Bourdon tube straightens proportionally, and this motion drives the pointer.

**Pressure–Temperature relation (Class I):**

$$P = \frac{P_0 \cdot T}{T_0}$$

Where:
- P₀ and T₀ are the filling conditions (reference pressure at reference temperature)
- T is the absolute temperature of the bulb

**Advantage:** Bulb volume is small → fast response.
**Disadvantage:** The capillary and Bourdon tube must be **completely filled with liquid**, requiring a **fill fluid** (e.g., glycerin) for capillary compensation. Ambient temperature affects the entire system → needs **compensation**.

### 2.4.2 Gas-Filled Thermometer (Class II or III)

**Sensing fluid:** Nitrogen or inert gas (helium) at high pressure (typically 20–30 atm).

**Principle:** Follows the **ideal gas law** (Gay-Lussac's Law, constant volume):

$$P = \frac{P_0 \cdot T}{T_0}$$

**Advantages over liquid-filled:**
- Bulb volume is much **larger** (good sensitivity).
- **Simple and rugged** — capillary can be partially filled with the same gas.
- Less affected by ambient temperature along the capillary (because gas pressure is uniform).

**Disadvantage:** Larger bulb → slower response.

### 2.4.3 Mercury-Filled Vapor Pressure Thermometer (Class V)

Uses a **volatile liquid** (e.g., Freon, methyl chloride, or toluene) in the bulb. The vapor pressure of the liquid is a strong function of temperature, and this pressure is transmitted to the Bourdon tube.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The pressure-temperature relation is **NOT linear** in vapor-pressure thermometers (it's exponential). Use a calibrated scale or non-linear indicator.

### Compensation in Pressure Thermometers

Because the **capillary and Bourdon tube are also exposed to ambient temperature**, they generate spurious pressure signals. The remedy is the **filled system compensation**:

- The capillary is filled with a **second fluid** whose expansion coefficient is chosen to **exactly cancel** the volume changes of the mercury and capillary due to ambient temperature.
- An **invar rod** may also be used inside the capillary to provide dimensional compensation.

---

## 2.5 Pyrometry and Blackbody Radiation — Detailed Theory

### 2.5.1 Thermal Radiation Spectrum

A body at temperature T emits a continuous spectrum of electromagnetic radiation. The spectral distribution is governed by **Planck's Law**:

$$E_{\lambda,b} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1} \quad \text{[W·m}^{-2}\text{·μm}^{-1}\text{]}$$

Where:
- h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant)
- c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s (speed of light)
- k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K (Boltzmann constant)
- λ = wavelength
- T = absolute temperature

### 2.5.2 Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength at which the spectral exitance is maximum is inversely proportional to temperature:

$$\lambda_m \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As a metal is heated, it first glows dull red (~700 °C), then orange, yellow, white, and finally bluish-white. This color shift is Wien's law in action: peak wavelength decreases as temperature increases.

### 2.5.3 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Integrated Form)

Integrating Planck's Law over all wavelengths (0 to ∞) gives:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

This is the **foundation of total radiation pyrometry**.

### 2.5.4 Emissivity and Real Bodies

Most real surfaces are **gray bodies**, with emissivity ε < 1. Emissivity depends on:
- Material
- Surface finish
- Temperature
- Wavelength (spectral emissivity) and direction (directional emissivity)

For a real body:

$$E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^4$$

---

## 2.6 Total Radiation Pyrometer

### Construction
1. **Lens system** (often a convex lens of quartz or fluorite) — focuses the incoming radiation from the hot target onto a **detector**.
2. **Detector** — a **thermopile** (series of tiny thermocouples) that converts radiation to EMF.
3. **Reference junction** — kept at a known temperature (often ambient or active temperature-compensated).
4. **Indicating meter** — calibrated directly in °C.

### Working Principle
The thermopile generates an EMF:

$$e = K \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4$$

Where K is a constant depending on the optical geometry and detector properties.

### Advantages
- Measures **total radiation** across all wavelengths → no wavelength dependence needed.
- Continuous measurement with a recorder.
- Simple calibration.

### Disadvantages
- **Emissivity correction is critical** — assumes the target is a blackbody.
- Affected by dust, smoke, and atmospheric absorption.
- Target must be large enough to fill the optical field of view.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mantra:** A radiation pyrometer actually measures the **brightness temperature**, not the true temperature. To get true temperature, you must know and correct for emissivity using **inverse Stefan-Boltzmann**:

$$T_{true} = T_{apparent} \cdot \varepsilon^{-1/4}$$

---

## 2.7 Optical (Disappearing Filament) Pyrometer

### Construction
1. **Objective lens** — forms an image of the hot target.
2. **Reference filament** — a calibrated tungsten wire inside the instrument, heated by a variable current.
3. **Red filter** (λ ≈ 0.65 μm) — provides monochromatic (single-wavelength) operation, eliminating the need for emissivity over a wide range (assumes λ-emissivity ≈ 1 for many materials at this wavelength).
4. **Eyepiece** with **Ramsden disc** — the observer sees the target and the filament superimposed.
5. **Variable current source** with calibrated dial.

### Working Principle
The observer adjusts the filament current until the filament **"disappears"** against the background of the target image. At this point, the brightness of the filament and the target at the chosen wavelength are equal.

The brightness of the target (at wavelength λ) follows the **Wien approximation** of Planck's law:

$$L_\lambda = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

Where:
- C₁ = 2πhc² ≈ 3.7418 × 10⁻¹⁶ W·m²
- C₂ = hc/k_B ≈ 1.4388 × 10⁻² m·K

> [!NOTE]
> The human eye is used as the **null detector** — high accuracy because it compares two adjacent regions, not absolute brightness.

### Advantages
- **High accuracy** (manual operator, null method).
- Independent of emissivity in the red region (for many metals and oxides).
- Self-calibrating using the known filament temperature–current relationship.
- Portable; widely used in foundries and steel plants.

### Disadvantages
- **Manual operation** — requires a skilled operator.
- Single-wavelength → small errors from unknown spectral emissivity.
- Cannot record continuously (although photoelectric versions exist).

---

## 2.8 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Kelvin–Celsius conversion | T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15 | — |
| 2 | Linear expansion | L = L₀(1 + αΔT) | α in K⁻¹ |
| 3 | Volume expansion | V = V₀(1 + βΔT) | β ≈ 3α for solids |
| 4 | Bimetallic strip deflection | δ = K·L²·ΔT/t | K depends on metals |
| 5 | Wheatstone bridge balance | R₁/R₂ = R₃/R₄ | Balanced → V_g = 0 |
| 6 | Bridge output (single active arm) | V₀ = V_s·ΔR/(4R) | Approximation for small ΔR |
| 7 | Gas-filled thermometer (Class II) | P = P₀·T/T₀ | Ideal gas, constant V |
| 8 | Stefan-Boltzmann (blackbody) | E_b = σT⁴ | σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ |
| 9 | Stefan-Boltzmann (real body) | E = εσT⁴ | 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1 |
| 10 | Wien's displacement law | λ_m·T = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K | — |
| 11 | Planck's spectral exitance | E_{λ,b} = (2πhc²/λ⁵)·[1/(e^{hc/λk_BT}−1)] | W·m⁻²·m⁻¹ |
| 12 | Wien approximation | L_λ ≈ (C₁/λ⁵)·e^(−C₂/λT) | C₁ = 3.74×10⁻¹⁶, C₂ = 1.44×10⁻² |
| 13 | True vs. apparent temperature (radn. pyr.) | T_true = T_app·ε^(−1/4) | Assumes small-band radiation |
| 14 | Total radiant energy emitted | Q = εσAT⁴ | A in m², Q in W |
| 15 | 4–20 mA signal range | I = 4 + 16·(T − T_min)/(T_max − T_min) | Linear with temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** Always state the **units** when plugging numbers into these formulas. Many students lose marks by writing "T" without specifying K or °C.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Bimetallic thermometers** are used in HVAC systems, industrial dryers, food processing, and chemical reactors.
- **Pressure thermometers** (liquid/gas-filled) are preferred in **remote temperature indication** where electricity cannot be used (explosive atmospheres) — e.g., oil refineries, chemical plants.
- **Total radiation pyrometers** are used in **steel rolling mills**, **glass furnaces**, and **kilns** where temperatures exceed 1000 °C.
- **Optical pyrometers** are the **gold standard** in **foundries** for measuring molten metal temperatures and are also used in **calibration laboratories** as transfer standards.
- **4–20 mA loops** are the **lingua franca** of process industries (oil & gas, power, chemical) for transmitting temperature, pressure, flow, and level signals.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stefan-Boltzmann Law (from Planck's Law)

The Stefan-Boltzmann Law can be obtained by **integrating Planck's spectral distribution** over all wavelengths (0 → ∞) and over the entire hemisphere of directions.

### Step 1: Start with Planck's spectral exitance (per unit wavelength)

$$E_{\lambda,b} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1} \quad \text{[W·m}^{-2}\text{·m}^{-1}\text{]}$$

### Step 2: Total hemispherical exitance by integration

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,b} \, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1} \, d\lambda$$

### Step 3: Substitute to simplify the integral

Let $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}$, so that $\lambda = \frac{hc}{x k_B T}$ and $d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{x^2 k_B T} \, dx$.

Substituting:

$$E_b = \int_\infty^0 \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\left(\frac{hc}{x k_B T}\right)^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^x - 1} \cdot \left(-\frac{hc}{x^2 k_B T}\right) dx$$

The two negatives cancel (limits swap back), and after simplification:

$$E_b = \frac{2 \pi k_B^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the standard integral

The integral $\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx$ is a known result equal to $\frac{\pi^4}{15}$.

Therefore:

$$E_b = \frac{2 \pi k_B^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} \cdot T^4$$

### Step 5: Define the Stefan-Boltzmann constant

$$E_b = \sigma T^4, \quad \text{with} \quad \sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.670374419 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation. The T⁴ dependence is a **direct consequence of quantum mechanics** (Planck's hypothesis) — classical physics (Rayleigh-Jeans law) would have predicted a divergent result (the famous *ultraviolet catastrophe*).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You don't have to reproduce the entire integral on the exam. Writing the substitution, identifying the standard integral, and stating the final result is usually enough for full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Bimetallic Strip Deflection (Conceptual)

Consider a straight bimetallic strip of length L, total thickness t, with two layers of equal thickness t/2. Let the two materials have expansion coefficients α₁ (higher) and α₂ (lower), and identical Young's moduli E.

### Step 1: Curvature due to differential expansion

When the temperature changes by ΔT, the differential free expansion is:

$$\Delta L = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 2: This differential expansion produces a curvature κ

The radius of curvature R of the strip is given by:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \kappa = \frac{6 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T (1 + m)^2}{t \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\left(m^2 + \frac{1}{mn}\right) \right]}$$

Where:
- m = t₁/t₂ (ratio of layer thicknesses)
- n = E₁/E₂ (ratio of Young's moduli)

For the **simple case** where m = 1 and n = 1 (symmetric strip), the expression simplifies dramatically to:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \kappa = \frac{2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{t}$$

### Step 3: Deflection at the free end (small deflection approximation)

For a strip clamped at one end, the deflection at the free end is:

$$\delta \approx \frac{L^2}{2R} = \frac{L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{t}$$

This is the form given as the "K" constant in Section 2.2:

$$\delta = K \cdot \frac{L^2 \cdot \Delta T}{t}, \quad \text{where} \quad K = (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The deflection scales as **L²/t**. Designers make strips long and thin to amplify the deflection. A coiled (helical) form multiplies this deflection further — the pointer can sweep 270° from a few degrees of strip bend.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Gas-Filled Thermometer Equation

Consider a fixed volume V of an ideal gas in the bulb. By the ideal gas law:

$$PV = nRT \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$$

For a sealed system with **constant volume** (V = constant), the relationship between pressure and temperature is **linear**:

$$P = \frac{P_0}{T_0} \cdot T$$

This is a **direct proportionality**: P is linear in absolute temperature T.

### Application example

A gas-filled thermometer is filled at 20 °C (293 K) at a pressure of 25 atm. What is the pressure when the bulb is at 200 °C (473 K)?

**Step 1:** Apply the linear relation P = (P₀/T₀)·T.

$$P = \frac{25}{293} \times 473 = 40.36 \text{ atm}$$

### Note on Class I (Liquid-Filled)

For a Class I liquid-filled thermometer, the situation is more complex because:
- The **liquid is incompressible**, so volume change is what matters.
- The volume change of the liquid in the bulb is constrained by the fixed-volume Bourdon tube.
- This volume change produces a **pressure increase** transmitted to the Bourdon tube.
- The governing equation involves both the bulk modulus of the liquid and the geometry of the system.

For a simplified analysis (assuming the system is completely filled with the same liquid, and ambient compensation is perfect):

$$\Delta P = \beta \cdot \frac{V_{bulb}}{V_{total}} \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- β = coefficient of cubic expansion of the liquid
- V_bulb = volume of liquid in the bulb
- V_total = total volume of the system

> [!NOTE]
> For higher KTU marks, mention that the **complete Class I equation** must include compensation for the change in volume of mercury in the capillary and Bourdon tube due to ambient temperature — leading to the **filled-system compensation** mentioned in Section 2.4.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Total Radiation Pyrometer

**Problem:** A total radiation pyrometer views a hot body of emissivity ε = 0.8 through a lens of diameter 5 cm. The thermopile receives a radiant power of 0.4 W. If the lens focuses the image entirely on the thermopile and the pyrometer is calibrated for a blackbody, find the true temperature of the body, given that the pyrometer reads (apparent) 1500 K.

### Step 1: Stefan-Boltzmann for real body

The pyrometer actually measures the **radiant exitance equivalent to a blackbody** of temperature T_app. The true exitance is ε·σ·T_true⁴.

Setting them equal:

$$\sigma T_{app}^4 = \varepsilon \sigma T_{true}^4$$

### Step 2: Solve for true temperature

$$T_{true}^4 = \frac{T_{app}^4}{\varepsilon}$$

$$T_{true} = T_{app} \cdot \varepsilon^{-1/4}$$

### Step 3: Plug in numbers

$$T_{true} = 1500 \times (0.8)^{-1/4} = 1500 \times (1.25)^{0.25} = 1500 \times 1.0574 \approx 1586 \text{ K}$$

**Conversion to °C:** 1586 − 273 = 1313 °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students use ε in the wrong direction. Remember: real body emits **less** than a blackbody → to get the same signal, the real body must be **hotter** than the apparent temperature. So T_true > T_app. If you got T_true < T_app, you inverted the formula.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Bimetallic Strip

**Problem:** A bimetallic strip made of brass (α₁ = 19 × 10⁻⁶ /K) and invar (α₂ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ /K) has length L = 50 mm and thickness t = 0.5 mm. If the temperature increases by 100 K, find the deflection at the free end.

### Step 1: Use the simple deflection formula

$$\delta = \frac{L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{t}$$

### Step 2: Plug in values (using SI units: L = 0.05 m, t = 0.0005 m)

$$\delta = \frac{(0.05)^2 \times (19 - 1) \times 10^{-6} \times 100}{0.0005}$$

$$\delta = \frac{0.0025 \times 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 100}{0.0005}$$

$$\delta = \frac{0.0025 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0005} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0005}$$

$$\delta = 9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 9 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** A 9 mm deflection — easily visible and measurable.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Wien's Displacement Law

**Problem:** A furnace's peak emission is at wavelength 1.5 μm. Estimate its temperature.

### Step 1: Apply Wien's law

$$\lambda_m T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

### Step 2: Solve for T

$$T = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 1932 \text{ K} = 1659 \text{ °C}$$

**Engineering check:** A furnace glowing most strongly at 1.5 μm would appear **red-orange** — consistent with a temperature around 1600 °C. ✓

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Bimetallic | Liquid-in-Glass | Liquid-Filled (Pressure) | Gas-Filled (Pressure) | Radiation Pyrometer |
|-----------|------------|------------------|---------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Range (°C) | –50 to +500 | –200 to +600 | –50 to +650 | –200 to +600 | +600 and above |
| Accuracy | ±1–2% | ±0.1–1% | ±0.5–1% | ±0.5–1% | ±1–2% (with ε correction) |
| Remote reading | No (mechanical) | No | Yes (Bourdon + linkage) | Yes | Yes |
| Response time | Slow (large mass) | Slow | Medium | Slow (large bulb) | Fast (optical) |
| Power needed | No | No | No | No | Yes (for detector) |
| Cost | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| Hazardous area safe | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (electronic) |
| Measures moving target | No | No | No | No | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The current loop's output is independent of cable length (up to a few km) because the **current is set by the transmitter** and the receiver is essentially a short circuit. This is why 4–20 mA is the gold standard in industrial plants.
