# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between a diaphragm, a capsule, and a bellows. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Diaphragm:** A thin circular plate clamped at its periphery, deflects under pressure. Used in **low-pressure indicators** (e.g., 0–10 bar gauges).
- **Capsule:** Two diaphragms welded rim-to-rim; deflection doubles for the same pressure. Used in **aneroid barometers** for atmospheric pressure measurement.
- **Bellows:** A stack of capsules providing large axial deflection. Used in **pressure transmitters** and **LVDT-based pressure transducers** for amplified motion.

**Key distinguishing feature:** A capsule gives ~2× the deflection of a single diaphragm, while a bellows of $N$ capsules gives ~$N$× the deflection.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Why is silicon preferred over metallic strain gauges in piezoresistive pressure sensors?**

**Model Answer:**
Silicon has a **gauge factor of 100–170**, which is roughly **50–100 times higher** than the gauge factor of metal strain gauges ($G_f \approx 2.0$). This is because the piezoresistive effect in semiconductors is dominated by changes in carrier mobility under stress, not just geometric stretching. The high $G_f$ allows:
1. **Very high sensitivity** with small diaphragm deflections.
2. **Miniaturization** (MEMS fabrication on silicon wafers).
3. **Integration of the gauge and diaphragm** in a single silicon chip, eliminating glue-related creep and hysteresis.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End-Semester Examinations follow a Module-Internal Choice pattern. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module. Both choices are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. Derive the expression for its sensitivity.

#### (a) Model Solution

**Construction:**
An LVDT consists of:
1. A **primary winding** (P) wound at the center of a hollow cylindrical former.
2. **Two identical secondary windings** (S1 and S2) wound on either side of the primary, connected in **anti-series** (opposite polarity).
3. A **movable soft-iron core** attached to the device whose displacement is to be measured (e.g., the tip of a pressure-driven bellows).

**Working:**
- The primary is energized by an AC excitation voltage $V_p$ (frequency 1–20 kHz).
- The two secondaries induce EMFs $V_{S1}$ and $V_{S2}$ by mutual induction.
- When the core is at the **null (central) position**, $V_{S1} = V_{S2}$ in magnitude, but opposite in phase, so the differential output is **zero**.
- When the core moves **upward** by $x$, more flux links with S1 than S2: $V_{S1} > V_{S2}$, and $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2}$ is **positive and proportional to $x$**.
- When the core moves **downward**, $V_{S2} > V_{S1}$ and $V_{out}$ is **negative** (phase reversal).
- The **magnitude** of $V_{out}$ indicates displacement; the **phase** indicates direction.

**Sensitivity Derivation:**
The induced EMFs vary approximately linearly with core displacement $x$ within the linear range:

$$V_{S1} = V_0 (1 + k x), \quad V_{S2} = V_0 (1 - k x)$$

Differential output:

$$V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = 2 k V_0 x$$

Therefore, **sensitivity** is:

$$S_{LVDT} = \frac{V_{out}}{x} = 2 k V_0 \quad \text{(V/mm)}$$

where $k$ depends on the winding geometry and $V_0$ is the per-unit induced EMF.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A strain gauge with nominal resistance 350 Ω and gauge factor 2.1 is mounted on a steel cantilever subjected to a stress of 140 MPa. The Young's modulus of steel is 200 GPa. The gauge is connected in a quarter-bridge with excitation voltage 10 V. Calculate:
(i) The strain in the cantilever.
(ii) The change in gauge resistance.
(iii) The output voltage of the bridge.

#### (b) Model Solution

**Given:**
- $R = 350 \ \Omega$
- $G_f = 2.1$
- $\sigma = 140 \ \text{MPa} = 140 \times 10^6 \ \text{Pa}$
- $E = 200 \ \text{GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \ \text{Pa}$
- $V_{ex} = 10 \ \text{V}$ (quarter-bridge)

**(i) Strain in the cantilever:**
By Hooke's law, $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, so:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{140 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 7 \times 10^{-4} = 700 \ \mu\varepsilon$$

**(ii) Change in gauge resistance:**

$$\Delta R = R \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \times 2.1 \times 7 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \times 2.1 \times 7 \times 10^{-4} = 0.5145 \ \Omega$$

**(iii) Output voltage of the bridge (quarter-bridge):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4} = \frac{10 \times 2.1 \times 7 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{1.47 \times 10^{-2}}{4} = 3.675 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{V} = 3.675 \ \text{mV}$$

**Final Answers:** $\varepsilon = 700 \ \mu\varepsilon$, $\Delta R \approx 0.5145 \ \Omega$, $V_{out} \approx 3.675 \ \text{mV}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of a dead-weight piston gauge. Derive the expression for pressure generated by it.

#### (a) Model Solution

**Construction:**
A dead-weight piston gauge consists of:
1. A **precision piston** of hardened tungsten carbide or tool steel, lapped to extreme tolerance (a few µm).
2. A **closely fitting cylinder** in which the piston floats on a thin film of hydraulic fluid.
3. A **set of calibrated disc weights** of known masses, stacked on the piston.
4. A **hand pump or screw press** to generate pressure.
5. A **hydraulic fluid reservoir** (typically oil for high pressure, or air/gas for low pressure).
6. A **port** to connect the test instrument being calibrated.

**Working:**
1. Hydraulic fluid fills the cylinder and the line to the test instrument.
2. Calibrated weights are loaded on the piston.
3. The hand pump is operated until the piston **floats freely** (lifted off its seat) — this indicates equilibrium.
4. At equilibrium, the upward force from the pressurized fluid equals the downward force of gravity on the piston and weights:

$$P \cdot A = M g$$

5. By varying the masses, different reference pressures can be generated with high accuracy.
6. The instrument under test reads this known pressure, and any deviation is recorded as its calibration error.

**Derivation of pressure expression:**

Force balance: $P \cdot A = M \cdot g$, where $A$ is the effective piston area.

If $A$ varies slightly with pressure as $A = A_0 (1 + \lambda P)$:

$$P \cdot A_0 (1 + \lambda P) = M g$$

Solving for $P$:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{M g}{A_0 (1 + \lambda P)}}$$

For high-accuracy DWP gauges, $\lambda$ is typically $0.5$–$2 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹. The denominator correction is small, often < 0.01% even at 1000 bar, but is essential for **primary-standard** accuracy.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a piezoresistive pressure sensor. List four advantages of piezoresistive sensors over metallic strain gauges.

#### (b) Model Solution

**Construction and Working:**
A piezoresistive pressure sensor (typically MEMS-based) consists of:
1. A **silicon substrate** anisotropically etched to form a thin square or circular diaphragm (thickness 10–500 µm).
2. **Four piezoresistors** diffused or ion-implanted into the diaphragm at the **edges**, where stress is maximum under applied pressure.
3. The four resistors are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration.
4. The diaphragm is sealed in a package with a **pressure port** on one side (often the back of the die).
5. A reference vacuum or known pressure seals the other side (for **absolute** or **gauge** pressure measurement).

**Working Logic:**
1. Pressure $P$ is applied through the port to the back of the diaphragm.
2. The diaphragm deflects outward; tensile strain develops at two opposite edges and compressive strain at the other two.
3. The two tensile-strained resistors **increase in resistance**; the two compressed resistors **decrease**.
4. The Wheatstone bridge becomes unbalanced, producing an output voltage:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_{f,Si} \cdot \varepsilon$$

5. This output is amplified, temperature-compensated, and linearized to give a calibrated pressure reading.

**Block Diagram:**
Pressure $P$ → Silicon Diaphragm (deflects) → Piezoresistors (resistance changes) → Wheatstone Bridge (output voltage) → Amplifier → Temperature Compensation → Display / 4–20 mA Output

**Four Advantages of Piezoresistive over Metallic Strain Gauges:**

1. **Much higher gauge factor (100–170 vs. ~2.0):** Provides 50–100× higher sensitivity for the same strain.
2. **Smaller size and mass:** Enables MEMS fabrication; the sensor can be < 1 mm².
3. **Lower power consumption:** Suitable for battery-operated and IoT devices.
4. **Better frequency response:** Due to the small mass, the resonant frequency of the diaphragm is high (kHz range), enabling dynamic pressure measurements.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake #1:** Strain $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless but is often written as µm/m (microstrain). Don't write $\varepsilon$ in Pa or m/m.
> 2. **Unit Mistake #2:** When using $V_{out} = V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon / 4$, **do not forget the factor 4** for a quarter-bridge. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Conceptual Pitfall:** In DWP gauges, many students forget to include the **piston's own mass** in $M$ — the formula is $P = (M_{piston} + M_{weights}) \cdot g / A_0$.
> 4. **Confusion Trap:** Piezoresistive ≠ Piezoelectric. Writing "piezoelectric" when asked about piezoresistive costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Formula Trap:** For capacitive sensors, do not write $C = \varepsilon A / d^2$. The correct form is $C = \varepsilon A / d$. Examiners are strict on this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (Print & Memorize Before Exam):**

- [ ] A **diaphragm** is a thin clamped plate; a **capsule** is two diaphragms welded together; a **bellows** is a stack of capsules.
- [ ] **Flat diaphragm** central deflection: $w_{max} = 3 P R^4 (1-\nu^2) / (16 E h^3)$. **Max stress at edge:** $\sigma = 3 P R^2 / (8 h^2)$.
- [ ] **Corrugated diaphragms** have 3–5× larger linear range than flat ones.
- [ ] **LVDT** output is linear in core displacement: $V_{out} = K \cdot x$. **Phase** indicates direction.
- [ ] **LVDT AC excitation:** 1–10 V RMS, 1–20 kHz.
- [ ] **Strain gauge equation:** $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$.
- [ ] **Gauge factor** for metal ≈ 2.0; for silicon ≈ 100–170.
- [ ] **Quarter-bridge** output: $V_o = V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon / 4$. **Half-bridge:** factor is $1/2$. **Full-bridge:** factor is $1$.
- [ ] **Capacitive sensor:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$. Small-signal sensitivity: $\Delta C / C_0 \approx \Delta d / d_0$.
- [ ] **Piezoresistive ≠ Piezoelectric.** Piezoresistive changes resistance; piezoelectric generates charge.
- [ ] **Resonator sensor** output is **frequency** (digital, noise-immune). Stress $\uparrow$ $\Rightarrow$ $f$ $\uparrow$.
- [ ] **Dead-Weight Piston equation:** $P = M g / A_0 (1 + \lambda P)$. Standard $g = 9.80665$ m/s².
- [ ] **DWP gauge** is the **primary standard** for pressure — accuracy ±0.005% to ±0.02%.
- [ ] **DWP range:** up to 2500 bar; made of tungsten carbide for high-pressure versions.
- [ ] **Most common KTU mistake:** forgetting the bridge factor (4, 2, or 1) in strain-gauge output calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mnemonics:**
> - **"D-C-B-B"** = **D**iaphragm → **C**apsule → **B**ellows → **B**ig deflection (each step doubles the motion).
> - **"F G H"** strain-gauge bridges = **F**ull (1), **H**alf (½), **Q**uarter (¼) — factor in front of $V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon$.
> - **"PieZo = Zero resistance change; PieZoResistive = Resistance change."** (Trick: "Resistive" contains "R" for Resistance.)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Modulus Cells, Measurement of Vacuum: McLeod Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bulk Modulus Cells, Measurement of Vacuum: McLeod Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bulk Modulus Cells — Formal Definition

A **Bulk Modulus Cell** (also termed a *dead-weight pressure tester* in some standards, but more accurately classified as a *hydraulic elastic-pressure transducer*) is a primary pressure-sensing element in which the applied pressure produces a measurable elastic deformation of a confined fluid volume. The underlying physical law is the definition of the **Bulk Modulus of Elasticity (K)**:

$$K = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{dV}$$

The negative sign signifies that an *increase* in pressure causes a *decrease* in volume. The cell converts a small change in fluid volume under pressure into a displacement, which is then translated by a secondary transducer (LVDT, strain gauge, or capacitive pick-up) into an electrical output proportional to pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bulk Modulus Cells are categorised under *Mechanical/Elastic-Type Pressure Transducers* (Module 2 of OEICT833). The cell is the **sensing element** — not the complete transducer. A full transducer requires a secondary displacement-to-electrical converter.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Squeezed Water Balloon"

Imagine a sealed, thick-walled rubber balloon completely filled with water, connected to a thin capillary tube. If you press on the balloon (apply pressure), the water compresses *very slightly* and the water level in the capillary rises. The **rise in the capillary** is the measurable signal.

- The **balloon** = the Bulk Modulus Cell
- The **water** = the incompressible working fluid (often silicone oil, mercury, or de-ionised water)
- The **capillary rise** = the displacement that the secondary transducer reads

The *stiffness* of the balloon (its resistance to compression) is what defines the Bulk Modulus. A stiffer cell gives a smaller displacement — so for low-pressure measurement, you need a more compliant (softer) cell, and vice versa.

> [!NOTE]
> The working fluid is selected so that its bulk modulus is **highly stable over the temperature range** of operation. For industrial cells, this is typically **silicone oil (K ≈ 1.5 GPa)** or **mercury (K ≈ 25 GPa)**.

## 1.3 Vacuum — Formal Definition

**Vacuum** is defined as any pressure that is *below atmospheric pressure* (i.e., less than **101.325 kPa** or **760 Torr** at standard conditions). The degree of vacuum is quantified by the *absolute pressure* of the residual gas in the evacuated space.

| Vacuum Regime | Pressure Range (Torr) | Pressure Range (Pa) |
|---|---|---|
| **Rough Vacuum** | 760 → 1 | 1.013 × 10⁵ → 1.33 × 10² |
| **Medium Vacuum** | 1 → 10⁻³ | 1.33 × 10² → 1.33 × 10⁻¹ |
| **High Vacuum** | 10⁻³ → 10⁻⁶ | 1.33 × 10⁻¹ → 1.33 × 10⁻⁴ |
| **Ultra-High Vacuum (UHV)** | < 10⁻⁶ | < 1.33 × 10⁻⁴ |

## 1.4 McLeod Gauge — Formal Definition

The **McLeod Gauge** is a *primary absolute vacuum measuring instrument* based on the application of **Boyle's Law** of ideal gases. It traps a known volume of gas at the unknown low pressure, compresses it isothermally into a much smaller volume, and measures the compressed volume or column height. The unknown pressure is then calculated by direct application of $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The McLeod Gauge is a **primary standard** for vacuum calibration in the range **10⁻⁵ Torr to 10 Torr**. It is a *manometric* device and does **not** require prior calibration against another instrument.

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — "The Syringe Trap"

Picture a syringe connected to the vacuum chamber. You draw a fixed amount of gas from the chamber into the syringe, then *plonk* your finger over the nozzle and compress the plunger all the way down. The trapped gas, originally filling the whole chamber at very low pressure, is now squashed into a tiny space — so its pressure has shot up and can be read on a regular gauge. The McLeod Gauge is exactly this principle, built with mercury as the compression piston and a sealed capillary as the tiny volume.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bulk Modulus Cells — Classification and Operating Principle

### 2.1.1 Types of Bulk Modulus Cells

Bulk modulus cells are typically classified by the **geometry of the elastic element** that contains the working fluid:

1. **Bellows-Type Bulk Modulus Cell**
   - A metallic bellows is filled with fluid.
   - External pressure squeezes the bellows, displacing the fluid into a capillary.
   - Displacement is read by an LVDT or capacitive probe.

2. **Diaphragm-Type Bulk Modulus Cell**
   - A flexible diaphragm forms one wall of a fluid-filled chamber.
   - Pressure-induced deflection of the diaphragm pushes fluid into a secondary volume.
   - Often used as a *differential* pressure sensor.

3. **Spiral-Bourdon / C-Spring Type**
   - Less common as a *bulk modulus* device, but when filled with fluid, behaves similarly.

### 2.1.2 Working Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. The unknown pressure $P_{in}$ is applied to the outer face of the elastic element (bellows or diaphragm).
2. The pressure is transmitted hydraulically through the working fluid to the inner cavity.
3. The fluid volume decreases by $\Delta V$ according to the bulk modulus equation.
4. This volume change causes a displacement $\Delta x$ in a sensing element (a piston or a column of fluid in a capillary).
5. A secondary transducer converts $\Delta x$ into an electrical signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Choice:** The *hydraulic fluid* is the heart of the cell. Its bulk modulus determines the sensitivity. For high-pressure applications, mercury (K ≈ **25 GPa**) is preferred. For differential/low-pressure work, silicone oil (K ≈ **1.5 GPa**) gives higher sensitivity.

### 2.1.3 The Governing Equation

Starting from the definition of bulk modulus:

$$K = -V \frac{dP}{dV}$$

Rearranging:

$$dV = -\frac{V}{K} dP$$

Integrating for a finite pressure change $\Delta P$:

$$\Delta V = -\frac{V_0}{K} \Delta P$$

The negative sign indicates volume reduction with rising pressure. The **volumetric sensitivity** is:

$$S_v = \left| \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P} \right| = \frac{V_0}{K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sensitivity is **directly proportional to initial volume** and **inversely proportional to bulk modulus**. To increase sensitivity, designers use a larger cell volume with a *low-K* fluid.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility of Bulk Modulus Cells

- **Aircraft Altimeters & Air Data Computers:** The aneroid capsules are essentially bulk-modulus cells where the trapped gas is the working medium.
- **Process Industries:** Differential pressure flowmeters (orifice + DP cell) use bulk-modulus sensing.
- **Barometric Measurement:** Weather station barographs.
- **Hydraulic Systems:** Pressure monitoring where galvanic isolation from the process fluid is required.

---

## 2.2 McLeod Gauge — Operating Principle

### 2.2.1 Constructional Features

A McLeod Gauge consists of the following sub-assemblies:

1. **Bulb (B):** A glass sphere of precisely known volume $V_b$ — typically **100 cm³ to 500 cm³**.
2. **Closed Capillary (C₁):** A precision-bore capillary tube sealed at its top, mounted directly above the bulb.
3. **Open Capillary (C₂):** A second capillary, open to atmosphere, mounted parallel to C₁ for *level-comparison* measurement.
4. **Reference Capillary (R):** A third capillary that mirrors the volume of C₁ to **cancel the mercury meniscus error** (the *R needle* correction).
5. **Mercury Reservoir (M):** A large glass bulb at the bottom that can be raised or lowered via an external connection (often to a vacuum line or via a flexible bellows).
6. **Vacuum Connection (P):** A side-arm connecting the gauge bulb to the vacuum system being measured.

### 2.2.2 Operating Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. The gauge is first *evacuated* to the same pressure as the vacuum system (P) — i.e., the mercury is fully lowered so the bulb is empty.
2. Mercury is then **raised** in the gauge by admitting atmospheric pressure to the reservoir M.
3. As mercury rises, it **isolates** a known volume $V_b$ of gas at the unknown pressure $P_u$.
4. The trapped gas is then **compressed isothermally** into the closed capillary C₁ as mercury continues to rise.
5. The compression continues until the mercury in the open capillary C₂ reaches a *reference mark* (set at the same height as the closed end of C₁).
6. At this point, the difference in mercury heights — the **closed-end to open-end** height difference $h$ — is read on a scale.
7. The **compressed volume** of gas is $V_c = A \cdot L$, where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the capillary and $L$ is the length of the gas column in C₁.

### 2.2.3 Derivation of the McLeod Gauge Equation

**Assumption:** The trapped gas obeys the ideal gas law, and compression is isothermal.

**Step 1 — Initial state (gas in bulb at unknown pressure):**

$$P_1 = P_u, \quad V_1 = V_b$$

**Step 2 — Final state (gas compressed into the closed capillary):**

The gas is compressed to a *smaller* volume $V_2 = A \cdot L$ at a *higher* pressure $P_2$.

By Boyle's Law (isothermal):

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

$$P_u \cdot V_b = P_2 \cdot A \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Relating $P_2$ to the measured column height:**

The compressed gas pushes the mercury down. The pressure of the gas in the closed capillary equals the *difference* between atmospheric pressure pushing down the open capillary and the mercury column difference $h$:

$$P_2 = P_{atm} - h \cdot \rho \cdot g \quad \text{(in absolute pressure units)}$$

But since the measurement is performed under vacuum conditions, the *gauge* of $h$ gives the difference directly. In vacuum gauge practice, we treat the difference as $P_2 = h$ (when $h$ is in Torr and mercury density is the reference density).

For low-vacuum work, $P_{atm} \gg h$, and the equation reduces to the *classical* McLeod form:

$$P_u \cdot V_b = h \cdot A \cdot L$$

Since $L = V_b / A$ is the *length* of the capillary that the gas would occupy at atmospheric pressure — this is the **capillary constant** — we get:

$$\boxed{P_u = \frac{A \cdot L^2}{V_b} = K \cdot L^2}$$

where $K = A/V_b$ is the **gauge constant** of the McLeod gauge.

### 2.2.4 Two Standard Scale Conventions

**Type 1 — Constant Volume (Closed Capillary Scale):**
- Mercury in the closed capillary is brought to a *fixed* reference mark.
- The compressed volume $V_c$ is constant; the *level difference* $h$ in the open capillary is the measured variable.
- Equation: $P_u = A \cdot h^2 / V_b$

**Type 2 — Constant Pressure (Open Capillary Scale):**
- Mercury in the open capillary is brought to a *fixed* reference mark.
- The compressed length $L$ in the closed capillary is the measured variable.
- Equation: $P_u = K \cdot L^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Most-Asked Result:** The pressure measured by a McLeod Gauge is **proportional to the SQUARE of the column length (or height difference)**. This $P \propto L^2$ or $P \propto h^2$ scaling is the *signature* of the McLeod Gauge and is the most-frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 2.

### 2.2.5 The "R-Needle" Correction

If the mercury in the closed capillary is not exactly at the *zero mark*, the meniscus error becomes significant. To correct this, a **reference capillary R** of the same bore as C₁ is mounted beside it. The reading of the *level in R* is added to the level in C₁, effectively ensuring that the *meniscus* at the top of C₁ is consistently read.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Table** — All key formulas for this topic in one place. Memorise this for KTU Module 2 viva and ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols / Units | Boundary / Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bulk Modulus Definition | $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$ | K in Pa, V in m³ | $K > 0$ always for stable fluids |
| 2 | Volumetric Sensitivity of Cell | $S_v = \dfrac{V_0}{K}$ | m³/Pa | Linear only for small $\Delta P$ |
| 3 | Displacement in a Piston Cell | $\Delta x = \dfrac{V_0}{K \cdot A_p} \cdot \Delta P$ | $\Delta x$ in m, $A_p$ = piston area | Piston is frictionless |
| 4 | Boyle's Law (isothermal) | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | P in Pa, V in m³ | Ideal gas only |
| 5 | McLeod: Constant-Volume Form | $P_u = \dfrac{A \cdot h^2}{V_b}$ | $P_u$ in Torr (or Pa) | $A, V_b$ known precisely |
| 6 | McLeod: Constant-Pressure Form | $P_u = K \cdot L^2$, with $K = A/V_b$ | L in mm, $K$ in Torr/mm² | $L \le$ full capillary length |
| 7 | McLeod Capillary Constant | $K = \dfrac{A}{V_b}$ | $K$ in 1/length² | Dimensioned by manufacturer |
| 8 | Gas Mass Trapped (if needed) | $m = P_u V_b \cdot \dfrac{M}{R T}$ | M = molar mass, R = 8.314 J/mol·K | Isothermal compression |
| 9 | Compressed Volume | $V_c = A \cdot L$ | m³ | $L$ = length of gas column |
| 10 | Mercury Column to Pressure | $P = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$ | $P$ in Pa, $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \times 10^3$ kg/m³ | h in metres |

> [!NOTE]
> The two classical forms of the McLeod equation both lead to a **square-law** relationship between the measured column and the unknown pressure. This is a critical point for KTU.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility — Where McLeod Gauges Are Used

1. **Calibration Standard:** McLeod gauges are the *de facto* primary standard for calibrating other vacuum gauges (Pirani, Penning, ionisation gauges) in the **1 Torr to 10⁻⁵ Torr** range.
2. **Vacuum Metallurgy & Coating:** Reference gauge in thin-film deposition systems.
3. **Space Simulation Chambers:** Used in laboratory leak-testing.
4. **Cryogenic & UHV Research:** As a cross-check, although they cannot measure below 10⁻⁵ Torr (mercury vapour limits).

> [!WARNING]
> The McLeod Gauge **fails** for gases that *condense* (e.g., water vapour, CO₂ at high pressure ratios) because the Boyle's-Law assumption is violated. This is the most common examiner trap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensitivity of a Bulk Modulus Cell with a Piston Read-out

**Scenario:** A cylindrical bulk modulus cell of internal volume $V_0$ is filled with a fluid of bulk modulus $K$. A frictionless piston of cross-sectional area $A_p$ is fitted to the cell. A pressure $\Delta P$ is applied.

**Goal:** Find the piston displacement $\Delta x$ in terms of $V_0$, $K$, $A_p$, and $\Delta P$.

**Step 1:** Start from the bulk modulus definition:

$$K = -V \frac{dP}{dV}$$

For finite changes, replace differentials with deltas:

$$K = -V_0 \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\Delta V$:

$$\Delta V = -\frac{V_0 \cdot \Delta P}{K}$$

**Step 3:** The volume swept by the moving piston equals the volumetric compression of the fluid:

$$|\Delta V| = A_p \cdot \Delta x$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions:

$$A_p \cdot \Delta x = \frac{V_0 \cdot \Delta P}{K}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the displacement $\Delta x$:

$$\boxed{\Delta x = \frac{V_0}{K \cdot A_p} \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Step 6 — Sensitivity coefficient:**

$$S = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta P} = \frac{V_0}{K \cdot A_p}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical sanity check (example):**
- $V_0 = 10$ cm³ $= 10^{-5}$ m³
- $K = 1.5 \times 10^9$ Pa (silicone oil)
- $A_p = 1$ cm² $= 10^{-4}$ m²
- $\Delta P = 10$ kPa $= 10^4$ Pa

$$\Delta x = \frac{10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^9 \cdot 10^{-4}} \times 10^4 = \frac{10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^5} \times 10^4$$

$$\Delta x = 6.67 \times 10^{-10} \times 10^4 = 6.67 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 6.67 \; \mu m$$

This 6.67 μm displacement is easily resolved by an LVDT — confirming the practicality of the design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — McLeod Gauge Pressure Equation (Full)

**Setup:** A McLeod gauge has a bulb of volume $V_b$, a closed capillary C₁ of uniform cross-section $A$, and an open comparison capillary C₂. A gas at unknown pressure $P_u$ (the vacuum system) is first isolated in the bulb, then compressed into C₁ by raising mercury. The compression is stopped when the mercury in C₂ aligns with the *sealed end* of C₁. The gas column in C₁ has length $L$, and the mercury level in C₂ is at a height $h$ *below* the sealed end of C₁.

**Goal:** Derive the relation between $P_u$, $L$, and $h$.

**Step 1 — State 1 (gas isolated in bulb, mercury just closed off the bulb):**

$$P_1 = P_u, \quad V_1 = V_b$$

**Step 2 — State 2 (gas compressed into capillary C₁):**

After compression:
- The gas occupies only the closed capillary C₁, from its sealed end down to the mercury meniscus.
- Volume of gas: $V_2 = A \cdot L$
- Pressure of gas: $P_2 = ?$ (to be determined from mercury column heights)

**Step 3 — Apply Boyle's Law (isothermal compression):**

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

$$P_u \cdot V_b = P_2 \cdot A \cdot L \quad \quad (1)$$

**Step 4 — Pressure balance in the manometer:**

The compressed gas in C₁ pushes the mercury down. The pressure in the open capillary C₂ at the same horizontal level as the gas-mercury interface in C₁ is the *atmospheric* pressure. The difference in mercury column heights between the open capillary C₂ and the closed capillary C₁ represents the *gauge* pressure of the compressed gas (above the vacuum reference, which is essentially the same as the system vacuum):

$$P_2 = P_{atm} - \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h \quad \quad (2)$$

**Step 5 — For low-vacuum regime (where $P_u \ll P_{atm}$):**

The compressed-gas pressure $P_2$ is small compared to atmospheric, so $P_2 \approx \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$. When the column height $h$ is measured in **Torr** (1 Torr = 1 mmHg), the conversion is implicit: $h$ in mm = pressure in Torr.

$$P_2 = h \quad \text{[in consistent units, e.g., mmHg or Torr]} \quad \quad (3)$$

**Step 6 — Combine (1) and (3):**

$$P_u \cdot V_b = h \cdot A \cdot L$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the unknown pressure:**

$$P_u = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot h}{V_b} \quad \quad (4)$$

**Step 8 — Use the geometry to eliminate $L$.**

In the **constant-volume** configuration, $L$ is held constant (it is the length of the closed capillary to a fixed mark), and the variable measured is $h$. Setting $L$ = constant = $L_0$:

$$\boxed{P_u = \frac{A \cdot L_0}{V_b} \cdot h = K \cdot h}$$

where $K = A \cdot L_0 / V_b$ is the gauge constant.

> Wait — this gives a *linear* relation, not a *square* law. The square-law appears in the **constant-pressure** form, which is the *standard* KTU derivation. Let us continue with that case.

**Step 9 — In the constant-pressure configuration, the mercury in C₂ is brought to a fixed reference level.** The closed-capillary length $L$ is the variable measured. From the geometry of a U-tube where both capillaries have equal cross-section $A$:

$$h = L$$

(The mercury that left the bulb and entered C₂ rose by exactly the same amount that the gas column in C₁ is short, by conservation of mercury volume.)

**Step 10 — Substitute $h = L$ in (4):**

$$P_u = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot L}{V_b} = \frac{A \cdot L^2}{V_b}$$

$$\boxed{P_u = \frac{A}{V_b} \cdot L^2 = K \cdot L^2}$$

This is the **canonical McLeod equation** and shows the characteristic **square-law** scaling.

**Step 11 — Numerical example (KTU-style problem):**

Given: $V_b = 200$ cm³, $A = 1$ mm² = $10^{-6}$ m², observed gas column $L = 100$ mm = 0.1 m.

Gauge constant:

$$K = \frac{A}{V_b} = \frac{10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{-2}$$

$$P_u = K \cdot L^2 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.1)^2 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{(in units of m-Hg)}$$

Converting to Torr: 1 m-Hg ≈ 1000 Torr, so

$$P_u = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000 = 0.05 \text{ Torr} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Torr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked to find the **smallest pressure measurable**, plug in the *minimum* readable $L$ (typically 0.1 mm) into the equation. This gives the *lowest limit* of the gauge.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error in the McLeod Gauge

| # | Error Source | Mechanism | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mercury vapour effect** | Hg has finite vapour pressure (~10⁻³ Torr at 25 °C) | Adds to trapped-gas pressure at low $P_u$ | Use cooled/ refrigerated mercury; high-vacuum correction |
| 2 | **Capillary depression** | Mercury meniscus curvature depends on bore | Few % of reading | Use wide-bore capillaries; calibrate |
| 3 | **Condensable vapours** | Water vapour condenses under compression | Reading artificially low | Use a cold trap upstream |
| 4 | **Non-isothermal compression** | Heat of compression raises gas T | ~1% error | Allow time to equilibrate; adiabatic correction factor |
| 5 | **Capillary bore variation** | Glass tubes are not perfectly uniform | Affects $A$ and thus $K$ | Use precision-bore tubing; calibrate $K$ |
| 6 | **R-needle / meniscus error** | Meniscus shape in C₁ vs C₂ | Few % of $h$ | Use a reference capillary R |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The McLeod Gauge is *inherently* a **manometric** device — it measures *total* pressure regardless of gas composition, which is why it is used as a *calibration reference*. However, it is **blind to condensable species**, which is the main limitation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Bulk Modulus Cell Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Pressure Pin] --> B[Elastic Element]
    B --> C[Working Fluid Chamber]
    C --> D[Piston or Bellows]
    D --> E[Displacement Output]
    E --> F[LVDT Primary Coil]
    F --> G[Demodulator Circuit]
    G --> H[Amplifier Stage]
    H --> I[Display or DCS Output]
    J[Reference Vacuum] --> K[Compensation Block]
    K --> G
```

**Functional Description of Each Block:**

| Node ID | Block Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A | Process Pressure Pin | Where the unknown pressure is admitted |
| B | Elastic Element | Diaphragm or bellows that flexes under pressure |
| C | Working Fluid Chamber | Sealed volume of oil/mercury |
| D | Piston or Bellows | Converts fluid compression to mechanical motion |
| E | Displacement Output | Linear motion proportional to pressure |
| F | LVDT Primary Coil | Inductive pick-up for displacement |
| G | Demodulator Circuit | Converts AC LVDT output to DC |
| H | Amplifier Stage | Boosts signal for transmission |
| I | Display or DCS Output | Final user interface |
| J | Reference Vacuum | Used for differential / absolute measurement |
| K | Compensation Block | Temperature / drift compensation |

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — McLeod Gauge (Constant-Pressure Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Vacuum System Connection P] --> B[Bulb Volume Vb]
    B --> C1[Closed Capillary C1]
    B --> C2[Open Comparison Capillary C2]
    B --> C3[Reference Capillary R]
    M[Mercury Reservoir M] --> B
    ATM[Atmospheric Pressure] --> C2
    SCALE[Millimetre Scale h and L] --> C1
    SCALE --> C2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- **P** is the inlet from the vacuum chamber.
- **B** is the glass bulb of precisely known volume $V_b$.
- **C1** is the closed capillary; the gas is compressed into it. Length $L$ is measured on the scale.
- **C2** is the open capillary at atmospheric pressure; it is used for the level comparison.
- **C3** is the *reference capillary* (R-needle) for meniscus correction.
- **M** is the mercury reservoir raised/lowered to perform the compression.
- **SCALE** is the linear millimetre scale read by the operator.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operating Sequence of the McLeod Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Evacuate the gauge to system pressure] --> S2[Step 2: Isolate gas sample of volume Vb in bulb]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Raise mercury reservoir M]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Trap gas at unknown pressure Pu]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compress gas isothermally into C1]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Mercury in C2 reaches reference level]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Read column length L in C1]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Apply Boyle law Pu = A L squared divided by Vb]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute and report Pu in Torr or Pa]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Bulk Modulus Cell vs McLeod Gauge (Application Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRESSURE_DOMAIN[Pressure Measurement Domain]
    D1[Medium to High Pressure 1 bar to 1000 bar]
    D2[Low Absolute Pressure 1 mbar to 1 microbar]
    end
    PRESSURE_DOMAIN --> CELLDECISION{Measurement Requirement}
    D1 --> CELLDECISION
    D2 --> CELLDECISION
    CELLDECISION -->|Continuous electrical output| BMC[Bulk Modulus Cell with LVDT or strain gauge readout]
    CELLDECISION -->|Absolute calibration reference| MCG[McLeod Gauge with mercury manometer readout]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **Bulk Modulus Cell** route is taken when you need a *continuous, electronic* pressure signal (process control, automation).
- The **McLeod Gauge** route is taken when you need a *calibration-grade absolute* reading in a laboratory or vacuum-system context.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern. Marks are distributed as per the official template: **Part A (3 marks each)**, **Part B (14 marks each, with internal choice)**.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Bulk Modulus Cell". Mention the role of the working fluid in such a cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Bulk Modulus Cell** is a primary pressure-sensing element in which the applied pressure produces a small, measurable elastic deformation of a confined fluid volume. It works on the principle that for a given fluid of bulk modulus K, the volumetric strain $\Delta V / V$ is proportional to the applied pressure $\Delta P$:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = -\frac{\Delta P}{K}$$

**Role of the working fluid (2 marks for explanation):**

1. **Pressure Transmission:** The fluid transmits the external pressure uniformly to the elastic element (piston, bellows or diaphragm), so the cell acts as a *hydraulic coupler* between the process and the secondary transducer.
2. **Sensitivity Determinant:** The bulk modulus K of the fluid directly sets the cell sensitivity $S_v = V_0 / K$. A fluid with a *low* K (e.g., silicone oil, **K ≈ 1.5 GPa**) gives higher displacement per unit pressure than mercury (K ≈ 25 GPa).
3. **Galvanic Isolation:** The fluid isolates the process medium from the secondary transducer, protecting the electronics from corrosive or hot process fluids.
4. **Temperature Stability:** The fluid is chosen for a stable bulk modulus over the operating temperature range to minimise thermal drift.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner awards the third mark specifically for the *sensitivity* role — do not omit it.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is the McLeod Gauge considered a "primary standard" for vacuum measurement? State its working range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The McLeod Gauge is classified as a **primary standard** because its measurement is based directly on the **ideal gas law (Boyle's Law)**, an absolute physical law that does not require prior calibration against any other instrument. *(1 mark)*

The unknown pressure is calculated *ab initio* from measurable geometric quantities (bulb volume, capillary cross-section, and column height) using:

$$P_u = \frac{A \cdot L^2}{V_b}$$

Since $A$, $L$, and $V_b$ are all traceable to fundamental SI standards of length, the resulting pressure is *traceable to primary standards* without any intermediate calibration. *(1 mark)*

**Working Range:** From approximately **10 Torr (rough vacuum) down to 10⁻⁵ Torr (high vacuum)**, i.e., spanning **six orders of magnitude**. The lower limit is set by the mercury vapour pressure; the upper limit is set by the practical capillary length. *(1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an *internal choice* between two sub-questions of 7 marks each. The model answers below give full step-by-step derivations.

---

### Question A. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Bulk Modulus Cell** used as a pressure transducer. Discuss the role of the working fluid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk modulus cell has a working fluid with bulk modulus **K = 2.0 × 10⁹ Pa**. The initial volume of fluid in the cell is **V₀ = 50 cm³**. A pressure of **ΔP = 5 × 10⁵ Pa** is applied. Calculate:
- (i) The change in volume of the fluid. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The displacement of a piston of cross-sectional area **Aₚ = 2 cm²** connected to the cell. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

A bulk modulus cell consists of:

1. **An elastic pressure-receiving element** — typically a diaphragm, bellows, or a piston-cylinder assembly.
2. **A working fluid chamber** — sealed, filled with a fluid of known bulk modulus.
3. **A displacement transducer** — LVDT, strain gauge, or capacitive pick-up.
4. **A reference / compensation port** — for differential or absolute measurements.

**Working (logical flow):**
- The unknown pressure $P_{in}$ acts on the elastic element.
- The elastic element deforms, displacing the working fluid.
- The fluid transmits the pressure to a secondary displacement element (piston or column).
- The displacement is read by the secondary transducer and converted to an electrical output.

**Role of the working fluid (3 sub-points):**
- **Hydraulic transmission** of pressure.
- **Sets the sensitivity** via its bulk modulus K.
- **Galvanic isolation** of the secondary transducer from the process.

The clean block diagram from SECTION 4.1 above is the recommended illustration for full marks.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $K = 2.0 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $V_0 = 50$ cm³ $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
- $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $A_p = 2$ cm² $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m²

**Part (i) — Change in volume (3 marks):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_0 \cdot \Delta P}{K}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^5}{2.0 \times 10^9}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{2.5 \times 10^1}{2.0 \times 10^9} = \frac{25}{2.0 \times 10^9}$$

$$\Delta V = 12.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3$$

**Part (ii) — Piston displacement (4 marks):**

The displaced volume equals the swept volume of the piston:

$$A_p \cdot \Delta x = \Delta V$$

$$\Delta x = \frac{\Delta V}{A_p} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\Delta x = 6.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 62.5 \; \mu m$$

**Result:** The piston moves by **62.5 μm** for a 5 bar pressure change — easily measured by an LVDT.

---

### Question B. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a labelled schematic, describe the construction and working of a **McLeod Gauge**. State and derive the working equation for the *constant-pressure* (linear) type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A McLeod gauge has a bulb volume **V_b = 250 cm³** and a closed capillary of uniform cross-section **A = 0.5 mm²**. When the gauge is operated in the constant-pressure mode, the trapped gas column in the closed capillary is found to have a length **L = 80 mm**. Calculate:
- (i) The unknown pressure $P_u$ in **Torr** and **Pa**. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) The smallest pressure measurable if the smallest readable column length is **L_min = 0.5 mm**. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

A McLeod Gauge consists of:
- A glass **bulb (B)** of precisely known volume $V_b$.
- A **closed capillary (C₁)** mounted above the bulb, sealed at the top.
- An **open comparison capillary (C₂)** mounted beside C₁, open to atmosphere.
- A **reference capillary (R)** for meniscus correction.
- A **mercury reservoir (M)** at the base, connected to a flexible bellows or external line.
- A **vacuum connection (P)** to the system under test.

**Working:**
1. Initially, the gauge is evacuated to the system pressure; mercury is fully lowered.
2. Mercury is raised, isolating a known volume $V_b$ of gas at the unknown pressure $P_u$.
3. Continued mercury rise compresses the gas into C₁.
4. The compression is stopped when the mercury in C₂ reaches the level of the closed end of C₁ (the *reference* level).
5. The length of the gas column $L$ in C₁ is read on the millimetre scale.

**Derivation of working equation (constant-pressure type):**

Let the gas be initially at $(P_u, V_b)$ and finally compressed to $(P_2, A \cdot L)$ where $P_2$ is the gauge pressure of the compressed gas above the system vacuum. By Boyle's Law:

$$P_u \cdot V_b = P_2 \cdot A \cdot L \quad \quad (1)$$

The pressure of the compressed gas equals the mercury column difference $h$. In the constant-pressure (linear) configuration, the gas column length is the variable measured. By mercury volume conservation, the height difference $h$ equals $L$:

$$P_2 = h = L \quad \text{[in consistent units of length]} \quad \quad (2)$$

Substituting (2) into (1):

$$P_u \cdot V_b = L \cdot A \cdot L = A \cdot L^2$$

$$\boxed{P_u = \frac{A \cdot L^2}{V_b} = K \cdot L^2}$$

where $K = A / V_b$ is the **gauge constant** of the McLeod gauge.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_b = 250$ cm³ $= 250 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
- $A = 0.5$ mm² $= 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²
- $L = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m

**Part (i) — Unknown pressure (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the gauge constant K:**

$$K = \frac{A}{V_b} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the McLeod equation:**

$$P_u = K \cdot L^2 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.08)^2$$

$$P_u = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-3} = 1.28 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{(in metres of mercury)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Torr:** 1 metre of Hg = 1000 Torr

$$P_u = 1.28 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000 = 1.28 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{Torr} = 0.0128 \; \text{Torr}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Pa:** 1 Torr = 133.322 Pa

$$P_u = 0.0128 \times 133.322 = 1.7065 \; \text{Pa} \approx 1.71 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Part (ii) — Smallest measurable pressure (3 marks):**

Plug in $L_{min} = 0.5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m:

$$P_{u,min} = K \cdot L_{min}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times (5 \times 10^{-4})^2$$

$$P_{u,min} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \times 10^{-8} = 50 \times 10^{-11} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \; \text{m-Hg}$$

Converting to Torr:

$$P_{u,min} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \times 1000 = 5 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{Torr} = 0.5 \; \mu\text{Torr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This answer demonstrates the *six-decade* dynamic range of a McLeod gauge — from 10 Torr down to 10⁻⁶ Torr — by varying $L$ from ~150 mm to ~0.5 mm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the square law:** Many students write $P_u = A \cdot L / V_b$ (linear) instead of $P_u = A \cdot L^2 / V_b$. This loses 4 of 7 marks on the derivation.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in numerical:** If the capillary area is in mm² and the bulb volume in cm³, students often forget to *convert both to the same unit system* before dividing. Always convert to m² and m³ (or cm² and cm³) *consistently*.
> 3. **Missing the meniscus / R-needle:** A full construction description must mention the *reference capillary* and explain why it is needed. Skipping it loses 1 mark in Part (a).
> 4. **Ignoring the bulk modulus sign:** The equation $K = -V \, dP/dV$ has a *negative* sign. Students often drop it, leading to a sign error in the displacement formula.
> 5. **Confusing constant-volume and constant-pressure forms:** Be explicit about which configuration is being analysed. KTU examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting to state the assumption:** Always state *"isothermal compression, ideal gas behaviour, and uniform capillary bore"* in the McLeod derivation. These are 1-mark items in the marking scheme.

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Bulk Modulus Cells & McLeod Gauge**

### Bulk Modulus Cells
- A **Bulk Modulus Cell** is a fluid-filled elastic pressure sensor that converts pressure into volume change, governed by $K = -V \, dP/dV$.
- **Working fluid** choice is critical: silicone oil for high sensitivity, mercury for high stiffness, de-ionised water for general use.
- Sensitivity is $S_v = V_0 / K$ — large volume and low K both *increase* sensitivity.
- Piston displacement is $\Delta x = V_0 \cdot \Delta P / (K \cdot A_p)$.
- Cells are *primary pressure sensors*; they must be paired with a *secondary displacement transducer* (LVDT, strain gauge, capacitive).
- Real-world applications: altimeters, differential pressure flowmeters, barographs, hydraulic monitoring.

### McLeod Gauge
- A **primary, absolute, manometric vacuum standard** based on Boyle's Law.
- Construction: bulb + closed capillary + open capillary + reference capillary + mercury reservoir.
- The canonical equation (constant-pressure form) is $P_u = A \cdot L^2 / V_b = K \cdot L^2$.
- The alternative (constant-volume form) is $P_u = A \cdot h^2 / V_b$.
- Both forms show the **square-law** dependence on the measured length — *the signature of the McLeod gauge*.
- Working range: **10 Torr → 10⁻⁵ Torr** (six decades).
- **Limitations:** cannot measure condensable vapours (water, CO₂), mercury vapour adds error at UHV, requires careful temperature control.
- **R-needle / reference capillary** corrects the meniscus error in the closed capillary.
- Use the gauge for **calibration** of other vacuum gauges (Pirani, Penning, ionisation) and for leak-testing.

### Quick-Reference Equations (Memory)
- Bulk modulus definition: $K = -V \, dP/dV$
- Cell displacement: $\Delta x = V_0 \, \Delta P / (K \, A_p)$
- Boyle's Law: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$
- McLeod (constant-pressure): $P_u = A \cdot L^2 / V_b$
- McLeod (constant-volume): $P_u = A \cdot h^2 / V_b$
- Gauge constant: $K_{gauge} = A / V_b$
- Mercury-to-pressure: $P = \rho_{Hg} \, g \, h$

### Common KTU Traps to Avoid
- Sign error in bulk modulus equation.
- Linear vs. square-law confusion in McLeod.
- Unit inconsistency in numerical problems.
- Omitting the working fluid role in cell design questions.
- Forgetting to state the *isothermal* and *ideal gas* assumptions in McLeod derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Conductivity Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Conductivity Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Conductivity Gauge (TCG):** A secondary type of vacuum pressure transducer that infers gas pressure by exploiting the pressure-dependent thermal conductivity of the gas surrounding a heated sensing element. It belongs to the family of **indirect (inferential) vacuum gauges** and operates efficiently in the **medium to high vacuum regime** (approximately **10⁻⁴ mbar to 100 mbar**).

The two principal industrial variants of the thermal conductivity gauge are:

1. **Pirani Gauge** – senses the change in **electrical resistance** of a hot filament as its temperature varies with gas pressure.
2. **Thermocouple Gauge** – senses the change in **thermo-EMF (Seebeck voltage)** generated by a thermocouple junction welded to the hot filament.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the OEICT833 (Industrial Instrumentation) Module 2 syllabus, thermal conductivity gauges are grouped under *Vacuum Measurement* alongside the Bourdon, Bellows, Diaphragm, and McLeod gauges. Students are expected to derive the operating principle, sketch the bridge circuit, and explain the calibration characteristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small electric heater (the **filament**) glowing inside a sealed jar. Now, imagine pumping air out of the jar (creating a vacuum):

- When **lots of air molecules** are present (high pressure), they bump into the hot filament frequently and **carry heat away efficiently** → the filament cools down.
- When **very few air molecules** are present (low pressure / high vacuum), there are hardly any molecules to take the heat away → the filament stays **hot and bright**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English:** The Thermal Conductivity Gauge simply **watches how hot a tiny wire stays** when it is electrically heated. The hotter it stays, the better the vacuum. It does not directly count molecules — it infers pressure from the **ease with which the surrounding gas carries heat away**.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Filament material | **Tungsten (W)** or **Platinum (Pt)** |
| Operating filament temperature | **100 °C – 200 °C** (above ambient) |
| Measurement range | **10⁻⁴ mbar – 100 mbar** (≈ 10⁻⁴ – 75 torr) |
| Bridge excitation voltage | **6 V DC** (typical) |
| Time constant | **0.1 s – 10 s** |
| Sensitivity | **mV per decade of pressure change** |
| Output | **Analog voltage** (linearised to pressure) |
| Reference standard | **Physical constant: Stefan-Boltzmann σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** |

## 1.4 Physical Principle Underlying TCG Operation

The fundamental physics is **gas-phase heat conduction**, which is described by Fourier's Law for a gas at low pressure:

$$Q = \frac{K_{gas} \cdot A}{d} \cdot (T_{f} - T_{w})$$

where:
- $Q$ = rate of heat loss from filament (W)
- $K_{gas}$ = thermal conductivity of the gas (W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹)
- $A$ = surface area of filament (m²)
- $d$ = mean free path / distance to cold wall (m)
- $T_{f}$ = filament absolute temperature (K)
- $T_{w}$ = wall (envelope) absolute temperature (K)

The key insight is that at low pressures, the **mean free path of gas molecules** becomes comparable to or larger than the gap between the filament and the chamber wall. This makes the effective thermal conductivity **pressure-dependent**, which is the cornerstone of TCG operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why Thermal Conductivity Depends on Pressure

In gases, heat is transferred by molecular collisions. The efficiency of this process depends on two competing length scales:

1. The **mean free path** of gas molecules: $\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d_m^2 P}$
2. The **geometric gap** $d$ between the hot filament and the cold wall.

> **Two Regimes of Operation:**

### Regime 1 — High Pressure (Viscosity / Continuum Regime)
- $\lambda \ll d$
- Molecule-molecule collisions dominate.
- $K_{gas}$ is **independent of pressure** (depends only on gas species and temperature).
- The gauge becomes **insensitive** → upper measurement limit.

### Regime 2 — Low Pressure (Molecular / Knudsen Regime)
- $\lambda \gg d$
- Molecule-wall collisions dominate over molecule-molecule collisions.
- $K_{gas,eff} \propto P$
- The gauge is **most sensitive** → lower measurement limit reached when conductive heat loss is overwhelmed by radiative heat loss (the **lower limit of detection**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This pressure-dependence is what makes the TCG so useful — it provides a **continuous, analog, electrical readout of vacuum** suitable for feedback control of vacuum processes in semiconductor fabrication, metallurgy, freeze-drying, and particle accelerators.

## 2.2 Mechanism of the Pirani Gauge (Resistance-Based)

1. A **tungsten or platinum filament** is electrically heated to ~120 °C above the wall temperature.
2. The filament is one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge**.
3. The other three arms contain reference resistors (one or more placed inside a sealed reference vacuum tube to compensate for ambient temperature).
4. At balance, the bridge output is **zero**.
5. As pressure in the measurement chamber changes, $K_{gas}$ changes, $T_f$ changes, $R_f$ changes, the **bridge becomes unbalanced**, and the output voltage $V_{out}$ is read on a calibrated meter.

The filament resistance is a function of its absolute temperature:

$$R_f = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T_f - T_0) \right]$$

where:
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (often 0 °C = 273.15 K)
- $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient of resistance (for W: ~ 0.0045 /°C; for Pt: ~ 0.0039 /°C)

## 2.3 Mechanism of the Thermocouple Gauge (EMF-Based)

1. A **thermocouple junction** (typically Chromel-Alumel, i.e., **Type K**) is spot-welded to the centre of a heated filament.
2. The reference (cold) junction is held at the wall temperature.
3. The thermo-EMF generated is $E = S (T_f - T_w)$, where $S$ is the **Seebeck coefficient** (~ 40 µV/°C for Type K).
4. As pressure decreases, $T_f$ rises, and $E$ rises.
5. A micro-voltmeter reads the EMF, which is **calibrated in pressure units**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** a KTU 2024 student must memorise for this topic. Units are shown in brackets. Avoid using the vertical pipe `|` in the table.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Where Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{K_{gas} A}{d} (T_f - T_w)$ | Fourier heat conduction in gas | Core principle |
| 2 | $P \cdot V = n \cdot R_u \cdot T$ | Ideal gas law (link to $n$, the molecular density) | Connecting P to molecular count |
| 3 | $K_{gas,eff} = K_0 \cdot \dfrac{P}{P + P_0}$ | Effective thermal conductivity in transition regime | Calibration curve |
| 4 | $R_f = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T_f - T_0) \right]$ | Resistance vs. temperature of filament | Pirani gauge |
| 5 | $E = S \, (T_f - T_w)$ | Seebeck EMF of thermocouple | Thermocouple gauge |
| 6 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R_f}{R_f}$ | Wheatstone bridge output (approx.) | Pirani circuit |
| 7 | $P_{min} \approx \dfrac{4 \, \epsilon \, \sigma \, A_f \, T_f^3}{K_0 \, A_f / d}$ | Radiative lower limit of detection | Sensitivity analysis |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d_m^2 P}$ | Mean free path of gas | Regime classification |
| 9 | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{d}$ | Knudsen number (regime indicator) | Continuum vs. molecular |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{m_f c_p}{h A}$ | Thermal time constant of filament | Dynamic response |

**Key Physical Constants to Memorise:**

- $k_B$ (Boltzmann constant) = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $R_u$ (Universal gas constant) = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $\sigma$ (Stefan-Boltzmann constant) = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- $\epsilon$ (emissivity of tungsten) ≈ **0.3**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor industry:** Monitors chamber pressure during plasma etching, CVD, and PVD processes.
- **Vacuum metallurgy:** Controls degassing and melting under vacuum.
- **Lamp and CRT manufacturing:** Measures evacuation quality.
- **Space simulation chambers:** Monitors test vacuum.
- **Refrigeration / cryogenics:** Used as a low-cost, robust vacuum indicator.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Balance of the Filament at Steady State

At thermal steady state, the **electrical power input** equals the **sum of all heat loss mechanisms**.

**Step 1 — State the electrical power input** to the filament through its current $I_f$:

$$P_{in} = I_f^2 \cdot R_f \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 2 — State the heat loss mechanisms.** For a TCG, three modes exist:

1. **Conduction by gas:** $Q_{cond} = \dfrac{K_{gas} \, A_f}{d} (T_f - T_w)$
2. **Conduction through filament end-supports:** $Q_{sup} = K_{sup} (T_f - T_w)$ (often small)
3. **Radiation to the wall:** $Q_{rad} = \epsilon \, \sigma \, A_f \left( T_f^4 - T_w^4 \right)$

**Step 3 — Apply the energy balance** at steady state ($dT_f/dt = 0$):

$$I_f^2 R_f = \frac{K_{gas} A_f}{d} (T_f - T_w) + K_{sup} (T_f - T_w) + \epsilon \sigma A_f \left( T_f^4 - T_w^4 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Linearise the radiation term** for small $\Delta T = (T_f - T_w)$ around $T_w$ using the first two terms of the Taylor series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f^4 - T_w^4 &= (T_w + \Delta T)^4 - T_w^4 \\
&= 4 T_w^3 \Delta T + 6 T_w^2 \Delta T^2 + \ldots \\
&\approx 4 T_w^3 (T_f - T_w) \quad \text{(keeping only the first term)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute** the linearised radiation term back into the energy balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_f^2 R_f &= \frac{K_{gas} A_f}{d} (T_f - T_w) + K_{sup} (T_f - T_w) + 4 \epsilon \sigma A_f T_w^3 (T_f - T_w) \\
I_f^2 R_f &= \left[ \frac{K_{gas} A_f}{d} + K_{sup} + 4 \epsilon \sigma A_f T_w^3 \right] (T_f - T_w)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Define effective conductances** for compactness:

$$I_f^2 R_f = \left[ G_{gas}(P) + G_{sup} + G_{rad} \right] (T_f - T_w)$$

This is the **governing equation** of the TCG. The **only pressure-dependent term** is $G_{gas} = K_{gas}(P) \cdot A_f / d$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pirani Gauge Output Voltage

The Pirani filament is placed in **arm 1** of a Wheatstone bridge. The other three arms are fixed precision resistors $R_2, R_3, R_4$.

**Step 1 — General bridge output equation:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_f}{R_f + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Step 2 — At initial balance** (with high-vacuum reference), $R_f = R_{eq}$ and $V_{out} = 0$. For a symmetric bridge, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ and the balance condition $R_f = R$ is assumed.

**Step 3 — For a small change $\Delta R_f$** in the filament resistance, perform a Taylor expansion of the first term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_f + \Delta R_f}{R_f + \Delta R_f + R} &\approx \frac{R}{2R} + \frac{\Delta R_f}{2R} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R_f}{4R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the reference arm** (which stays at $1/2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_{ex} \left[ \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R_f}{4R} \right) - \frac{1}{2} \right] \\
V_{out} &= \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R_f}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Link $\Delta R_f$ to pressure** through the temperature dependence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R_f &= R_0 \, \alpha \, (T_f - T_{f,ref}) \\
&= R_0 \, \alpha \, \Delta T_f(P)
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **final pressure-dependent output** is:

$$V_{out}(P) = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{R_0 \, \alpha}{R} \cdot \Delta T_f(P)$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Sensitivity and Lower Limit of Detection

At the **lower limit of detection**, the gas-conduction term becomes so small that the **radiative term dominates**. Equating $G_{gas} = G_{rad}$:

$$\frac{K_{gas} A_f}{d} = 4 \epsilon \sigma A_f T_w^3$$

Solving for the **minimum detectable thermal conductivity**, and recalling that $K_{gas,eff} = K_0 P / (P + P_0)$:

$$K_{min} = 4 \epsilon \sigma T_w^3$$

$$P_{min} \approx \frac{K_{min} \cdot d}{K_0 \cdot A_f / A_f} = \frac{4 \epsilon \sigma T_w^3 d}{K_0}$$

**Numerical estimate (for air, $K_0 \approx 0.026$ W/m·K, $T_w = 300$ K, $d = 5$ mm, $\epsilon = 0.3$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{min} &= 4 \times 0.3 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (300)^3 \\
&= 1.224 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W/m·K} \\
P_{min} &= \frac{1.224 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.026} \\
&\approx 2.35 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa} \approx 1.76 \times 10^{-6} \text{ torr}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows why TCG **cannot measure ultra-high vacuum** — beyond ~10⁻⁴ mbar, the gauge saturates because radiation dominates.

## 3.4 Calibration Curve (Analytical)

A simplified two-parameter empirical fit to the calibration curve widely used in textbooks is:

$$V_{out} = \frac{a \cdot P}{1 + b \cdot P}$$

where:
- $a$ = sensitivity at low pressure (V per unit pressure)
- $b$ = roll-off constant (1 per unit pressure)

The **low-pressure asymptote** ($P \to 0$): $V_{out} \approx a P$ (linear region — useful).
The **high-pressure asymptote** ($P \to \infty$): $V_{out} \to a/b$ (saturates — measurement limit).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pirani Gauge System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vacuum Chamber] --> B[Pirani Sensor Head]
    B --> C[Hot Filament Tungsten Wire]
    B --> D[Reference Vacuum Tube]
    C --> E[Wheatstone Bridge Network]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Differential Amplifier]
    F --> G[Signal Conditioner and Lineariser]
    G --> H[Microcontroller ADC]
    H --> I[Digital Pressure Display]
    H --> J[Alarm and Trip Circuit]
    H --> K[Analog 4-20 mA Output to PLC]
    L[Precision Bridge Excitation 6V DC] --> E
    M[Ambient Temperature Sensor PT100] --> F
    style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#8b0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#006400,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#4b0082,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#b8860b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **Pirani sensor head** is mounted directly on the vacuum chamber.
- The **hot filament** sits in the chamber; the **reference tube** contains the same filament sealed at high vacuum to provide temperature compensation.
- Both filaments form opposite arms of the **Wheatstone bridge**.
- The differential output is amplified, linearised (because the raw response is non-linear — see calibration equation), and sent to the display and to the plant control system (PLC/DCS) via a **4-20 mA current loop** (industry standard).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Thermocouple Gauge Head (Internal Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heated Filament W or Pt] --> B[Type K Thermocouple Junction Spot Welded]
    B --> C[Cold Junction at Chamber Wall]
    C --> D[Thermo EMF Micro voltmeter]
    D --> E[Calibrated Pressure Scale mbar]
    F[Heater Current Source Constant] --> A
    style A fill:#8b0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#006400,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#4b0082,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:**

- A **constant heater current** keeps the electrical input to the filament constant — this is essential so that any change in filament temperature is **only due to gas conduction changes**, not due to supply variations.
- The thermo-EMF generated between the hot and cold junction is a direct measure of $(T_f - T_w)$.
- The micro-voltmeter is **factory-calibrated in pressure units** (mbar or torr).

## 4.3 Process Flow — Measurement Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Vacuum Process Starts]) --> T1[Step 1: Energise Filament to Set Current]
    T1 --> T2[Step 2: Wait for Thermal Equilibrium 2-5 sec]
    T2 --> T3[Step 3: Sense Filament Temperature T_f]
    T3 --> T4{Step 4: Compare to Reference T_ref}
    T4 --> T5[Step 5: Compute delta_T = T_f - T_ref]
    T5 --> T6[Step 6: Apply Calibration Polynomial to Obtain P]
    T6 --> T7[Step 7: Display P and Transmit to Controller]
    T7 --> T8{Pressure in Safe Range?}
    T8 --> T9[Continue Process] 
    T8 --> T10[Trigger Interlock and Alarm]
    T9 --> S
    T10 --> S
    style S fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T10 fill:#8b0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T9 fill:#006400,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:**

This diagram captures the **closed-loop measurement algorithm** used inside modern digital TCG controllers. The interlock step (T10) is the reason why TCGs are popular in safety-critical vacuum systems (e.g., semiconductor etch tools) — they provide **real-time fail-safe protection** against vacuum loss.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define a thermal conductivity gauge. State any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** A thermal conductivity gauge is a secondary vacuum measurement instrument that determines gas pressure by measuring the pressure-dependent thermal conductivity of the gas surrounding a heated filament.

> **Operating principle (1 mark):** It works on the principle that the rate of heat loss from a hot filament to the surrounding gas depends on the number of gas molecules present, which in turn depends on pressure. At low pressure, fewer molecules are available to conduct heat, so the filament runs hotter.

> **Applications (1 mark):**
> 1. Monitoring chamber pressure in **semiconductor plasma etching and CVD equipment**.
> 2. Controlling vacuum in **metallurgical melting and degassing furnaces**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between a Pirani gauge and a thermocouple gauge in terms of sensing principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pirani Gauge | Thermocouple Gauge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sensing element** | Filament whose **resistance** varies with temperature | Filament with **thermocouple junction** welded to it |
| **Measured variable** | Change in **electrical resistance** $\Delta R_f$ | Thermo-**EMF** generated (Seebeck effect) |
| **Readout circuit** | **Wheatstone bridge** + voltmeter | **Micro-voltmeter** (high-impedance) |
| **Output** | Bridge unbalance voltage | Direct EMF in µV |
| **Sensitivity** | Higher (resistance change is easier to detect) | Lower (requires very sensitive µV meter) |

> *(1 mark for clear statement of principle difference, 1 mark for sensing element, 1 mark for any other valid differentiating parameter such as output signal type or sensitivity.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Pirani gauge**. Derive the relationship between the bridge output voltage and the change in filament resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pirani gauge uses a tungsten filament of resistance $R_0 = 50 \, \Omega$ at 20 °C. The temperature coefficient of resistance of tungsten is $\alpha = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} /°C$. The filament is operated in a Wheatstone bridge with all other arms equal to 50 Ω, and the bridge excitation is $V_{ex} = 6$ V. When the chamber pressure changes, the filament temperature rises by 18 °C. Calculate: **(i)** the change in filament resistance, and **(ii)** the bridge output voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A **thin tungsten or platinum wire** (filament) is mounted inside a tube connected to the vacuum chamber.
- The filament is one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge**. The opposite arm contains an **identical filament sealed inside a reference vacuum tube** (pressure $\sim 10^{-6}$ mbar).
- The other two arms contain **precision fixed resistors**.
- A **DC excitation** (typically 6 V) is applied across the bridge diagonally.
- A **voltmeter or amplifier** is connected across the other diagonal to read the bridge unbalance.

**Working (4 marks):**
- The filaments are heated by the bridge current to ~120 °C above the wall.
- At high pressure (near atmospheric), the gas surrounding the measuring filament conducts heat away efficiently → filament temperature drops → its resistance drops → bridge is unbalanced.
- At very low pressure, gas conduction is poor → filament temperature rises → resistance rises → bridge output changes accordingly.
- The reference filament (in sealed vacuum) provides automatic **temperature compensation** for ambient changes.
- The bridge output voltage is **calibrated to read pressure directly**.

**Derivation of Bridge Output vs. $\Delta R_f$:**

The general bridge equation with $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_f}{R_f + R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$

For a small change $\Delta R_f$, using a first-order Taylor expansion around $R_f = R$:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R_f}{R_f}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $R_0 = 50 \, \Omega$ at $T_0 = 20 \, °C$
- $\alpha = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} /°C$
- All other bridge arms $R = 50 \, \Omega$
- $V_{ex} = 6$ V
- $\Delta T = 18 \, °C$

**(i) Change in filament resistance (3 marks):**

The change in resistance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R_f &= R_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \\
\Delta R_f &= 50 \times (4.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 18 \\
\Delta R_f &= 50 \times 0.081 \\
\Delta R_f &= 4.05 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Bridge output voltage (4 marks):**

The new filament resistance is $R_f = R_0 + \Delta R_f = 50 + 4.05 = 54.05 \, \Omega$.

The exact bridge output is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_f}{R_f + R} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right) \\
V_{out} &= 6 \left( \frac{54.05}{54.05 + 50} - \frac{50}{50 + 50} \right) \\
V_{out} &= 6 \left( \frac{54.05}{104.05} - 0.5 \right) \\
V_{out} &= 6 \left( 0.51946 - 0.5 \right) \\
V_{out} &= 6 \times 0.01946 \\
V_{out} &= 0.1168 \, \text{V} \approx 116.8 \, \text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-check using the linearised formula** (for small $\Delta R_f$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &\approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R_f}{R} \\
V_{out} &\approx \frac{6}{4} \cdot \frac{4.05}{50} \\
V_{out} &\approx 1.5 \times 0.081 \\
V_{out} &\approx 0.1215 \, \text{V} = 121.5 \, \text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

The two values agree to within ~4 %, which is acceptable given the approximation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to include the factor of **1/4** in the linearised bridge output formula. A common mistake is writing $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R_f / R$, which gives an answer that is **4 times too large**. Always derive the formula step by step before plugging in numbers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **thermocouple gauge**. Derive the expression for thermo-EMF in terms of filament and wall temperatures. State the typical Seebeck coefficient of a Type K thermocouple. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermocouple gauge is used in a vacuum chamber. The wall temperature is $T_w = 30 \, °C = 303$ K. The hot junction temperature is $T_f = 150 \, °C = 423$ K. The Seebeck coefficient of the Type K thermocouple is $S = 40 \, \mu V/°C$. Calculate the **thermo-EMF** generated. If the gauge is calibrated such that a pressure of 1 mbar corresponds to 5 mV, estimate the **measured pressure**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Thermocouple Gauge Principle and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
- A thermocouple gauge uses the **Seebeck effect** — when two dissimilar metals are joined and the junction is heated, an EMF is generated proportional to the temperature difference.
- A **Type K thermocouple** (Chromel-Alumel) junction is spot-welded to the centre of a heated filament.
- A **constant heater current** is supplied so that any change in filament temperature is due **only** to changes in gas conduction.
- As pressure decreases, fewer gas molecules carry away heat → filament temperature rises → thermo-EMF rises.

**Derivation of EMF (3 marks):**

The Seebeck EMF between the hot junction at $T_f$ and the cold junction at $T_w$ is:

$$E = \int_{T_w}^{T_f} S(T) \, dT$$

For a small range and approximately constant Seebeck coefficient:

$$\boxed{E = S \, (T_f - T_w)}$$

**Seebeck Coefficient (1 mark):** For a **Type K (Chromel-Alumel)** thermocouple, $S \approx 40 \, \mu V / °C$ (in the range 0-200 °C).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T_w = 303$ K, $T_f = 423$ K
- $S = 40 \, \mu V / °C = 40 \times 10^{-6} \, V/°C$
- Calibration: 1 mbar ↔ 5 mV

**(i) Thermo-EMF calculation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_f - T_w = 423 - 303 = 120 \, °C \\
E &= S \times \Delta T = 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 120 \\
E &= 4800 \times 10^{-6} \, V = 4.8 \, mV
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Pressure estimation (3 marks):**

Using the linear calibration (assuming the gauge is in its linear region):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{E}{\text{Sensitivity}} = \frac{E}{5 \, mV / mbar} \\
P &= \frac{4.8 \, mV}{5 \, mV / mbar} \\
P &= 0.96 \, mbar
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert the Seebeck coefficient from µV/°C to V/°C before multiplying, producing a value that is **one million times too small**. Always check the units first. Also, do not confuse the absolute temperatures (in K) with the temperature difference (in °C) — for a linear Seebeck coefficient, use °C; for integration over a non-linear $S(T)$, use K.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Exam-Ready**

- **Core Principle:** Heat loss from a hot filament to the surrounding gas is **pressure-dependent** at low pressures (molecular flow regime). This is the only principle behind both Pirani and Thermocouple gauges.

- **Measurement Range:** **10⁻⁴ mbar to 100 mbar**. Below 10⁻⁴ mbar, radiative heat loss dominates → gauge saturates. Above 100 mbar, gas thermal conductivity becomes pressure-independent → gauge saturates.

- **Two Variants:**
  - **Pirani → senses change in electrical resistance** of the filament. Uses a **Wheatstone bridge**.
  - **Thermocouple → senses change in thermo-EMF** (Seebeck effect). Uses a **micro-voltmeter**.

- **Filament Material:** **Tungsten** is preferred (high melting point, stable $\alpha$). Platinum is also used for corrosion resistance.

- **Key Equations to Memorise:**
  - $Q = K_{gas} A (T_f - T_w) / d$ (Fourier's law for gas)
  - $R_f = R_0 [1 + \alpha (T_f - T_0)]$ (filament R-T relation)
  - $E = S (T_f - T_w)$ (Seebeck EMF)
  - $V_{out} \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R_f}{R}$ (Pirani bridge output)
  - $K_{gas,eff} = K_0 \cdot \dfrac{P}{P + P_0}$ (effective thermal conductivity)

- **Calibration Curve Shape:** **Non-linear**. Linear at low P, saturates at high P. Often fit by $V = aP / (1 + bP)$.

- **Lower Limit of Detection:** Set by **radiative heat loss** from the filament. Cannot be improved by making the filament hotter (radiation grows as $T^4$).

- **Upper Limit of Detection:** Set by the transition from molecular to continuum regime, where $K_{gas}$ becomes pressure-independent.

- **Compensation:** A **reference filament in a sealed vacuum** is used in Pirani gauges to automatically cancel out ambient temperature fluctuations.

- **Constant Heater Current:** In a thermocouple gauge, the heater current must be **held constant** so that the EMF reading is solely a function of gas pressure, not supply variations.

- **Comparison with Other Vacuum Gauges:**
  - **Bourdon/Bellows/Diaphragm** → for **high vacuum / atmospheric** (mechanical).
  - **McLeod** → for **calibration reference** (manual, mercury).
  - **Pirani / Thermocouple** → for **continuous, electrical readout in medium vacuum**.

- **Industrial Output:** 4-20 mA current loop or digital display, suitable for **PLC/DCS integration**.

- **Most Common Mistake in Numerical Problems:** Forgetting the **1/4 factor** in the Pirani bridge output formula, or mixing up the **units of Seebeck coefficient** (µV/°C vs. V/°C).

> **Final Note:** In a KTU 2024 ESE answer, always draw a **neat labelled diagram**, **state the principle in the first sentence**, **derive the key equation**, and **end with a clear limitation statement** ("The gauge saturates below 10⁻⁴ mbar due to radiative heat loss"). This four-part structure is the **examiner's expected template** and guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
