topics.title: Ionization Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ionization Gauges # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ionization Gauge**: An ionization gauge is a **pressure measurement instrument** used in the **ultra-high vacuum (UHV) regime** (typically from **10⁻³ to 10⁻¹⁰ Torr** or lower). It operates on the principle of **ionization of residual gas molecules** by a stream of electrons and the subsequent measurement of the **positive ion current** produced, which is directly proportional to the number density of gas molecules — and hence to the absolute pressure of the vacuum. The device is fundamentally a specialized **vacuum triode** (or its modern derivatives) where: - A **heated filament (cathode)** emits electrons via thermionic emission. - These electrons are accelerated toward a positively biased **grid (anode)**. - During their transit, they collide with residual gas molecules, ionizing them. - The **positive ions** are collected by a low-potential **ion collector**. - The resulting **ion current ($I^+$)** is measured using a sensitive **electrometer amplifier**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Ionization gauges are classified into **two principal families** — **Hot Cathode (Thermionic) Ionization Gauges** and **Cold Cathode (Field Emission / Magnetron / Penning) Ionization Gauges**. Both must be studied in detail along with their construction, working principle, sensitivity equations, advantages, disadvantages, and limitations. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are standing in a **very foggy, enclosed room** and you want to estimate **how dense the fog is** (i.e., the number of water droplets per cubic meter of air). You have a **water pistol** that fires tiny invisible pellets (electrons) across the room. Some of these pellets **hit fog droplets** and knock them around, producing a measurable **splash** (ion current). Now, the denser the fog, the **more splashes** you record. If the fog is extremely thin, you get almost no splashes. **The Ionization Gauge works on exactly this principle:** | Real World Analogy | Gauge Equivalent | |---|---| | Foggy room | Vacuum chamber | | Water pistol | Hot filament emitting electrons | | Invisible pellets | Accelerated electrons (typically **100 eV**) | | Fog droplets | Residual gas molecules | | Splashes counted | Positive ion current ($I^+$) | | Dense fog (more droplets) | High pressure (more molecules) | | Thin fog (less droplets) | Low pressure (less molecules) | So basically: **More gas molecules in the chamber → More ion collisions → Higher ion current → Higher indicated pressure.** > [!TIP] > **Quick Memory Hook for KTU Exams**: **"More Pressure = More Molecules = More Ions = More Current"**. The relationship is essentially **linear** in the working range of the gauge. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and standard values are essential for KTU 2024 numerical problems: - **Electron charge**: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ - **Electron rest mass**: $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$ - **Permittivity of free space**: $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ - **Standard ionization energy of N₂**: $\approx 15.6\ \text{eV}$ - **Standard ionization energy of O₂**: $\approx 12.1\ \text{eV}$ - **Electron energy used in gauge**: typically **70 eV to 150 eV** (optimum near **100 eV**) - **Typical pressure range**: **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-10}$ Torr** (Hot cathode), **$10^{-5}$ to $10^{-11}$ Torr** (Cold cathode) - **1 Torr = 133.322 Pa** (SI conversion constant) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Ionization Gauges The complete taxonomy as per KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus is given below: ### A. Hot Cathode (Thermionic) Ionization Gauges 1. **Conventional Triode Gauge (e.g., VG-1A, Philips gauge)** 2. **Bayard-Alpert Gauge (BAG)** — Most widely used 3. **Modulated Bayard-Alpert Gauge (Extractor gauge, Helmer gauge)** ### B. Cold Cathode (Field Emission) Ionization Gauges 1. **Penning Gauge (Crossed E and B fields)** 2. **Magnetron Gauge** 3. **Inverted Magnetron Gauge** 4. **Diode Cold Cathode Gauge** --- ## 2.2 Hot Cathode Ionization Gauge — Operational Logic ### Construction - **Filament (Cathode)**: A tungsten or thoria-coated iridium wire heated to $\approx 2200\ \text{K}$. Emits electrons thermionically. - **Grid (Anode)**: A helical or cylindrical wire mesh surrounding the filament, held at a positive potential of **+100 V to +250 V** relative to the filament. - **Ion Collector**: A thin wire (in BAG) or a fine loop, held at ground or slightly negative potential ($\approx -25\ \text{V}$), placed at the **center of the grid** (BAG) or **outside the grid** (Triode). ### Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Thermionic Emission:** The heated filament emits electrons according to the **Richardson-Dushman equation**: $$J = A_R T^2 \exp\left(-\frac{W}{kT}\right)$$ where $J$ is the emission current density, $A_R$ is Richardson's constant, $W$ is the work function, and $T$ is filament temperature in Kelvin. **Step 2 — Electron Acceleration:** The emitted electrons are accelerated toward the positively biased grid, gaining kinetic energy of approximately: $$E_{e^-} = e \cdot V_{grid}$$ Typically $V_{grid} = +150\ \text{V}$, giving $E_{e^-} \approx 150\ \text{eV}$. **Step 3 — Ionization of Gas Molecules:** On their oscillatory trajectory between grid and filament, these energetic electrons collide with residual gas molecules. If the electron energy exceeds the **ionization potential** of the gas ($E_i \approx 12$–$16\ \text{eV}$), ionization occurs: $$e^- + M \longrightarrow M^+ + 2e^-$$ **Step 4 — Ion Collection:** The positive ions drift toward the low-potential ion collector, producing an ion current $I^+$. **Step 5 — Current Measurement:** $I^+$ is typically in the range of **$10^{-9}\ \text{A}$ to $10^{-14}\ \text{A}$** (nA to sub-pA) and is measured using a **vibrating reed electrometer** or **FET-input op-amp electrometer**. --- ## 2.3 The Fundamental Ionization Gauge Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Equation for KTU 2024 Exams**: > > $$I^{+} = I^{-} \cdot S \cdot P$$ > > where $I^{+}$ is the **positive ion current** (Amperes), $I^{-}$ is the **emission (electron) current** (Amperes), $S$ is the **gauge sensitivity** (Torr$^{-1}$), and $P$ is the **pressure** (Torr). Rearranged for pressure: $$P = \frac{I^{+}}{I^{-} \cdot S}$$ **Why is this equation linear?** The number of ionization events per unit path length is directly proportional to the gas number density $n$, which by the **ideal gas law** is directly proportional to pressure $P$ at constant temperature. --- ## 2.4 Bayard-Alpert Gauge (BAG) — The Modern Standard The Bayard-Alpert Gauge is an **improved version** of the conventional triode. In the conventional triode, the ion collector is **outside** the grid, which causes significant **X-ray induced photoemission** at the collector — creating a false "residual current" that limits the lowest measurable pressure to $\approx 10^{-8}$ Torr. **Key Innovations of BAG:** 1. **Inverted geometry**: The ion collector is a **thin wire placed at the CENTER of the grid** (or a fine axial filament). 2. **Reduced X-ray limit**: The X-ray induced photocurrent is reduced by a factor of $\approx 100$–$1000$. 3. **Lower pressure limit**: $\approx 10^{-10}$ Torr (X-ray limit) or $\approx 10^{-11}$ Torr (with modulation techniques). | Parameter | Conventional Triode | Bayard-Alpert | |---|---|---| | Collector position | Outside grid | Inside grid (center) | | X-ray limit | $\sim 10^{-8}$ Torr | $\sim 10^{-10}$ Torr | | Sensitivity (N₂) | $\approx 10$ Torr$^{-1}$ | $\approx 12$–$25$ Torr$^{-1}$ | | Geometry complexity | Simple | Moderate | --- ## 2.5 Cold Cathode Ionization Gauge — Penning Gauge ### Working Principle In a cold cathode gauge, no filament is used. Instead, a high voltage ($\approx 2$–$6\ \text{kV}$) is applied between a cathode and anode, and a **magnetic field** ($\approx 0.1$ to $0.2\ \text{T}$, i.e., 1000–2000 Gauss) is provided by a permanent magnet. The crossed **electric field ($\vec{E}$)** and **magnetic field ($\vec{B}$)** cause electrons to follow long, **spiral (helical) trajectories** between the cathode and anode. This greatly increases the path length and thus the probability of ionization. ### Penning Equation (Equivalent of Hot Cathode Equation): $$I^{+} = K \cdot P^{n}$$ where $K$ is a gauge constant and $n$ is typically between $1.0$ and $1.15$ (close to linear, but not perfectly linear). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility**: Cold cathode gauges are used where **stability, robustness, and absence of filament burnout** are critical — e.g., space simulation chambers, plasma processing equipment, particle accelerators, and semiconductor process tools. --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Formula Compilation for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination)** | # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary / Validity | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I^{+} = I^{-} \cdot S \cdot P$ | Master ionization gauge equation | $I^{+}$ in A, $I^{-}$ in A, $P$ in Torr | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-10}$ Torr (BAG) | | 2 | $P = \dfrac{I^{+}}{I^{-} \cdot S}$ | Pressure calculation from currents | Torr | Linear region only | | 3 | $J = A_R T^2 \exp(-W/kT)$ | Richardson-Dushman thermionic emission | A/m² | $T > 1500$ K | | 4 | $E_{e^-} = e \cdot V_{grid}$ | Electron energy gained in grid field | eV | $V_{grid} = 100$–$250$ V | | 5 | $I^{+} = K \cdot P^{n}$ | Penning (cold cathode) pressure-current relation | A, Torr | $n \approx 1.0$–$1.15$ | | 6 | $\lambda_{e^-} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma \cdot n} = \dfrac{kT}{\sigma \cdot P}$ | Electron mean free path in gas | m | UHV regime, $\sigma$ is ionization cross-section | | 7 | $S = \dfrac{I^{+}/I^{-}}{P}$ | Definition of gauge sensitivity | Torr$^{-1}$ | $S_{N_2} \approx 12$–$25$ Torr$^{-1}$ for BAG | | 8 | $P_{x-ray} \approx I_{x-ray}/(I^{-} \cdot S)$ | X-ray limit / residual current limit | Torr | BAG: $\approx 10^{-10}$ Torr | | 9 | $r_L = \dfrac{m_e v_\perp}{eB}$ | Electron Larmor radius in cold cathode | m | Cold cathode gauge, $B$ in Tesla | | 10 | $1\ \text{Torr} = 133.322\ \text{Pa}$ | SI conversion | Pa | Universal | > **Memory Tip**: Notice that formulas 1, 2, and 7 are all **mathematically equivalent** — they are just three different algebraic arrangements of the **same master relationship**. --- ## 2.7 Sensitivity Values for Common Gases (Bayard-Alpert Gauge, $E_e = 100$ eV) | Gas | Symbol | Ionization Energy (eV) | Relative Sensitivity (w.r.t. N₂) | |---|---|---|---| | Nitrogen | N₂ | 15.6 | 1.00 (reference) | | Oxygen | O₂ | 12.1 | 1.0 – 1.1 | | Hydrogen | H₂ | 15.4 | 0.46 | | Helium | He | 24.6 | 0.18 | | Argon | Ar | 15.8 | 1.3 | | Water vapor | H₂O | 12.6 | 0.9 | | Carbon dioxide | CO₂ | 13.8 | 1.4 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall**: Students often assume gauge sensitivity is **constant** for all gases. **It is NOT!** The sensitivity depends on the **ionization cross-section** of the gas, which varies with the molecular structure. Always specify **"sensitivity for N₂"** unless otherwise stated. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Gauge Equation $I^+ = I^- \cdot S \cdot P$ ### Assumption Set We assume: - Single-component gas of molecular number density $n$ (molecules per m³). - Electrons traverse a total path length $L$ through the gas. - The ionization cross-section at the operating electron energy is $\sigma_i$ (m²). - The probability of ionizing a molecule per electron per unit path length is therefore $\sigma_i \cdot n$. ### Step 1 — Ionization Events Per Electron The number of ionization events produced by a single electron along path length $L$ is: $$N_{ion} = n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L$$ ### Step 2 — Total Ion Current from Emission Current If $I^- / e$ is the number of electrons per second (from emission current $I^-$), then the total ionization events per second are: $$\dot{N}_{ion} = \frac{I^{-}}{e} \cdot n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L$$ ### Step 3 — Each Ionization Produces One Positive Ion Carrying Charge $+e$ Therefore, the total positive ion current is: $$I^{+} = \dot{N}_{ion} \cdot e = I^{-} \cdot n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Ideal Gas Law to Replace $n$ with Pressure From the ideal gas law: $$P \cdot V = N \cdot k_B \cdot T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = \frac{N}{V} = \frac{P}{k_B T}$$ Substituting: $$I^{+} = I^{-} \cdot \left(\frac{P}{k_B T}\right) \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L$$ ### Step 5 — Group All Constants Into a Single Sensitivity Factor Define the **gauge sensitivity**: $$S = \frac{\sigma_i \cdot L}{k_B T}$$ This has units of $(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{m}) / (\text{J/K} \cdot \text{K}) = \text{m}^3 / \text{J} = \text{Pa}^{-1}$ (or **Torr$^{-1}$** if pressure is in Torr). ### Step 6 — Final Master Equation $$\boxed{\,I^{+} = I^{-} \cdot S \cdot P\,}$$ **Conclusion**: The positive ion current is **directly proportional** to the emission current and the gas pressure, with the proportionality constant $S$ being the **gauge sensitivity** that captures all the atomic-scale physics (cross-section, geometry, temperature). --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Pressure Calculation **Problem**: A Bayard-Alpert gauge is operated with an **emission current $I^- = 1\ \text{mA}$**. The measured **ion current is $I^+ = 1.2 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{A}$**. The gauge sensitivity for nitrogen is **$S = 12\ \text{Torr}^{-1}$**. Calculate the chamber pressure. ### Solution **Step 1**: Write the rearranged master equation: $$P = \frac{I^{+}}{I^{-} \cdot S}$$ **Step 2**: Substitute the given values: $$P = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{A}}{1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A} \times 12\ \text{Torr}^{-1}}$$ **Step 3**: Compute denominator: $$I^{-} \cdot S = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 12 = 12 \times 10^{-3} = 0.012\ \text{A/Torr}$$ **Step 4**: Compute pressure: $$P = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-8}}{0.012} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Torr}$$ **Step 5**: Convert to SI units (Pascals): $$P = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Torr} \times 133.322\ \text{Pa/Torr} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa}$$ **Final Answer**: $P = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ Torr $\approx 1.33 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Sensitivity Determination **Problem**: A triode ionization gauge is used to measure a known calibration pressure of **$5 \times 10^{-6}$ Torr** (nitrogen). The emission current is set to **$I^- = 100\ \mu\text{A}$**, and the measured ion current is **$I^+ = 6 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}$**. Determine the **gauge sensitivity**. ### Solution **Step 1**: Rearrange the master equation for $S$: $$S = \frac{I^{+}}{I^{-} \cdot P}$$ **Step 2**: Substitute the values: $$S = \frac{6 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}}{100 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Torr}}$$ **Step 3**: Compute denominator: $$I^{-} \cdot P = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \times 100 = 5 \times 10^{-10}$$ Wait, let me recompute: $100 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$. So $I^{-} \cdot P = 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ A·Torr. **Step 4**: Compute $S$: $$S = \frac{6 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-10}} = 12\ \text{Torr}^{-1}$$ **Final Answer**: $S = 12\ \text{Torr}^{-1}$, which is consistent with the typical BAG sensitivity for nitrogen. ✓ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #3 — X-ray Limit Analysis **Problem**: A Bayard-Alpert gauge has an **X-ray induced photocurrent** of $I_{x-ray} = 1 \times 10^{-11}$ A at the ion collector. The emission current is $I^- = 1$ mA and the sensitivity is $S = 12$ Torr$^{-1}$. What is the **lowest measurable pressure (X-ray limit)** of this gauge? ### Solution **Step 1**: The X-ray induced current mimics a real ion current, giving a **false pressure reading**: $$P_{x-ray} = \frac{I_{x-ray}}{I^{-} \cdot S}$$ **Step 2**: Substitute: $$P_{x-ray} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-11}}{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 12} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-11}}{1.2 \times 10^{-2}}$$ **Step 3**: Compute: $$P_{x-ray} = 0.833 \times 10^{-9} = 8.33 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{Torr}$$ **Final Answer**: $P_{x-ray} \approx 8.3 \times 10^{-10}$ Torr. This is the **theoretical lower detection limit** of this BAG due to the X-ray photoemission effect. --- ## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Hot vs. Cold Cathode) | Parameter | Hot Cathode (BAG) | Cold Cathode (Penning) | |---|---|---| | Electron source | Thermionic filament | Cold cathode + crossed E-B fields | | Typical pressure range | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-10}$ Torr | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-11}$ Torr | | Sensitivity for N₂ | $12$ – $25$ Torr$^{-1}$ | $0.5$ – $5$ Torr$^{-1}$ | | Starting pressure problem | None | Cannot start above $\approx 10^{-3}$ Torr (gas discharge instability) | | Outgassing | Filament outgasses over time | No outgassing source | | Magnetic field required | No | Yes ($\approx 0.1$–$0.2$ T) | | Linearity | Linear in mid-range | $I^+ \propto P^{1.0–1.15}$ (nearly linear) | | Response time | Fast (ms) | Slow (1–10 s) | | Power consumption | High (filament heating) | Low | | Robustness | Filament burnout risk | Robust, no filament | | X-ray limit | Yes ($\approx 10^{-10}$ Torr) | Field emission noise limit ($\approx 10^{-11}$ Torr) | | Typical application | UHV systems, surface science, semiconductor | Plasma tools, space chambers, particle accelerators | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Bayard-Alpert Gauge — Construction Schematic > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note**: Mermaid is used here to render a **detailed block-level functional architecture** showing the electrode arrangement and electrical connections, as physical 3D drawings of gauge internals are not natively supported. ```mermaid flowchart TB FIL["FILAMENT CATHODE
Tungsten Wire
Heated to 2200 K
Emits Electrons"] GRID["CYLINDRICAL GRID ANODE
Wire Mesh Helix
Biased at +150 V
Accelerates Electrons"] COLL["CENTRAL ION COLLECTOR
Thin Axial Wire
Held at Ground or -25 V
Collects Positive Ions"] GAS["RESIDUAL GAS MOLECULES
Inside Vacuum Chamber
Density n = P / kT"] IONS["POSITIVE IONS
Drift to Collector
Current I-plus
Range 10 to power -9 to 10 to power -14 A"] ELEC["EMISSION ELECTRONS
Path Length L
Energy 100 eV
Current I-minus 1 mA"] EM["ELECTROMETER AMPLIFIER
FET Input Op-Amp
Measures I-plus
Output is Voltage Signal"] METER["PRESSURE INDICATOR
Digital Display
Reads P in Torr or Pa"] XRAY["X-RAY INDUCED PHOTOCURRENT
Limits Lower Detection
P-x-ray approx 10 to power -10 Torr"] FIL -->|thermionic emission| ELEC ELEC -->|acceleration through grid| GRID GRID -->|oscillatory trajectory| ELEC GAS -->|collisions with electrons| ELEC ELEC -->|ionization events| IONS IONS -->|collected at| COLL COLL -->|I-plus signal| EM EM -->|amplified voltage| METER GRID -.->|soft X-rays emitted| XRAY XRAY -.->|false current at| COLL ``` --- ## 4.2 Cold Cathode Penning Gauge — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR HV["HIGH VOLTAGE SOURCE
2 kV to 6 kV DC"] ANODE["ANODE
Cylindrical Tube
Held at High Positive Potential"] CATH["CATHODES
Two Disc Plates
Held at Ground"] MAG["PERMANENT MAGNET
0.1 to 0.2 Tesla
Axial B Field"] ELEC["ELECTRONS
Spiral Helical Path
Long Effective Path Length L"] GAS2["RESIDUAL GAS
Low Pressure P"] ION2["POSITIVE IONS
Drift to Cathode
Current I-plus Measured"] ION_AMP["ION CURRENT AMPLIFIER
High Gain DC Amp
FET Input"] DISP2["PRESSURE DISPLAY
P proportional to I-plus"] HV --> ANODE ANODE --> ELEC CATH --> ELEC MAG --> ELEC ELEC -->|spiral motion| ELEC ELEC -->|ionizing collisions| GAS2 GAS2 --> ION2 ION2 --> CATH CATH --> ION_AMP ION_AMP --> DISP2 ``` --- ## 4.3 Functional Flow — Signal Processing Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Vacuum Chamber Pressure P Torr"] B["Ionization Event Rate proportional to P"] C["Ion Current I-plus Ampere"] D["Electrometer Amplifier Gain G"] E["Output Voltage V-out Volt"] F["ADC and Microcontroller"] G["Digital Pressure Display"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G ``` > **Interpretation of the Flow**: The chain shows how a physical quantity (pressure) is converted through a sequence of physical and electronic transformations into a readable digital output. The **electrometer amplifier** is the **most critical stage** because it must measure currents as small as $10^{-14}$ A with high input impedance ($> 10^{14}\ \Omega$) and very low input bias current. --- ## 4.4 Sensitivity Curve — Bayard-Alpert Gauge (Qualitative) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Low Pressure Region
X-ray Limited
10 to power -10 to 10 to power -8 Torr
Non-linear / Flat"] B["Linear Operating Region
10 to power -8 to 10 to power -4 Torr
I-plus proportional to P
Best Accuracy"] C["High Pressure Region
Above 10 to power -4 Torr
Electron Mean Free Path Small
Gauge Saturates"] D["Hot Cathode Burnout Risk
Above 10 to power -3 Torr"] E["Cold Cathode Discharge Region
10 to power -3 to 10 to power -1 Torr"] F["Pirani / Thermocouple Region
10 to power -1 to 10 to power 3 Torr"] G["Capacitance Manometer Region
Atmospheric to 10 to power -1 Torr"] A --> B --> C C --> D C --> E E --> F F --> G ``` > [!TIP] > **Vacuum Gauge Range Mapping (KTU 2024 Module 2 — Complete Coverage)**: Different gauges cover different pressure ranges. The ionization gauge is **only used in the UHV regime**. Below $10^{-4}$ Torr for hot cathode and $10^{-5}$ Torr for cold cathode, **other gauges** (Pirani, thermocouple, capacitance manometer) take over. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] > **"Define ionization gauge. State the principle of operation of a hot cathode ionization gauge."** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** > An **ionization gauge** is a vacuum measuring instrument used in the **ultra-high vacuum (UHV) range** ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-10}$ Torr) that determines pressure by **ionizing the residual gas molecules** and measuring the resulting **positive ion current**. > > **Principle of Hot Cathode Operation**: > A heated tungsten filament emits electrons by **thermionic emission**. These electrons are accelerated toward a positively biased grid (anode) at about **+150 V**. During their oscillatory motion between filament and grid, the electrons collide with residual gas molecules, **ionizing** them. The positive ions are collected at the **ion collector** (held at low/ground potential), producing an **ion current $I^+$** that is **directly proportional to the gas pressure** according to the relation: > > $$I^{+} = I^{-} \cdot S \cdot P$$ > > where $I^-$ is the emission current, $S$ is the gauge sensitivity, and $P$ is the pressure. The ion current is measured by an **electrometer amplifier** and converted to a pressure reading. *(Mark distribution: Definition — 1M, Principle with diagram hint — 1M, Formula — 1M)* --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] > **"Distinguish between hot cathode and cold cathode ionization gauges. Mention any two advantages of cold cathode gauges."** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** | Feature | Hot Cathode Gauge | Cold Cathode Gauge | |---|---|---| | Electron source | Heated filament (thermionic) | Field emission from cold cathode | | Magnetic field | Not required | Required (crossed E-B fields) | | Pressure range | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-10}$ Torr | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-11}$ Torr | **Two Advantages of Cold Cathode Gauges**: 1. **No filament** — therefore no risk of filament burnout, and no outgassing from hot surfaces. This makes them **more stable and reliable** for long-term operation. 2. **Lower power consumption** and **lower X-ray background** compared to hot cathode types, enabling measurement of lower pressures (down to $10^{-11}$ Torr). *(Mark distribution: Tabular distinction — 2M, Two advantages — 1M)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice ### Question A (Choice Option) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2] > **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Bayard-Alpert ionization gauge**. Discuss the **advantages** of BAG over a conventional triode gauge. **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** Derive the **fundamental equation** relating ion current, emission current, sensitivity, and pressure for an ionization gauge. A Bayard-Alpert gauge has an emission current of **$I^- = 5$ mA** and measures an ion current of **$I^+ = 6 \times 10^{-8}$ A**. If the gauge sensitivity for nitrogen is **$S = 12$ Torr$^{-1}$**, find the **pressure** in the chamber. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Construction of Bayard-Alpert Gauge:** The BAG consists of three main electrodes inside a vacuum port: 1. **Filament (Cathode)**: A thin tungsten (or thoria-coated iridium) wire located **outside** the cylindrical grid, heated to approximately **2200 K** by an external power supply. It emits electrons thermionically. 2. **Grid (Anode)**: A **helical wire mesh** (or open cylinder) surrounding the ion collector, biased at a positive potential of **+100 to +250 V** (typically +150 V) relative to the filament. The grid accelerates the emitted electrons and collects the emission current $I^-$. 3. **Ion Collector**: A **fine axial wire** placed at the **geometric center** of the grid structure, held at **ground potential** (or slightly negative, e.g., $-25$ V). This is the **key innovation** of the BAG. **Working:** - Electrons emitted by the filament are accelerated toward the grid. - Due to the grid being an open mesh, most electrons **pass through the grid** and oscillate back and forth (oscillatory trajectory) before finally being collected. - During this long path, they undergo **ionizing collisions** with gas molecules, producing positive ions. - The positive ions drift to the central collector (lowest potential) and produce the ion current $I^+$. - The collector wire is connected to a **vibrating-reed electrometer** (or modern FET-input electrometer) that measures $I^+$. **Advantages of BAG over Conventional Triode:** 1. **Drastically reduced X-ray limit**: In the conventional triode, the collector is **outside** the grid. It presents a large solid angle to the soft X-rays generated at the grid, which causes photoemission of electrons from the collector surface. This **spurious electron current** mimics an ion current and sets a lower limit of $\approx 10^{-8}$ Torr. In the BAG, the collector is a **thin wire at the center**, presenting a much smaller area to X-rays, reducing the X-ray photocurrent by **100 to 1000 times**. The X-ray limit drops to **$\approx 10^{-10}$ Torr**. 2. **Better linearity and higher sensitivity**: The central collector geometry provides a more uniform electric field and a longer effective electron path length, giving higher sensitivity ($S = 12$ to $25$ Torr$^{-1}$ for N₂). 3. **Lower pumping speed effect**: The thin central collector intercepts fewer gas molecules, reducing the "pumping" effect of the gauge on the vacuum. *(Diagram: Use the Mermaid block from Section 4.1 as a schematic reference. Allocate: Construction — 3M, Working — 2M, Advantages — 2M)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Derivation of Fundamental Equation:** Consider a stream of electrons from the filament. If: - $n$ = number density of gas molecules (molecules/m³) - $\sigma_i$ = ionization cross-section (m²) at the operating electron energy - $L$ = total effective path length of an electron through the gas The **number of ionizations per electron** is $n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L$. The **number of electrons per second** emitted is $I^-/e$. Therefore, the **total ionizations per second** is: $$\dot{N}_{ion} = \frac{I^{-}}{e} \cdot n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L$$ Since each ionization event produces one positive ion of charge $+e$, the **ion current** is: $$I^{+} = e \cdot \dot{N}_{ion} = I^{-} \cdot n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L$$ From the **ideal gas law**, $P = n k_B T$, so $n = P / (k_B T)$. Substituting: $$I^{+} = I^{-} \cdot \frac{P}{k_B T} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L$$ Define the **gauge sensitivity** $S = \sigma_i L / (k_B T)$ to obtain the master equation: $$\boxed{\,I^{+} = I^{-} \cdot S \cdot P\,}$$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given: - $I^- = 5$ mA $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ A - $I^+ = 6 \times 10^{-8}$ A - $S = 12$ Torr$^{-1}$ Required: $P = ?$ Rearranging the master equation: $$P = \frac{I^{+}}{I^{-} \cdot S}$$ Substituting: $$P = \frac{6 \times 10^{-8}}{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 12}$$ Compute the denominator: $$I^{-} \cdot S = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 12 = 60 \times 10^{-3} = 6 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{A·Torr}^{-1}$$ Therefore: $$P = \frac{6 \times 10^{-8}}{6 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Torr}$$ Converting to SI units: $$P = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Torr} \times 133.322\ \text{Pa/Torr} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa}$$ **Final Answer**: $\boxed{P = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Torr} = 1.33 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa}}$ *(Mark distribution: Derivation — 4M, Final formula — 1M, Numerical substitution — 1M, Final answer with units — 1M)* --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2] > **(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a cold cathode ionization gauge (Penning gauge)** with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of the **magnetic field** in extending the electron path length. **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** Explain the **X-ray limit** of a Bayard-Alpert gauge. A BAG has an emission current of **$I^- = 2$ mA** and an X-ray induced photocurrent of **$I_{x-ray} = 1.5 \times 10^{-11}$ A**. The sensitivity is **$S = 15$ Torr$^{-1}$**. Find the **lowest measurable pressure (X-ray limit)**. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Construction of Penning Gauge:** The Penning gauge consists of: 1. **Anode**: A **cylindrical tube** (or ring) made of stainless steel, mounted coaxially. 2. **Two Cathodes**: Two **disc-shaped plates** placed at the open ends of the anode cylinder, perpendicular to the axis. 3. **Permanent Magnet**: Provides an **axial magnetic field** $B$ of approximately **0.1 to 0.2 Tesla** (1000–2000 Gauss) parallel to the axis of the cylinder. 4. **High Voltage Source**: A DC voltage of **2 kV to 6 kV** applied between the anode (positive) and cathodes (grounded). 5. **Ion Current Measuring Circuit**: The cathodes are connected to a sensitive ammeter to measure the collected ion current. **Working:** - A high voltage is applied between the anode and cathodes, creating a strong **electric field** $\vec{E}$ along the axis (from anode to cathode). - Simultaneously, the **magnetic field** $\vec{B}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{E}$ (axial direction). - Any free electron in the gas (e.g., from cosmic rays or field emission) experiences: - **Electric force** $e\vec{E}$ — accelerates it toward the anode. - **Magnetic Lorentz force** $e\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ — perpendicular to its velocity. - The combination causes the electron to follow a **long spiral (helical) trajectory** between the cathodes, oscillating back and forth along the axis while rotating around it. - The **effective path length** $L_{eff}$ can be **hundreds of meters** (compared to a few cm in a hot cathode gauge), dramatically increasing the probability of ionizing collisions. - The ionized positive ions are accelerated toward the cathodes and collected, producing the ion current $I^+$. **Role of the Magnetic Field — Detailed Explanation:** The magnetic field **does not change the kinetic energy** of the electron (because the Lorentz force is always perpendicular to velocity), but it **constrains the electron to move in a cycloidal/spiral path**. The radius of the spiral is the **Larmor radius**: $$r_L = \frac{m_e v_\perp}{eB}$$ For typical operating conditions ($v_\perp \approx 10^7$ m/s, $B \approx 0.1$ T): $r_L \approx (9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^7) / (1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.1) \approx 5.7 \times 10^{-4}$ m = **0.57 mm**. This very small radius means the electron spirals tightly around the magnetic field lines. Without the magnetic field, the electron would travel in a straight line from cathode to anode and exit in $\approx 10^{-8}$ seconds. **With the magnetic field**, it is trapped for **microseconds to milliseconds**, increasing the path length by a factor of $\approx 10^3$ to $10^5$. This dramatically enhances the ionization probability and makes the Penning gauge sensitive to very low pressures. **Advantages**: No filament (long life), robust, no outgassing. **Disadvantages**: Cannot operate above $\approx 10^{-3}$ Torr (gas discharge instability), slower response time, non-linear at high pressures. *(Mark distribution: Construction — 2M, Working — 2M, Magnetic field role with Larmor radius — 2M, Advantages/Disadvantages — 1M)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **X-ray Limit of Bayard-Alpert Gauge — Explanation:** When energetic electrons (typically 100 eV) strike the grid (anode) of a BAG, they generate **soft X-rays** (bremsstrahlung) as a byproduct of their deceleration. These X-rays travel outward and strike the central ion collector. The X-ray photons cause **photoemission of electrons** from the collector surface. This **photoemission current** is indistinguishable from a real positive ion current — it produces the same polarity of signal at the electrometer. The measured ion current therefore includes a **spurious component** $I_{x-ray}$: $$I_{measured}^{+} = I_{true\,ion}^{+} + I_{x-ray}$$ At very low pressures, the true ion current becomes so small that it is comparable to the X-ray induced current. The lowest pressure that can be measured is when $I_{true\,ion}^{+} = I_{x-ray}$: $$P_{min} = \frac{I_{x-ray}}{I^{-} \cdot S}$$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given: - $I^- = 2$ mA $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ A - $I_{x-ray} = 1.5 \times 10^{-11}$ A - $S = 15$ Torr$^{-1}$ Required: $P_{min} = ?$ $$P_{min} = \frac{I_{x-ray}}{I^{-} \cdot S} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-11}}{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 15}$$ Denominator: $2 \times 10^{-3} \times 15 = 30 \times 10^{-3} = 3 \times 10^{-2}$ A·Torr$^{-1}$. $$P_{min} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-11}}{3 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-9} = 5.0 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{Torr}$$ Converting to SI: $P_{min} = 5.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 133.322 = 6.67 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa. **Final Answer**: $\boxed{P_{min} = 5.0 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{Torr} = 6.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{Pa}}$ *(Mark distribution: Explanation of X-ray effect — 3M, Formula for $P_{min}$ — 1M, Numerical substitution — 2M, Final answer with units — 1M)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Ionization Gauge Questions** > > 1. **Forgetting units**: Students frequently compute numerical answers in mixed units (e.g., mA and $\mu$A together) and lose 1–2 marks. **Always convert to SI base units (Amperes) before substituting.** > > 2. **Confusing the role of $I^-$ vs $I^+$**: Some students think $I^+$ is the input (emission) current. **Remember**: $I^-$ is the **emission current** (the known, controlled input, typically 0.1–10 mA), and $I^+$ is the **measured ion current** (the output, typically $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-14}$ A). > > 3. **Skipping the conversion to Pascals**: KTU evaluators **often award an extra 0.5–1 mark** for explicit SI unit conversion. Always show the Torr → Pa conversion step. > > 4. **Omitting the diagram**: For 7-mark construction questions, **a neat labeled diagram is mandatory** (typically 1–2 marks allocated). Do not skip it. > > 5. **Not specifying "for nitrogen"**: Gauge sensitivity depends on the gas. Always write "$S = X$ Torr$^{-1}$ **for N₂**" unless otherwise specified. > > 6. **Mixing up the X-ray limit formula**: The X-ray limit is the pressure at which the **true ion current equals the X-ray photocurrent**, not the pressure at which the gauge physically stops working. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Final 5-Minute Revision Summary for KTU 2024 ESE** **Core Concepts:** - [x] Ionization gauges measure pressure in the **UHV range** ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-10}$ Torr). - [x] **Hot cathode** uses a heated filament; **cold cathode** uses crossed E-B fields. - [x] **Bayard-Alpert gauge** is the industry standard for hot cathode UHV measurement. - [x] **Penning gauge** is the most common cold cathode ionization gauge. **Master Equations (Must Memorize):** - [x] $I^+ = I^- \cdot S \cdot P$ — **The most important equation in this module.** - [x] $P = I^+ / (I^- \cdot S)$ — For pressure calculation. - [x] $S = I^+ / (I^- \cdot P)$ — For sensitivity determination. - [x] $P_{x-ray} = I_{x-ray} / (I^- \cdot S)$ — For X-ray limit calculation. - [x] $r_L = m_e v_\perp / (eB)$ — Larmor radius for cold cathode. **Key Numerical Values to Remember:** - [x] Typical emission current: $I^- = 1$ mA (range: 0.1–10 mA) - [x] Typical ion current at UHV: $I^+ = 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-14}$ A - [x] Typical sensitivity (BAG for N₂): $S = 12$ – $25$ Torr$^{-1}$ - [x] Grid voltage: $V_{grid} = +100$ to $+250$ V (optimum at 150 V) - [x] Electron energy: $E_{e^-} = 100$ eV (optimum for ionization cross-section) - [x] Magnetic field in Penning gauge: $B = 0.1$ – $0.2$ T - [x] High voltage in Penning gauge: $V = 2$ – $6$ kV - [x] **1 Torr = 133.322 Pa** **Conceptual Differentiations:** - [x] Triode has **collector outside** grid; BAG has **collector at center** of grid. - [x] BAG has **lower X-ray limit** ($\sim 10^{-10}$ Torr) than triode ($\sim 10^{-8}$ Torr). - [x] Hot cathode has **higher sensitivity**; cold cathode has **better stability**. - [x] Cold cathode cannot start above $\sim 10^{-3}$ Torr (discharge problem). - [x] Sensitivity is **gas-dependent** — do not assume universal. **Things You Should Be Able to Draw:** - [x] BAG electrode arrangement (filament, grid, central collector). - [x] Penning gauge (cylinder anode, two cathode discs, magnet). - [x] Block diagram of signal processing: chamber → ions → electrometer → ADC → display. **Engineering Applications (For "Real-World Utility" Questions):** - [x] Semiconductor fabrication (vacuum deposition, etching). - [x] Particle accelerators (CERN, etc.). - [x] Space simulation chambers. - [x] Surface science and thin-film research. - [x] Plasma processing equipment. - [x] Nuclear fusion research (tokamak vacuum vessels).

topics.title: Cold Cathode Type and Hot Cathode Type, Pressure Gauge Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cold Cathode Type and Hot Cathode Type, Pressure Gauge Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ionization Vacuum Gauges — The Big Picture
In the domain of **Industrial Instrumentation**, measurement of very low pressures (vacuum) is essential for semiconductor fabrication, vacuum metallurgy, space simulation chambers, particle accelerators, and thin-film coating processes. The instruments designed to measure pressures in the high-vacuum and ultra-high-vacuum (UHV) regions are called **Ionization Gauges**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Ionization Vacuum Gauge** is a secondary pressure transducer that infers gas pressure by measuring the rate of ion production within a defined volume of gas, exploiting the principle that the number of ions produced is directly proportional to the number of gas molecules present (and hence to pressure).

There are two principal sub-families of ionization gauges, differentiated by **how the ionizing electrons are generated**:

| Sub-Family | Electron Source | KTU Common Name |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold Cathode Type** | High-voltage field emission + crossed E & B fields | Penning Gauge / Magnetron Gauge |
| **Hot Cathode Type** | Thermionic emission from heated filament | Bayard–Alpert (B–A) Gauge, Triode Gauge |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bowling Alley vs. The Rocket Engine"**
> 
> - Imagine trying to count the number of mosquitoes flying in a dark room.
> 
> - **Cold Cathode (Penning) Gauge = "The Bowling Alley with Magnets":** You set up a long, twisting lane (a strong magnetic field) that forces your "bowling balls" (electrons) to spiral and zigzag for a very long time before hitting a pin (anode). The longer they travel, the more mosquitoes they smack into. The "balls" are generated by a violent high-voltage spark, not a gentle heat source — it is "cold" because no filament is glowing hot.
> 
> - **Hot Cathode (Bayard–Alpert) Gauge = "The Rocket Engine with a Net":** A small tungsten wire (filament) is gently heated red-hot, like a tiny rocket igniter, and "spits out" electrons in a controlled stream. These electrons are funneled through a charged wire-mesh grid, ionizing gas molecules along the way. A thin central wire (ion collector) acts like a "net" that catches the positively charged ions. The current in this net tells you how many mosquitoes were flying around.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics
The governing constants for both gauges are:
- **Electron charge, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$**
- **Electron mass, $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$**
- **Boltzmann Constant, $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$**
- **Standard reference temperature, $T = 273.15\ \text{K}$ (or 296 K for many practical gauges)**
- **Pressure ranges covered:** Cold cathode: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-7}$ Torr · Hot cathode: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-10}$ Torr (and lower with B–A)

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Always remember that ionization gauges are **secondary (indirect) transducers** — they do NOT measure pressure mechanically; they convert the *ion current* (an electrical signal) into a pressure reading using a known proportionality constant called **sensitivity (S)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cold Cathode Gauge (Penning / Magnetron Type)

#### 2.1.1 Construction
- A **cathode plate** and an **anode ring/cylinder** are placed inside a glass or metal envelope connected to the vacuum system.
- A strong **permanent magnet** (typically AlNiCo or rare-earth) provides a magnetic field $B$ (typically $0.1$–$0.2\ \text{T}$) perpendicular to the electric field.
- A high DC voltage (typically $2$–$6\ \text{kV}$) is applied between cathode and anode.

#### 2.1.2 Operating Principle (The "Why" and "How")
1. A high voltage applied across the cathode–anode gap creates a strong radial electric field $E$.
2. Residual gas molecules, by chance, release a few "seed" electrons (cosmic-ray induced or field-emitted).
3. The crossed $E \times B$ fields force the electrons into long **helical cycloidal trajectories** between the electrodes, dramatically increasing the electron path length (often by a factor of 1000× or more).
4. These spiraling electrons collide with and ionize neutral gas molecules, producing positive ions and additional secondary electrons (sustaining a self-maintaining discharge).
5. The positive ions are collected at the cathode, producing a measurable **ion current $I_i$**.
6. The ion current is directly proportional to pressure $P$.

#### 2.1.3 Why It's Called "Cold"
There is **no heated filament**. Electron emission is achieved purely through **cold-field emission** and the avalanche-like secondary emission sustained by the crossed fields. Hence the term "cold cathode."

### 2.2 Hot Cathode Gauge (Bayard–Alpert / Triode Type)

#### 2.2.1 Construction
- A **thoriated-tungsten filament (F)** is mounted outside a helical wire-mesh **anode/grid (G)**.
- A thin central wire (the **ion collector, I**) runs along the axis of the grid.
- Typical electrode potentials (with respect to ground): Filament at $+30\ \text{V}$, Grid at $+180\ \text{V}$, Collector at $0\ \text{V}$ (ground).

#### 2.2.2 Operating Principle
1. The filament is heated to about $2400\ \text{K}$ and emits electrons via **thermionic emission** (Richardson–Dushman equation).
2. Electrons are accelerated toward the positively charged grid and oscillate through it many times.
3. These energetic electrons (typically $100$–$150\ \text{eV}$) ionize residual gas molecules.
4. The positive ions are repelled by the grid and collected by the central ion collector wire, producing **ion current $I_i$**.
5. The **electron current $I_e$** flowing to the grid is kept constant by an emission regulator.
6. The ratio $I_i / I_e$ is proportional to pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bayard–Alpert Replaced Triode:**
> The original triode had its ion collector *outside* the grid, where soft X-rays generated by electron impact on the grid would strike the collector, producing a spurious photoelectric current that set a pressure-floor. Bayard and Alpert (1950) placed the ion collector *inside* the grid, drastically reducing the X-ray–induced error and lowering the low-pressure measurement limit to $10^{-10}$ Torr.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units | Validity / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ion current ↔ Pressure (general) | $I_i = S \cdot I_e \cdot P$ | A, Torr$^{-1}$, A, Torr | Defines gauge sensitivity $S$ |
| 2 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{I_i}{I_e \cdot P}$ | Torr$^{-1}$ (often 10–100) | Calibrated for N₂ |
| 3 | Hot cathode thermionic emission (Richardson–Dushman) | $J_e = A_R T^2 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\phi}{k_B T}\right)$ | A/m² | $A_R \approx 60\ \text{A/cm}^2\text{K}^2$ for W; $\phi$ = work function |
| 4 | Cold cathode ion current (Penning) | $I_i = K \cdot P^n$ | A | $K$ = gauge constant, $n \approx 0.9$–$1.1$ |
| 5 | Mean free path of gas molecule | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^2 P}$ | m | $d$ = molecular diameter |
| 6 | Cyclotron radius of electron | $r_c = \dfrac{m_e v_\perp}{eB}$ | m | Determines path length |
| 7 | Sensitivity variation with gas type | $S_{\text{gas}} = S_{N_2} \cdot R_{\text{gas}}$ | — | $R_{\text{gas}}$: relative sensitivity factor |
| 8 | X-ray photo-current limit (B–A) | $I_{XR} \propto I_e \cdot P_{\text{eff}}$ | A | Sets lower pressure floor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in vacuum process control for **semiconductor lithography, vacuum metallurgy, freeze-drying, plasma TV/display manufacturing, and particle accelerator beam lines** — anywhere the exact residual gas pressure critically affects product yield or experimental outcome.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Ion Current–Pressure Linear Relationship (Generic Ionization Gauge)

Consider a defined volume $V$ of low-pressure gas at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$. The molecular number density $n$ is given by the ideal gas law:

$$
P \cdot V = n \, k_B T \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = \frac{P}{k_B T}
$$

**Step 1 — Electron Beam Setup:** An electron beam of constant current $I_e$ passes through the gauge volume. If each electron traverses an effective path length $L_{\text{eff}}$, the total volume swept by the beam per unit time is:

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{swept}} = L_{\text{eff}} \cdot A_{\text{beam}}
$$

where $A_{\text{beam}}$ is the effective cross-sectional area of the electron beam.

**Step 2 — Ionization Cross-Section:** The probability that an electron ionizes a gas molecule in its path depends on the **ionization cross-section** $\sigma_i$ (units: m²). The number of ion pairs produced per unit time (ionization rate $\dot{N}_i$) is:

$$
\dot{N}_i = n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L_{\text{eff}} \cdot \dot{N}_e
$$

where $\dot{N}_e = I_e / e$ is the number of electrons per second leaving the filament.

**Step 3 — Ion Current:** Each ion carries one elementary charge $e$, so the ion current is:

$$
I_i = e \cdot \dot{N}_i = e \cdot n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{I_e}{e} = n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L_{\text{eff}} \cdot I_e
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $n$ from Ideal Gas Law:**

$$
I_i = \frac{P}{k_B T} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L_{\text{eff}} \cdot I_e
$$

**Step 5 — Define Sensitivity $S$:**

$$
\boxed{\,S \;\equiv\; \frac{\sigma_i \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}{k_B T} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I_i = S \cdot I_e \cdot P\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation of every ionization gauge**, both cold cathode and hot cathode. The sensitivity $S$ is a calibrated constant (typically $10$–$100\ \text{Torr}^{-1}$ for N₂).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cycloidal Path Length in Cold Cathode Gauge

In a Penning gauge, an electron moves under the simultaneous influence of:
- Radial electric field: $E = V / r$ (between cathode and anode)
- Axial magnetic field: $B$ (from the permanent magnet)

The Lorentz force on an electron with charge $e$ and velocity $\vec{v}$ is:

$$
\vec{F} = -e(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})
$$

**Step 1 — Centripetal force balance in the crossed-field region** gives the cyclotron radius:

$$
r_c = \frac{m_e \, v_\perp}{e \, B}
$$

**Step 2 — The electron undergoes cycloidal motion.** The path consists of a fast circular motion (gyration) superimposed on a slower drift. The drift velocity perpendicular to both $E$ and $B$ is:

$$
v_{\text{drift}} = \frac{E}{B}
$$

**Step 3 — The average path length between cathode and anode**, instead of the direct $d$ (a few mm), becomes the cycloidal arc length $L_{\text{eff}}$:

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = N_{\text{turns}} \cdot 2 \pi r_c
$$

In a typical Penning geometry, $N_{\text{turns}} \approx 30$–$100$ turns, so $L_{\text{eff}} \gg d$.

**Step 4 — Substituting into the general ionization equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,I_i = \frac{\sigma_i \cdot (N_{\text{turns}} \cdot 2 \pi r_c)}{k_B T} \cdot I_e \cdot P \;\propto\; P\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The high $L_{\text{eff}}$ in a cold cathode gauge makes it extremely sensitive *without* needing a hot filament. This is why cold cathode gauges are mechanically robust, immune to filament burn-out, and ideal for **process control in harsh industrial environments**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Thermionic Emission (Hot Cathode Filament)

The Richardson–Dushman equation for the saturation current density emitted by a heated filament:

$$
J_e = A_R \, T^2 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{e \phi}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Take natural log on both sides:**

$$
\ln J_e = \ln A_R + 2 \ln T - \frac{e \phi}{k_B T}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $T$ to find how $J_e$ changes with filament temperature:**

$$
\frac{d(\ln J_e)}{dT} = \frac{2}{T} + \frac{e \phi}{k_B T^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the temperature sensitivity:** A small $\Delta T$ around an operating point of $2400\ \text{K}$ (with $\phi \approx 4.5\ \text{eV}$ for thoriated W) gives:

$$
\frac{\Delta J_e}{J_e} \approx \left(2 + \frac{e \phi}{k_B T}\right) \frac{\Delta T}{T} \approx 23 \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is why hot cathode gauges need an emission regulator!** A $1\%$ change in filament temperature produces a $\sim 23\%$ change in emission current, which would corrupt the pressure reading. The $I_e$ is therefore electronically held constant to within $0.1\%$.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Cold Cathode (Penning) | Hot Cathode (B–A) |
|---|---|---|
| Electron source | Cold field emission, crossed $E \times B$ | Thermionic emission (heated filament) |
| Typical high voltage | $2$–$6\ \text{kV}$ | $100$–$200\ \text{V}$ (grid) |
| Magnetic field | $0.1$–$0.2\ \text{T}$ required | Not required |
| Lower pressure limit | $\sim 10^{-7}$ Torr | $\sim 10^{-10}$ Torr (B–A) |
| Upper pressure limit | $\sim 10^{-2}$ Torr | $\sim 10^{-3}$ Torr |
| Sensitivity $S$ | $1$–$10\ \text{Torr}^{-1}$ | $10$–$100\ \text{Torr}^{-1}$ |
| Response time | Slow (1–10 s) | Fast ($<0.1$ s) |
| Filament burn-out risk | **None** | **Yes — major issue** |
| Sensitivity to inrush gas | Can extinguish discharge | Filament can burn out |
| Ruggedness | High (no fragile filament) | Lower |
| Cost | Moderate | Moderate-to-high (electronics) |
| Power consumption | Low | Higher (filament heating) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
