## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** *Differentiate between a cold cathode gauge and a hot cathode gauge with respect to (i) electron generation method, (ii) typical pressure range, and (iii) any one disadvantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cold Cathode (Penning) | Hot Cathode (Bayard–Alpert) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Electron generation | Cold field emission under crossed $E \times B$ fields; no heated filament | Thermionic emission from a heated tungsten filament ($\sim 2400\ \text{K}$) |
| (ii) Pressure range | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-7}$ Torr | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-10}$ Torr |
| (iii) Disadvantage | Slow response time; discharge can extinguish at high pressures; sensitive to mounting orientation due to magnet | Filament can burn out; requires emission regulator; cannot tolerate sudden inrush of gas (overpressure) |

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]** *State the fundamental equation relating ion current, electron current, sensitivity, and pressure in an ionization vacuum gauge. Define the term "sensitivity" of the gauge.*

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental governing equation is:

$$
\boxed{\,I_i = S \cdot I_e \cdot P\,}
$$

where:
- $I_i$ = ion current collected (Amperes)
- $I_e$ = electron emission current (Amperes)
- $P$ = pressure in the gauge (Torr)
- $S$ = **sensitivity** of the gauge (Torr$^{-1}$)

**Definition of Sensitivity:** Sensitivity $S$ is defined as the ion current produced per unit electron current per unit pressure:

$$
S = \frac{I_i}{I_e \cdot P}
$$

It depends on the geometry of the gauge, the ionization cross-section of the gas, and the effective path length of the ionizing electrons. It is calibrated using nitrogen (N₂) as the reference gas.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of a **Penning cold cathode ionization gauge**. Derive the relation between ion current and pressure.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A Penning gauge has a calibration constant $K = 0.05\ \mu\text{A/Torr}$. If the measured ion current is $3.2\ \mu\text{A}$, determine the gas pressure inside the vacuum chamber. Comment on the operating condition.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working:**
The Penning gauge consists of:
- A **cathode assembly** (two flat plates) and an **anode ring** placed coaxially inside a glass/metal envelope.
- A **permanent magnet** providing an axial magnetic field $B \approx 0.1$–$0.2\ \text{T}$.
- A high DC voltage ($2$–$6\ \text{kV}$) applied between cathode and anode.

**Working:**
1. A few "seed" electrons (from cosmic rays or field emission) are accelerated toward the anode by the high $E$-field.
2. The axial $B$-field constrains their motion into **helical cycloidal paths**, increasing the effective path length $L_{\text{eff}}$ by 30–100×.
3. These long-path electrons collide with and ionize residual gas molecules, producing positive ions and secondary electrons (self-sustaining discharge).
4. The positive ions are collected at the cathode, producing measurable ion current $I_i$.

**Derivation of $I_i$ vs $P$:**

Consider an electron with average path length $L_{\text{eff}} = N \cdot 2\pi r_c$ between the electrodes. The ionization rate is:

$$
\dot{N}_i = n \cdot \sigma_i \cdot L_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{I_e}{e}
$$

Using ideal gas law $n = P / (k_B T)$:

$$
I_i = e \dot{N}_i = \frac{\sigma_i L_{\text{eff}}}{k_B T} \cdot I_e \cdot P
$$

Defining sensitivity $S = \sigma_i L_{\text{eff}} / (k_B T)$:

$$
\boxed{\,I_i = S \cdot I_e \cdot P\,}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $K = S \cdot I_e = 0.05\ \mu\text{A/Torr}$ (combined gauge constant), $I_i = 3.2\ \mu\text{A}$.

Using the relation $I_i = K \cdot P$:

$$
P = \frac{I_i}{K} = \frac{3.2\ \mu\text{A}}{0.05\ \mu\text{A/Torr}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P = 64\ \text{Torr}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #1:**
> Students often confuse the gauge constant. If only $S$ is given (in Torr$^{-1}$), you must look up the actual emission current $I_e$ to compute $P$. Here $K$ is the *combined* constant ($S \cdot I_e$), so direct division is correct. Always read whether the constant is "sensitivity" or "gauge constant."

> **Comment on Operating Condition:** A pressure of $64$ Torr is **far above the operating range of a Penning gauge** (which works between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-7}$ Torr). At this pressure, the gas density is too high — the discharge may either (i) become unstable and draw excessive current, or (ii) transition into a glow discharge and damage the cathode. **The reading is unreliable; a Pirani or capacitance manometer should be used at this range.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the construction and working of a **Bayard–Alpert hot cathode ionization gauge** with a neat diagram. Why is it preferred over the conventional triode gauge for ultra-high-vacuum measurement?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *In a Bayard–Alpert gauge, the electron current is regulated at $I_e = 1.0\ \text{mA}$ and the measured ion current is $I_i = 4.0 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{A}$ when nitrogen is used. If the sensitivity $S$ for N₂ is $25\ \text{Torr}^{-1}$, determine the pressure inside the chamber. Also compute the corresponding molecular number density at $T = 300\ \text{K}$.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of B–A Gauge:**

Construction (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):
- **Filament (F):** External thoriated-tungsten wire, heated to $\sim 2400\ \text{K}$, emits electrons thermionically.
- **Grid (G):** A helical wire-mesh structure surrounding the central axis, held at $+180\ \text{V}$ relative to ground. Accelerates and collects electrons.
- **Ion Collector (I):** A thin central wire, held at $0\ \text{V}$ (ground), collects positive ions.

Working:
1. Filament emits electrons (Richardson–Dushman emission).
2. Electrons oscillate through the grid, gaining $\sim 150\ \text{eV}$, ionizing gas molecules.
3. Ions are repelled by the grid and collected by the central wire → measurable $I_i$.
4. $I_e$ is held constant by an emission regulator.
5. $P = I_i / (S \cdot I_e)$.

**Why Preferred over Triode Gauge:**
In a conventional triode, the ion collector is **outside** the grid. Electrons striking the grid produce soft X-rays ($\sim 100\ \text{eV}$), which strike the external collector and liberate photoelectrons. This produces a spurious **X-ray–induced current $I_{XR}$** that does not decrease with pressure, setting a **lower limit of $\sim 10^{-8}$ Torr**. In the B–A gauge, the ion collector is **inside** the grid, so it "sees" very little of the X-ray flux. The X-ray limit is reduced by $\sim 100\times$, pushing the lower measurement limit to **$10^{-10}$ Torr** and below.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $I_e = 1.0\ \text{mA} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$, $I_i = 4.0 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{A}$, $S = 25\ \text{Torr}^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Compute pressure using $I_i = S \cdot I_e \cdot P$:**

$$
P = \frac{I_i}{S \cdot I_e} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-8}}{25 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
P = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-8}}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.6 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Torr}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P = 1.6 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Torr} \approx 1.6\ \mu\text{Torr}\,}
$$

This is in the **high-vacuum range**, well within the B–A gauge's capability.

**Step 2 — Molecular number density from ideal gas law:**

First convert pressure to Pascals:
$$
1\ \text{Torr} = 133.322\ \text{Pa} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P = 1.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 133.322 = 2.133 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa}
$$

Now apply $P = n k_B T$:

$$
n = \frac{P}{k_B T} = \frac{2.133 \times 10^{-4}}{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}
$$

$$
n = \frac{2.133 \times 10^{-4}}{4.143 \times 10^{-21}} = 5.149 \times 10^{16}\ \text{molecules/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{\,n \approx 5.15 \times 10^{16}\ \text{molecules/m}^3 \approx 5.15 \times 10^{10}\ \text{molecules/cm}^3\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #2:**
> Two common mistakes:
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Ion current in Amperes, electron current in Amperes, sensitivity in Torr$^{-1}$ → pressure comes out directly in Torr. Do NOT mix SI Pa into $S$ unless you also convert $S$.
> 2. **Confusing molecular number density $n$ with molecular concentration $C$:** They are identical for an ideal gas ($C = n$). Students sometimes incorrectly multiply by Avogadro's number thinking $n$ is in moles — it is NOT; $n$ here is molecules per unit volume.

---

### 5.3 Pressure Gauge Selection — KTU-Level Summary Table

| Pressure Range (Torr) | Recommended Gauge | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $760$ – $10$ | Bourdon tube, diaphragm gauge | Mechanical |
| $10$ – $10^{-3}$ | Pirani gauge, thermocouple gauge | Thermal conductivity |
| $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-7}$ | Hot cathode B–A *or* cold cathode Penning | Ionization |
| $< 10^{-7}$ | B–A, extractor gauge, or spinning rotor gauge | Ionization / Molecular |

### 5.4 Selection Criteria Checklist (used in KTU problems)

1. **Pressure range** — must encompass operating and accidental overpressure.
2. **Gas type** — different gases have different sensitivities; calibration is for N₂.
3. **Accuracy required** — ionization gauges are most accurate at low pressure.
4. **Response time** — Penning is slow; B–A is fast.
5. **Robustness** — Penning is more rugged (no filament).
6. **Cost and maintenance** — Penning has longer life; B–A needs filament replacement.
7. **Environmental constraints** — magnetic fields, vibration, contamination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision):**
> 
> - **Cold Cathode (Penning)** = No filament; uses crossed $E \times B$ fields; range $10^{-2}$–$10^{-7}$ Torr; rugged, slow, may extinguish at high pressure.
> - **Hot Cathode (Bayard–Alpert)** = Heated filament, thermionic emission; range $10^{-3}$–$10^{-10}$ Torr; sensitive, fast, filament can burn out.
> - **Master Equation:** $I_i = S \cdot I_e \cdot P$ — universally true for both types.
> - **Sensitivity** $S$ is calibrated for N₂; other gases need a relative-sensitivity correction factor.
> - **Filament emission** follows Richardson–Dushman: $J_e \propto T^2 \exp(-e\phi / k_B T)$ — explains why an emission regulator is mandatory.
> - **B–A's X-ray limit** is $\sim 100\times$ lower than triode because the ion collector is *inside* the grid, hiding from X-ray photoemission.
> - **Penning's long path** is achieved by helical motion in crossed $E \times B$ fields: $r_c = m_e v_\perp / (eB)$.
> - **Gauge selection** is dictated by pressure range, gas type, response time, ruggedness, and cost.
> - **Cold cathode advantages:** no filament burn-out, can tolerate inrush gas better, rugged.
> - **Cold cathode disadvantages:** slower response, sensitive to magnetic orientation, lower sensitivity ($S$).
> - **Conversion:** $1\ \text{Torr} = 133.322\ \text{Pa}$; $n = P/(k_B T)$ for number density.
> - Always quote **N₂-calibrated sensitivity** unless otherwise stated in the problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Viscosity: Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Viscosity: Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Viscosity

**Viscosity** is defined as the internal friction or internal resistance offered by a fluid (liquid or gas) to the relative motion of its adjacent layers. It is a fundamental transport property that quantifies a fluid's resistance to flow and deformation under an applied shear stress.

Mathematically, viscosity is the proportionality constant that relates the **shear stress** ($\tau$) in a fluid to the **rate of shear strain** (velocity gradient, $\frac{du}{dy}$), as expressed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The definition of viscosity, its physical significance, units (SI, CGS, and Industrial), and the mathematical form of Newton's Law of Viscosity form the **core definitional foundation** for all subsequent measurement techniques (capillary, rotational, falling-ball, and oscillatory viscometers).

## 1.2 The Two Principal Types of Viscosity

There are two fundamental types of viscosity that every KTU student must master:

| Type | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard SI Unit |
|------|--------|------------------|------------------|
| **Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity** | $\mu$ (mu) | Resistance to flow due to internal molecular friction | **Pa·s** (Pascal-second) |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu$ (nu) | Resistance to flow under the influence of gravity | **m²/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relationship:** The two are related through the fluid density ($\rho$):
> $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$
> This relationship is **frequently tested** in KTU examinations and is the foundation for converting between measurement systems.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Honey vs. Water Test":**
> Imagine pouring **honey** and **water** down the same inclined surface. Water flows rapidly and reaches the bottom almost instantly, while honey creeps down slowly. This difference is **viscosity**. The honey has *higher viscosity* because its molecules cling more strongly to each other, resisting the relative motion between layers. Now imagine the same honey being heated — it flows much faster. This is because **viscosity is highly temperature-dependent** (decreases for liquids, increases for gases as temperature rises).

**Another Intuition — Stack of Cards:**
Picture a deck of cards as a fluid. If you push the top card horizontally, only the top card moves while the bottom card stays glued to the table. The intermediate cards slide over one another at progressively slower speeds. This **layered sliding** with **velocity gradient** is exactly how a real fluid behaves, and **viscosity is the "stickiness"** between these sliding layers.

## 1.4 Physical Significance & Engineering Importance

Viscosity is critical in numerous industrial processes:

- **Petroleum Refining:** Quality grading of crude oil, diesel, lubricating oils (SAE grades)
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Syrups, injectables, blood-flow modeling
- **Food & Beverage:** Chocolate, ketchup, dairy products quality control
- **Polymer Manufacturing:** Resin flow characterization, melt processing
- **Paint & Coatings:** Brushing, spraying, and leveling behavior
- **Cosmetics:** Lotion, cream, and gel flow properties
- **Chemical Process Plants:** Reactor design, pumping, and pipeline sizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics:**
> - **Dynamic Viscosity of Water at 20 °C** = **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** = **1.002 cP (centipoise)**
> - **Kinematic Viscosity of Water at 20 °C** = **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** = **1.004 cSt (centistokes)**
> - **Dynamic Viscosity of Air at 20 °C** ≈ **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Law of Viscosity — The Foundational Postulate

Issac Newton's foundational postulate (1687) states that for a **Newtonian fluid**, the shear stress between adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa or N/m²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient or rate of shear strain (s⁻¹)

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** The tangential force per unit area acting between two adjacent fluid layers. It has units of pressure.
2. **Velocity Gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$):** The change in fluid velocity ($u$) with respect to the perpendicular distance ($y$) from a fixed surface. It is the rate at which one layer slides relative to the next.
3. **Proportionality Constant ($\mu$):** The viscosity. It is the fluid's intrinsic resistance to this shearing motion.
4. **Isothermal Assumption:** Newton's law assumes constant temperature — important because viscosity is highly temperature-dependent.

## 2.2 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** This classification is a **guaranteed question** in the KTU module examinations.

| Fluid Behavior Type | Definition | Shear Stress vs. Shear Rate | Examples |
|--------------------|------------|------------------------------|----------|
| **Newtonian** | $\mu$ is constant; obeys $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | Linear, straight line through origin | Water, air, most gases, light oils, alcohol |
| **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning)** | Apparent viscosity decreases with shear rate | Concave-down curve | Paint, ketchup, blood, polymer solutions |
| **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)** | Apparent viscosity increases with shear rate | Concave-up curve | Cornstarch slurry, wet sand, sugar solutions |
| **Bingham Plastic** | Requires a yield stress before flow begins | Linear but does not pass through origin | Toothpaste, drilling mud, mayonnaise |
| **Thixotropic** | Viscosity decreases with time under constant shear | Time-dependent | Gelatin, honey, printer's ink |
| **Rheopectic** | Viscosity increases with time under constant shear | Time-dependent (rare) | Gypsum paste, some lubricants |

## 2.3 The Role of Temperature and Pressure

**Temperature Effect:**
- **Liquids:** Viscosity **decreases** sharply with temperature (e.g., lubricating oil thins when hot). Governed by the **Andrade equation**:
$$\mu = A \cdot e^{B/T}$$
- **Gases:** Viscosity **increases** with temperature (governed by kinetic theory, $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$).

**Pressure Effect:**
- For liquids, viscosity generally **increases** with pressure (molecules are forced closer, increasing intermolecular forces).
- For gases at moderate pressures, viscosity is largely **independent** of pressure.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | Pa, Pa·s, s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity = dynamic / density | m²/s |
| 3 | $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ | Dynamic viscosity from kinematic | Pa·s |
| 4 | $\mu_{\text{Poise}} = \mu_{\text{Pa·s}} \times 10$ | CGS (Poise) to SI conversion | 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s |
| 5 | $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$ | Centipoise to Pascal-second | — |
| 6 | $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m²/s}$ | Centistokes to m²/s | — |
| 7 | $1 \text{ reyn} = 6890 \text{ cP}$ | Imperial reyn (rarely used) | — |
| 8 | $\frac{F}{A} = \mu \cdot \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta y}$ | Discrete form of Newton's law | — |
| 9 | $\mu = A \cdot e^{B/T}$ | Andrade equation (liquids) | — |
| 10 | $\mu_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i} x_i \mu_i$ | Mixture rule (ideal blending) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference for Exam:**
> - **1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP**
> - **1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt**
> - Always use **SI units** in KTU 2024 scheme unless otherwise specified.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The viscosity concept is the **heart of fluid mechanics** and rheology. Without precise viscosity data:
- **Pipeline designers** cannot predict pressure drops (Hagen-Poiseuille equation requires $\mu$).
- **Pump manufacturers** cannot specify motor power (affects NPSH calculations).
- **Lubricant engineers** cannot grade engine oils (SAE J300 uses kinematic viscosity at 100 °C).
- **Polymer engineers** cannot predict extrusion behavior.
- **Medical device designers** cannot model blood flow in stents and dialysis machines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving the SI Unit of Dynamic Viscosity from Newton's Law

Starting from Newton's Law of Viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 1 — Express the unit of shear stress ($\tau$):**
Shear stress is force per unit area:

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} \implies \text{Unit} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Express the unit of velocity gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$):**

Velocity has unit m/s, and distance has unit m. Therefore:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{\text{m/s}}{\text{m}} = \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange Newton's law to solve for $\mu$:**

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\left( \frac{du}{dy} \right)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the units:**

$$\mu = \frac{\text{Pa}}{\text{s}^{-1}} = \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the SI unit:**

$$\boxed{\mu_{\text{SI}} = \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{m}^2} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}}$$

This is the dimensional form most often requested in KTU derivations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CGS Unit Conversion (Poise to Pascal-second)

The **CGS** (Centimeter-Gram-Second) system uses the **poise (P)** as the unit of dynamic viscosity, named after **Jean Léonard Marie Poiseuille**.

**Step 1 — State the conversion factor:**
By definition:

$$1 \text{ P} = 1 \frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm} \cdot \text{s}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert each unit to SI:**
- $1 \text{ g} = 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$
- $1 \text{ cm} = 10^{-2} \text{ m}$
- $1 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ s}$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the poise definition:**

$$1 \text{ P} = \frac{10^{-3} \text{ kg}}{10^{-2} \text{ m} \cdot 1 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$1 \text{ P} = 10^{-3 - (-2)} \cdot \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}} = 10^{-1} \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize this is exactly the SI unit definition:**

$$1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s} = 10^{-1} \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-2} \text{ P} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}}$$

This explains why water at 20 °C has viscosity ≈ 1 cP.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kinematic Viscosity Unit Derivation

**Step 1 — Start with the defining relationship:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the SI units:**
- $\mu$ has units of $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}$
- $\rho$ has units of $\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}$

**Step 3 — Perform the unit division:**

$$\nu = \frac{\left( \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}} \right)}{\left( \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \right)}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by canceling kilograms and combining m exponents:**

$$\nu = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}} \times \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the SI unit:**

$$\boxed{\nu_{\text{SI}} = \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

The CGS unit **stokes (St)** is defined as 1 cm²/s, hence:

$$1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}, \quad 1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Conversion Between Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity (with Worked Numerical Example)

**Given:** A lubricating oil has dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.25 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ and density $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Apply the conversion formula:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\nu = \frac{0.25 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}}{880 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the unit equivalences and simplify:**

$$\nu = \frac{0.25 \text{ kg/(m} \cdot \text{s)}}{880 \text{ kg/m}^3} = \frac{0.25}{880} \cdot \frac{\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^3}{\text{kg} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{s}}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the kilograms:**

$$\nu = \frac{0.25}{880} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the numerical evaluation:**

$$\nu = 2.8409 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to centistokes for industrial reporting:**

$$\nu = 2.8409 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \times \frac{10^6 \text{ cSt}}{1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}} = 284.09 \text{ cSt}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\nu = 2.841 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 284.1 \text{ cSt}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Viscosity Types

| Parameter | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
|-----------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Physical meaning | Internal friction between layers | Resistance to flow under gravity |
| Includes density? | **No** | **Yes** (via $\rho$) |
| Symbol | $\mu$ | $\nu$ |
| SI unit | Pa·s | m²/s |
| CGS unit | poise (P) | stokes (St) |
| Common industrial sub-unit | centipoise (cP) | centistokes (cSt) |
| Measured by | Rotational viscometer, falling-ball | Capillary viscometer (e.g., Redwood) |
| Temperature dependence | Strong (liquids), via Andrade eqn. | Strong (via both $\mu$ and $\rho$) |
| Used in | Momentum transport equations | Gravitational flow calculations |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Viscosity Definitions & Classification

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy of viscosity as a study aid for revision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["FLUID PROPERTY: VISCOSITY"] --> nodeB["Internal resistance to flow"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Defined by Newton's Law: tau = mu times du/dy"]
    
    nodeB --> nodeD["Dynamic Viscosity mu"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Kinematic Viscosity nu = mu over rho"]
    
    nodeD --> nodeF["SI Unit: Pa.s"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["CGS Unit: Poise P"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Sub-unit: cP = 10 power -3 Pa.s"]
    
    nodeE --> nodeI["SI Unit: m squared per s"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["CGS Unit: Stokes St"]
    nodeE --> nodeK["Sub-unit: cSt = 10 power -6 m squared per s"]
    
    nodeC --> nodeL["Newtonian Fluids: mu constant"]
    nodeC --> nodeM["Non-Newtonian Fluids: mu varies"]
    
    nodeM --> nodeN["Pseudoplastic"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["Dilatant"]
    nodeM --> nodeP["Bingham Plastic"]
    nodeM --> nodeQ["Thixotropic"]
    nodeM --> nodeR["Rheopectic"]
    
    nodeF --> nodeS["Temperature Dependence"]
    nodeI --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeT["Liquids: mu decreases as T rises"]
    nodeS --> nodeU["Gases: mu increases as T rises"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Newton's Law of Viscosity in a Measurement System

This block diagram represents how the definitional concept of viscosity translates into a real measurement chain in an industrial viscometer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["SHEAR STRESS APPLIED tau"] --> node2["FLUID SAMPLE IN TEST SECTION"]
    node2 --> node3["VELOCITY GRADIENT du by dy IS GENERATED"]
    node3 --> node4["MEASUREMENT SENSOR Detects force or flow"]
    node4 --> node5["SIGNAL CONDITIONING Amplifier and filter"]
    node5 --> node6["COMPUTATION UNIT Applies tau = mu times du/dy"]
    node6 --> node7["DYNAMIC VISCOSITY mu OUTPUT in Pa.s"]
    node6 --> node8["KINEMATIC VISCOSITY nu OUTPUT in cSt after density input"]
    node8 --> node9["DISPLAY OR TRANSMITTER 4-20 mA output to DCS or PLC"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Velocity Profile of a Newtonian Fluid Between Two Parallel Plates

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    plateA["TOP MOVING PLATE Velocity U"]
    plateB["BOTTOM FIXED PLATE Velocity zero"]
    arrow1["Layer 1: U top"]
    arrow2["Layer 2: 0.75 U"]
    arrow3["Layer 3: 0.5 U"]
    arrow4["Layer 4: 0.25 U"]
    arrow5["Layer 5: 0 bottom"]
    
    plateA --> arrow1
    arrow1 --> arrow2
    arrow2 --> arrow3
    arrow3 --> arrow4
    arrow4 --> arrow5
    arrow5 --> plateB
    
    force["APPLIED FORCE F SHEAR STRESS tau = F by A"] --> arrow1
```

**Interpretation:** Each horizontal layer moves at a progressively decreasing velocity, creating a **linear velocity profile**. The slope of this profile ($\frac{du}{dy}$) is the velocity gradient, and the shear stress $\tau$ required to maintain this profile is governed by $\mu$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is defined as the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion between its adjacent layers. Quantitatively, it is the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to the velocity gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$) for a Newtonian fluid.

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\left( \frac{du}{dy} \right)}$$

**SI Unit:** Pa·s (Pascal-second) or equivalently $\text{kg/(m·s)}$.

**Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**SI Unit:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (meter squared per second).

---

### **Question 2: State and explain Newton's Law of Viscosity. What is a Newtonian fluid?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Newton's Law of Viscosity** states that the shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain or velocity gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$), and the constant of proportionality is the dynamic viscosity ($\mu$).

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy} \implies \tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Newtonian Fluid:** A fluid that obeys Newton's law of viscosity strictly, i.e., its viscosity $\mu$ remains **constant** irrespective of the applied shear rate or shear stress. The shear stress vs. shear rate plot is a **straight line passing through the origin**. Examples: water, air, light hydrocarbons, alcohol.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the SI unit of dynamic viscosity from Newton's law. Convert 1 poise into SI units.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Starting equation:**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 1 — Identify units of shear stress:**

$$\tau = \frac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Identify units of velocity gradient:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{\text{m/s}}{\text{m}} = \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $\mu$:**

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{(du/dy)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the units:**

$$\mu = \frac{\text{Pa}}{\text{s}^{-1}} = \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Express in fundamental SI base units:**

$$\mu = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}$$

**Final SI unit:** $\boxed{\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s} = \text{kg/(m} \cdot \text{s)}}$

**Conversion of 1 Poise to SI:**

By definition: $1 \text{ P} = 1 \text{ g/(cm} \cdot \text{s)}$

Converting:
- $1 \text{ g} = 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$
- $1 \text{ cm} = 10^{-2} \text{ m}$

$$1 \text{ P} = \frac{10^{-3} \text{ kg}}{10^{-2} \text{ m} \cdot \text{s}} = 10^{-1} \text{ kg/(m} \cdot \text{s)}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}}$$

Therefore, $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$.

---

**(b) Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with suitable examples and stress-rate diagrams.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| S.No. | Feature | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|-------|---------|-----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Viscosity $\mu$ | Constant (independent of shear rate) | Apparent viscosity varies with shear rate |
| 2 | Newton's law | Strictly obeys $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$ | Does not obey linear relationship |
| 3 | $\tau$ vs. $du/dy$ plot | Straight line through origin | Curved or shifted (with yield stress) |
| 4 | Examples | Water, air, alcohol, light oils | Blood, paint, ketchup, toothpaste |
| 5 | Molecular structure | Simple, low molecular weight | Complex, long-chain polymers |

**Categories of Non-Newtonian Fluids:**

1. **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning):** Apparent viscosity decreases as shear rate increases. Example: paint, blood.
2. **Dilatant (Shear-thickening):** Apparent viscosity increases as shear rate increases. Example: cornstarch suspension.
3. **Bingham Plastic:** Behaves like a solid until a yield stress is exceeded, then flows linearly. Example: toothpaste, drilling mud.
4. **Thixotropic:** Viscosity decreases with time under constant shear. Example: gelatin.
5. **Rheopectic:** Viscosity increases with time under constant shear. Example: gypsum paste.

**Conclusion:** Only Newtonian fluids have a single, well-defined viscosity value; for non-Newtonian fluids, "viscosity" is an apparent value that depends on the measurement conditions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Define kinematic viscosity. A fluid has dynamic viscosity 0.5 Pa·s and density 900 kg/m³. Calculate its kinematic viscosity in m²/s and centistokes.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$) is the ratio of dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) to the fluid density ($\rho$).

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.5 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$
- $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$\nu = \frac{0.5 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}}{900 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Pa·s to fundamental units:**

$$0.5 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s} = \frac{0.5 \text{ kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division:**

$$\nu = \frac{0.5}{900} \cdot \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}} \times \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$\nu = \frac{0.5}{900} \cdot \text{m}^2/\text{s} = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to centistokes:**
Using $1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 10^6 \text{ cSt}$:

$$\nu = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6 \text{ cSt} = 555.6 \text{ cSt}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\nu = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 555.6 \text{ cSt}}$$

---

**(b) Explain the effect of temperature on the viscosity of liquids and gases. State the Andrade equation.** **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**For Liquids:**
As temperature increases, the viscosity of a liquid **decreases sharply**. This is because:
- Increased thermal energy reduces intermolecular cohesive forces.
- Molecules slide past each other more easily.
- The fluid flows more readily at higher temperatures.

**Example:** Heavy lubricating oil becomes very fluid in summer; molasses flows faster when heated.

**Andrade Equation (for liquids):**

$$\mu = A \cdot e^{B/T}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $A$ and $B$ = empirical constants specific to the fluid

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(\mu) = \ln(A) + \frac{B}{T}$$

A plot of $\ln(\mu)$ versus $1/T$ is a **straight line**, with slope $B$ and intercept $\ln(A)$.

**For Gases:**
As temperature increases, the viscosity of a gas **increases**. This is because:
- Molecular momentum transfer dominates over intermolecular forces.
- Higher temperature means higher molecular velocities and more frequent collisions.
- This transfers momentum more effectively between layers.

The kinetic theory of gases predicts:

$$\mu_{\text{gas}} \propto \sqrt{T}$$

**Comparative Summary:**

| Property | Liquids | Gases |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Trend with increasing T | Viscosity **decreases** | Viscosity **increases** |
| Governing mechanism | Reduced cohesive forces | Increased momentum transfer |
| Governing equation | Andrade: $\mu = A e^{B/T}$ | $\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$ |
| Typical magnitude | $10^{-3}$ to $10^3$ Pa·s | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa·s |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently write "1 cP = 1 Pa·s" — this is **wrong**. The correct conversion is $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting Density in Kinematic Viscosity:** When asked for kinematic viscosity, students sometimes report the dynamic viscosity value directly. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing $\mu$ and $\nu$:** Swapping the symbols in derivations. Always remember: $\mu$ = dynamic, $\nu$ = kinematic = $\mu/\rho$.
> 4. **Not Drawing $\tau$ vs. $du/dy$ Diagrams:** In 7-mark questions on Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian fluids, **diagrams are mandatory**. Missing them costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Wrong Temperature Trend:** Stating that liquid viscosity increases with temperature — it actually **decreases** for liquids. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping Units in Derivations:** Always carry the units through every step; KTU examiners specifically check dimensional consistency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Viscosity** is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow, defined via Newton's Law: $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$.
- **Dynamic viscosity $\mu$** has SI unit **Pa·s** and CGS unit **poise (P)**; $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$.
- **Kinematic viscosity $\nu$** has SI unit **m²/s** and CGS unit **stokes (St)**; $1 \text{ St} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Crucial relationship:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ where $\rho$ is the fluid density.
- **Newtonian fluids** have constant $\mu$ (water, air, alcohol); **Non-Newtonian fluids** do not (paint, blood, toothpaste).
- **Liquids:** viscosity **decreases** with temperature (Andrade equation: $\mu = A e^{B/T}$).
- **Gases:** viscosity **increases** with temperature ($\mu \propto \sqrt{T}$).
- **Common sub-units:** **cP** ($= 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) and **cSt** ($= 10^{-6}$ m²/s) — used in industry for convenience.
- **Five Non-Newtonian categories to remember:** Pseudoplastic, Dilatant, Bingham Plastic, Thixotropic, Rheopectic.
- **Always carry units through derivations** — dimensional consistency is heavily rewarded in KTU marking schemes.
- **At 20 °C, water** has $\mu \approx 1 \text{ cP}$ and $\nu \approx 1 \text{ cSt}$ — useful as a quick reference benchmark.
- **Industrial significance:** Viscosity measurement is essential in petroleum, pharmaceutical, food, polymer, paint, and chemical industries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
