topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Units in Industrial Instrumentation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Instrumentation**, **Units** are standardized, internationally agreed-upon reference quantities used to express the magnitude of physical variables (such as pressure, temperature, flow, level, force, displacement, and electrical quantities) measured and controlled by process instruments. A unit is a definite magnitude of a physical quantity, defined and adopted by convention, with which other magnitudes of the same kind are compared to express their value.

The **International System of Units (SI - Système International d'Unités)** is the modern form of the metric system and is the universally accepted standard in industrial process control and instrumentation engineering. The SI system is governed by the **International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM – Bureau International des Poids et Mesures)** located in Sèvres, France.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 Scheme, the topic "Units" encompasses **SI base units, derived units, supplementary units, prefixes (multiples & sub-multiples), and the specific engineering units used in process industries** for variables like pressure (Pa, bar, psi, mmHg), temperature (°C, K, °F), flow (m³/s, L/min), and electrical quantities (V, A, Ω).

## 1.2 The Two Broad Classes of Units

| Class | Definition | Examples |
|-------|------------|----------|
| **Fundamental (Base) Units** | Independent units that are not derived from other units. Defined by physical standards or natural constants. | metre (m), kilogram (kg), second (s), ampere (A), kelvin (K), mole (mol), candela (cd) |
| **Derived Units** | Formed by combining base units through multiplication/division. | Newton (N), Pascal (Pa), Joule (J), Watt (W), Hertz (Hz) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Ruler of Reality"

Imagine you are buying rice in a market. The shopkeeper asks, "How much?" If you simply say "five," the question is incomplete — *five what?* Five kilograms? Five pounds? Five bags? The number **5** is meaningless without a **unit** of measurement.

In industrial instrumentation, every reading on a Pressure Gauge, Thermocouple display, or Flow transmitter is a **number combined with a unit**. The transmitter outputs, for instance, **4–20 mA** representing **0–10 bar**. Strip away the unit, and the reading becomes physically meaningless. Units are therefore the **"grammar"** that converts raw numerical data into comprehensible, communicable engineering information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant Highlight:** The speed of light in vacuum, **c = 299,792,458 m/s**, is now used to define the metre. The Planck constant **h = 6.62607015 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** defines the kilogram. These constants form the bedrock of modern metrology.

## 1.4 Historical Evolution of Units

The path to standardized units:

- **1790s** – French Revolution → Metric system (metre, kilogram) introduced.
- **1875** – **Metre Convention** signed by 17 nations; BIPM established.
- **1960** – 11th CGPM (General Conference on Weights and Measures) officially adopted the name "SI".
- **2019** – SI redefined in terms of fixed fundamental constants (not physical artefacts).
- **Present** – Used globally in industry, science, and commerce.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** In KTU 2024 OEICT833 Module 3, students must master the SI framework, common industrial unit conversions, and dimensional analysis applied to process variables.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven SI Base Units

| # | Base Quantity | SI Unit Name | Symbol | Definition Reference |
|---|---------------|--------------|--------|----------------------|
| 1 | Length | metre | **m** | Distance light travels in 1/299,792,458 s |
| 2 | Mass | kilogram | **kg** | Fixed by Planck constant h |
| 3 | Time | second | **s** | 9,192,631,770 cycles of Cs-133 hyperfine transition |
| 4 | Electric current | ampere | **A** | Fixed by elementary charge e |
| 5 | Temperature | kelvin | **K** | Fixed by Boltzmann constant k |
| 6 | Amount of substance | mole | **mol** | 6.02214076 × 10²³ entities (Avogadro constant) |
| 7 | Luminous intensity | candela | **cd** | Fixed by luminous efficacy of 540 THz radiation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Questions frequently ask students to *list the 7 base units*, *identify derived units*, or *state which SI unit corresponds to a given physical quantity* like force, pressure, energy, or power.

## 2.2 Important Derived SI Units in Industrial Instrumentation

| Derived Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol | Expression in Base Units |
|------------------|---------|--------|--------------------------|
| Force | newton | N | kg·m·s⁻² |
| Pressure / Stress | pascal | Pa | N·m⁻² = kg·m⁻¹·s⁻² |
| Energy / Work / Heat | joule | J | N·m = kg·m²·s⁻² |
| Power | watt | W | J·s⁻¹ = kg·m²·s⁻³ |
| Frequency | hertz | Hz | s⁻¹ |
| Electric charge | coulomb | C | A·s |
| Electric potential difference | volt | V | W·A⁻¹ = kg·m²·s⁻³·A⁻¹ |
| Electric resistance | ohm | Ω | V·A⁻¹ = kg·m²·s⁻³·A⁻² |
| Electric capacitance | farad | F | C·V⁻¹ = kg⁻¹·m⁻²·s⁴·A² |
| Magnetic flux | weber | Wb | V·s = kg·m²·s⁻²·A⁻¹ |
| Magnetic flux density | tesla | T | Wb·m⁻² = kg·s⁻²·A⁻¹ |
| Inductance | henry | H | Wb·A⁻¹ = kg·m²·s⁻²·A⁻² |
| Luminous flux | lumen | lm | cd·sr |

## 2.3 SI Prefixes (Multiples & Sub-multiples)

The SI system uses decimal-based prefixes. In industrial transmitters, you frequently encounter **kPa, MPa, mA, μA, mV**.

| Prefix | Symbol | Factor | Decimal |
|--------|--------|--------|---------|
| tera | T | 10¹² | 1,000,000,000,000 |
| giga | G | 10⁹ | 1,000,000,000 |
| mega | M | 10⁶ | 1,000,000 |
| kilo | k | 10³ | 1,000 |
| hecto | h | 10² | 100 |
| deca | da | 10¹ | 10 |
| (none) | — | 10⁰ | 1 |
| deci | d | 10⁻¹ | 0.1 |
| centi | c | 10⁻² | 0.01 |
| milli | m | 10⁻³ | 0.001 |
| micro | μ | 10⁻⁶ | 0.000001 |
| nano | n | 10⁻⁹ | 0.000000001 |
| pico | p | 10⁻¹² | 0.000000000001 |

## 2.4 Industrial Unit Conversion Cheat Sheet

Industrialists worldwide still use non-SI units. Mastering conversions is a **high-yield KTU topic**.

| Quantity | SI Unit | Common Industrial Units | Conversion Formula |
|----------|---------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Pressure | Pa (pascal) | bar, psi, atm, mmHg, Torr, kgf/cm² | 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa; 1 atm = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa; 1 psi = 6894.76 Pa; 1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa; 1 kgf/cm² = 9.80665 × 10⁴ Pa |
| Temperature | K (kelvin) | °C, °F, °R | K = °C + 273.15; °F = (9/5)·°C + 32 |
| Flow rate | m³/s | L/min, m³/h, gpm, scfh | 1 m³/h = 16.667 L/min; 1 gpm (US) = 3.785 L/min |
| Length | m | mm, μm, inch, ft | 1 inch = 0.0254 m; 1 ft = 0.3048 m |
| Mass | kg | g, tonne, lb | 1 lb = 0.4536 kg; 1 tonne = 1000 kg |
| Force | N | kgf, lbf | 1 kgf = 9.80665 N; 1 lbf = 4.44822 N |
| Energy | J | kWh, cal, BTU | 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J; 1 BTU = 1055.06 J |
| Viscosity (dynamic) | Pa·s | cP (centipoise) | 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s |
| Viscosity (kinematic) | m²/s | cSt (centistoke) | 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s |

## 2.5 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Unit Standardization

**Why standardized units?**
- **Global Interoperability:** A transmitter manufactured in Germany can interface with a controller in India seamlessly.
- **Safety:** Misinterpretation of pressure units in a chemical plant can lead to catastrophic accidents (e.g., confusing psi with bar in a reactor vessel).
- **Legal & Trade Compliance:** Most national and international standards (ISO 9001, IEC 61511, OSHA) mandate the use of SI units in process documentation.

**How is dimensional consistency maintained?**
- Through **Dimensional Analysis**: Every equation must be dimensionally homogeneous. Adding metres to seconds is meaningless.
- Using **conversion factors** that are pure numbers (dimensionless ratios) like:

$$
1 \text{ bar} = 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1 \text{ bar}}{10^{5} \text{ Pa}} = 1 \text{ (dimensionless)}
$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$
\boxed{K = °C + 273.15}
$$

$$
\boxed{°F = \frac{9}{5} \cdot °C + 32}
$$

$$
\boxed{°C = \frac{5}{9} \cdot (°F - 32)}
$$

$$
\boxed{1 \text{ bar} = 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 14.5038 \text{ psi}}
$$

$$
\boxed{1 \text{ atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa} = 760 \text{ mmHg}}
$$

$$
\boxed{1 \text{ kgf/cm}^{2} = 9.80665 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}}
$$

$$
\boxed{1 \text{ cal} = 4.184 \text{ J}}
$$

$$
\boxed{1 \text{ BTU} = 1055.06 \text{ J}}
$$

$$
\boxed{1 \text{ hp (metric)} = 735.5 \text{ W}}
$$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Wellhead pressure is commonly expressed in **psi** in the US and **bar** in Europe. Engineers must convert rapidly.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Cleanroom temperature is controlled in **°C**, while US FDA filings may use **°F** or **K**.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler steam pressure uses **MPa**; flow rate uses **T/h (tonnes per hour)**.
- **Instrumentation Calibration:** Calibration certificates (e.g., ISO 17025) report uncertainties in SI units with traceability to national standards (e.g., NIST, NPL).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Kelvin–Celsius Relationship

The Celsius scale fixes 0 °C at the freezing point of water and 100 °C at the boiling point (at 1 atm). The Kelvin scale sets its zero at **absolute zero** (0 K = −273.15 °C). Since both scales use the same incremental unit size, the relationship is linear.

**Step 1:** Let T (in K) be the absolute temperature and t (in °C) be the Celsius temperature.

**Step 2:** Recognize that a temperature *interval* of 1 °C equals 1 K exactly (a defining property of the SI).

**Step 3:** When t = 0 °C, the absolute temperature is T = 273.15 K (by definition).

**Step 4:** Build a linear equation: T = t + 273.15.

**Step 5:** Conclude:

$$
T_{K} = t_{°C} + 273.15
$$

**Verification:** At t = 100 °C (boiling point of water), T = 373.15 K. ✓ At t = −273.15 °C, T = 0 K (absolute zero). ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Fahrenheit–Celsius Relationship

**Step 1:** The Fahrenheit scale assigns 32 °F to the freezing point and 212 °F to the boiling point of water.

**Step 2:** Compute the Fahrenheit interval: 212 − 32 = 180 °F spans the same temperature range as 100 °C − 0 °C = 100 °C.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the slope of °F vs °C is 180/100 = 9/5.

**Step 4:** The intercept (offset) is 32 (since 0 °C = 32 °F).

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$
T_{°F} = \frac{9}{5} \cdot T_{°C} + 32
$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging for °C:

$$
T_{°C} = \frac{5}{9} \cdot (T_{°F} - 32)
$$

**Verification:** At t = 100 °C: °F = (9/5)·100 + 32 = 180 + 32 = 212 °F. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pressure Unit Conversion (psi ↔ bar ↔ Pa)

**Step 1:** Define the SI unit: 1 Pa = 1 N/m².

**Step 2:** Define 1 bar exactly as 10⁵ Pa (this is the IUPAC definition, not an approximation).

**Step 3:** Define 1 psi as the pressure from 1 pound-force applied over 1 square inch. We know:
- 1 lbf = 4.4482216152605 N
- 1 in² = (0.0254 m)² = 6.4516 × 10⁻⁴ m²

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$
1 \text{ psi} = \frac{4.44822 \text{ N}}{6.4516 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}} = 6894.76 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 5:** Express in bar:

$$
1 \text{ psi} = \frac{6894.76}{10^{5}} \text{ bar} = 0.0689476 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$
1 \text{ bar} = \frac{1}{0.0689476} \text{ psi} = 14.5038 \text{ psi}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Industrial Pressure Transmitter Conversion

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter in a refinery reads **3.5 bar**. Convert this to (a) Pa, (b) psi, (c) atm, (d) mmHg.

**Solution:**

**(a) Pa:**
$$
P = 3.5 \text{ bar} \times \frac{10^{5} \text{ Pa}}{1 \text{ bar}} = 3.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 350 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(b) psi:**
$$
P = 3.5 \text{ bar} \times \frac{14.5038 \text{ psi}}{1 \text{ bar}} = 50.7633 \text{ psi}
$$

**(c) atm:**
$$
P = 3.5 \text{ bar} \times \frac{1 \text{ atm}}{1.01325 \text{ bar}} = 3.4544 \text{ atm}
$$

**(d) mmHg:**
$$
P = 3.5 \text{ bar} \times \frac{10^{5} \text{ Pa}}{1 \text{ bar}} \times \frac{1 \text{ mmHg}}{133.322 \text{ Pa}} = 2625.36 \text{ mmHg}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Temperature Conversion in a Furnace

**Problem:** A furnace operates at **1832 °F**. Express this in (a) °C and (b) K.

**Solution:**

**(a) °C:**
$$
T_{°C} = \frac{5}{9} \cdot (1832 - 32) = \frac{5}{9} \cdot 1800 = 1000 \text{ °C}
$$

**(b) K:**
$$
T_{K} = 1000 + 273.15 = 1273.15 \text{ K}
$$

This is the typical operating temperature of a **steel re-heat furnace** or **glass melting tank**.

---

## 3.6 Dimensional Analysis — Verification of an Equation

**Problem:** Verify dimensional consistency of the ideal gas law: $PV = nRT$

**Step 1:** List the dimensions of each quantity.
- $P$ [pressure] = M·L⁻¹·T⁻²
- $V$ [volume] = L³
- $n$ [amount] = mol (dimensionless in pure SI, but treated as a base)
- $R$ [gas constant] = ?
- $T$ [temperature] = K

**Step 2:** Equate dimensions on both sides:

$$
[P] \cdot [V] = [n] \cdot [R] \cdot [T]
$$

$$
(M \cdot L^{-1} \cdot T^{-2}) \cdot (L^{3}) = [mol] \cdot [R] \cdot [K]
$$

$$
M \cdot L^{2} \cdot T^{-2} = [R] \cdot [mol] \cdot [K]
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore $[R] = M \cdot L^{2} \cdot T^{-2} \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$ which is the correct dimension of $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)} = \text{kg·m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-2} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$. ✓

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Unit Categories Used in Process Plants

| Process Variable | Primary SI Unit | Transmitter Output | Common Field Units | Conversion Notes |
|------------------|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Pressure (gauge) | Pa | 4–20 mA | bar, psi, kg/cm² | 1 bar = 14.504 psi |
| Differential Pressure | Pa | 4–20 mA / HART | mbar, inH₂O | 1 inH₂O = 249.089 Pa |
| Temperature | K | RTD / Thermocouple mV | °C, °F | °C = K − 273.15 |
| Flow (volumetric) | m³/s | 4–20 mA | m³/h, L/min | 1 m³/h = 16.67 L/min |
| Flow (mass) | kg/s | 4–20 mA | T/h (tonne/h), kg/h | 1 T/h = 1000 kg/h |
| Level | m | 4–20 mA / ultrasonic | mm, % | 1 m = 1000 mm |
| Viscosity (dynamic) | Pa·s | — | cP, poise | 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s |
| pH (dimensionless) | — | mV | — | Nernst: 59.16 mV/pH |
| Conductivity | S/m | 4–20 mA | μS/cm | 1 S/m = 10 mS/cm |

---

## 3.8 KTU Analytical Pitfall Matrix

| Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
|----------------|------------------|
| Writing "°K" (degree kelvin) | SI convention is just **K** (no degree sign) |
| Using "amps" in equations | Symbol is **A** (uppercase, no plural) |
| Confusing mass (kg) with weight (kgf or N) | Mass is invariant; weight depends on gravity |
| Using mm of water for high pressures | Use bar or MPa; mmH₂O is only for low ΔP (draft) |
| Treating °C and K as interchangeable in *differences* | Interval: ΔT(°C) = ΔT(K). *Absolute* T differs by 273.15 |
| Forgetting that bar ≠ atm exactly | 1 bar = 100,000 Pa; 1 atm = 101,325 Pa |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Units in the SI System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the structural hierarchy of units, from the foundational SI base units to industrial application units.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[SI System of Units]
    nodeB[7 Base Units]
    nodeC[Derived Units with Special Names]
    nodeD[Derived Units without Special Names]
    nodeE[Supplementary Units]
    nodeF[Industrial Application Units]

    nodeG[m kg s A K mol cd]
    nodeH[N Pa J W Hz V ohm F Wb T H lm]
    nodeI[area m2, volume m3, velocity m/s, acceleration m/s2]
    nodeJ[radian rad, steradian sr]
    nodeK[bar psi mmHg atm kgf cm2 BTU cal]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF

    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Industrial Unit Conversion Flow

The diagram below maps how a physical process variable travels from the field sensor to the control room, undergoing unit transformation at each stage.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP[Physical Process Variable]
    nodeS[Field Sensor Transducer]
    nodeT[Transmitter Signal Conditioning]
    nodeC[Controller in Control Room]
    nodeD[Display HMI SCADA]
    nodeL[Local Indicator Gauge]
    nodeU[Unit Conversion Module]

    nodeP --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeT --> nodeL

    nodeU -.-> nodeS
    nodeU -.-> nodeT
    nodeU -.-> nodeC
    nodeU -.-> nodeD

    subgraph unitbank[Unit Conversion Database]
      unitbar[bar]
      unitpsi[psi]
      unitpa[Pa]
      unitc[degC]
      unitk[K]
      unitf[degF]
    end

    nodeU --- unitbank
```

**Explanation of flow:**

1. The **physical variable** (e.g., pressure in a pipe) is detected by the field sensor.
2. The **transmitter** converts it into a standardized electrical signal (4–20 mA).
3. The **controller** interprets the signal in engineering units.
4. The **HMI/SCADA** displays the value in operator-selected units (bar, psi, etc.).
5. The **Unit Conversion Module** is the algorithmic engine that performs real-time conversion using the **unit bank** of constants.

## 4.3 Conversion Pathway Map for Pressure

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Field Pressure bar]
    nodeB[SI Pa]
    nodeC[Imperial psi]
    nodeD[Atmospheric atm]
    nodeE[mmHg Torr]
    nodeF[kgf per cm2]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF

    nodeC --> nodeB
    nodeD --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeF --> nodeB
```

**Reading the map:** The SI unit (Pa) acts as the central **hub** through which all pressure conversions pass. To convert *bar → psi*, one must traverse *bar → Pa → psi*, applying the conversion factors 10⁵ and 6894.76 respectively.

## 4.4 Temperature Scale Comparison Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Absolute Zero]
    nodeB[Freezing Point of Water]
    nodeC[Boiling Point of Water]

    nodeD[0 K]
    nodeE[273.15 K]
    nodeF[373.15 K]

    nodeG[minus 273.15 degC]
    nodeH[0 degC]
    nodeI[100 degC]

    nodeJ[minus 459.67 degF]
    nodeK[32 degF]
    nodeL[212 degF]

    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeA --> nodeJ

    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeK

    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeL
```

**Interpretation:** The three physical reference points (absolute zero, water freezing, water boiling) correspond to three numerical values on each of the three major temperature scales (K, °C, °F).

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Learning Aid:** Students should memorize the three reference points: **0 K = −273.15 °C = −459.67 °F**, **273.15 K = 0 °C = 32 °F**, **373.15 K = 100 °C = 212 °F**. This forms the basis for *all* temperature conversion questions in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the SI system of units. List the seven base quantities and their corresponding SI units. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **SI (Système International d'Unités)** is the internationally accepted decimal metric system of measurement established by the 11th General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM) in 1960. It is maintained by the **International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM)**.

The **seven base quantities** and their SI units are:

| # | Base Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol |
|---|---------------|---------|--------|
| 1 | Length | metre | m |
| 2 | Mass | kilogram | kg |
| 3 | Time | second | s |
| 4 | Electric current | ampere | A |
| 5 | Temperature | kelvin | K |
| 6 | Amount of substance | mole | mol |
| 7 | Luminous intensity | candela | cd |

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition (1 mark) + listing 7 base quantities (1.5 marks) + correct symbols (0.5 mark) = **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between fundamental units and derived units. Give two examples of each. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Fundamental Units | Derived Units |
|-------------------|---------------|
| Independent, not derivable from other units | Formed by algebraic combination of fundamental units |
| Defined by physical standards or fundamental constants | Defined by mathematical expressions involving base units |
| Examples: metre (m), kilogram (kg), second (s) | Examples: newton (N = kg·m·s⁻²), pascal (Pa = N/m²) |

**Marks Distribution:** Differentiation (1 mark) + examples of each (2 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of the International System of Units (SI). List the seven base units, two supplementary units, and any five derived units with their symbols. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The SI system can be visualized as a hierarchical pyramid structure:

**Block Diagram (Text Representation):**

```
                    ┌──────────────────────┐
                    │   SI SYSTEM OF       │
                    │   MEASUREMENT        │
                    └──────────┬───────────┘
                               │
        ┌──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┐
        │                      │                      │
   ┌────▼─────┐         ┌──────▼──────┐        ┌──────▼──────┐
   │  Base    │         │  Derived    │        │Supplementary│
   │  Units   │         │  Units      │        │  Units      │
   │  (7)     │         │  (Named)    │        │  (2)        │
   └────┬─────┘         └──────┬──────┘        └──────┬──────┘
        │                      │                      │
   m, kg, s, A, K,       N, Pa, J, W, Hz,        rad, sr
   mol, cd               V, Ω, F, Wb, T, H
```

**Seven Base Units:** m (length), kg (mass), s (time), A (electric current), K (temperature), mol (amount of substance), cd (luminous intensity). **(2 marks)**

**Two Supplementary Units:** radian (rad) for plane angle, steradian (sr) for solid angle. **(1 mark)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The 1995 CGPM resolution reclassified the supplementary units as *dimensionless derived units*, but the traditional nomenclature is still taught in KTU modules.

**Five Derived Units with symbols:**
1. Force → newton → **N** = kg·m·s⁻²
2. Pressure → pascal → **Pa** = N·m⁻²
3. Energy → joule → **J** = N·m
4. Power → watt → **W** = J·s⁻¹
5. Frequency → hertz → **Hz** = s⁻¹ **(4 marks)**

---

**(b)** Convert the following:
- (i) **150 °C** to K
- (ii) **5 bar** to Pa
- (iii) **1.2 kgf/cm²** to psi
- (iv) **68 °F** to °C **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) 150 °C → K:**
$$
T_{K} = T_{°C} + 273.15 = 150 + 273.15 = 423.15 \text{ K}
$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**(ii) 5 bar → Pa:**
$$
P = 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 500 \text{ kPa}
$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**(iii) 1.2 kgf/cm² → psi:**

First convert kgf/cm² to Pa:
$$
P = 1.2 \times 9.80665 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa} = 1.1768 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}
$$

Then convert Pa to psi:
$$
P = \frac{1.1768 \times 10^{5}}{6894.76} \text{ psi} = 17.067 \text{ psi}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**(iv) 68 °F → °C:**
$$
T_{°C} = \frac{5}{9} \times (68 - 32) = \frac{5}{9} \times 36 = 20 \text{ °C}
$$
**(2 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the difference between mass and weight with respect to their SI units. A vessel has a mass of **250 kg**. Determine its weight in (i) newton, (ii) kgf. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Difference between Mass and Weight:**

| Mass | Weight |
|------|--------|
| Measure of the amount of matter in a body | Measure of the gravitational force on a body |
| SI unit: **kilogram (kg)** | SI unit: **newton (N)** |
| Invariant; does not change with location | Varies with gravitational field |
| Measured using a balance | Measured using a spring scale |

**(2 marks)**

**Calculations:**

Given: m = 250 kg, g = 9.80665 m/s²

**(i) Weight in Newton:**
$$
W = m \times g = 250 \times 9.80665 = 2451.66 \text{ N}
$$
**(2.5 marks)**

**(ii) Weight in kgf:**
Since 1 kgf = 9.80665 N:
$$
W = \frac{2451.66 \text{ N}}{9.80665 \text{ N/kgf}} = 250 \text{ kgf}
$$

(Verification: Numerically, weight in kgf numerically equals mass in kg on Earth.) **(2.5 marks)**

---

**(b)** A thermocouple measures a temperature of **650 °C** in a chemical reactor. Convert this to (i) Kelvin, (ii) Fahrenheit, and (iii) Rankine. The Rankine scale has its zero at absolute zero and uses the Fahrenheit degree interval. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** T = 650 °C

**(i) Kelvin:**
$$
T_{K} = 650 + 273.15 = 923.15 \text{ K}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Fahrenheit:**
$$
T_{°F} = \frac{9}{5} \times 650 + 32 = 1170 + 32 = 1202 \text{ °F}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Rankine:**

The Rankine scale (°R) has 0 °R at absolute zero and 1 °R = 1 °F. Since 1 K = 1.8 °R:

$$
T_{°R} = 923.15 \times 1.8 = 1661.67 \text{ °R}
$$

Alternatively, directly:
$$
T_{°R} = T_{°F} + 459.67 = 1202 + 459.67 = 1661.67 \text{ °R}
$$
**(3 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting 273.15:** Many students write 273 instead of 273.15. KTU examiners *do* deduct 0.5 marks for this. Use **273.15**.
> 2. **°K vs K:** Writing "degree kelvin" or "°K" is **strictly wrong** post-1967. The correct symbol is just **K**.
> 3. **Pipes and units:** Never write "meters/second²" in equations. Use SI symbols: m/s².
> 4. **Pressure mix-up:** 1 bar ≠ 1 atm. The difference (1.325%) can cause errors in safety-critical calculations.
> 5. **Mass vs Force:** Marking weight as "kg" without "kgf" or "N" is a 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Significant figures:** Always maintain consistency. If a problem says "5 bar", final answer should reflect at most 1 significant figure unless otherwise stated.
> 7. **Conversion factor truncation:** Use exact values like 273.15, 1.01325, 9.80665 when required.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **SI system** has **7 base units**: m, kg, s, A, K, mol, cd.
- [x] The symbol for kilogram is **kg** (not "Kg" or "kgs").
- [x] **Kelvin** has no degree sign; always write **K**, never **°K**.
- [x] The defining constant of the **metre** is the speed of light **c = 299,792,458 m/s**.
- [x] The defining constant of the **kilogram** is the Planck constant **h = 6.62607015 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**.
- [x] **Pressure** SI unit is **Pa**; **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa** (exact).
- [x] **1 atm = 101,325 Pa** ≠ 1 bar.
- [x] **1 psi = 6894.76 Pa** = 0.0689476 bar.
- [x] **1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa** (used in vacuum and blood pressure measurements).
- [x] Temperature conversion: **K = °C + 273.15**.
- [x] Fahrenheit conversion: **°F = (9/5)·°C + 32**.
- [x] Rankine conversion: **°R = °F + 459.67** = **1.8 × K**.
- [x] **Newton (N)** is a derived unit of force: **N = kg·m·s⁻²**.
- [x] **Pascal (Pa)** is a derived unit of pressure: **Pa = N/m²**.
- [x] **Joule (J)** is a derived unit of energy: **J = N·m**.
- [x] **Watt (W)** is a derived unit of power: **W = J/s**.
- [x] SI prefixes follow powers of 10: **k = 10³, M = 10⁶, μ = 10⁻⁶, n = 10⁻⁹**.
- [x] **Flow rate** SI unit: **m³/s**; common industrial: **m³/h**, **L/min**, **T/h**.
- [x] **Viscosity** dynamic: **Pa·s**; common: **cP** where 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s.
- [x] **Supplementary units** in traditional SI: **radian (rad)**, **steradian (sr)**.
- [x] **Mass (kg) is invariant; weight (N or kgf) depends on g.**
- [x] In calibration, the **SI base unit of time** is defined using **Cs-133** atomic clock transitions.
- [x] Dimensional analysis verifies that both sides of an equation have identical dimensions.
- [x] Industrial transmitters use **4–20 mA** as a unit-neutral current signal; the engineering unit is assigned at the receiver (controller/HMI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For Part B questions on units, **always show the conversion factor explicitly** with units, and **box your final answer**. Examiners award **method marks** even if the arithmetic slips — but only if your unit-cancellation logic is visible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Instrumentation** and **Process Fluid Mechanics**, the rheological classification of fluids into **Newtonian** and **Non-Newtonian** categories is fundamental to the proper selection of flow meters, pumps, viscosity sensors, and pipeline sizing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newtonian Fluid (Rheological Definition):**
> A fluid is said to exhibit **Newtonian behaviour** if its viscosity remains **constant** (independent of the applied shear rate) and the **shear stress is directly proportional to the shear rate** at a given temperature and pressure. The proportionality constant is known as the **Dynamic Viscosity (μ)**.

Mathematically, this relationship is expressed as **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy} = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$

Where:
- **τ** = Shear Stress (N/m² or Pa)
- **μ** = Dynamic Viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- **dv/dy** = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow = Shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$) in s⁻¹

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Newtonian Fluid (Rheological Definition):**
> A fluid is classified as **Non-Newtonian** if its viscosity is **NOT constant** and varies as a function of the applied shear rate, shear history, or time. The relationship between shear stress and shear rate is **non-linear**, and the apparent viscosity ($\mu_a$) is shear-rate dependent.

The general power-law form for many non-Newtonian fluids is given by:

$$\tau = K \left(\frac{dv}{dy}\right)^n = K \dot{\gamma}^n$$

Where:
- **K** = Consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- **n** = Flow behaviour index (dimensionless)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

### 🍯 The Honey vs. Water Analogy

Imagine two scenarios:

**Scenario 1 — Newtonian (Water):**
Take a glass of water and stir it slowly, then vigorously. The water's "thickness" or resistance to flow feels **exactly the same** regardless of how fast you stir. The viscosity is a *fixed physical property* tied only to temperature.

**Scenario 2 — Non-Newtonian (Toothpaste / Ketchup):**
Take a tube of toothpaste. When it sits on the brush (no shear), it holds its shape like a solid. But when you squeeze (apply shear), it flows easily. Squeeze harder (higher shear rate) and it flows even *more easily* — its viscosity **decreases**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary for KTU Students:**
> - **Newtonian = Predictable** (μ constant, plot is a straight line through origin).
> - **Non-Newtonian = Moody/Complex** (μ changes with shear, plot is curved or has a yield point).

### 1.3 Common Examples (Must-Memorize for KTU Exams)

| Fluid Type | Common Industrial Examples | Behaviour at Rest |
|------------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| **Newtonian** | Water, Air, Alcohol, Glycerine, Light oils, Mercury | Flows immediately under smallest stress |
| **Non-Newtonian** | Blood, Paint, Ketchup, Toothpaste, Slurries, Polymers, Tar | May resist flow until threshold stress is exceeded |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Conditions:**
> Dynamic viscosity of **water at 20°C** = **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** (= 1 cP)
> Kinematic viscosity of **water at 20°C** = **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** (= 1 cSt)
> These values are often assumed in KTU numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

---

## 1.4 Industrial Instrumentation Significance

In industrial flow measurement:
- **Differential Pressure flow meters** (orifice, Venturi) are calibrated assuming **Newtonian** behaviour.
- For **Non-Newtonian fluids**, the Reynolds number is **redefined** using the *generalized Reynolds number* formula, and specialized flow meters (e.g., Coriolis, positive displacement) are preferred.
- Viscosity sensors and online rheometers are deployed for non-Newtonian process streams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Shear Stress — Shear Rate Relationship: The Governing Physics

### 2.1.1 Origin of Viscosity in Fluids

Viscosity in fluids arises from **molecular momentum transfer** between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities. When a fluid flows, faster-moving layers transfer momentum to slower-moving layers, creating an internal resistance to deformation.

- In a **Newtonian fluid**, this momentum transfer mechanism is **linear** — doubling the shear rate exactly doubles the shear stress.
- In a **Non-Newtonian fluid**, structural changes (particle alignment, polymer chain uncoiling, floc breakage) cause the momentum transfer to be **non-linear**.

### 2.1.2 The Three Key Rheological Parameters

For any fluid, three derived parameters are critical:

1. **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** Resistance to shearing deformation (Pa·s).
2. **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density (m²/s).
3. **Apparent Viscosity (μₐ):** Viscosity as "perceived" at a specific shear rate — used for non-Newtonian fluids.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{(Kinematic Viscosity)}$$

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} \quad \text{(Apparent Viscosity, shear-rate dependent)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **Newtonian fluid**, $\mu_a = \mu$ (constant). For a **non-Newtonian fluid**, $\mu_a$ changes with $\dot{\gamma}$.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Non-Newtonian Fluids (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Non-Newtonian fluids are sub-classified into **time-independent** and **time-dependent** categories.

### 2.2.1 Time-Independent Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Type | Behaviour | Power-Law Form | Examples |
|------|-----------|----------------|----------|
| **Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning)** | Viscosity **decreases** with ↑ shear rate | n < 1, no yield stress | Paint, blood, polymer solutions, ketchup |
| **Dilatant (Shear-Thickening)** | Viscosity **increases** with ↑ shear rate | n > 1, no yield stress | Starch suspensions, sand slurries, wet concrete |
| **Bingham Plastic** | Behaves as solid until yield stress τ₀, then flows linearly | τ = τ₀ + μₚγ̇ | Toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge |
| **Generalized Bingham (Herschel-Bulkley)** | Has yield stress, then flows as power-law | τ = τ₀ + Kγ̇ⁿ | Chocolate, certain slurries, coal slurries |

### 2.2.2 Time-Dependent Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Type | Behaviour | Examples |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Thixotropic** | Viscosity **decreases** with time at constant shear; recovers gradually at rest | Yogurt, gels, printing inks |
| **Rheopectic** | Viscosity **increases** with time at constant shear | Some lubricants, gypsum suspensions |

### 2.2.3 Viscoelastic Fluids

These exhibit both **viscous** (fluid-like) and **elastic** (solid-like) properties. Examples: polymer melts, dough, biological fluids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:**
> "Classify Non-Newtonian fluids with examples." This is a guaranteed **Part A (3-mark)** or **Part B sub-question** topic.

---

## 2.3 Rheological Behaviour Diagrams (Conceptual)

The qualitative shear stress vs. shear rate curves for each fluid type:

- **Newtonian:** Straight line through origin.
- **Pseudoplastic:** Concave-down curve through origin.
- **Dilatant:** Concave-up curve through origin.
- **Bingham Plastic:** Straight line with x-intercept at τ₀ (yield stress).
- **Herschel-Bulkley:** Curved line starting from τ₀ on the shear-stress axis.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the basis of all KTU numerical and theoretical questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity (Newtonian) | Pa |
| 2 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Shear rate definition | s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Dynamic viscosity | Pa·s |
| 4 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s |
| 5 | $\mu_a = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent viscosity (non-Newtonian) | Pa·s |
| 6 | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Power-law model (Ostwald-de Waele) | Pa |
| 7 | $n = 1$ | Newtonian limit (μ = K) | — |
| 8 | $n < 1$ | Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) | — |
| 9 | $n > 1$ | Dilatant (shear-thickening) | — |
| 10 | $\tau = \tau_0 + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Bingham Plastic model | Pa |
| 11 | $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Herschel-Bulkley model | Pa |
| 12 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (Newtonian) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $Re_g = \dfrac{D^n v^{2-n} \rho}{8^{n-1} K \left(\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n}$ | Generalized Reynolds number (power-law fluid) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ | Fanning friction factor (laminar, Newtonian) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\mu_w = K \left(\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n \dot{\gamma}_w^{n-1}$ | Wall apparent viscosity (Metzner-Reed) | Pa·s |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoided:** In the table above, all absolute-value-like expressions and divisions use the `dfrac` macro wrapped in `$...$` to keep them safely inside table cells without breaking markdown column separation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Why Rheology Matters |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Petrochemical** | Crude oil, polymer pipelines | Determines pumping power and pipe diameter |
| **Food Processing** | Chocolate, sauce, dairy | Quality control and texture consistency |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Blood, drug formulations | Injection force, IV drip calibration |
| **Paints & Coatings** | Spray application | Avoid sagging (dilatant) or dripping (pseudoplastic) |
| **Cement / Construction** | Concrete pumping | Slump and flow rate design |
| **Cosmetics** | Lotions, gels | Spreadability and skin feel |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Apparent Viscosity from the Power-Law Model

This is the most-asked KTU derivation in this module. We derive the expression for apparent viscosity as a function of shear rate for a power-law fluid.

### Step 1: Start with the Power-Law Constitutive Equation

The general non-Newtonian (power-law) model is:

$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$$

**Explanation:** This is the empirical equation proposed by **Ostwald and de Waele**, where K captures the fluid's "consistency" and n describes the deviation from Newtonian behaviour.

### Step 2: Rearrange for Apparent Viscosity

By definition, apparent viscosity is:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Explanation:** Just like the Newtonian case, we define apparent viscosity as the *ratio* of shear stress to the corresponding shear rate, but now τ depends non-linearly on $\dot{\gamma}$.

### Step 3: Substitute the Power-Law Expression

Substitute the power-law τ into the apparent viscosity equation:

$$\mu_a = \frac{K \dot{\gamma}^n}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Explanation:** We are simply replacing τ with $K \dot{\gamma}^n$ in the numerator.

### Step 4: Simplify the Exponent

Using the laws of exponents ($\dot{\gamma}^n / \dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$):

$$\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Explanation:** The "1" in the exponent comes from dividing by $\dot{\gamma}^1$ in the denominator.

### Step 5: Interpret the Result

$$\boxed{\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If **n = 1** → $\mu_a = K$ = constant → **Newtonian fluid** (K becomes μ).
- If **n < 1** → exponent (n−1) is **negative** → $\mu_a$ **decreases** as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases → **Pseudoplastic**.
- If **n > 1** → exponent (n−1) is **positive** → $\mu_a$ **increases** as $\dot{\gamma}$ increases → **Dilatant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** "n less than one = Pseudoplastic; n greater than one = Dilatant." Mnemonic: **L**ess than one = **L**iquid flows more easily (P**L**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pressure Drop for a Power-Law Fluid in Laminar Pipe Flow

This is a classic KTU 14-mark derivation. We derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equivalent for a non-Newtonian power-law fluid.

### Step 1: Force Balance on a Fluid Cylinder in a Pipe

Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius **r** and length **L** in a pipe of radius **R**, with pressure drop $\Delta P$ across length L.

Force balance (pressure force = viscous force):

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

**Explanation:** The pressure force on the cross-section ($\pi r^2$) pushes the fluid, while the shear force on the lateral surface ($2\pi r L$) resists it.

### Step 2: Solve for Shear Stress τ(r)

Rearranging:

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$

**Explanation:** Shear stress varies **linearly** with radial position r. Maximum at the wall (r = R), zero at the centre (r = 0).

### Step 3: Apply the Power-Law Model

For a power-law fluid:

$$\tau = K \left(-\frac{dv}{dr}\right)^n$$

**Explanation:** The negative sign indicates velocity decreases with increasing r (parabolic-like profile). We take the absolute value for now.

### Step 4: Equate the Two Expressions for τ

$$\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L} = K \left(-\frac{dv}{dr}\right)^n$$

**Explanation:** This links the macroscopic pressure gradient to the local velocity gradient.

### Step 5: Solve for the Velocity Gradient

$$\left(-\frac{dv}{dr}\right)^n = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L K}$$

$$-\frac{dv}{dr} = \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} r^{1/n}$$

**Explanation:** Taking the nth root of both sides and isolating dv/dr.

### Step 6: Integrate Across the Pipe Radius

Integrate from r (where velocity is v) to R (where velocity is 0, the no-slip condition):

$$v(r) = \int_r^R \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} r'^{1/n} dr'$$

**Explanation:** We flip limits (negative sign) and integrate.

### Step 7: Perform the Integration

Using the integral $\int r^{1/n} dr = \dfrac{r^{1/n + 1}}{1/n + 1} = \dfrac{n}{n+1} r^{(n+1)/n}$:

$$v(r) = \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} \left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right)$$

### Step 8: Express the Final Velocity Profile

$$\boxed{v(r) = \frac{n}{n+1} \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n} \left(R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n}\right)}$$

**Verification (Newtonian limit n = 1, K = μ):**

$$v(r) = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2 L \mu}\right) (R^2 - r^2) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

This reduces to the classical **Hagen-Poiseuille parabolic profile** for Newtonian flow. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This self-consistency check (reducing to the Newtonian limit when n = 1) is the **key mark-winning step** in KTU evaluations. Examiners specifically look for this verification.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Apparent Viscosity Calculation (Typical KTU 3-Mark Problem)

**Problem:**
A non-Newtonian fluid follows the power-law model with consistency index **K = 2.5 Pa·sⁿ** and flow behaviour index **n = 0.4**. Calculate the apparent viscosity at a shear rate of **500 s⁻¹**.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Use the derived apparent viscosity formula.

$$\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

Step 2: Substitute the values.

$$\mu_a = 2.5 \times (500)^{0.4 - 1} = 2.5 \times (500)^{-0.6}$$

Step 3: Compute the exponent.

$$(500)^{0.4} \approx 11.77 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (500)^{-0.6} = \frac{1}{(500)^{0.6}}$$

Step 4: Numerical evaluation.

$$(500)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \times \ln 500} = e^{0.6 \times 6.2146} = e^{3.7288} \approx 41.66$$

Step 5: Final value.

$$\mu_a = \frac{2.5}{41.66} \approx 0.0600 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\mu_a \approx 0.060 \text{ Pa·s} = 60 \text{ cP}}$$

**Interpretation:** At this high shear rate (500 s⁻¹), the pseudoplastic fluid (n < 1) shows a much lower apparent viscosity than its consistency index K — confirming shear-thinning behaviour.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Classification of Fluids

This block-level architecture diagram maps the **complete rheological classification tree** as required by the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["FLUID BEHAVIOUR"] --> node2["NEWTONIAN FLUID"]
    node1 --> node3["NON-NEWTONIAN FLUID"]

    node2 --> node2a["Viscosity constant"]
    node2 --> node2b["Examples: Water, Air, Glycerine"]
    node2 --> node2c["tau = mu times gamma dot"]

    node3 --> node4["Time Independent"]
    node3 --> node5["Time Dependent"]
    node3 --> node6["Viscoelastic"]

    node4 --> node4a["Pseudoplastic"]
    node4 --> node4b["Dilatant"]
    node4 --> node4c["Bingham Plastic"]
    node4 --> node4d["Herschel Bulkley"]

    node4a --> node4a1["n less than 1, no yield stress"]
    node4a --> node4a2["Examples: Paint, Blood, Ketchup"]

    node4b --> node4b1["n greater than 1, no yield stress"]
    node4b --> node4b2["Examples: Starch Slurry, Wet Sand"]

    node4c --> node4c1["Has yield stress tau zero"]
    node4c --> node4c2["Examples: Toothpaste, Drilling Mud"]

    node4d --> node4d1["Has yield stress and power law flow"]
    node4d --> node4d2["Examples: Chocolate, Coal Slurry"]

    node5 --> node5a["Thixotropic"]
    node5 --> node5b["Rheopectic"]

    node5a --> node5a1["Viscosity decreases with time"]
    node5a --> node5a2["Examples: Yogurt, Printing Ink"]

    node5b --> node5b1["Viscosity increases with time"]
    node5b --> node5b2["Examples: Gypsum Suspension"]

    node6 --> node6a["Viscous plus Elastic Properties"]
    node6 --> node6b["Examples: Polymer Melts, Dough, Bitumen"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Instrumentation Flow for Rheological Measurement

This diagram illustrates how **industrial instrumentation systems** detect and quantify Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian behaviour in a process plant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Process Pipe / Tank"] --> nodeB["Flow Stream"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Rheology Classification Sensor"}
    nodeC -->|Newtonian| nodeD["Standard DP Flow Meter"]
    nodeC -->|Non-Newtonian| nodeE["Coriolis or Positive Displacement Meter"]
    nodeC -->|Online Rheometer| nodeF["Viscosity and K, n values"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["PLC and SCADA"]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Control Valve and Pump Adjustment"]
    nodeH --> nodeA
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Plot Concept: Shear Stress vs Shear Rate Behaviour (Qualitative Mapping)

This block diagram is a textual schematic of how each fluid type appears on a τ vs. γ̇ plot. (Quantitative curves are presented in the explanation text.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axis0["Origin: 0,0"] --> axis1["Shear Rate gamma dot axis"]
    axis0 --> axis2["Shear Stress tau axis"]
    axis1 --> p1["Newtonian: Straight Line through origin"]
    axis1 --> p2["Pseudoplastic: Curve concave down"]
    axis1 --> p3["Dilatant: Curve concave up"]
    axis1 --> p4["Bingham: Straight Line starting from tau zero on y axis"]
    axis1 --> p5["Herschel Bulkley: Curved Line starting from tau zero on y axis"]
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Mermaid Diagram: Power-Law Index n and Fluid Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nVal["Flow Behaviour Index n"] --> nLow["n less than 1"]
    nVal --> nOne["n equals 1"]
    nVal --> nHigh["n greater than 1"]
    nLow --> labelA["PSEUDOPLASTIC SHEAR THINNING"]
    nOne --> labelB["NEWTONIAN mu constant"]
    nHigh --> labelC["DILATANT SHEAR THICKENING"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip for KTU Viva / Practical Exam:** If asked to draw a shear stress vs. shear rate plot, always:
> 1. Label both axes with units.
> 2. Mark the origin (0,0) clearly.
> 3. For Bingham and Herschel-Bulkley, mark the **yield stress τ₀ on the y-axis** (not the origin).
> 4. Use a **ruler** for Newtonian and Bingham; use a **smooth curve** for the others.

---
