# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Newtonian fluid. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Newtonian fluid** is one in which the **shear stress is directly proportional to the rate of shear (velocity gradient)** and the proportionality constant — the **dynamic viscosity (μ)** — remains **constant** at a given temperature and pressure. The governing equation is **τ = μ (dv/dy)**.
>
> **Two Examples:**
> 1. **Water** (μ ≈ 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s at 20°C)
> 2. **Air** (μ ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s at 20°C)
>
> Other valid examples: glycerine, ethyl alcohol, mercury, kerosene.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between pseudoplastic and dilatant fluids.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning) | Dilatant (Shear-Thickening) |
|---------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Power-law index n** | n < 1 | n > 1 |
| **Viscosity behaviour** | Decreases as shear rate increases | Increases as shear rate increases |
| **τ vs. γ̇ curve** | Concave-down (curves towards γ̇ axis) | Concave-up (curves towards τ axis) |
| **Examples** | Paint, blood, ketchup, polymer solutions | Starch suspension, wet sand, sugar in water |
| **Industrial utility** | Smooth brush/spread application | Shock absorption, bulletproof vests (cornstarch + water) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **apparent viscosity of a power-law fluid**. Explain how the flow behaviour index *n* classifies the fluid as Newtonian, pseudoplastic, or dilatant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pseudoplastic fluid flowing in a pipe follows the power-law model with **K = 3.0 Pa·sⁿ** and **n = 0.6**. The flow is in a smooth circular pipe of diameter **D = 50 mm**. The mean velocity is **v = 1.2 m/s**. Calculate: **(i)** the apparent viscosity at the wall, and **(ii)** the generalized Reynolds number (use the Metzner-Reed formula). Assume fluid density **ρ = 1100 kg/m³**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Start with the power-law constitutive equation.

$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$$

**Step 2:** Apparent viscosity is defined as:

$$\mu_a = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and simplify:

$$\mu_a = \frac{K \dot{\gamma}^n}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

**Step 4 — Classification:**

- If **n = 1** → $\mu_a = K$ = constant → **Newtonian fluid**.
- If **n < 1** → exponent (n − 1) is negative → $\mu_a$ decreases as $\dot{\gamma}$ rises → **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**.
- If **n > 1** → exponent (n − 1) is positive → $\mu_a$ increases as $\dot{\gamma}$ rises → **Dilatant (shear-thickening)**.

$$\boxed{\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- K = 3.0 Pa·sⁿ
- n = 0.6
- D = 50 mm = 0.05 m
- v = 1.2 m/s
- ρ = 1100 kg/m³

**Step 1: Wall shear rate for a power-law fluid in a pipe:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right) \cdot \frac{8v}{D}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \left(\frac{3(0.6)+1}{4(0.6)}\right) \cdot \frac{8 \times 1.2}{0.05}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \left(\frac{2.8}{2.4}\right) \cdot \frac{9.6}{0.05} = (1.1667)(192) \approx 224 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Apparent viscosity at the wall:**

$$\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}_w^{n-1} = 3.0 \times (224)^{0.6 - 1} = 3.0 \times (224)^{-0.4}$$

$$(224)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \times \ln 224} = e^{0.4 \times 5.4116} = e^{2.1646} \approx 8.71$$

$$\mu_a = \frac{3.0}{8.71} \approx 0.3444 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 3: Generalized Reynolds Number (Metzner-Reed):**

$$Re_g = \frac{D^n v^{2-n} \rho}{8^{n-1} K \left(\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n}$$

Compute numerator:

$$D^n v^{2-n} \rho = (0.05)^{0.6} \times (1.2)^{1.4} \times 1100$$

$$(0.05)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \times \ln 0.05} = e^{0.6 \times (-2.9957)} = e^{-1.7974} \approx 0.1657$$

$$(1.2)^{1.4} = e^{1.4 \times \ln 1.2} = e^{1.4 \times 0.1823} = e^{0.2552} \approx 1.2907$$

$$D^n v^{2-n} \rho = 0.1657 \times 1.2907 \times 1100 \approx 235.2$$

Compute denominator:

$$8^{n-1} = 8^{-0.4} = \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} = \frac{1}{e^{0.4 \times 2.0794}} = \frac{1}{e^{0.8318}} = \frac{1}{2.2974} \approx 0.4353$$

$$\left(\frac{3n+1}{4n}\right)^n = \left(\frac{2.8}{2.4}\right)^{0.6} = (1.1667)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \times \ln 1.1667} = e^{0.6 \times 0.1542} = e^{0.0925} \approx 1.0969$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.4353 \times 3.0 \times 1.0969 \approx 1.4325$$

**Step 4: Final $Re_g$:**

$$Re_g = \frac{235.2}{1.4325} \approx 164.2$$

$$\boxed{\mu_a \approx 0.344 \text{ Pa·s}, \quad Re_g \approx 164}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $Re_g \approx 164 \ll 2100$ (typical laminar limit for non-Newtonian fluids), the flow is **laminar**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Sample, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **shear stress vs. shear rate behaviour** for Newtonian, pseudoplastic, dilatant, and Bingham plastic fluids. Give one industrial example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Bingham plastic fluid** has a yield stress **τ₀ = 12 Pa** and a plastic viscosity **μₚ = 0.25 Pa·s**. It flows in a pipe of diameter **D = 100 mm** with mean velocity **v = 0.8 m/s** and density **ρ = 1050 kg/m³**. Compute: **(i)** the wall shear stress, and **(ii)** the Reynolds number (use Buckingham-Reiner equation: $Re = \dfrac{D v \rho}{\mu_p}$ for Bingham plastics). Comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Behaviour Plot (described; sketch expected in exam):**

| Curve | Plot Characteristic | Example |
|-------|---------------------|---------|
| **Newtonian** | Straight line passing through origin; slope = μ | Water, Air |
| **Pseudoplastic** | Concave-down curve through origin; slope decreases with γ̇ | Tomato ketchup, blood |
| **Dilatant** | Concave-up curve through origin; slope increases with γ̇ | Starch suspension, wet sand |
| **Bingham Plastic** | Straight line **NOT** through origin; starts at τ₀ on y-axis | Toothpaste, drilling mud |

The diagram should be drawn with:
- **x-axis:** Shear rate γ̇ (s⁻¹)
- **y-axis:** Shear stress τ (Pa)
- All four curves labelled clearly
- Yield stress τ₀ marked on y-axis for the Bingham plastic

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- τ₀ = 12 Pa
- μₚ = 0.25 Pa·s
- D = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- v = 0.8 m/s
- ρ = 1050 kg/m³

**Step 1: Wall shear stress in a pipe (Hagen-Poiseuille form):**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4 L} = \frac{D \cdot f \cdot \rho v^2}{2}$$

For a Bingham plastic in laminar flow, $\tau_w$ is related to $\tau_0$ via:

$$\tau_w = \frac{D \Delta P}{4 L}$$

We can use the **Buckingham equation** to find τw given the velocity profile. However, a simpler KTU approach is the direct computation via the standard wall shear stress formula assuming laminar flow equilibrium:

Using mean velocity in laminar pipe flow (generalized):

$$\tau_w = \mu_p \left(\frac{8v}{D}\right) + \tau_0 \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{\tau_0}{\tau_w}\right)^{...}\right]$$

A simpler exam-friendly approach: **Compute Reynolds number first**, then evaluate.

**Step 2: Reynolds Number (Buckingham-Reiner form, simplified):**

$$Re = \frac{D v \rho}{\mu_p} = \frac{0.1 \times 0.8 \times 1050}{0.25} = \frac{84}{0.25} = 336$$

**Step 3: Critical Reynolds number for Bingham plastic:**

For Bingham plastics, the critical Reynolds number (transition to turbulent flow) is given by the **Hedström number (He)**:

$$He = \frac{D^2 \rho \tau_0}{\mu_p^2} = \frac{(0.1)^2 \times 1050 \times 12}{(0.25)^2}$$

$$He = \frac{0.01 \times 1050 \times 12}{0.0625} = \frac{126}{0.0625} = 2016$$

The critical Reynolds number from the **Wilson-Thomas** chart for Bingham plastics is typically $Re_{cr} \approx 2100$ for low He. Since $Re = 336 \ll 2100$, the flow is **laminar**.

**Step 4: Wall shear stress (using laminar pressure drop relation for Bingham):**

For Bingham plastic in laminar flow, the average velocity is given by:

$$v = \frac{D^2}{32 \mu_p} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} \left[1 - \frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{\tau_0}{\tau_w}\right) + \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{\tau_0}{\tau_w}\right)^4\right]$$

where $\tau_w = \dfrac{D \Delta P}{4L}$.

Solving iteratively (using the simplified KTU convention where $\tau_w \approx \dfrac{8 v \mu_p}{D} + \tau_0$ in the limit of small yield ratio):

$$\tau_w \approx \tau_0 + \frac{8 \mu_p v}{D} = 12 + \frac{8 \times 0.25 \times 0.8}{0.1} = 12 + 16 = 28 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_w \approx 28 \text{ Pa}, \quad Re = 336 \text{ (laminar)}}$$

**Comment:** The flow is in the **laminar regime**. Special care is required in pipe sizing because Bingham plastics exhibit a **plug flow region** in the centre (where shear stress < τ₀ and the fluid moves as a solid plug).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units of K:** K is in **Pa·sⁿ**, NOT Pa·s. Forgetting the exponent leads to dimensional errors and **zero credit** in the unit step.
> 2. **Confusing apparent viscosity with dynamic viscosity:** Apparent viscosity is **shear-rate dependent**; never write "μ = 0.5 Pa·s" for a non-Newtonian fluid without specifying the shear rate.
> 3. **Mixing up pseudoplastic and dilatant curves:** Pseudoplastic = **concave-down** (drops quickly then flattens). Dilatant = **concave-up** (rises slowly then steepens). Examiners check the curvature direction carefully.
> 4. **Not verifying the Newtonian limit:** When asked to derive a non-Newtonian formula, always substitute **n = 1** to show it reduces to the Newtonian form. This is a **2-mark bonus** in most KTU marking schemes.
> 5. **Forgetting the yield stress on Bingham plots:** The Bingham plastic line must start from **τ₀ on the y-axis**, NOT from the origin. This is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Using the wrong Reynolds number formula:** The standard Newtonian $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ **does not apply** to non-Newtonian fluids. Always use the **generalized Reynolds number** with K and n.
> 7. **Mixing thixotropy and rheopexy:** Thixotropy = **viscosity decreases with time** (yogurt). Rheopexy = **viscosity increases with time** (gypsum). Mnemonic: "Thixo-**t**ime decay" vs "Rheo-**r**ise".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3:**
>
> - ✅ **Newtonian definition:** τ = μ γ̇, μ constant, plot = straight line through origin.
> - ✅ **Water at 20°C:** μ = 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s; ν = 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s.
> - ✅ **Newton's Law of Viscosity** is the **defining equation** for Newtonian fluids.
> - ✅ **Non-Newtonian** = μ depends on γ̇, time, or stress history.
> - ✅ **Power-law model:** τ = K γ̇ⁿ — K is consistency index, n is flow behaviour index.
> - ✅ **n = 1** → Newtonian; **n < 1** → Pseudoplastic; **n > 1** → Dilatant.
> - ✅ **Apparent viscosity:** $\mu_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ — derived, memorize.
> - ✅ **Bingham Plastic:** τ = τ₀ + μₚ γ̇ — has **yield stress τ₀**; example: toothpaste.
> - ✅ **Herschel-Bulkley:** τ = τ₀ + K γ̇ⁿ — yield stress **plus** power-law flow; example: chocolate.
> - ✅ **Thixotropic** = viscosity **decreases with time** at constant shear.
> - ✅ **Rheopectic** = viscosity **increases with time** at constant shear.
> - ✅ **Viscoelastic** = viscous + elastic behaviour (polymer melts, bitumen, dough).
> - ✅ **Generalized Reynolds Number** (Metzner-Reed) **must** be used for non-Newtonian flow regime determination.
> - ✅ **Laminar velocity profile for power-law fluid:** Must reduce to **Hagen-Poiseuille** when n = 1 (verification step).
> - ✅ **Industrial significance:** Rheology dictates **flow meter selection** (DP meters only for Newtonian; Coriolis/PD for non-Newtonian).
> - ✅ **Shear stress in a pipe varies linearly with r:** $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$ — applies to **all** fluid types.
> - ✅ **Wall shear stress for pipe flow:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{D \Delta P}{4L}$.
> - ✅ **Hedström number He** governs Bingham plastic flow regime transitions.
> - ✅ For KTU exams, **always label axes, units, and the yield stress τ₀** on any rheological plot.
> - ✅ **Dimensional consistency check** is a must for all numerical problems (K in Pa·sⁿ, μ in Pa·s, γ̇ in s⁻¹).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Viscosity Using Laboratory Viscometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Viscosity Using Laboratory Viscometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Viscosity

**Viscosity (η)** is defined as the measure of a fluid's internal resistance to flow or deformation under applied shear stress. Mathematically, it is the proportionality constant in **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \eta \frac{dv}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m²)
- $\eta$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\frac{dv}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹), also called shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall):**
> - **Dynamic Viscosity (η): Pa·s** (SI), **poise (P)** or **centipoise (cP)** in CGS
> - Conversion: **1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP**
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (ν): m²/s** (SI), **stokes (St)** or **centistokes (cSt)** in CGS
> - Conversion: **1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt**
> - Relation: $\nu = \eta / \rho$ where $\rho$ is fluid density (kg/m³)

## 1.2 Types of Viscosity

| Type | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Dynamic Viscosity** | η | Resistance to shear stress |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | ν | Dynamic viscosity normalized by density |
| **Apparent Viscosity** | ηₐ | Viscosity of non-Newtonian fluids at a specific shear rate |
| **Relative Viscosity** | ηᵣ | Ratio of solution viscosity to solvent viscosity |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Honey vs. Water Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine pouring two liquids down a slope — **water** flows like a nervous sprint runner, molecules sliding past each other effortlessly. **Honey**, on the other hand, is a lazy stroller — its molecules cling to each other through internal friction. That "clinging force" is what we call viscosity. The **higher the internal friction, the higher the viscosity**. In lubrication oils, paints, and polymers, viscosity dictates how easily the material flows, spreads, or coats a surface — making its measurement critically important in process industries.

## 1.4 Why Measure Viscosity?

- **Quality control** in petroleum, food, paint, pharmaceutical, and polymer industries
- Ensures correct **lubrication** in machinery (oil grading: SAE 10W-30, etc.)
- Monitors **fermentation and chemical reactions**
- Controls **fuel injection** and **coating thickness**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of this module is on **laboratory viscometers** — instruments designed for high-precision, controlled-environment measurement of fluid viscosity, as opposed to industrial inline viscometers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Laboratory Viscometers

Laboratory viscometers are classified based on the **physical principle** used to measure flow resistance:

### A) Capillary Tube Viscometers
Operate on **Poiseuille's Law**. A fixed volume of fluid flows through a narrow tube under gravity or applied pressure; the time of flow is proportional to viscosity.

1. **Ostwald Viscometer** – U-tube with a capillary, two bulbs (upper and lower). Used for low-viscosity, transparent Newtonian fluids.
2. **Ubbelohde Viscometer** – A "suspended level" design where the fluid volume below the capillary hangs freely. The **kinetic energy correction** and **head variation** are eliminated, making it highly accurate.
3. **Cannon-Fenske Viscometer** – Reverse-flow design, used for **opaque liquids** (oils, dark petroleum fractions).

### B) Falling Ball / Falling Body Viscometers
Based on **Stokes' Law**. A ball falls through the fluid; the terminal velocity is inversely proportional to viscosity.

### C) Rotational Viscometers
A spindle rotates in the fluid; the **torque** required to maintain a constant rotational speed is proportional to viscosity.
- **Brookfield Viscometer** – The industry standard; uses a rotating spindle (1–7) immersed in fluid.
- **Cone-and-Plate Viscometer** – Ideal for non-Newtonian fluids and very small sample volumes.

### D) Efflux (Empirical) Viscometers
Measure time for a fixed volume to flow through a standard orifice. Viscosity is reported in **arbitrary time units** (e.g., Saybolt Seconds, Redwood Seconds).
- **Saybolt Viscometer** (US standard)
- **Redwood Viscometer** (British standard)
- **Engler Viscometer** (European/German standard)

## 2.2 Operational Principles — Step by Step

### 2.2.1 Capillary Viscometer (Ostwald Type)
**Step 1:** A fixed volume $V$ of test liquid is pipetted into the lower reservoir bulb.
**Step 2:** The liquid is drawn up into the upper bulb by suction until it passes the upper timing mark.
**Step 3:** The liquid is allowed to flow back down by gravity through the calibrated capillary.
**Step 4:** The time $t$ for the meniscus to fall between the two timing marks is recorded.
**Step 5:** Viscosity is computed by comparing with a reference fluid of known viscosity (typically water).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it works:** Under laminar flow, the pressure drop across the capillary is balanced by viscous shear at the walls. Longer flow times = higher viscosity.

### 2.2.2 Falling Ball Viscometer (Höppler)
**Step 1:** A precision ball of known radius $r$ and density $\rho_s$ is introduced.
**Step 2:** The tube is inclined (typically 10° from vertical) and the ball falls through the fluid under gravity.
**Step 3:** The time $t$ for the ball to fall a calibrated distance $L$ is measured.
**Step 4:** Terminal velocity $v = L/t$ is used in Stokes' equation to compute $\eta$.

### 2.2.3 Brookfield Rotational Viscometer
**Step 1:** A spindle is immersed in the sample held in a temperature-controlled cup.
**Step 2:** The spindle rotates at a set angular velocity $\omega$ (rpm).
**Step 3:** The viscous drag creates a torque $M$ on the spindle, measured by a spring deflection sensor.
**Step 4:** Viscosity is computed from: $\eta = \frac{M}{k \cdot \omega}$, where $k$ is a spindle-geometry constant.

### 2.2.4 Saybolt Universal Viscometer
**Step 1:** A 60 mL sample is heated to a specified temperature (e.g., 100°F, 210°F).
**Step 2:** The sample is allowed to drain through a calibrated orifice.
**Step 3:** The time in seconds to collect 60 mL is recorded — this is the **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Viscometer Type | Governing Equation | Variables | Notes |
|-----------------|--------------------|-----------|-------|
| **Capillary (Poiseuille)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi r^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$ | $r$ = capillary radius, $L$ = length, $V$ = volume, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop, $t$ = flow time | For absolute measurement |
| **Ostwald (Relative)** | $\dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} = \dfrac{\rho_1 \, t_1}{\rho_2 \, t_2}$ | Subscripts 1, 2 = test fluid, reference (water) | Most common lab form |
| **Falling Ball (Stokes)** | $\eta = \dfrac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) t}{9 L}$ | $\rho_s, \rho_f$ = ball and fluid densities; $g$ = gravity; $r$ = ball radius | For low-viscosity fluids |
| **Brookfield Rotational** | $\eta = \dfrac{M}{k \, \omega}$ | $M$ = torque, $k$ = spindle constant, $\omega$ = angular velocity | k in cP/rpm |
| **Saybolt Universal** | $\mu = 0.226 t - \dfrac{195}{t}$ (cP), valid for $t > 100$ s | $t$ = Saybolt seconds | Empirical |
| **Redwood No. 1** | $\mu = 0.0026 t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ (cP) | $t$ = Redwood seconds | Empirical |
| **Engler** | $\mu = 0.00147 t - \dfrac{3.74}{t}$ (cP), $t$ in Engler degrees | $t$ = Engler time | Empirical |
| **Newton's Law** | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$ = shear stress, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate | Fundamental |
| **Kinematic Relation** | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | $\rho$ = density | SI: m²/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL EXAM PITFALL:** Never confuse Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS) with Saybolt Furol Seconds (SFS). SFS uses a larger orifice and is used for **heavier oils** (fuel oils, road oils). They are NOT interchangeable.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refineries:** Saybolt viscometers grade lubricating oils (e.g., ISO VG 32, VG 46).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Brookfield viscometers ensure IV fluids and syrups have correct flow characteristics.
- **Food Industry:** Falling ball and rotational viscometers control chocolate, ketchup, and dairy consistency.
- **Polymer Manufacturing:** Cone-and-plate viscometers characterize shear-thinning behavior of polymer melts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Poiseuille's Equation for a Capillary Viscometer

We start with the Navier-Stokes equation for steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal cylindrical tube of radius $R$ and length $L$.

### Step 1 — Setup of the Flow
Consider a fluid element at radial position $r$ inside the tube. The flow is axisymmetric (no variation in $\theta$) and fully developed (no variation in $x$ after entry length). The only non-zero velocity component is $v_x(r)$.

### Step 2 — Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element
Take a cylindrical fluid slug of radius $r$, length $dx$. Two forces act axially:
- Pressure force on the left face: $P \cdot \pi r^2$ (pushing in $+x$ direction)
- Pressure force on the right face: $-(P + dP) \cdot \pi r^2$ (pushing in $-x$ direction)
- Viscous shear on the lateral surface: $\tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dx$

For steady flow, net force = 0:

$$
(P)(πr^2) - (P + dP)(πr^2) + \tau (2πr)(dx) = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the Force Balance
Dividing by $\pi r \, dx$:

$$
-dP \cdot r + 2\tau = 0
$$

Solving for shear stress:

$$
\tau = \frac{r}{2} \left( -\frac{dP}{dx} \right)
$$

Since pressure drop is linear: $-\frac{dP}{dx} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}$, we get:

$$
\tau = \frac{r \, \Delta P}{2 L}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity
Newton's law states $\tau = \eta \dfrac{dv_x}{dr}$. Wait — sign convention requires $\tau = -\eta \dfrac{dv_x}{dr}$ since velocity decreases with increasing $r$. Therefore:

$$
-\eta \frac{dv_x}{dr} = \frac{r \, \Delta P}{2 L}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
dv_x = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \eta L} r \, dr
$$

### Step 5 — Integrate Across the Tube Radius
Boundary condition: at $r = R$ (tube wall), $v_x = 0$ (no-slip condition). Integrating from $r = r$ to $r = R$:

$$
\int_{v_x}^{0} dv_x = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \eta L} \int_{r}^{R} r \, dr
$$

Performing integration:

$$
-v_x = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \eta L} \cdot \frac{R^2 - r^2}{2}
$$

$$
v_x(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} (R^2 - r^2)
$$

This is the **parabolic (Poiseuille) velocity profile** — maximum at center, zero at walls.

### Step 6 — Compute Volumetric Flow Rate via Ring Integration
Consider an annular ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$. Area = $2\pi r \, dr$. Volumetric flow rate:

$$
Q = \int_0^R v_x(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4 \eta L} (R^2 - r^2) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \eta L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr
$$

### Step 7 — Evaluate the Integral
Performing the integration term by term:

$$
\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr = \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{R^4}{4}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \eta L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}
$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation** — the foundation of all capillary viscometry.

### Step 8 — Express Viscosity for Capillary Viscometer
If a volume $V$ flows in time $t$, then $Q = V/t$, so:

$$
\frac{V}{t} = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}
$$

Solving for viscosity:

$$
\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on corrections:** The Hagenbach correction for kinetic energy must be added for high-precision work:
> $$\eta_{\text{true}} = \eta_{\text{meas}} - \frac{\rho V}{8 \pi L t}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Falling Ball Viscometer (Stokes' Law Application)

### Step 1 — Forces on the Falling Ball
Three forces act on a sphere falling through a viscous fluid:
- Weight (downward): $F_g = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s g$
- Buoyancy (upward): $F_b = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g$
- Viscous drag (upward, opposing motion): $F_d = 6 \pi \eta r v$ (Stokes' drag)

### Step 2 — Terminal Velocity Condition
At terminal velocity, acceleration = 0, so net force = 0:

$$
F_g - F_b - F_d = 0
$$

$$
\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s g - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g - 6\pi \eta r v = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify
Factor out common terms:

$$
\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) = 6 \pi \eta r v
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Viscosity
Dividing both sides by $6\pi r$:

$$
\eta = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v}
$$

Substituting $v = L/t$:

$$
\boxed{\eta = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) t}{9 L}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity:** Stokes' law requires **laminar flow** around the ball (low Reynolds number, $Re < 1$). Höppler viscometers use a long, inclined tube to ensure terminal velocity is reached quickly.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Viscometer

| Property / Sample Type | Recommended Viscometer | Reason |
|------------------------|------------------------|--------|
| Transparent, low-viscosity Newtonian fluid | Ostwald | Simple, fast, accurate |
| High-precision absolute measurement | Ubbelohde | Eliminates head and kinetic energy errors |
| Opaque petroleum oils | Cannon-Fenske (reverse flow) | Allows viewing of meniscus at top |
| Viscous oils, gels | Falling Ball (Höppler) | Wide range, no evaporation issue |
| Non-Newtonian fluids (paints, polymers) | Brookfield / Cone-and-Plate | Shear rate can be varied |
| Quality control of petroleum (field/lab) | Saybolt / Redwood / Engler | Industry-standard empirical units |
| Small sample volume (< 1 mL) | Cone-and-Plate | Minimal sample needed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capillary Viscometer (Ostwald) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sample Liquid Inlet] --> B[Lower Reservoir Bulb]
    B --> C[Capillary Tube of Length L and Radius R]
    C --> D[Upper Measuring Bulb]
    D --> E[Upper Timing Mark]
    D --> F[Lower Timing Mark]
    E --> G[Stopwatch Start Trigger]
    F --> H[Stopwatch Stop Trigger]
    H --> I[Time t Recorded]
    I --> J[Apply Poiseuille Equation]
    J --> K[Viscosity eta Output]
    L[Reference Liquid of Known Viscosity] --> M[Calibration Constant Lookup]
    M --> J
    N[Constant Temperature Bath] --> B
    N --> C
    N --> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree — Laboratory Viscometers

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Laboratory Viscometers] --> S[Capillary Tube Type]
    R --> T[Falling Body Type]
    R --> U[Rotational Type]
    R --> V[Efflux Empirical Type]
    S --> S1[Ostwald Viscometer]
    S --> S2[Ubbelohde Viscometer]
    S --> S3[Cannon Fenske Viscometer]
    T --> T1[Hoppler Falling Ball]
    T --> T2[Falling Piston Viscometer]
    U --> U1[Brookfield Spindle Type]
    U --> U2[Cone and Plate Viscometer]
    U --> U3[Coaxial Cylinder Viscometer]
    V --> V1[Saybolt Universal]
    V --> V2[Redwood No 1]
    V --> V3[Engler Viscometer]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Brookfield Rotational Viscometer Operation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Spindle Immersed in Sample Cup] --> B[Motor Rotates Spindle at Set RPM]
    B --> C[Fluid Exerts Viscous Drag Torque M]
    C --> D[Spring Deflection Sensor Measures M]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    E --> F[Microcontroller Computes eta]
    F --> G[Digital Display of Viscosity cP]
    H[Temperature Probe PT100] --> F
    I[Spindle Geometry Constant k] --> F
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Viscometer Selection Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Need to Measure Viscosity] --> Q1{Is Sample Transparent?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is High Accuracy Required?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Use Cannon Fenske Reverse Flow]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Use Ubbelohde]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5[Use Ostwald]
    Start --> Q6{Is Sample Non Newtonian?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7[Use Brookfield or Cone Plate]
    Start --> Q8{Need Industry Standard Empirical Units?}
    Q8 -->|Yes - Petroleum| Q9[Use Saybolt or Redwood]
    Start --> Q10{Is Sample Very Viscous?}
    Q10 -->|Yes| Q11[Use Falling Ball Hoppler]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define viscosity. Distinguish between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity.**

**Model Answer:**
Viscosity is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to deformation or flow under applied shear stress.
- **Dynamic Viscosity (η):** The ratio of shear stress to shear rate. Units: **Pa·s** or **poise**. It is a measure of the internal friction of the fluid.
- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density, i.e., $\nu = \eta / \rho$. Units: **m²/s** or **stokes**. It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under gravity.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**State Newton's Law of Viscosity. What is a Newtonian fluid?**

**Model Answer:**
Newton's Law of Viscosity states that the shear stress between adjacent fluid layers is proportional to the velocity gradient (shear rate) perpendicular to the flow direction:
$$\tau = \eta \frac{dv}{dy}$$
A **Newtonian fluid** is one in which the dynamic viscosity $\eta$ remains constant irrespective of the applied shear rate. Examples: water, air, most light oils. In contrast, **non-Newtonian fluids** (paints, blood, polymer solutions) have viscosity that varies with shear rate.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an **Ostwald viscometer**. Derive the equation used to determine the viscosity of an unknown liquid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard liquid of known viscosity 1.20 cP and density 0.998 g/cm³ takes 75 seconds to flow between the timing marks of an Ostwald viscometer at 30°C. An unknown liquid of density 1.05 g/cm³ takes 95 seconds under identical conditions. Calculate the viscosity of the unknown liquid in cP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction:** An Ostwald viscometer is a U-shaped glass tube with:
- A wide upper bulb (measuring bulb) with two etched timing marks $M_1$ (upper) and $M_2$ (lower)
- A calibrated capillary tube of length $L$ and radius $R$ connecting it to a lower reservoir bulb
- A side-arm tube for charging the sample

**Working:**
1. A known volume $V$ of test liquid is introduced into the lower bulb.
2. The liquid is sucked up the side arm into the upper bulb until the meniscus is above $M_1$.
3. The suction is released; the liquid flows down through the capillary under gravity.
4. The time $t$ taken for the meniscus to fall from $M_1$ to $M_2$ is recorded.
5. The process is repeated with a reference liquid (typically water) of known viscosity $\eta_w$ and density $\rho_w$, and time $t_w$ is recorded.

**Derivation:** Applying the Hagen-Poiseuille equation, for the same viscometer the constants $\pi, R, L, V, \Delta P$ are fixed. Since the pressure head is the same for both liquids:

$$\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$$

Taking the ratio for two liquids:

$$\frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} = \frac{\rho_1 t_1}{\rho_2 t_2}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
- Reference liquid: $\eta_2 = 1.20$ cP, $\rho_2 = 0.998$ g/cm³, $t_2 = 75$ s
- Unknown liquid: $\rho_1 = 1.05$ g/cm³, $t_1 = 95$ s

Using the relative viscosity formula:

$$\eta_1 = \eta_2 \cdot \frac{\rho_1 \cdot t_1}{\rho_2 \cdot t_2}$$

Substituting values:

$$\eta_1 = 1.20 \cdot \frac{1.05 \cdot 95}{0.998 \cdot 75}$$

Computing the numerator: $1.05 \times 95 = 99.75$
Computing the denominator: $0.998 \times 75 = 74.85$

$$\eta_1 = 1.20 \cdot \frac{99.75}{74.85} = 1.20 \cdot 1.3327$$

$$\boxed{\eta_1 \approx 1.60 \text{ cP}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the principle and working of a **Brookfield rotational viscometer** with a suitable diagram. State the equation used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the working equation of a **falling ball viscometer** based on Stokes' law. What are its limitations? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Principle:** A Brookfield viscometer measures the torque required to rotate a spindle at a constant speed while immersed in a fluid. The torque is directly proportional to the fluid's viscosity.

**Construction:**
- A motor that drives a spindle (1 to 7 types, varying in size) at selectable rotational speeds (0.3 to 100 rpm)
- A calibrated beryllium-copper spring that measures the torque
- A graduated dial or digital display
- A guarded leg to protect the spring from side loads
- A temperature probe (PT100) for compensation

**Working:**
1. The spindle is immersed in the test fluid held in a beaker.
2. The motor rotates the spindle at a fixed $\omega$ (rpm).
3. Viscous drag of the fluid exerts a torque $M$ on the spindle, deflecting the spring.
4. The deflection is read on the dial and converted using the spindle's calibration constant.
5. Viscosity is given by:

$$\eta = \frac{M}{k \, \omega}$$

where $k$ is the spindle constant (provided by manufacturer).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation (already shown in Section 3.2):** Starting with force balance on a falling sphere at terminal velocity $v$:

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g = 6 \pi \eta r v$$

Solving for viscosity:

$$\eta = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v} = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) t}{9 L}$$

**Limitations:**
1. Valid only for **laminar flow** ($Re < 1$); for higher Reynolds numbers, correction factors (Oseen's correction) must be applied.
2. Assumes the ball is **rigid, smooth, and spherical** with no slip at the wall.
3. Requires accurate knowledge of **ball density and fluid density**.
4. Cannot be used for **non-Newtonian fluids** with the simple Stokes formula.
5. **Wall effects** become significant if the ball is large relative to the tube diameter.
6. **Temperature control** is critical since viscosity is highly temperature-dependent.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas (Examiner's Pitfall List):**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing SI (Pa·s) and CGS (poise/cP) units in the same calculation. Always convert: 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s.
> 2. **Forgetting density in Ostwald formula:** Students often write $\eta_1/\eta_2 = t_1/t_2$. This is wrong — the density ratio must be included unless the fluids have equal density.
> 3. **Confusing Saybolt and Redwood equations:** Each has its own empirical constant. Don't mix them.
> 4. **Missing the kinematic energy correction** in capillary viscometer derivations.
> 5. **Not stating boundary conditions** (e.g., $v = 0$ at $r = R$ for no-slip) in derivations — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this.
> 6. **Brookfield equation:** Writing $\eta = M/k$ without including $\omega$ in the denominator.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Viscosity** is a fluid's resistance to flow; it determines how easily a fluid deforms under shear.
- **Dynamic viscosity (η)** has units of **Pa·s**; **Kinematic viscosity (ν)** has units of **m²/s**; $\nu = \eta / \rho$.
- The four major classes of laboratory viscometers are: **Capillary, Falling Ball, Rotational, and Efflux (Empirical)**.
- The **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8 \eta L)$ is the foundation of capillary viscometry.
- For the **Ostwald viscometer**, viscosity is determined by **relative comparison** with a reference fluid: $\eta_1/\eta_2 = (\rho_1 t_1)/(\rho_2 t_2)$.
- The **Falling Ball viscometer** uses **Stokes' law**: $\eta = 2r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) t / (9L)$.
- The **Brookfield rotational viscometer** measures **torque** at a fixed rotational speed: $\eta = M/(k \omega)$.
- **Saybolt, Redwood, and Engler viscometers** are empirical and use **standard orifice flow times**; each has its own conversion equation.
- **Laminar flow** is mandatory for valid measurements; Reynolds number must be low.
- **Temperature control** is critical — viscosity can change by 1–2% per °C for many oils.
- The **Hagenbach correction** must be added for high-precision capillary viscometer work.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Viscometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Viscometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Viscosity** is an intrinsic fluid property that quantifies a fluid's internal resistance to gradual deformation caused by shear stress or tensile stress. An **Industrial Viscometer** is a precision measurement instrument designed to quantify the viscosity of process fluids under controlled industrial conditions (temperature, pressure, and shear rate), enabling continuous monitoring of fluid quality in pipelines, reactors, and storage tanks.

Mathematically, for a **Newtonian fluid**, viscosity is defined by Newton's Law of Viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Industrial viscometers form a core part of Module 3 of OEICT833. Students must master (i) the working principle, (ii) the governing equation, and (iii) the industrial application of each viscometer type.

### 1.2 Standard Units & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI Unit of Dynamic Viscosity:** **Pascal-second (Pa·s)** = **1 N·s/m² = 1 kg/(m·s)**
> **CGS Unit:** **Poise (P)** where **1 P = 0.1 Pa·s** ; **1 cP = 1 mPa·s**
> **Kinematic Viscosity:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ (m²/s or Stokes); **1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s**
> **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
> **Water viscosity at 20°C:** **1.002 cP**

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pouring **honey** versus pouring **water** from identical containers. Honey flows sluggishly because its molecules cling to each other strongly — it has **high viscosity**. Water flows freely because its molecular bonds slip easily — it has **low viscosity**. A viscometer is essentially an "engineered faucet test" that quantifies this exact resistance to flow under precisely controlled conditions, replacing subjective observation with reproducible numerical output.

| Fluid Example | Approximate Viscosity (cP) | Industrial Context |
|---|---|---|
| Water (20 °C) | **1.0** | Process solvent baseline |
| Milk | **3.0** | Dairy quality control |
| Olive Oil | **84** | Food industry lubrication |
| Honey | **10,000** | Agitator design checks |
| Tar / Bitumen | **> 100,000** | Heavy petroleum refining |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Industrial Viscometers

Industrial viscometers are broadly classified into **five families** based on their measurement principle:

1. **Capillary Viscometers** — measure pressure drop across a known geometry.
2. **Falling Body Viscometers** — measure terminal velocity under gravity.
3. **Rotational Viscometers** — measure torque on a rotating element.
4. **Efflux (Orifice) Viscometers** — measure efflux time of a fixed volume (Saybolt, Redwood).
5. **Vibrational Viscometers** — measure damping of a vibrating probe immersed in fluid.

### 2.2 Newton's Law of Viscosity — Detailed Logic Steps

- **Step 1:** Consider two parallel plates separated by a fluid film of thickness $dy$.
- **Step 2:** The bottom plate is stationary; the top plate moves at velocity $u$.
- **Step 3:** The fluid adheres to both plates (no-slip boundary condition).
- **Step 4:** A linear velocity profile develops: $u(y) = u \cdot (y / h)$.
- **Step 5:** Force $F$ per unit area $A$ (shear stress $\tau$) is proportional to the velocity gradient.
- **Step 6:** The proportionality constant $\mu$ is the **dynamic viscosity**.

### 2.3 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids

- **Newtonian fluids:** Viscosity is constant regardless of applied shear rate (water, air, oils).
- **Non-Newtonian fluids:** Viscosity varies with shear rate (blood, paints, slurries, polymers).
- Industrial viscometers used for non-Newtonian fluids must specify the **shear rate** at which measurement is taken.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is essential for KTU 2024 ESE — memorize every entry.**

| Viscometer Type | Governing Equation | Solved Variable | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capillary (Hagen-Poiseuille) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^{4}}$ | $\mu = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 L Q}$ | Pa·s |
| Falling Sphere (Stokes) | $F_{drag} = 6 \pi \mu r v$ | $\mu = \dfrac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 v}$ | Pa·s |
| Rotational (Concentric Cylinder) | $T = \dfrac{4 \pi \mu L \omega}{\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}^{2}} - \frac{1}{r_{2}^{2}}\right)}$ | $\mu = \dfrac{T}{4 \pi \omega L}\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}^{2}} - \frac{1}{r_{2}^{2}}\right)$ | Pa·s |
| Saybolt Universal | $SUS \approx 0.226 \, t - \dfrac{195}{t}$ (for $t > 100$ s) | Converts $t$ to SUS | Seconds |
| Kinematic Relation | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ | m²/s ↔ Pa·s |
| Kinematic Energy Correction | $\nu = c \cdot t$ (where $c$ is calibrated constant) | Time-of-flow method | m²/s |

### 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Petroleum Refining:** Viscosity grading of crude oils, lubricants, and bunker fuels (ASTM D445).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Quality control of syrups, injectables, and ointments.
- **Food & Beverage:** Consistency of chocolate, ketchup, dairy products.
- **Polymer Manufacturing:** Reactor monitoring for melt-flow index (MFI) of plastics.
- **Paint Industry:** Sprayability and brushability specifications.
- **Power Plants:** Monitoring fuel-oil viscosity for burner atomization efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation for Capillary Viscometer

Consider a fluid flowing through a cylindrical capillary of radius $r$ and length $L$ under a steady pressure difference $\Delta P$.

**Step 1:** Apply force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $y$:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi y^{2} = -\tau \cdot 2 \pi y L$$

**Step 2:** Solve for shear stress $\tau$ at radius $y$:

$$\tau = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot y}{2 L}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Newton's law of viscosity $\tau = \mu \, \dfrac{du}{dy}$:

$$\mu \frac{du}{dy} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot y}{2 L}$$

**Step 4:** Separate variables and integrate from $y = 0$ (centerline, max velocity) to $y = r$ (wall, zero velocity by no-slip):

$$\int_{u_{max}}^{0} du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{r} y \, dy$$

**Step 5:** Perform the integration:

$$0 - u_{max} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2}$$

$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \, r^{2}}{4 \mu L}$$

**Step 6:** The velocity profile is parabolic:

$$u(y) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (r^{2} - y^{2})$$

**Step 7:** Compute total volumetric flow rate by integrating over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{r} u(y) \cdot 2\pi y \, dy = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{r} (r^{2} y - y^{3}) \, dy$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{r^{2} y^{2}}{2} - \frac{y^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{r} = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, r^{4}}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 8 (Final Governing Equation):**

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, r^{4}}{8 L Q}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Falling Sphere Viscometer (Stokes' Law)

**Step 1:** A sphere of radius $r$ and density $\rho_{s}$ falls through a fluid of density $\rho_{f}$. Three forces act: weight (downward), buoyancy (upward), and drag (upward).

**Step 2:** At terminal velocity $v_{t}$, acceleration = 0, so net force = 0:

$$W - F_{B} - F_{D} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Express each force:

$$W = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \rho_{s} g, \quad F_{B} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \rho_{f} g, \quad F_{D} = 6 \pi \mu r v_{t}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and simplify:

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f}) g = 6 \pi \mu r v_{t}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\mu$:

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 v_{t}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity:** This derivation assumes laminar flow with $Re < 1$ (creeping flow regime). For industrial high-viscosity fluids, the falling-ball method is preferred for its simplicity.

### 3.3 Derivation: Concentric Cylinder (Rotational) Viscometer

**Step 1:** Inner cylinder of radius $r_{1}$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$; outer cylinder of radius $r_{2}$ is stationary. Fluid fills the annular gap of height $L$.

**Step 2:** Torque transmitted through the fluid to the stationary outer cylinder:

$$T = \tau \cdot A \cdot r_{1} = (\mu r_{1} \omega) \cdot (2 \pi r_{1} L) \cdot \frac{1}{r_{2}/r_{1}}$$

**Step 3:** For a thin gap approximation, the velocity gradient is linear:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{\omega r_{1}}{r_{2} - r_{1}}$$

**Step 4:** Shear stress at the inner cylinder surface:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{\omega r_{1}}{r_{2} - r_{1}}$$

**Step 5:** Torque balance:

$$T = \tau \cdot 2 \pi r_{1}^{2} L = \frac{2 \pi \mu \omega r_{1}^{3} L}{r_{2} - r_{1}}$$

**Step 6 (Final):**

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{T (r_{2} - r_{1})}{2 \pi \omega r_{1}^{3} L}}$$

For the **generalized (exact)** case without the thin-gap approximation:

$$\mu = \frac{T}{4 \pi \omega L} \left( \frac{1}{r_{1}^{2}} - \frac{1}{r_{2}^{2}} \right)$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A capillary viscometer has radius $r = 1.5$ mm, length $L = 200$ mm. The pressure drop is $\Delta P = 800$ Pa, and the volumetric flow rate $Q = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s. Determine the dynamic viscosity of the fluid.

**Solution:**

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, r^{4}}{8 L Q}$$

$$r^{4} = (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{4} = 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{4}$$

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \cdot 800 \cdot 5.0625 \times 10^{-12}}{8 \cdot 0.200 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\mu = \frac{1.2724 \times 10^{-8}}{4.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.0318 \text{ Pa·s} = 31.8 \text{ cP}$$

This corresponds to a medium-viscosity industrial oil.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units and the relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**
Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) is the measure of a fluid's internal resistance to shear deformation, defined by Newton's law $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$. Its SI unit is the **Pascal-second (Pa·s)**. Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$) is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density, representing the fluid's resistance to flow under gravity. Its SI unit is **m²/s**. The relationship is:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid density in kg/m³. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List any four types of industrial viscometers and state one industrial application of each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Capillary viscometer** — Used in petroleum refineries for crude-oil grading.
2. **Falling sphere viscometer** — Used in glass-industry molten-glass viscosity measurement.
3. **Rotational viscometer (Brookfield)** — Used in paint and coating quality control.
4. **Saybolt viscometer** — Used in petroleum industry as per ASTM D88 standard.

(½ mark each = 2 marks for listing, 1 mark for applications) **(3 marks total)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently lose marks by (a) forgetting to convert **mm to m** in radius terms of capillary equations, (b) confusing **dynamic vs. kinematic** viscosity, and (c) omitting the **temperature compensation** statement. Always show units in every step and mention the governing standard (e.g., ASTM D445).

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for a capillary-tube viscometer and obtain the expression for dynamic viscosity in terms of measurable quantities. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**
The full derivation is provided in Section 3.1 of these notes. The student must write:

1. Assumptions (2 marks): laminar steady flow, incompressible Newtonian fluid, no-slip at wall, fully developed parabolic profile.
2. Force balance on cylindrical element (2 marks): $\Delta P \cdot \pi y^{2} = \tau \cdot 2\pi y L$.
3. Substitution and integration yielding $u(y) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(r^{2} - y^{2})$ (1 mark).
4. Final boxed expression:

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, r^{4}}{8 L Q}$$

(2 marks)

---

**(b)** A capillary viscometer of length 250 mm and internal diameter 2 mm is used to measure the viscosity of an oil sample. The pressure drop across the capillary is 1.2 kPa and the flow rate is $3 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s. Calculate the dynamic and kinematic viscosity if the oil density is 880 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

Given: $L = 0.250$ m, $d = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, so $r = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\Delta P = 1200$ Pa, $Q = 3 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s, $\rho = 880$ kg/m³.

**Step 1:** Compute $r^{4}$:

$$r^{4} = (10^{-3})^{4} = 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hagen-Poiseuille:

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \cdot 1200 \cdot 10^{-12}}{8 \cdot 0.250 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-7}}$$

**Step 3:** Numerator: $\pi \cdot 1200 \cdot 10^{-12} = 3.7699 \times 10^{-9}$

**Step 4:** Denominator: $8 \cdot 0.250 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-7} = 6.0 \times 10^{-7}$

**Step 5:** Divide:

$$\mu = \frac{3.7699 \times 10^{-9}}{6.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.283 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} = 6.283 \text{ cP}$$

**Step 6:** Kinematic viscosity:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{6.283 \times 10^{-3}}{880} = 7.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}\text{/s} = 0.0714 \text{ St}$$

**Final Answer:** $\mu = 6.283 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\nu = 7.14 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a rotational concentric-cylinder viscometer with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for dynamic viscosity in terms of torque and angular velocity. **(7 marks)**

**Solution Outline (7 marks):**

- **Working principle (3 marks):** An inner cylinder (spindle/bob) rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega$ inside a stationary outer cylinder (cup) filled with fluid. The viscous drag transmits a torque $T$ to the inner cylinder (or the cup, depending on design), measured by a torque transducer. The torque is proportional to viscosity.

- **Assumptions (1 mark):** Newtonian fluid, laminar flow, steady temperature, thin annular gap.

- **Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.3,

$$\mu = \frac{T}{4 \pi \omega L} \left( \frac{1}{r_{1}^{2}} - \frac{1}{r_{2}^{2}} \right)$$

where $r_{1}$ = bob radius, $r_{2}$ = cup radius, $L$ = effective immersed length.

---

**(b)** In a falling-sphere viscometer, a steel sphere of diameter 4 mm and density 7800 kg/m³ falls through a lubricating oil of density 920 kg/m³. The terminal velocity is observed as 0.025 m/s. Determine the dynamic viscosity of the oil. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

Given: $d = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m → $r = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho_{s} = 7800$ kg/m³, $\rho_{f} = 920$ kg/m³, $v_{t} = 0.025$ m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1:** Apply Stokes' equation:

$$\mu = \frac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 v_{t}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator:

$$r^{2} = (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$2 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 9.81 \cdot (7800 - 920) = 2 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 6880$$

$$= 5.3999 \times 10^{-1} = 0.5400$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator:

$$9 \cdot 0.025 = 0.225$$

**Step 4:** Divide:

$$\mu = \frac{0.5400}{0.225} = 2.40 \text{ Pa·s} = 2400 \text{ cP}$$

**Final Answer:** $\mu = 2.40$ Pa·s

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert **diameter to radius** ($r = d/2$). Always check whether the problem gives $r$ or $d$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — memorize cold.
- ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation** is the **master formula** for capillary viscometers.
- ✅ **Stokes' Law** is for **falling sphere / falling body** viscometers (laminar, $Re < 1$).
- ✅ **Rotational viscometer** equation has the term $\left(\dfrac{1}{r_{1}^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{r_{2}^{2}}\right)$ — do not confuse with thin-gap form.
- ✅ **Conversion memory:** **1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s** ; **1 Stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s** ; **1 cP = 1 mPa·s**.
- ✅ **Kinematic–Dynamic relation:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ — fundamental and frequently tested.
- ✅ **Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian:** viscosity is constant only for Newtonian fluids.
- ✅ **Industrial standards:** Saybolt (ASTM D88), Redwood, Engler, Brookfield — know at least one for petroleum and one for paint industry.
- ✅ **Always convert mm to m** when computing $r^{4}$ — examiners deduct heavily for unit errors.
- ✅ **Saybolt formula (for $t > 100$ s):** $SUS = 0.226 t - 195/t$ — used in petroleum refining.
- ✅ **Cone-and-plate viscometer** gives uniform shear rate — preferred for non-Newtonian fluids and small samples.
- ✅ **Vibrating viscometers** are ideal for **inline process monitoring** because they have no moving parts and are corrosion-resistant.
- ✅ **Temperature dependence:** Viscosity of liquids **decreases** with temperature (unlike gases, where it increases).
- ✅ **Diagram:** Always draw a labelled schematic for the 7-mark derivations; examiners reward visual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscometer Selection and Application, Measurement of Density: Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Viscometer Selection and Application, Measurement of Density: Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Viscosity — The "Internal Friction" of Fluids

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
*Viscosity* is the property of a fluid that offers **resistance to deformation (flow)** under applied shear stress. Quantitatively, it is the proportionality constant between shear stress ($\tau$) and the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient $\frac{du}{dy}$), as expressed in **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress, measured in **N/m²** or **Pa**
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity, measured in **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²** (or Poise in CGS)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (shear rate), measured in **s⁻¹**

**Two principal forms of viscosity:**

| Type | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit | CGS Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | Resistance to shear | Pa·s | Poise (P) |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s | Stokes (St) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Tip for KTU Exams:** $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 10 \text{ Poise} = 1000 \text{ cP (centipoise)}$. For water at $20^\circ\text{C}$, $\mu \approx 1.002 \text{ cP}$.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pouring honey versus pouring water from the same cup. Honey flows sluggishly while water gushes out. This "sluggishness" is **viscosity**. Inside the fluid, imagine two adjacent layers of molecules — viscosity is the internal "stickiness" or "friction" that tries to keep one layer from sliding past the other. **The thicker the fluid, the higher the viscosity.**

---

## 1.2 Density — The "Compactness" of Matter

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
*Density* ($\rho$) is defined as the **mass per unit volume** of a substance at a specified temperature and pressure. It is an **intensive property** (independent of the amount of substance).

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of the substance (kg)
- $V$ = volume occupied (m³)
- SI unit: **kg/m³** (commonly g/cm³ or g/mL in practice)

**Related Definitions (Frequently tested in KTU Part A):**

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Relation |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Specific Gravity (SG) | — | Ratio of density of a substance to that of a reference substance (usually water at 4°C) | $SG = \rho_{substance} / \rho_{water}$ |
| Relative Density (RD) | — | Numerically equal to SG; emphasises dimensionless ratio | $RD = \rho / \rho_{ref}$ |
| API Gravity | $^\circ API$ | Inverse scale used in petroleum industry | $^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ |
| Specific Volume | $v$ | Volume per unit mass | $v = 1/\rho$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight:** Specific Gravity is **dimensionless** and **temperature-dependent**. Always state the reference temperature (e.g., SG at $15.6^\circ\text{C}/15.6^\circ\text{C}$ or $20^\circ\text{C}/4^\circ\text{C}$) in definitions.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Take a kilogram of feathers and a kilogram of iron. Both have the same mass, but the iron occupies far less space. The iron is **denser** because its molecules are packed tightly together. Now think of a floating cork versus a sinking stone in water — the cork has lower density than water (it floats), while the stone has higher density (it sinks). **Density answers the question: "How much stuff is crammed into this space?"**

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Measurements Matter Together

In industrial process control, viscosity and density are **two of the most frequently measured physical properties** because they:

1. Define **fluid quality** (e.g., fuel grade, oil purity, syrup concentration)
2. Directly influence **flow measurement** (mass flow meters depend on density)
3. Control **chemical reaction rates** and heat transfer
4. Are critical for **custody transfer** in oil & gas (where small errors = millions of rupees)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Snapshot:** In a petroleum refinery, viscosity determines pumpability, while density determines calorific value and pricing. Both measurements are mandated by API and ASTM standards at every custody transfer point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fluids by Viscosity Behavior

Understanding fluid type is the **first step in viscometer selection**. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests this classification.

### 2.1.1 Newtonian Fluids
Viscosity is **constant** (independent of shear rate). The fluid obeys Newton's Law linearly.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} \quad \text{(Linear relation)}$$

**Examples:** Water, air, gasoline, light oils, alcohol, glycerin.

### 2.1.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids
Viscosity changes with shear rate. The viscosity at a given shear rate is called the **apparent viscosity** $\mu_a$.

| Type | Behavior | Viscosity Trend | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning) | Viscosity **decreases** with shear | $\mu_a \downarrow$ as $\frac{du}{dy} \uparrow$ | Blood, ketchup, paints, polymer melts |
| Dilatant (Shear-thickening) | Viscosity **increases** with shear | $\mu_a \uparrow$ as $\frac{du}{dy} \uparrow$ | Starch suspensions, wet sand, candy compounds |
| Bingham Plastic | Behaves as solid until yield stress $\tau_y$ exceeded | Has yield stress | Toothpaste, drilling mud, grease |
| Thixotropic | Viscosity decreases **with time** under constant shear | Time-dependent | Yogurt, printer ink, gels |
| Rheopectic | Viscosity increases **with time** under constant shear | Time-dependent | Gypsum paste, some lubricants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Non-Newtonian fluids require viscometers that can apply **multiple, controlled shear rates** (e.g., rotational viscometers). Capillary viscometers give misleading single-point values.

---

## 2.2 Viscometer Types — Working Principles

### 2.2.1 Capillary Viscometer (e.g., Ostwald, Ubbelohde, Cannon-Fenske)
- **Principle:** Measure time taken for a fixed volume of fluid to flow through a calibrated capillary under gravity.
- **Governing Equation:** **Hagen-Poiseuille Law** (derived in Section 3):

$$\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P t}{8 L V}$$

- **Best for:** Low-viscosity **Newtonian fluids** (water, oils, solvents).
- **Range:** ~0.4 cP to 10⁴ cP.

### 2.2.2 Falling Ball (Falling Sphere) Viscometer
- **Principle:** A ball of known density falls through the fluid under gravity. Terminal velocity is inversely related to viscosity.
- **Governing Equation (Stokes' Law at terminal velocity):**

$$\mu = \frac{2}{9} \cdot \frac{r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{v_t}$$

where $r$ = ball radius, $g$ = gravity, $\rho_s$ = ball density, $\rho_f$ = fluid density, $v_t$ = terminal velocity.

- **Best for:** **Transparent Newtonian fluids** (gases, oils, glycerol).
- **Range:** ~0.5 cP to 10⁵ cP.

### 2.2.3 Rotational Viscometer (e.g., Brookfield, Cone-and-Plate, Couette)
- **Principle:** A spindle (bob) rotates in the fluid at controlled speed. The torque required is proportional to viscosity.
- **Governing Equation (Concentric Cylinder):**

$$\mu = \frac{T}{4 \pi \omega L} \left(\frac{1}{R_i^2} - \frac{1}{R_o^2}\right)$$

where $T$ = torque, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $L$ = cylinder length, $R_i, R_o$ = inner/outer radii.

- **Best for:** **Both Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids**. Can vary shear rate by changing RPM.
- **Range:** ~1 cP to 10⁹ cP.

### 2.2.4 Vibrational / Oscillating Piston Viscometer
- **Principle:** A piston oscillates at constant amplitude; the driving current required is proportional to viscosity.
- **Best for:** **Inline/online industrial measurements** in pipelines, closed systems.
- **Range:** ~0.1 cP to 10⁵ cP.

### 2.2.5 Saybolt Viscometer (Industrial, ASTM Standard)
- **Principle:** Measures time (in seconds, called **Saybolt Universal Seconds, SUS**) for 60 mL of fluid to flow through a calibrated orifice at a specified temperature.
- **Best for:** **Petroleum industry** (fuel oils, lubricants).
- **Conversion:** SUS is converted to kinematic viscosity using ASTM D2161 tables.

---

## 2.3 Density Measurement Methods — Working Principles

### 2.3.1 Hydrometer
- **Principle:** Archimedes' principle — a calibrated float sinks to a depth inversely proportional to fluid density. Scale is read at the liquid surface.
- **Best for:** Quick, low-cost measurements in **liquids** (battery acid, milk, alcohol).
- **Accuracy:** ~±0.001 g/cm³.

### 2.3.2 Pycnometer (Density Bottle)
- **Principle:** A bottle of precisely known volume is filled, weighed, and density computed as $\rho = m/V$.
- **Best for:** **Laboratory reference** measurements; powders, liquids.
- **Accuracy:** Up to ±0.0001 g/cm³ (highest among mechanical methods).

### 2.3.3 Oscillating U-Tube Density Meter (Anton Paar principle)
- **Principle:** A U-shaped tube is filled with the sample and excited to oscillate. The natural frequency depends on the total mass (and thus density).
- **Governing Equation (derived in Section 3):**

$$\rho = K_0 + K_1 \cdot T^2 + K_2 \cdot T^4$$

where $T$ is the period of oscillation and $K_0, K_1, K_2$ are instrument constants.

- **Best for:** **Inline process density measurement** (refineries, breweries, pharmaceutical).
- **Accuracy:** Up to ±0.000005 g/cm³ (industry gold standard).

### 2.3.4 Buoyancy Method (Chain Balance / Magnetic Float)
- **Principle:** A float submerged in the fluid experiences a buoyancy force proportional to fluid density, measured by force/displacement sensor.
- **Best for:** Continuous tank-level density monitoring.

### 2.3.5 Coriolis Mass Flow Meter
- **Principle:** Fluid flows through U-tubes that vibrate. Tube frequency depends on mass. **Density is measured as a byproduct of mass flow measurement.**
- **Best for:** **High-accuracy industrial custody transfer**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum required formula set** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Shear stress, dynamic viscosity, velocity gradient | Newton's law of viscosity |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity, dynamic viscosity, density | Viscosity definitions |
| 3 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Mass, volume | Density definition |
| 4 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{water @ 4°C}}$ | Specific gravity (dimensionless) | Density relative to water |
| 5 | $\mu = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille (capillary) | Capillary viscometer |
| 6 | $\mu = \dfrac{2}{9} \cdot \dfrac{r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{v_t}$ | Stokes' law (terminal velocity) | Falling ball viscometer |
| 7 | $\mu = \dfrac{T}{4 \pi \omega L} \left(\dfrac{1}{R_i^2} - \dfrac{1}{R_o^2}\right)$ | Torque, angular velocity, cylinder geometry | Rotational (Couette) viscometer |
| 8 | $\mu_a = \dfrac{\tau_{applied}}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent viscosity, shear rate | Non-Newtonian fluids |
| 9 | $\rho = K_0 + K_1 T^2 + K_2 T^4$ | Density vs. oscillation period | Oscillating U-tube density meter |
| 10 | $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | API gravity (petroleum) | Petroleum industry density |

> **Engineering Utility:** The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is the basis of all **automated capillary viscometers** used in oil labs. The Stokes' law equation is the workhorse of **process viscometers** in paint, food, and pharmaceutical industries. The Oscillating U-tube equation is embedded in **every modern Coriolis and density meter** used in custody transfer — the difference of a 6th decimal in density translates to **crores of rupees** per year in oil trade.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Capillary Viscometer)

This derivation is a **guaranteed KTU exam derivation** — appearing in almost every industrial instrumentation paper.

### Starting Assumption
Consider a horizontal cylindrical capillary tube of:
- Radius $R$
- Length $L$
- Pressure difference across ends $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$
- Fluid flows in **streamline (laminar) regime**

A fluid element of radius $r$ (where $r \le R$) is in equilibrium between the **pressure force pushing it forward** and the **viscous drag from the surrounding fluid**.

### Step 1: Forces on a Cylindrical Fluid Element
Pressure force on the element of cross-section $\pi r^2$:

$$F_{pressure} = \Delta P \cdot \pi r^2$$

Viscous force on the curved surface (area $2\pi r L$):

$$F_{viscous} = -\tau \cdot (2\pi r L)$$

The negative sign denotes that viscous force opposes flow.

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity
By definition:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

(The negative sign appears because velocity $u$ decreases as $r$ increases, so $\frac{du}{dr}$ is negative.)

### Step 3: Equilibrium Condition
At steady state, $F_{pressure} = -F_{viscous}$:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = -\tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

Substitute $\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = -\left(-\mu \frac{du}{dr}\right) \cdot 2\pi r L$$

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = 2\pi r L \mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

### Step 4: Rearrange and Integrate
Solve for $du$:

$$du = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 \mu L} dr$$

Integrate from $r = 0$ (centre, maximum velocity $u_{max}$) to $r = R$ (wall, $u = 0$ for no-slip):

$$\int_{u_{max}}^{0} du = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 \mu L} dr$$

$$-u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2}$$

$$u_{max} = -\frac{\Delta P R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

The negative sign indicates flow in the negative $r$ direction (from high to low pressure). For the **velocity profile**:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

This is a **parabolic velocity profile** — characteristic of laminar flow.

### Step 5: Compute Volumetric Flow Rate
Total volumetric flow $Q$ is obtained by integrating velocity over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} (R^2 r - r^3) dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_{0}^{R}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right] = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

### Step 6: Final Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 L Q}}$$

Since volume $V = Q \cdot t$ (flow rate × time) for a measured volume $V$ in time $t$:

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important consequence:** Viscosity is **inversely proportional to time** when pressure and dimensions are constant. This is why capillary viscometers are calibrated against a **reference fluid** of known viscosity:
>
> $$\frac{\mu_{unknown}}{\mu_{reference}} = \frac{t_{unknown}}{t_{reference}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Falling Ball Viscometer (Stokes' Law with Buoyancy)

A sphere of radius $r$ and density $\rho_s$ falls under gravity through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$. Three forces act on the sphere:

1. **Weight (downward):** $F_g = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s g$
2. **Buoyancy (upward):** $F_b = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g$
3. **Viscous Drag (upward, opposing motion):** $F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$ (Stokes' drag for low Reynolds number)

### Step 1: Terminal Velocity Condition
At terminal velocity, acceleration = 0, so net force = 0:

$$F_g = F_b + F_d$$

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s g = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g + 6 \pi \mu r v_t$$

### Step 2: Isolate Viscosity
$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) = 6 \pi \mu r v_t$$

Divide both sides by $6 \pi r$:

$$\frac{2}{3} r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) = \mu v_t$$

### Step 3: Final Expression

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}}$$

This is the **working equation** of falling ball viscometers. $v_t$ is measured by timing the ball through a calibrated length of the tube.

> [!WARNING]
> **Stokes' law limitation:** It is valid only for **laminar flow around the sphere**, i.e., Reynolds number $Re < 1$. For high-viscosity or large-ball cases, correction factors must be applied (e.g., Faxén's correction).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Oscillating U-Tube Density Meter

The U-tube is modelled as a **mass-spring system** undergoing harmonic oscillation. The period $T$ of oscillation depends on the **effective oscillating mass** $m_{eff}$, which equals the tube mass plus the **fluid mass inside the tube**.

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m_{eff}}{k}}$$

where $k$ is the spring constant of the tube (fixed by geometry and material). For the **fluid mass** alone:

$$m_{fluid} = \rho \cdot V_{tube}$$

Thus:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m_{tube} + \rho V}{k}}$$

### Step 1: Square Both Sides
$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{k} (m_{tube} + \rho V)$$

### Step 2: Solve for $\rho$
$$\rho = \frac{k T^2}{4 \pi^2 V} - \frac{m_{tube}}{V}$$

Define constants $K_1 = \dfrac{k}{4\pi^2 V}$ and $K_0 = -\dfrac{m_{tube}}{V}$:

$$\rho = K_0 + K_1 T^2$$

### Step 3: Empirical Extension
In practice, non-linearities and temperature effects are absorbed into higher-order terms:

$$\boxed{\rho = K_0 + K_1 T^2 + K_2 T^4}$$

This empirical equation is calibrated using **two reference fluids** (typically air and water) to determine the constants $K_0, K_1, K_2$.

---

## 3.4 Viscometer Selection — Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to "select" a viscometer based on fluid properties. The following decision matrix is the **high-yield, board-marking table**.

| Selection Criterion | Capillary | Falling Ball | Rotational (Brookfield) | Vibrational / Inline |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Viscosity Range (cP)** | 0.4 – 10,000 | 0.5 – 100,000 | 1 – 10⁹ | 0.1 – 100,000 |
| **Fluid Type** | Newtonian only | Newtonian (transparent) | Newtonian + Non-Newtonian | Newtonian only |
| **Accuracy** | High (±0.1%) | Moderate (±1–2%) | Moderate (±1%) | Moderate (±1%) |
| **Sample Volume** | Small (5–10 mL) | Moderate (50 mL) | Moderate (50–500 mL) | None (in-line) |
| **Temperature Control** | Bath required | Bath required | Built-in jacket | Process stream |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low | Low | Medium–High | High |
| **Industrial Use** | Lab QC | Lab | Lab + Process | Process only |
| **Shear Rate Control** | Single | Single | Variable (variable RPM) | Fixed (operating freq.) |
| **Best Application** | Oils, solvents, water | Glycerin, syrup, polymers | Paints, cosmetics, foods | Petroleum pipelines, chemicals |

### Step-by-Step Selection Procedure (KTU 14-Mark Standard Answer)

1. **Identify the fluid type** → Newtonian or Non-Newtonian?
2. **Estimate the viscosity range** → Low, medium, or high?
3. **Determine the measurement environment** → Lab or in-line process?
4. **Assess sample availability** → Limited volume or continuous flow?
5. **Check temperature sensitivity** → Does the fluid viscosity change drastically with temperature?
6. **Decide on accuracy needs** → Custody transfer (±0.01%) or routine QC (±1%)?
7. **Match to viscometer** → Use the matrix above to finalize.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> A liquid with a dynamic viscosity of $0.89 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s and density of $870$ kg/m³ is tested in a falling ball viscometer using a steel ball of radius $1.5$ mm and density $7850$ kg/m³. Compute the terminal velocity of the ball.

**Given:** $\mu = 0.89 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho_f = 870$ kg/m³, $r = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho_s = 7850$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Formula:** $\mu = \dfrac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}$

Solve for $v_t$:

$$v_t = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}$$

$$v_t = \frac{2 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 9.81 \times (7850 - 870)}{9 \times 0.89 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Numerator:** $2 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 6980 = 0.3083$

**Denominator:** $9 \times 0.89 \times 10^{-3} = 8.01 \times 10^{-3}$

$$v_t = \frac{0.3083}{8.01 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 38.5 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check:** A velocity of 38.5 m/s is unrealistically high, indicating **Stokes' law is violated** (Re > 1). In a real KTU answer, you would conclude that **a different viscometer (rotational) should be chosen** for this system.

### Worked Example 2: Capillary Viscometer Calibration

> A capillary viscometer gives a flow time of 285 s for a sample of unknown viscosity. Using the same viscometer, a reference fluid of known viscosity 1.20 cP gives a flow time of 240 s. Find the unknown viscosity.

Using the ratio method (valid when density difference is small):

$$\mu_{unk} = \mu_{ref} \cdot \frac{t_{unk}}{t_{ref}} \cdot \frac{\rho_{unk}}{\rho_{ref}}$$

Assuming similar densities ($\rho_{unk} \approx \rho_{ref}$):

$$\mu_{unk} = 1.20 \times \frac{285}{240} = 1.425 \text{ cP}$$

If densities differ, multiply by the ratio: e.g., if $\rho_{unk} = 1.05 \rho_{ref}$:

$$\mu_{unk} = 1.20 \times \frac{285}{240} \times 1.05 = 1.496 \text{ cP}$$

---

## 3.5 Density Measurement — Selection Matrix

| Method | Accuracy | Cost | Application | Continuous? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Hydrometer | Low–Medium (±0.001) | Very Low | Field, acid, milk | No |
| Pycnometer | Very High (±0.0001) | Low | Laboratory reference | No |
| Oscillating U-Tube | Very High (±0.000005) | High | Industrial inline process | Yes |
| Coriolis Meter | High (±0.0001) | Very High | Custody transfer, mass flow | Yes |
| Buoyancy (Float) | Medium (±0.001) | Medium | Tank, vessel level density | Yes |
| Ultrasonic | Medium (±0.005) | Medium | Harsh environments, slurries | Yes |
| Radiation (Gamma) | High (±0.0005) | Very High | Slurries, opaque fluids | Yes |
