- [x] **Density** = mass/volume; SI unit **kg/m³**; standard reference for SG is **water at 4 °C** ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$).
- [x] **Specific gravity (SG)** is **dimensionless**; **API gravity** is used for petroleum; **Baumé** is used in chemicals.
- [x] **API formula (light liquids):** ${^{\circ}API = 141.5/SG - 131.5}$.
- [x] **Hydrometer** — floatation; **Pycnometer** — gravimetric lab method; **Displacer** — Archimedes.
- [x] **Displacer buoyant force:** $F_b = \rho g V_d$. Apparent weight: $W_{app} = (\rho_d - \rho) g V_d$.
- [x] **Hydrostatic head:** $\rho = \Delta P / (g \cdot \Delta h)$. Surface pressure cancels out.
- [x] **Vibrating U-tube:** $f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/(m_t + \rho V)}$; higher ρ → **lower** f.
- [x] **Calibrated density formula:** $\rho = k/(4\pi^2 V f^2) - m_t/V$.
- [x] **Coriolis densitometer:** $\rho = \dot{m}/Q$; density is a **derived** quantity.
- [x] **Gamma-ray densitometer:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho x}$; best for **slurries** and **corrosive** liquids.
- [x] **Ultrasonic:** $c = 1/\sqrt{\rho \beta}$; non-invasive; needs known $\beta(T)$.
- [x] **Refractometer:** suitable for **binary mixtures** (e.g., sugar-water); indirect.
- [x] **Temperature compensation** is **mandatory** in all industrial densitometers.
- [x] Method selection criteria: **accuracy**, **online/offline**, **corrosive/slurry environment**, **cost**, **maintenance**.
- [x] Highest accuracy → Pycnometer (lab) / Vibrating U-tube (industrial).
- [x] Best for slurries → **Gamma-ray densitometer** (clog-free, non-contact).
- [x] Always **state units** in the final answer; always **check dimensional consistency** in derivations.
- [x] KTU exam tip: For 14-mark questions, **draw the block diagram** (2–3 marks reserved) and **derive the formula** (3–4 marks) before numerical substitution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Densitometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas Densitometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**: A **Gas Densitometer** is a precision industrial instrument used to measure the **density (ρ) or specific gravity (SG)** of a process gas — usually expressed in **kg/m³** or as a ratio relative to a reference gas (typically dry air at **NTP: 0 °C, 1.01325 bar**).

The device operates on fundamental physical principles such as **centrifugal force, gas buoyancy, hydrostatic balance, thermal conductivity variation, or vibrational frequency shift** of a sensing element immersed in the gas stream. Gas densitometers are essential in **petrochemical refining, natural gas custody transfer, hydrogen plants, and air-quality monitoring** because density is a direct, non-dispersive indicator of molecular weight and composition.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Mapping**: This topic falls under **Module 3 – Measurement of Density, Specific Gravity, and Moisture**. The expected outcomes (CO3) require students to **identify, classify, and analyze** different gas densitometer constructions and their working principles.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are holding **two identical sealed plastic balls** — one filled with **helium (very light gas)** and the other with **carbon dioxide (heavier gas)**. Even though they look identical, if you spin them in a centrifuge, the **heavier CO₂ ball presses harder against the outer wall** than the helium one. By precisely measuring that **outward push**, you can determine the *molecular "weightiness"* of the gas inside.

**Gas densitometers do exactly this:**

| Real-Life Situation | Industrial Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Twirling a water-filled balloon vs. an air-filled balloon | Spinning a gas in an **impeller (centrifugal densitometer)** |
| Floating a cork in salt water vs. fresh water | Floating a **buoyant displacer** in a gas column |
| Blowing harder through a thin tube vs. a thick tube | Measuring **pressure drop** in a flowing gas |
| Plucking a guitar string — heavier string vibrates slower | Vibrating a **tuning fork** immersed in the gas |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight**: Density (ρ) is one of the few **intrinsic, temperature/pressure-dependent-yet-predictable** properties of a gas. Unlike color or odor, density cannot be faked — making it the **gold-standard fingerprint** for gas purity and identification in custody-transfer billing (e.g., natural gas sold by the SCM — Standard Cubic Meter, but priced per unit mass).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Conditions (Memorize!)

- **Universal Gas Constant** $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Temperature (NTP)** $T_0$ = **273.15 K (0 °C)**
- **Standard Pressure (NTP)** $P_0$ = **1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa (1 atm)**
- **Reference Gas — Dry Air** at NTP: $ρ_{air}$ = **1.293 kg/m³** (molar mass = 28.97 g/mol)
- **Acceleration due to gravity** $g$ = **9.80665 m/s²**
- **Specific Gravity (SG)** is **dimensionless** — it is the **ratio of gas density to reference air density**, both at identical $P$ and $T$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Students often confuse **Specific Gravity (dimensionless)** with **Density (kg/m³)**. Examiners will deduct marks if units are missing in a numerical answer. Always write $ρ = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$ or $SG = 1.20$ — **never** "1.20" alone.

---

## 1.4 Why Measure Gas Density? — Industry Relevance

Gas density measurement is **non-negotiable** in:

1. **Natural Gas Custody Transfer** — Billing is per unit mass, but the meter reads volume. Density bridges the gap.
2. **Hydrogen-Cooled Turbo-Generators** — Purity of H₂ leak detection (ρ drops drastically if air leaks in).
3. **Blast Furnace / Coke Oven Gas** — Calorific value estimation from density.
4. **LPG / LNG Storage** — Detecting vapor phase contamination.
5. **Stack Gas Monitoring** — Correlating density to CO₂ / SO₂ emissions.
6. **Catalyst Regeneration** — Monitoring reactant gas in petrochemical reactors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Gas Densitometers (KTU Module 3 Framework)

Gas densitometers are broadly classified by their **transduction principle**:

### **A. Centrifugal (Schultz-Type) Gas Densitometer**
- **Principle**: A high-speed spinning impeller draws the test gas radially outward, creating a density-dependent pressure gradient.
- **Application**: Continuous online monitoring in pipelines; rugged for field use.

### **B. Displacement (Buoyancy / Float-Type) Gas Densitometer**
- **Principle**: A buoyant float displaces a fixed volume of gas; its vertical position (detected by LVDT or differential transformer) is a function of the surrounding gas density.
- **Application**: Laboratory-grade precision; reference instrument.

### **C. Thermal Conductivity-Type Densitometer**
- **Principle**: A heated filament's heat loss depends on the gas's thermal conductivity, which is correlated to density (and composition).
- **Application**: Binary gas mixture analysis (e.g., H₂ in N₂).

### **D. Ultrasonic Gas Densitometer**
- **Principle**: The speed of an ultrasonic wave through a gas is $c = \sqrt{\gamma RT/M}$, so transit time $\propto \sqrt{1/M} \propto \sqrt{1/\rho}$.
- **Application**: Non-invasive, hygienic, ideal for food/pharma gases.

### **E. Vibrating Element (Tuning Fork / Cylinder) Densitometer**
- **Principle**: A mechanically excited element's natural frequency $f \propto \sqrt{k/m_{eff}}$, and $m_{eff}$ depends on the displaced gas mass (i.e., density).
- **Application**: Modern high-precision Coriolis-style gas meters.

### **F. Optical / Refractometric Gas Densitometer**
- **Principle**: The refractive index $n$ of a gas is linearly related to its density ($n - 1 \propto \rho$, Gladstone–Dale relation).
- **Application**: Laser-based purity monitoring.

---

## 2.2 Operational Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

### **2.2.1 Centrifugal (Schultz) Densitometer — Working Logic**

1. Test gas enters the **center** of a high-speed rotating impeller (ω rad/s).
2. Centrifugal acceleration ($a_c = ω²r$) throws gas molecules outward.
3. This generates a **radial pressure gradient** described by Euler's equation for fluid statics in a rotating frame.
4. The **higher the gas density, the greater the pressure rise** at any radius r.
5. By comparing this pressure rise against a reference gas (air), the density ratio is obtained.

**The "WHY" behind it**: Heavy gas molecules carry **more momentum** per unit volume. When accelerated outward by the same ω, they generate more "ram pressure" against the outer wall — like throwing a heavy ball vs. a light ball at the same speed: the heavy ball hits harder.

### **2.2.2 Displacement Densitometer — Working Logic**

1. A precisely machined **float (displacer)** of known mass $m_f$ and volume $V_f$ is suspended in a vertical chamber.
2. The chamber is filled with the test gas.
3. The **buoyant force** $F_b = \rho_{gas} \cdot V_f \cdot g$ acts upward.
4. **Net downward force** = $m_f g - \rho_{gas} V_f g$.
5. A feedback current (or mechanical spring) restores the float to its null position.
6. The **magnitude of restoring force is directly proportional to $\rho_{gas}$**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board Exam Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists **every equation** you are likely to need for a KTU 2024 question on Gas Densitometers. **No vertical pipes** are used — instead `\vert` is employed to preserve table formatting.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use in Problem |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ρ = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Density definition | Base definition |
| 2 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas law | Relating P, T to ρ |
| 3 | $ρ = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ | Density of ideal gas (M = molar mass in kg/mol) | Pure gas density |
| 4 | $SG = \dfrac{ρ_{gas}}{ρ_{air}}\big\vert_{P,T}$ | Specific gravity (dimensionless) | Comparing gases |
| 5 | $ρ_{mix} = \dfrac{\sum y_i M_i P}{RT}$ | Density of ideal gas mixture ($y_i$ = mole fraction) | Multi-component streams |
| 6 | $dP/dr = ρ ω² r$ | Pressure gradient in rotating gas (centrifugal) | Schultz densitometer |
| 7 | $P(r) - P(0) = \dfrac{1}{2} ρ ω² r²$ | Pressure rise in spinning gas | Radial pressure calc |
| 8 | $\dfrac{ρ_x}{ρ_{ref}} = \dfrac{h_{ref}}{h_x}$ | Density ratio by column height (Schultz column form) | Comparing columns |
| 9 | $F_b = ρ_{gas} V_f g$ | Buoyant force on float | Displacement meter |
| 10 | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_0 + ρ_{gas} V_e}}$ | Vibrating element frequency | Tuning fork densitometer |
| 11 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{γ R T}{M}}$ | Speed of sound in gas (γ = Cp/Cv) | Ultrasonic densitometer |
| 12 | $n - 1 = K \cdot ρ$ | Gladstone–Dale relation (K = refractivity) | Optical densitometer |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Why Gas Density is Measured |
|---|---|
| **Oil & Gas** | Custody transfer billing per mass, not volume |
| **Power Plants** | Hydrogen purity in synchronous generators |
| **Steel Industry** | Blast furnace gas calorific value estimation |
| **Semiconductor Fab** | Ultra-pure gas (N₂, Ar) contamination check |
| **Hospitals** | Medical O₂ / N₂O quality control |
| **Environmental** | Stack gas emissions quantification |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb**: In any gas mixture at fixed P, T, **density is a linear weighted average of molar masses**. This is the key insight behind why **density measurements can detect leaks, contamination, and composition shifts** in real time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Distribution in a Centrifugal (Schultz) Gas Densitometer

### **Statement (as appears in KTU exam)**

> "A centrifugal gas densitometer has an impeller rotating at ω rad/s. A test gas of density ρ enters at the center. Derive an expression for the pressure rise at a radial distance r from the axis. Hence, show that the ratio of densities of two gases can be obtained by comparing their pressure rises at the same r."

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

Consider a small fluid element at radius r, of thickness dr and cross-sectional area dA, rotating with the impeller at angular velocity ω.

In the rotating reference frame, each element experiences a centrifugal body force per unit volume equal to $ρ ω² r$ directed radially outward.

For static equilibrium in the rotating frame (since the gas column is steady relative to the impeller), the **radial pressure gradient must balance this centrifugal body force**.

The fundamental relation is:

$$\frac{dP}{dr} = \rho \, \omega^2 r$$

**Step 1 — Separate the variables** (treat ρ and ω as constants for an incompressible-flow assumption; valid because gas density variation in this case is dominated by pressure gradient, not by composition change with r):

$$dP = \rho \, \omega^2 r \, dr$$

**Step 2 — Integrate** from the center (r = 0, where P = P₀) to an arbitrary radius r (where P = P(r)):

$$P(r) - P_0 = \rho \, \omega^2 \int_0^r r' \, dr' = \rho \, \omega^2 \cdot \frac{r^2}{2}$$

This gives the **fundamental Schultz equation**:

$$P(r) - P_0 = \frac{1}{2} \rho \, \omega^2 r^2$$

**Step 3 — For two gases (test gas x and reference gas a) at the same ω and r:**

$$\Delta P_x = \frac{1}{2} \rho_x \, \omega^2 r^2$$

$$\Delta P_a = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a \, \omega^2 r^2$$

**Step 4 — Take the ratio:**

$$\frac{\Delta P_x}{\Delta P_a} = \frac{\rho_x}{\rho_a}$$

Thus:

$$\boxed{\rho_x = \rho_a \cdot \frac{\Delta P_x}{\Delta P_a}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**: The pressure rise is **quadratic in r and ω** but only **linear in ρ**. This is why the densitometer is calibrated at a known ω and r, and the *deviation* in pressure (compared to air) directly gives the density ratio.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Buoyancy Force and Float Position in a Displacement Densitometer

### **Statement**

> "A displacer of mass m_f and volume V_f is suspended in a chamber filled with gas of density ρ_g. Derive the relationship between the buoyant force and the gas density, and explain how the position signal is converted to a density reading."

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Step 1 — Forces acting on the float (vertical equilibrium):**

- **Weight (downward):** $W = m_f g$
- **Buoyant force (upward):** $F_b = \rho_g V_f g$ (Archimedes' principle applied to gas)
- **Restoring force (from feedback or spring):** $F_r$

At null position (float at reference height), the net force is zero:

$$F_r = m_f g - \rho_g V_f g = (m_f - \rho_g V_f) g$$

**Step 2 — Define the reference density** $\rho_0$ at which the float is calibrated (typically at the null position with air at NTP):

$$F_{r,0} = (m_f - \rho_0 V_f) g$$

**Step 3 — When the test gas replaces air**, the change in restoring force is:

$$\Delta F_r = (\rho_0 - \rho_g) V_f g$$

**Step 4 — Express density in terms of measured force:**

$$\boxed{\rho_g = \rho_0 - \frac{\Delta F_r}{V_f g}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note**: In modern instruments, the float is held at null by an **electromagnetic feedback coil** (no mechanical spring). The current $I$ required to hold the float is directly proportional to $\Delta F_r$, giving a clean **electrical output proportional to density**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vibrating Element Densitometer Frequency-Density Relation

### **Statement**

> "A tuning fork of mass m₀ and stiffness k is immersed in a gas of density ρ. Derive the expression for the natural frequency as a function of gas density."

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Step 1 — Effective mass of the oscillating system:**

When the fork vibrates, it must also accelerate a small parcel of surrounding gas. For a fork with effective gas-displacement volume $V_e$, the added mass is $\rho V_e$.

Total effective mass:

$$m_{eff} = m_0 + \rho V_e$$

**Step 2 — Natural frequency of a spring-mass system:**

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute m_eff:**

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_0 + \rho V_e}}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for ρ in terms of measured f:**

$$m_0 + \rho V_e = \frac{k}{(2\pi f)^2}$$

$$\rho = \frac{1}{V_e}\left[\frac{k}{(2\pi f)^2} - m_0\right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Calibration Procedure**: Measure $f_1$ and $f_2$ with two known-density gases (e.g., vacuum → $\rho_1 \approx 0$, and air → $\rho_2 = 1.293 \text{ kg/m}^3$). This yields both $k$ and $m_0$ for the specific fork, eliminating systematic errors.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Which Densitometer to Choose?

| Criterion | Centrifugal (Schultz) | Displacement (Float) | Vibrating Element | Ultrasonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | ±1% | ±0.1% | ±0.05% | ±0.5% |
| **Response Time** | Medium (5–10 s) | Slow (20–30 s) | Fast (1–2 s) | Very Fast (<1 s) |
| **Pressure Resistance** | Excellent (up to 100 bar) | Moderate (up to 10 bar) | Moderate | Limited |
| **Maintenance** | Bearing wear | Low | Low | Very Low (non-contact) |
| **Cost** | Medium | High | High | Medium |
| **Best Suited For** | Pipeline natural gas | Lab reference standard | Modern Coriolis meters | Clean, dry gases |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Board Style)

> **Question**: A centrifugal gas densitometer has an impeller rotating at 3000 rpm. At a radius of 5 cm, the pressure rise for air is 250 Pa. When methane (CH₄) is admitted under identical conditions, the pressure rise is 178 Pa. Find the density of methane. Take $ρ_{air}$ = 1.29 kg/m³.

### **Solution**

**Step 1:** Convert rotational speed to rad/s:

$$\omega = 3000 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute ω²:

$$\omega^2 = 98696 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 3:** Use the Schultz relation $P(r) - P_0 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \omega^2 r^2$, with r = 0.05 m:

$$r^2 = 0.0025 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Verify with air to confirm consistency:

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho_{air} \omega^2 r^2 = 0.5 \times 1.29 \times 98696 \times 0.0025$$

$$= 159.13 \text{ Pa}$$

(This differs from 250 Pa — the problem gives experimental values, so use the given ΔP directly.)

**Step 5:** Use the ratio relation (which cancels ω² and r²):

$$\frac{\rho_{CH_4}}{\rho_{air}} = \frac{\Delta P_{CH_4}}{\Delta P_{air}} = \frac{178}{250} = 0.712$$

**Step 6:** Solve:

$$\rho_{CH_4} = 0.712 \times 1.29 = 0.9185 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 7:** Compare with theoretical value:
From $ρ = PM/RT$ at NTP with M = 0.016 kg/mol:

$$\rho_{CH_4,theory} = \frac{1.01325 \times 10^5 \times 0.016}{8.314 \times 273.15} = 0.7145 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

The discrepancy indicates the experimental densitometer was operating at a higher temperature or the gas was impure (a teaching point on **systematic error analysis**).

> **Final Answer**: $\rho_{CH_4} \approx 0.92 \text{ kg/m}^3$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Centrifugal (Schultz) Gas Densitometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GASIN[TEST GAS INLET] --> IMP[ROTATING IMPELLER]
    AIRIN[REFERENCE AIR INLET] --> IMP
    MOT[AC DRIVE MOTOR] --> IMP
    IMP --> CHAM[ANNULAR MEASUREMENT CHAMBER]
    CHAM --> PTAP[PRESSURE TAP AT RADIUS R]
    CHAM --> PTAP0[PRESSURE TAP AT R ZERO]
    PTAP --> DPA[DELTA P TRANSDUCER]
    PTAP0 --> DPA
    DPA --> AMP[SIGNAL AMPLIFIER]
    AMP --> DSP[MICROPROCESSOR DENSITY COMPUTATION]
    DSP --> DISP[DENSITY DISPLAY KG PER M CUBED]
    DSP --> OUT[4 TO 20 MILLIAMP OUTPUT]
    DSP --> ALM[ALARM TRIP UNIT]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Displacement-Type Gas Densitometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GASIN2[TEST GAS INLET] --> CHAM2[VERTICAL CHAMBER]
    CHAM2 --> FLOAT[BUOYANT DISPLACER FLOAT]
    FLOAT --> LVDT[LVDT POSITION SENSOR]
    LVDT --> CTRL[FEEDBACK CONTROLLER]
    CTRL --> COIL[ELECTROMAGNETIC FORCE COIL]
    COIL --> FLOAT
    CTRL --> V2V[CURRENT TO VOLTAGE CONVERTER]
    V2V --> DSP2[MICROPROCESSOR]
    DSP2 --> DISP2[DENSITY READOUT]
    DSP2 --> SG_OUT[SPECIFIC GRAVITY OUTPUT]
    CHAM2 --> VENT[EXHAUST VENT]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Vibrating Element (Tuning Fork) Densitometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GASIN3[PROCESS GAS INLET] --> FORK[QUARTZ TUNING FORK ELEMENT]
    OSC[PIEZO DRIVE OSCILLATOR] --> FORK
    FORK --> PICK[PIEZO PICKUP SENSOR]
    PICK --> FCON[FREQUENCY COUNTER]
    FCON --> CALIB[CALIBRATION LOOKUP TABLE]
    CALIB --> OUT3[DENSITY OUTPUT]
    CALIB --> TCOMP[TEMPERATURE COMPENSATION MODULE]
    TCOMP --> CALIB
    FORK --> VENT3[VENT]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Ultrasonic Gas Densitometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GASIN4[TEST GAS] --> TUBE[MEASUREMENT TUBE SECTION]
    TX[ULTRASONIC TRANSMITTER 200 KHZ] --> TUBE
    TUBE --> RX[ULTRASONIC RECEIVER]
    RX --> TIMER[TRANSIT TIME MEASUREMENT]
    TX --> TIMER
    TIMER --> SPEED[SPEED OF SOUND CALC]
    SPEED --> MOLM[MOLAR MASS COMPUTATION]
    MOLM --> DENS[IDEAL GAS DENSITY OUTPUT]
    TCOMP4[TEMPERATURE SENSOR PT100] --> SPEED
    PCOMP[PRESSURE SENSOR] --> DENS
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Generic Density Measurement Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROC[INDUSTRIAL GAS PROCESS] --> SENSE[PRIMARY SENSING ELEMENT]
    SENSE --> TRAND[TRANSDUCER STAGE]
    TRAND --> SIGC[SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    SIGC --> ADC[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERSION]
    ADC --> PROC2[MICROPROCESSOR / DSP]
    PROC2 --> HMI[HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE]
    PROC2 --> CTRL[CONTROL VALVE / ACTUATOR]
    PROC2 --> NET[FIELDBUS / ETHERNET OUTPUT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams**: When asked to "draw the block diagram of a gas densitometer," the examiner expects at least: **Sensing element → Transducer → Signal conditioner → Display**. Adding feedback and temperature compensation wins extra marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied**: All node IDs use the `flowchart` keyword, all labels contain plain uppercase alphanumeric text only (no markdown, no pipes, no asterisks inside the labels). Numerical constants like "4 TO 20 MILLIAMP" are written as words to avoid symbol conflicts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define specific gravity of a gas. How is it related to molar mass for an ideal gas?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Specific gravity (SG) of a gas is defined as the ratio of the density of the gas to the density of dry air, both measured at the **same temperature and pressure**. It is dimensionless.

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{air}}\bigg\vert_{P,T}$$

For ideal gases, $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$, so at identical P, T:

$$SG = \frac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \frac{M_{gas}}{28.97}$$

where M is in g/mol. This shows that **SG is a direct measure of molar mass**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three industrial applications of gas densitometers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Natural gas custody transfer** – billing based on mass (or energy content inferred from density).
2. **Hydrogen purity monitoring** in turbo-generator cooling circuits.
3. **Combustion control** in boilers – density of flue gas indicates excess air / fuel-air ratio.
4. (Bonus) Leak detection in sealed gas systems; medical gas quality control in hospitals.

---

---

## **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)**

---

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Centrifugal Densitometer Focus**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a centrifugal (Schultz-type) gas densitometer**. State the governing equation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A centrifugal gas densitometer has an impeller running at **3600 rpm**. At a radius of **6 cm**, the pressure difference between the gas and a reference air column is **350 Pa**. If the density of air is **1.29 kg/m³**, determine **(i)** the density of the test gas, and **(ii)** the percentage error if the rotational speed drops by 5% during measurement. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Construction & Working — 7 marks**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The Schultz gas densitometer consists of:
- A **high-speed rotating impeller** (typically 1500–5000 rpm) driven by an electric motor through a shaft.
- A **central inlet port** for the test gas.
- A **radial discharge channel** for the gas to exit after measurement.
- **Two pressure taps** — one at the center (r = 0) and one at a known radius r.
- A **differential pressure transducer** connected across these taps.
- A **signal conditioner and display** unit.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. The impeller spins at a constant angular velocity ω.
2. Test gas enters at the center.
3. Centrifugal force flings the gas radially outward, creating a pressure gradient.
4. The pressure difference between the tap at radius r and the center is measured.
5. The governing equation is:

$$\Delta P = P(r) - P(0) = \frac{1}{2} \rho \, \omega^2 r^2$$

6. By comparing ΔP with that of a reference gas (air) at the same ω and r, the gas density is computed as:

$$\rho_{gas} = \rho_{air} \cdot \frac{\Delta P_{gas}}{\Delta P_{air}}$$

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid block diagram.

---

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- N = 3600 rpm → ω = 3600 × 2π/60 = **376.99 rad/s**
- r = 0.06 m
- ΔP_gas = 350 Pa
- ρ_air = 1.29 kg/m³

**(i) Find ρ_gas (3 marks):**

We need ΔP_air at the same ω and r. Using the equation:

$$\frac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{air}} = \frac{\Delta P_{gas}}{\Delta P_{air}}$$

From the equation $\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho\omega^2 r^2$ with r = 0.06 m, ω = 376.99 rad/s:

$$\frac{1}{2}\omega^2 r^2 = 0.5 \times (376.99)^2 \times (0.06)^2$$

$$= 0.5 \times 142121 \times 0.0036 = 255.82$$

So $\Delta P_{air} = 1.29 \times 255.82 = 330.0$ Pa

Therefore:

$$\rho_{gas} = 1.29 \times \frac{350}{330.0} = 1.29 \times 1.0606 = 1.368 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(ii) Find % error if ω drops by 5% (4 marks):**

New angular velocity: $\omega' = 0.95 \omega$

The new pressure difference becomes:

$$\Delta P' = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{gas} (\omega')^2 r^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{gas} (0.95)^2 \omega^2 r^2 = (0.95)^2 \cdot \Delta P_{gas}$$

$$\Delta P' = 0.9025 \times 350 = 315.875 \text{ Pa}$$

The instrument interprets this as:

$$\rho_{gas,measured} = \rho_{air} \cdot \frac{\Delta P'}{\Delta P_{air}} = 1.29 \times \frac{315.875}{330.0} = 1.235 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Percentage error:

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{\rho_{gas,measured} - \rho_{gas,actual}}{\rho_{gas,actual}} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{1.235 - 1.368}{1.368} \times 100 = -9.72\%$$

**Note**: A 5% drop in ω causes a **9.72% error** in density reading because the error is **quadratic in ω**.

$$\boxed{\rho_{gas} \approx 1.37 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad ; \quad \%\text{ Error} \approx -9.72\%}$$

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Displacement & Vibrating Element Focus**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a diagram the **working principle of a buoyancy-type (displacement) gas densitometer**. Derive the relationship between the buoyant force and gas density. Mention its **advantages and limitations**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **vibrating cylinder gas densitometer** has an effective gas-displacement volume of $V_e = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ and a tare mass of $m_0 = 4.2 \times 10^{-3}$ kg. When evacuated, the natural frequency is $f_0 = 1200$ Hz. Find **(i)** the spring constant k, and **(ii)** the frequency when the cylinder is filled with air at NTP ($ρ_{air}$ = 1.29 kg/m³). (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Buoyancy Gas Densitometer — 7 marks**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- A vertical cylindrical chamber with gas inlet and outlet.
- A precisely machined displacer (float) — usually a hollow metallic cylinder of known mass $m_f$ and volume $V_f$.
- An LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) to sense float position.
- An electromagnetic feedback coil to maintain the float at a null position.
- Signal processing electronics for density readout.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Gas fills the chamber at controlled T and P.
2. The float experiences weight $W = m_f g$ downward and buoyant force $F_b = \rho_{gas} V_f g$ upward.
3. The net force is balanced by the electromagnetic feedback.
4. The current $I$ required to hold the float is proportional to $(\rho_0 - \rho_{gas}) V_f g$, where $\rho_0$ is the calibration gas density.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$F_b = \rho_{gas} V_f g$$

Net downward force = $m_f g - F_b = (m_f - \rho_{gas} V_f) g$

At calibration density $\rho_0$: $F_{r,0} = (m_f - \rho_0 V_f) g$

Change: $\Delta F = (\rho_0 - \rho_{gas}) V_f g$

Therefore:

$$\rho_{gas} = \rho_0 - \frac{\Delta F}{V_f g}$$

**Advantages (0.5 mark):** High accuracy, direct measurement, good for laboratory standards.
**Limitations (0.5 mark):** Slow response, sensitive to vibration and pressure fluctuations.

---

**(b) Vibrating Cylinder Densitometer — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $V_e = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
- $m_0 = 4.2 \times 10^{-3}$ kg
- $f_0 = 1200$ Hz (evacuated, so $\rho \approx 0$)
- $ρ_{air} = 1.29$ kg/m³

**(i) Find k (3 marks):**

In vacuum, $\rho V_e = 0$, so:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_0}}$$

$$k = (2\pi f_0)^2 \cdot m_0$$

$$k = (2\pi \times 1200)^2 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$k = (7539.82)^2 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$k = 5.6848 \times 10^7 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3} = 2.388 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}$$

**(ii) Find f when filled with air (4 marks):**

Effective mass with air:

$$m_{eff} = m_0 + \rho_{air} V_e = 4.2 \times 10^{-3} + 1.29 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$m_{eff} = 4.2 \times 10^{-3} + 1.935 \times 10^{-6} = 4.201935 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$$

Frequency:

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2.388 \times 10^5}{4.201935 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{5.6832 \times 10^7} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 7538.7$$

$$f = 1199.9 \text{ Hz}$$

The frequency change is **Δf = 1200 − 1199.9 = 0.1 Hz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: The frequency change is **tiny** for gases (only 0.1 Hz) because gas density is much lower than the solid mass. This is why vibrating element densitometers are calibrated with very high-precision frequency counters (resolution 0.01 Hz) and are temperature-compensated — a 1 °C drift can mask the entire signal.

---

## **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Gas Densitometer Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** — 1 mark deduction. Always: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
> 2. **Mixing up density (kg/m³) with specific gravity (dimensionless)** — 0.5 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Missing temperature/pressure compensation in numerical** — Examiners explicitly state "at NTP" to test if you can connect the ideal gas law. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **In a displacement-type question, failing to draw the LVDT/feedback loop** — at least 1 mark is reserved for the diagram.
> 5. **In derivation questions, omitting the integration step** — you must show $\int_0^r r' dr' = r^2/2$, not just jump to the answer.
> 6. **Units on the final numerical answer** — Always end with $\boxed{\rho = X \text{ kg/m}^3}$.

---

## 📌 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Points — Pin These to Your Study Wall:**

- [x] **Gas Densitometer** = instrument to measure **density (ρ) or specific gravity (SG)** of a gas.
- [x] **Specific Gravity (SG)** is **dimensionless** = $\rho_{gas}/\rho_{air}$ at same P, T.
- [x] **Reference conditions**: **NTP = 273.15 K, 1.01325 bar**, with $ρ_{air} = 1.293$ kg/m³.
- [x] **Centrifugal (Schultz) Equation**: $P(r) - P_0 = \frac{1}{2} \rho \omega^2 r^2$ — pressure rise is **quadratic in ω and r**, **linear in ρ**.
- [x] **Two-gas ratio rule**: $\rho_x / \rho_a = \Delta P_x / \Delta P_a$ — cancels ω and r.
- [x] **Displacement (float) densitometer**: $F_b = \rho_{gas} V_f g$; rest force is proportional to density deviation from calibration.
- [x] **Vibrating element**: $f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_0 + \rho V_e}}$ — used in modern Coriolis-style meters.
- [x] **Ultrasonic**: $c = \sqrt{\gamma RT/M}$ — transit time gives density indirectly.
- [x] **Optical**: $n - 1 = K \cdot \rho$ (Gladstone–Dale) — laser refractometry.
- [x] **Error in centrifugal meter is quadratic in ω** — a 5% speed error → ~10% density error.
- [x] **Industry application #1**: Natural gas custody transfer (mass-based billing).
- [x] **Industry application #2**: Hydrogen purity in turbo-generators.
- [x] **Choice rule**: Use **centrifugal** for harsh pipelines; **displacement** for lab precision; **vibrating** for modern Coriolis; **ultrasonic** for clean non-contact use.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 ESE**: When asked *"compare two densitometers"*, always build the answer around a **3-column table** (Principle / Advantages / Limitations) — examiners love structured answers and award full marks for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application and Selection, Level Measurement: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application and Selection, Level Measurement: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Level Measurement** is defined as the continuous or point measurement of the position or height of the surface of a material (liquid, solid, slurry, or interface between two fluids) within a vessel, tank, silo, or any containment structure relative to a reference datum.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, level measurement forms a critical subset of **Industrial Process Instrumentation** where the primary objective is to determine:

1. The **absolute level** (height of material surface from the bottom of the vessel).
2. The **continuous level** (real-time variation of level over time).
3. The **point level** (whether material has reached a specific preset height).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 3 of OEICT833, students must clearly distinguish between *measurement of level* versus *measurement of volume* or *mass*. The output of a level sensor is fundamentally a **distance (m)** or a **pressure (Pa)**, which can be subsequently converted to volume using vessel geometry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are boiling water in a transparent kettle on a gas stove. To know how much water is in the kettle, you simply look at it and read the markings on the side. This is the most primitive form of level measurement: **sight glass / visual gauging**.

Now, imagine the same kettle is made of **opaque steel**, installed inside a closed, dark pipeline system carrying sulfuric acid at 200°C. You can no longer "look" at the water level. You need instruments.

- **If the kettle is on a weighing scale** → the scale reading correlates to the mass of water (Weight-based / Load Cell method).
- **If you drill a small pipe at the bottom** and connect a pressure gauge → the water pressure inside tells you how high the column is (Hydrostatic Pressure method).
- **If you dip a metal rod from the top** until it touches the water, and measure the rod length dipped → this is a **Dipstick / Conductivity probe** method.
- **If you send an ultrasonic pulse from the top** and listen for the echo from the water surface → this is the **Ultrasonic Level Measurement** method.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Level measurement is essentially answering the engineering question: *"How full is the container?"* — without needing to physically open it, see into it, or empty it.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and units are universally used in KTU board exam problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (4°C) | $\rho_{water}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of mercury | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13600 kg/m³** |
| Density of typical oil | $\rho_{oil}$ | **850–920 kg/m³** |
| Hydrostatic pressure | $P_h$ | **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²** |
| Standard level unit | $h$ | **metres (m)** or **millimetres (mm)** |
| Vessel capacity | $V$ | **m³** or **litres (L)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Classification of Level Measurement Methods

KTU examiners expect students to clearly classify the wide range of level measurement techniques into a logical hierarchy. The accepted KTU board classification is as follows:

### A. Direct Level Measurement Methods
The level is read directly through mechanical or visual means.

- **Sight Glass (Gauge Glass)** — A transparent tube fitted externally to a vessel, showing the liquid level by hydrostatic equilibrium.
- **Float and Tape / Float and Shaft** — A buoyant float rides on the liquid surface and mechanically transmits the level via a tape or shaft.
- **Dip Stick / Dip Tape** — A graduated rod lowered manually until it touches the surface.
- **Conductivity / Capacitance Point Probes** — Electrodes that detect contact with a conductive liquid at a preset height.

### B. Indirect Level Measurement Methods
The level is inferred from a related physical quantity.

- **Hydrostatic Pressure Method** — Measures the pressure exerted by the liquid column at the bottom.
- **Buoyancy Method (Displacer-type)** — A displacer submerged in liquid experiences a buoyancy force proportional to submerged depth.
- **Ultrasonic Method** — Time-of-flight of sound pulses reflected from the surface.
- **Radar (Microwave) Method** — Time-of-flight of electromagnetic waves.
- **Capacitive Method** — Variation of capacitance between two electrodes due to change in dielectric (the liquid level).
- **Radiation (Nuclear) Method** — Attenuation of gamma rays between a source and detector.
- **Laser / Optical Method** — Time-of-flight of laser pulses.
- **Weight-based Method (Load Cell)** — Total mass of vessel correlates to level.
- **Differential Pressure Method** — Two pressure taps at different heights.

## 2.2 Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Principle

### 2.2.1 Hydrostatic Pressure Method
- **Why it works:** A static column of liquid exerts pressure at the bottom proportional to its height.
- **How:** A pressure sensor is mounted at the bottom of the vessel. The pressure reading directly maps to level using the formula $P = \rho g h$.
- **Limitation:** Affected by changes in liquid density (e.g., temperature-induced density variation).

### 2.2.2 Buoyancy / Displacer Method
- **Why it works:** Archimedes' Principle — A body immersed in a fluid loses weight equal to the weight of fluid displaced.
- **How:** A cylindrical displacer of constant cross-section is suspended in the liquid. As level rises, more of the displacer is submerged → more buoyancy force → apparent weight changes.
- **Advantage:** Independent of surface waves, foam, or vapour.

### 2.2.3 Ultrasonic Method
- **Why it works:** Sound waves travel at a known velocity in air/vapour and reflect off the liquid surface.
- **How:** The transit time of the echo is measured and converted to distance using $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$.
- **Limitation:** Affected by temperature (changes velocity of sound), dust, foam, and vapour.

### 2.2.4 Radar (Microwave) Method
- **Why it works:** Electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light and reflect from the dielectric discontinuity at the surface.
- **How:** Same time-of-flight principle as ultrasonic but uses GHz-frequency microwaves.
- **Advantage:** Unaffected by temperature, dust, vapour, or foam. Most accurate non-contact method.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_h = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa ($\text{N/m}^2$) | Bubbler / DP cell |
| 2 | $h = \dfrac{P_h}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Level from hydrostatic pressure | m | Pressure-based level |
| 3 | $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged}$ | Buoyancy force on displacer | N | Displacer-type sensor |
| 4 | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Distance via time-of-flight | m | Ultrasonic / Radar |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$ | Parallel plate capacitance | F | Capacitance probe |
| 6 | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$ | Differential pressure between two taps | Pa | DP transmitter |
| 7 | $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$ | Mass of liquid in vessel of cross-section $A$ | kg | Load-cell method |
| 8 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho x}$ | Gamma ray attenuation | — | Nuclear level gauge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always specify units explicitly. The examiner deducts 0.5 marks for ambiguous units. Use SI units unless otherwise stated in the problem.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Level measurement is non-negotiable in the following industries (frequently cited in KTU questions):

1. **Petrochemical & Oil Refineries** — Crude oil tanks, LPG bullets, distillation columns.
2. **Power Plants** — Boiler drum level, deaerator level, condensate tanks.
3. **Water & Wastewater Treatment** — Clarifiers, sumps, filter beds.
4. **Food & Beverage** — Milk silos, fermentation tanks, syrup storage.
5. **Pharmaceutical** — API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) reactors, sterile storage.
6. **Chemical Processing** — Acid/alkali storage, mixing vessels.
7. **Cement & Mining** — Silos, hoppers, slurry pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure — Level Relationship

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
A closed vertical cylindrical tank contains a liquid of density $\rho$. A pressure transmitter is mounted at the bottom of the tank and reads $P$. Derive the relationship between the level $h$ and the pressure $P$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a column of liquid of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$, resting on the bottom of the tank.

**Step 1:** The mass of the liquid column is given by:
$$
m = \rho \cdot A \cdot h
$$
where $\rho$ is the mass density (kg/m³), $A$ is the cross-sectional area (m²), and $h$ is the height (m).

**Step 2:** The weight (force due to gravity) of this liquid column acting on the base is:
$$
W = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g
$$
where $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (m/s²).

**Step 3:** The pressure exerted by this liquid column on the base is defined as force per unit area:
$$
P_h = \dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g}{A}
$$

**Step 4:** Cancelling $A$ from numerator and denominator:
$$
P_h = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging to express level $h$ as a function of pressure $P_h$:
$$
h = \dfrac{P_h}{\rho \cdot g}
$$

**Step 6:** Including the effect of any pre-existing pressure above the liquid (e.g., vapour pressure $P_v$ or atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$), the gauge pressure at the bottom is:
$$
P_{gauge} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h + P_{v}
$$

Therefore, the true level is:
$$
h = \dfrac{P_{gauge} - P_{v}}{\rho \cdot g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** If the problem says "open tank", then $P_v = P_{atm}$ and we use gauge pressure directly. If the problem says "pressurised tank", we must subtract the vapour pressure to get the true hydrostatic head.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Buoyancy Force on a Displacer

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A cylindrical displacer of length $L$ and uniform cross-sectional area $A_d$ is suspended vertically inside a tank containing liquid of density $\rho$. Derive the expression for buoyancy force as a function of liquid level $h$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The volume of the displacer submerged in the liquid is:
$$
V_{sub} = A_d \cdot h
$$
where $h$ is the height of the liquid level measured from the bottom tip of the displacer.

**Step 2:** By Archimedes' Principle, the buoyancy force is:
$$
F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the submerged volume:
$$
F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot h
$$

**Step 4:** The apparent weight of the displacer (as sensed by the suspension system / force balance) is:
$$
W_{apparent} = W_{displacer} - F_B
$$

**Step 5:** Since $W_{displacer} = \rho_d \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot L$ (where $\rho_d$ is the displacer material density), we get:
$$
W_{apparent} = \rho_d \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot L - \rho \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot h
$$

**Step 6:** Factoring common terms:
$$
W_{apparent} = g \cdot A_d \cdot (\rho_d \cdot L - \rho \cdot h)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** This linear relationship $F_B \propto h$ is precisely why displacer-type sensors provide an inherently linear output — a major reason for their selection in industrial applications.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ultrasonic / Radar Time-of-Flight

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement
An ultrasonic level sensor transmits a pulse downward from the top of a tank. The pulse reflects from the liquid surface and returns to the sensor after time $t$. Derive the distance formula.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The pulse travels from the sensor to the surface and back — total path length = $2d$, where $d$ is the distance from sensor to surface.

**Step 2:** Using the basic kinematic relation: distance = velocity × time
$$
2d = v \cdot t
$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $d$:
$$
d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** If $H$ is the total height of the tank from sensor to bottom, then the actual liquid level is:
$$
h = H - d = H - \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 5:** For radar (electromagnetic wave), $v = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of light). For ultrasonic in air, $v \approx 343$ m/s at 20°C, with temperature correction:
$$
v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad (\text{T in } °\text{C}, \text{ v in m/s})
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection Criteria vs. Application

The following decision matrix is a **high-yield KTU board tool** used to justify the selection of a level measurement method.

| Selection Criterion | Best Suited Method | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Corrosive liquid (acid) | Radar / Capacitance / DP | Non-contact or chemically resistant diaphragm |
| High temperature (>200°C) | Radar / DP with remote seal | Ultrasonic fails at high T |
| Foaming surface | Guided Wave Radar / Displacer | Foam absorbs/attenuates ultrasonic |
| Slurry / viscous media | Capacitance / Nuclear / DP | Ultrasonic reflects poorly off slurry |
| Hygienic (food/pharma) | Radar / Capacitance | Smooth surface, CIP-compatible |
| Point level (high/low alarm) | Float switch / Conductivity probe | Simple, low-cost binary output |
| Very large tanks (>50 m) | Radar / Ultrasonic | Long-range capability |
| Interface measurement (oil/water) | Capacitance / Nuclear / DP | Differential dielectric sensing |
| Solid level (granules/powder) | Ultrasonic / Radar / Weight | Mechanical methods clog easily |
| Hazardous / explosive area | Intrinsically safe radar / DP | No spark risk |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often recommend "ultrasonic" as a universal solution. KTU examiners explicitly test whether you recognise the **limitations** of each method. Always mention at least one limitation when justifying a choice.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:**
A closed vertical tank contains oil of density $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$. A pressure transmitter mounted at the bottom reads a gauge pressure of $P_g = 45 \text{ kPa}$. The vapour pressure above the oil is $P_v = 12 \text{ kPa}$. Determine the level of oil in the tank. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the net hydrostatic pressure head:
$$
P_h = P_g - P_v = 45 \text{ kPa} - 12 \text{ kPa} = 33 \text{ kPa}
$$
$$
P_h = 33{,}000 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the hydrostatic formula $P_h = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$:
$$
33{,}000 = 880 \times 9.81 \times h
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $h$:
$$
h = \dfrac{33{,}000}{880 \times 9.81}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator:
$$
880 \times 9.81 = 8{,}632.8
$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:
$$
h = \dfrac{33{,}000}{8{,}632.8} = 3.823 \text{ m}
$$

**Answer:** The oil level is **$h = 3.82 \text{ m}$** (rounded to two decimal places).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Level Measurement Techniques (Mermaid Diagram)

The following Mermaid flowchart provides a board-exam-ready classification of level measurement methods as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LEVEL MEASUREMENT METHODS]:::root
    A --> B[DIRECT METHODS]:::branch
    A --> C[INDIRECT METHODS]:::branch

    B --> B1[Sight Glass]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Float and Tape]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Dip Stick]:::leaf
    B --> B4[Conductivity Probe]:::leaf

    C --> D[PRESSURE BASED]:::mid
    C --> E[ELECTRICAL BASED]:::mid
    C --> F[WAVE BASED]:::mid
    C --> G[OTHER]:::mid

    D --> D1[Hydrostatic Pressure]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Differential Pressure]:::leaf

    E --> E1[Capacitance]:::leaf
    E --> E2[Resistance]:::leaf
    E --> E3[Buoyancy Displacer]:::leaf

    F --> F1[Ultrasonic]:::leaf
    F --> F2[Radar Microwave]:::leaf
    F --> F3[Laser Optical]:::leaf

    G --> G1[Nuclear Radiation]:::leaf
    G --> G2[Load Cell Weight]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b2d4a,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b2d4a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leaf fill:#deebf7,color:#000000,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Level Measurement System

This diagram illustrates the functional signal flow in a typical industrial level measurement loop — a frequent topic for KTU 14-mark theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[PROCESS VESSEL]:::proc
    S[LEVEL SENSOR TRANSDUCER]:::sensor
    T[TRANSMITTER SIGNAL CONDITIONING]:::cond
    C[CONTROLLER PID PLC DCS]:::ctrl
    I[INDICATOR HMI SCADA]:::disp
    A[ACTUATOR VALVE PUMP]:::act

    V -->|Physical Variable h| S
    S -->|mV or Microamps Raw| T
    T -->|Standard Signal 4-20 mA| C
    C -->|Decision Logic| A
    T -->|Display Signal| I
    C -->|Setpoint Compare| I

    classDef proc fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sensor fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a14e0b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cond fill:#ffc000,color:#000000,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctrl fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px
    classDef disp fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2e5984,stroke-width:2px
    classDef act fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3d1656,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Method Selection (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Contact or Non-contact?}:::decision
    Q1 -->|Contact Allowed| Q2{Liquid or Solid?}:::decision
    Q1 -->|Non-contact Required| Q3{Corrosive or Hygienic?}:::decision

    Q2 -->|Liquid| Q4{Clean or Dirty Foam?}:::decision
    Q2 -->|Solid| Q5{Dust or Granule?}:::decision

    Q4 -->|Clean| R1[DP or Buoyancy Displacer]:::rec
    Q4 -->|Foam or Vapour| R2[Guided Wave Radar]:::rec

    Q5 -->|Granule| R3[Ultrasonic or Radar]:::rec
    Q5 -->|Dust| R4[Weight Load Cell]:::rec

    Q3 -->|Yes| R5[Radar or Nuclear]:::rec
    Q3 -->|No| R6[Ultrasonic or Capacitance]:::rec

    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rec fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000,stroke:#375623,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Sensor-to-Principle Mapping Table

| Physical Principle | Sensor Type | Output Signal | Typical Range | KTU Application Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic Pressure | DP Cell / Bubbler | 4–20 mA | 0–50 m | Boiler drum, sump |
| Buoyancy | Displacer + LVDT | 4–20 mA | 0–5 m | Interface, oil-water |
| Ultrasonic | Piezoelectric | 4–20 mA | 0–60 m | Wastewater, silos |
| Radar (FMCW) | Horn antenna | 4–20 mA + HART | 0–100 m | Crude oil, LNG |
| Capacitance | Probe + RF circuit | 4–20 mA | 0–30 m | Acids, slurries, interface |
| Conductivity | Electrodes | Relay / mA | Point level | High-low alarms |
| Float | Reed switch | Relay / mA | 0–10 m | Tanks, sumps |
| Nuclear | Gamma source + Geiger | 4–20 mA | 0–10 m | Extreme hostile environments |
| Load Cell | Strain gauge | 4–20 mA / V | Full vessel | Bins, silos, weigh tanks |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define level measurement. List any four level measurement techniques used in industrial instrumentation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Level measurement is the determination of the position of the surface of a material (liquid, solid, or interface) within a vessel, expressed as a height or distance from a reference datum.

**Four Techniques (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Hydrostatic Pressure Method** — Based on the pressure exerted by the liquid column.
2. **Ultrasonic Method** — Based on time-of-flight of sound pulses reflected from the surface.
3. **Capacitance Method** — Based on change in capacitance between two electrodes.
4. **Float Method** — Based on mechanical displacement of a buoyant float riding on the surface.

*Acceptable alternatives:* Radar, Buoyancy/Displacer, Nuclear, Conductivity, Dip Stick, Sight Glass.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between direct and indirect methods of level measurement. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Direct Method | Indirect Method |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Level read directly through visual or mechanical contact | Level inferred from a related physical quantity |
| Contact with process | Usually yes | May be contact or non-contact |
| Accuracy | Generally lower | Generally higher |
| Example | Sight Glass | Ultrasonic Level Sensor |

*Alternative direct example:* Dip Stick, Float Gauge.
*Alternative indirect example:* Hydrostatic Pressure, Radar, Capacitance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the hydrostatic pressure method of level measurement with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship between level and pressure. State its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Principle (2 Marks):**
A static column of liquid exerts pressure at the bottom of the vessel which is directly proportional to the height of the column. This pressure is sensed by a pressure transmitter mounted at (or near) the bottom of the tank.

**(ii) Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
        +-------------------+
        |     VAPOUR SPACE  |  <-- Pv
        +-------------------+
        |                   |
        |   LIQUID          |
        |   (density ρ)     |
        |                   |
        |                   |
        +-----=====---------+
              |||
        +-----=====---------+      |
        | PRESSURE          |  P = ρgh + Pv
        | TRANSMITTER       |      |
        +-------------------+
        |    REFERENCE      |
        +-------------------+
```

**(iii) Derivation (2 Marks):**
For a liquid column of height $h$ and density $\rho$:
$$
P_h = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \implies h = \dfrac{P_h}{\rho \cdot g}
$$
If vapour pressure $P_v$ exists above the liquid, then:
$$
h = \dfrac{P_{gauge} - P_v}{\rho \cdot g}
$$

**(iv) Advantages (0.5 Mark):**
- Simple, robust, economical.
- Suitable for closed tanks and corrosive liquids when remote seal is used.

**(v) Limitations (0.5 Mark):**
- Affected by changes in liquid density.
- Requires pressure tap inside the tank (maintenance issue).
- Not suitable for open tanks with fluctuating atmospheric pressure.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the ultrasonic method of level measurement with the help of a block diagram. Mention its advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Principle (1.5 Marks):**
An ultrasonic transducer emits high-frequency sound pulses (typically 20–200 kHz) toward the liquid surface. The pulses reflect from the surface and return to the transducer. The time of flight $t$ is measured and converted to distance using $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$, where $v$ is the velocity of sound in air.

**(ii) Block Diagram (2.5 Marks):**

```
  +--------------+      +---------------+      +----------------+
  |  PULSE       |----->|  TRANSMITTER  |----->|  ULTRASONIC    |
  |  GENERATOR   |      |  & RECEIVER   |      |  TRANSDUCER    |
  +--------------+      +---------------+      +----------------+
                                                          |
                                                          |  Echo
                                                          v
                        +-----------+      +-------------+
                        | DISPLAY / |<-----|  TIMER &    |
                        |  4-20 mA  |      |  PROCESSOR  |
                        +-----------+      +-------------+
```

**(iii) Working (1 Mark):**
The microcontroller triggers a pulse, starts a high-precision timer, and stops the timer when the echo is received. Distance $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$. Level $h = H - d$, where $H$ is the empty-tank distance.

**(iv) Advantages (1 Mark):**
- Truly non-contact — no corrosion, no contamination.
- Suitable for corrosive, hazardous, and hygienic liquids.
- Easy installation, no moving parts.

**(v) Disadvantages (1 Mark):**
- Affected by temperature variations (changes velocity of sound).
- Cannot operate in vacuum.
- Poor performance with foam, heavy vapour, or dusty environments.
- Accuracy reduced for very far distances.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the working principle of a buoyancy-type (displacer) level sensor with a diagram. Derive the expression for apparent weight as a function of level.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Principle (1.5 Marks):**
A cylindrical displacer of constant cross-sectional area is suspended inside the tank from a force-balance mechanism. As liquid level rises, more of the displacer becomes submerged, increasing the buoyancy force and reducing its apparent weight. This weight change is measured and converted to a level reading.

**(ii) Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
        +-------------------+
        |    VAPOUR SPACE   |
        +-------------------+
        |\\\               /|
        | \\\   LIQUID   // |
        |  \\\===========//  |
        |   \\  DISPLACER//  | <-- Volume submerged = A_d * h
        |    \\==========/   |
        +------|------------+
               |
               v
        +-------------+
        |  FORCE      |
        |  BALANCE /  |--> Output (4-20 mA)
        |  LVDT       |
        +-------------+
```

**(iii) Derivation (2 Marks):**
- Volume submerged: $V_{sub} = A_d \cdot h$
- Buoyancy force: $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot h$
- True weight: $W = \rho_d \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot L$
- Apparent weight: $W_{app} = W - F_B = g \cdot A_d \cdot (\rho_d \cdot L - \rho \cdot h)$

**(iv) Features (1.5 Marks):**
- Output is inherently linear with level.
- Independent of surface waves, foam, and turbulence.
- Suitable for interface measurement (oil-water, etc.).
- Limited to clean liquids (no heavy sludge build-up on displacer).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Discuss the selection criteria for choosing a level measurement method for (i) a corrosive acid storage tank, (ii) a foaming liquid in a chemical reactor, and (iii) a solid granular silo. Justify each choice with the limitations of alternative methods.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Corrosive Acid Storage Tank (2.5 Marks):**
**Recommended Method:** **Radar (Microwave) Level Transmitter** — non-contact, chemically inert antenna (typically PTFE-coated horn).

*Why not alternatives:*
- *Ultrasonic:* Sound waves are heavily attenuated by acidic vapours and mist.
- *Dip Stick / Float:* Will corrode rapidly; unsafe for personnel.
- *Hydrostatic:* Diaphragm of pressure sensor will corrode unless exotic alloys used (very costly).
- *Capacitance:* Suitable alternative if radar is too expensive.

**(ii) Foaming Liquid in Chemical Reactor (2.5 Marks):**
**Recommended Method:** **Guided Wave Radar (GWR)** or **Capacitance Probe**.

*Why not alternatives:*
- *Ultrasonic:* Foam absorbs and scatters ultrasonic pulses — total signal loss.
- *Standard (free-space) Radar:* Foam also attenuates free-space radar signals.
- *Guided Wave Radar:* The microwave pulse travels along a probe, bypassing the foam and reflecting from the true liquid surface below the foam layer.

**(iii) Solid Granular Silo (2 Marks):**
**Recommended Method:** **Ultrasonic** or **Radar** or **Weight (Load Cell)** method.

*Why not alternatives:*
- *Float / Displacer:* Mechanically impossible — floats cannot ride on granular solids.
- *Capacitance:* Probe would be damaged by impact of falling granules.
- *Hydrostatic:* No hydrostatic head in granular solids.
- *Ultrasonic / Radar:* Non-contact — ideal for dusty silo environments where probe damage is likely.
- *Load Cell:* Most accurate for inventory management but measures total mass, not local level.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in Level Measurement Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting density in hydrostatic problems** — Writing $h = P / g$ instead of $h = P / (\rho g)$. This costs **2 full marks** in board exams.
> 2. **Not specifying gauge vs absolute pressure** — Open tank problems must use gauge pressure.
> 3. **Missing vapour pressure correction** — Closed tanks have vapour pressure above liquid; neglecting it yields wrong answers.
> 4. **Confusing the factor of 2 in time-of-flight** — Using $d = v \cdot t$ instead of $d = v \cdot t / 2$.
> 5. **Listing methods without justification** — KTU 2024 scheme examiners demand **selection with reasoning**, not just naming a method.
> 6. **Wrong unit conversions** — $1 \text{ kPa} = 1000 \text{ Pa}$, $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $1 \text{ mH}_2\text{O} = 9.81 \text{ kPa}$.
> 7. **Forgetting to state assumptions** — Always state: "Assuming constant density", "Assuming steady-state conditions", etc.
> 8. **Not drawing a labelled diagram** — Even a simple sketch scores full marks where a description alone might lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Level measurement = determining the height of a material surface in a vessel.
- **Two Broad Classes:** Direct (sight glass, float, dip stick) and Indirect (pressure, ultrasonic, radar, capacitance, etc.).
- **Hydrostatic Principle:** $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ — the most fundamental equation.
- **Buoyancy Principle:** Archimedes' Principle — buoyancy $\propto$ submerged volume.
- **Time-of-Flight Principle:** $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ — used in ultrasonic and radar.
- **Capacitance Principle:** Change in dielectric between electrodes due to liquid level change.
- **Selection Drives Application:** Corrosive → radar/capacitance; Foaming → GWR; Slurry → DP/capacitance; Solid → ultrasonic/radar/load cell.
- **Pressure Units:** $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$, $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $1 \text{ mH}_2\text{O} \approx 9.81 \text{ kPa}$.
- **Velocity of Sound in Air:** $v \approx 331 + 0.6T$ m/s (T in °C).
- **Velocity of Light (Radar):** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Density of Water:** $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 4°C.
- **Density of Mercury:** $13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — relevant in manometer-based level systems.
- **Constant to Memorise:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **Board Trap:** Always include the **factor of 2** in ultrasonic / radar distance calculations.
- **Selection Must Include Justification:** Naming a method alone is incomplete — explain *why* it's selected over alternatives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Float Level Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Float Level Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Float Level Device** is a hydrostatic level measurement instrument that operates on **Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy**, wherein a buoyant element (float or displacer) experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the submerged portion of the element. The vertical position of this buoyant element, or the force/motion it generates, is then transduced into a usable output signal (mechanical, pneumatic, electrical, or electronic) proportional to the liquid level in a vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Float-operated instruments are explicitly classified under **Direct-Liquid-Level / Buoyancy-Based Level Sensors**. They are the most common, simplest, and least expensive form of continuous and point level measurement used in chemical, petrochemical, water-treatment, and process industries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Duck in a Bathtub" Intuition

Imagine pushing a rubber duck underwater in your bathtub. The moment you release it, the duck **pops up** to the surface. The duck rises because the water *pushes it upward* with a force equal to the weight of the water that was occupying the space the duck now fills.

- If the **water level is high**, the duck is lifted higher (more of it stays above the waterline).
- If the **water level is low**, the duck settles lower (more of it submerges).
- A **heavier duck** (higher specific gravity) sits lower; a **lighter duck** sits higher.

A float level device works **exactly** like this rubber duck — except instead of letting the float bob freely, we mechanically **couple it to a pointer, a pneumatic nozzle, or an electronic sensor** so that we can *read* its position. That reading is our level measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in Industry:** The buoyant force is always expressed in **Newtons (N)**, liquid density in **kg/m³**, gravitational acceleration as **g = 9.80665 m/s²**, and displacer volume in **m³**.

## 1.3 Classification of Float Level Devices

For KTU examination purposes, the device family splits into two main branches:

| Family | Buoyant Element | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Float-Operated Devices** | Hollow/foam-filled float riding on liquid surface | Mechanical (lever/cable) |
| **Displacer-Type Devices** | Solid/heavy cylindrical displacer suspended in liquid | Pneumatic or Electronic (via Torque Tube) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Physics — Archimedes' Principle

Archimedes' Principle states that a body wholly or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an **upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body**.

Let:
- $\rho_l$ = density of the process liquid (kg/m³)
- $\rho_f$ = density of the float material (kg/m³)
- $V$ = total volume of the buoyant element (m³)
- $V_s$ = submerged volume of the buoyant element (m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.80665 m/s²**

**Step 1 — Weight of the float (downward force):**
$$W_f = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V$$

**Step 2 — Buoyant force from the displaced liquid (upward force):**
$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_s$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition** (float is stationary):
$$F_b = W_f$$

This gives the fundamental relationship that determines the float's submerged depth.

## 2.2 The Torque Tube — Heart of the Displacer Transmitter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Torque Tube** is a *high-yield KTU topic*. Examiners frequently ask about its function, construction, and why it is preferred over conventional shaft-and-gland packings for high-pressure / corrosive service.

A **torque tube** is a sealed, hollow, torsionally flexible cylinder (typically Inconel or stainless steel) that transmits angular motion from a process-wet displacer to a dry, external position-sensing mechanism (LVDT, pneumatic lever, or strain gauge). The tube is welded to a stationary process connection at one end and clamped to the displacer shaft at the other end. When the buoyant force changes, the displacer shaft twists the tube by a few degrees; the **outside end never breaks the pressure boundary**, so leakage is impossible.

## 2.3 Operating Modes of a Displacer Transmitter

1. **Buoyancy-Torque Mode** — A constant-amplitude displacer is fully submerged. As level rises, more of the displacer is covered → more buoyant force → less net downward torque on the torque tube.
2. **Force-Torque Mode (Most Common)** — A short displacer is used; the buoyant force varies linearly with level because the displacer crosses the air/liquid interface.
3. **Specific-Gravity Compensation Mode** — Two displacers of different lengths are used to mathematically cancel SG variations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical bar `\vert` is used in place of the pipe character `\|` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buoyant force on a fully submerged displacer | $F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_d$ | N (Newtons) | $V_d$ = displacer volume |
| 2 | Net force on displacer (buoyancy – weight) | $F_{net} = (\rho_l - \rho_d) \cdot g \cdot V_d$ | N | Upward if $\rho_l > \rho_d$ |
| 3 | Submerged depth of a free float | $h_s = \dfrac{W_f}{\rho_l \cdot g \cdot A_f}$ | m (metres) | $A_f$ = cross-sectional area of float |
| 4 | Level measurement error due to SG change | $\Delta h = h \left( \dfrac{\rho_d}{\rho_d - \rho_l^{new}} - \dfrac{\rho_d}{\rho_d - \rho_l^{cal}} \right)$ | m | Cal = calibration; new = operating |
| 5 | Torque transmitted by torque tube | $T = K_t \cdot \theta$ | N·m | $K_t$ = tube spring constant, $\theta$ = twist angle |
| 6 | Sensitivity (Force Mode) | $S = \Delta F / \Delta h = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot A_d$ | N/m | $A_d$ = displacer cross-section |
| 7 | Hydrostatic head (reference) | $P = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa | For DP-type comparison |

**Real-World Utility:** Float devices are the **workhorse level instruments** in storage tanks, water reservoirs, fuel tanks in power plants, and chemical reactors operating up to **600 °C** and **300 bar** with proper material selection. They are immune to foam, vapors, and dielectric variations that plague capacitance and ultrasonic sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Submerged Depth of a Free Float (Cylindrical Geometry)

**Problem Setup:** A hollow cylindrical float of mass $m_f$, outer diameter $D$, and material density $\rho_f$ is placed on the surface of a liquid of density $\rho_l$. Derive the equilibrium submerged depth $h_s$.

**Step 1 — Write the weight of the float (downward).**
$$W_f = m_f \cdot g = \rho_f \cdot V_{total} \cdot g$$

For a cylinder, $V_{total} = A_f \cdot L_f$, where $A_f = \pi D^2 / 4$ and $L_f$ is the float length. So:
$$W_f = \rho_f \cdot A_f \cdot L_f \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Write the buoyant force (upward) acting on the submerged portion of height $h_s$.**
$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged} = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot A_f \cdot h_s$$

**Step 3 — Apply Archimedes' equilibrium condition $F_b = W_f$.**
$$\rho_l \cdot g \cdot A_f \cdot h_s = \rho_f \cdot A_f \cdot L_f \cdot g$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common terms $A_f$ and $g$ from both sides.**
$$\rho_l \cdot h_s = \rho_f \cdot L_f$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the submerged depth $h_s$.**
$$h_s = \frac{\rho_f \cdot L_f}{\rho_l}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The float's *submerged depth* depends only on the *ratio of its density to the liquid's density* — its absolute size cancels out. That is why floats are designed to be hollow or foam-filled with $\rho_f \ll \rho_l$, typically $\rho_f \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$ times $\rho_l$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Buoyant Force on a Displacer (Force-Mode Operation)

**Setup:** A solid cylindrical displacer of length $L_d$, diameter $D_d$, and density $\rho_d$ is suspended vertically in a tank. The liquid level rises from the bottom of the displacer to a height $h$ along its length. Find the buoyant force.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the displacer.**
$$A_d = \frac{\pi D_d^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Submerged volume of the displacer at level $h$.**
$$V_s = A_d \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Buoyant force from Archimedes' Principle.**
$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_s = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot h$$

**Step 4 — Net force experienced by the displacer (subtract its dry weight).**
$$F_{net} = F_b - W_d = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot h - \rho_d \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot L_d$$

**Step 5 — Factor out common terms to expose the level-dependence.**
$$F_{net} = g \cdot A_d \cdot \left( \rho_l \cdot h - \rho_d \cdot L_d \right)$$

**Step 6 — Identify the linear-in-$h$ component that drives the torque tube.**
$$\boxed{\Delta F = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot \Delta h}$$

This linear relationship is what makes the displacer transmitter inherently a *linear* level-to-force converter.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Gravity (SG) Error Correction

A displacer is calibrated for a specific liquid of density $\rho_{cal}$. The actual operating liquid has density $\rho_{new}$. Show that the indicated level $h_{ind}$ is related to the true level $h_{true}$ by:

$$h_{ind} = h_{true} \cdot \frac{\rho_{new} \cdot (\rho_d - \rho_{cal})}{\rho_{cal} \cdot (\rho_d - \rho_{new})}$$

**Step 1 — Calibration condition.** The instrument is calibrated so that at the *true level* $h_{true}$ it outputs a force that the electronics interpret as $h_{ind}$:
$$\rho_{cal} \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot h_{true} = \rho_{new} \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot h_{ind}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify.**
$$\rho_{cal} \cdot h_{true} = \rho_{new} \cdot h_{ind}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $h_{ind}$.**
$$h_{ind} = h_{true} \cdot \frac{\rho_{cal}}{\rho_{new}}$$

**Step 4 — Account for the displacer's own weight term in the transmitter's force-balance equation.** A more complete form (including the weight term $\rho_d L_d$) yields:
$$h_{ind} = h_{true} \cdot \frac{\rho_{new}(\rho_d - \rho_{cal})}{\rho_{cal}(\rho_d - \rho_{new})}$$

This is the equation examiners expect for "SG-correction" problems.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A displacer-type level transmitter has a cylindrical displacer of length $L_d = 0.5$ m, diameter $D_d = 0.05$ m, and density $\rho_d = 2400$ kg/m³. The tank contains a liquid of density $\rho_l = 800$ kg/m³. Calculate the buoyant force when the liquid level is 0.3 m up the displacer. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area.**
$$A_d = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Submerged volume.**
$$V_s = A_d \cdot h = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3 = 5.8905 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Buoyant force.**
$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_s = 800 \times 9.81 \times 5.8905 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$F_b = 4.6229 \text{ N (approx.)}$$

**Step 4 — Displacer weight (dry).**
$$V_{total} = A_d \cdot L_d = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 = 9.8175 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$
$$W_d = \rho_d \cdot g \cdot V_{total} = 2400 \times 9.81 \times 9.8175 \times 10^{-4} = 23.11 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Net force (upward) on the torque tube.**
$$F_{net} = F_b - W_d = 4.6229 - 23.11 = -18.49 \text{ N (downward)}$$

The negative sign simply indicates the displacer's weight exceeds buoyancy at this partial immersion — a normal, expected operating condition that the torque tube senses as a *reduced* downward load.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Displacer-Type Level Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tankA[Tank Process Liquid] --> displacerX[Displacer Buoyant Element]
    displacerX --> torqueTubeX[Sealed Torque Tube]
    torqueTubeX --> couplingX[Hermetic Coupling Flange]
    couplingX --> sensorX[Position Sensor LVDT or Hall]
    sensorX --> signalX[Signal Conditioning Electronics]
    signalX --> outputX[4 to 20 mA Current Loop Output]
    outputX --> dcsX[DCS PLC or Recorder]
    displacerX -.-> buoyantForceX[(Buoyant Force Proportional to Level)]
```

## 4.2 Operating Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startX([Process Tank Active]) --> levelRiseX[Liquid Level Changes]
    levelRiseX --> subVolumeX[Submerged Volume of Displacer Varies]
    subVolumeX --> archX[Archimedes Buoyant Force Updates]
    archX --> torqueX[Torque Tube Twists by Angle Theta]
    torqueX --> lvdtsenseX[LVDT Core Displaces]
    lvdtsenseX --> econdX[Electronics Convert to 4-20 mA]
    econdX --> displayX[Operator Reads Level on DCS HMI]
    displayX --> endX([Measurement Cycle Repeats])
```

## 4.3 Functional Subsystem Map of a Float-Operated System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tank1[Open or Closed Tank] --> float1[Surface Float Sphere or Cylinder]
    float1 --> arm1[Rigid Lever Arm or Flexible Cable]
    arm1 --> pointer1[External Indicator Dial]
    arm1 --> sw1[Reed Switch Array for Point Level]
    sw1 --> alarm1[High or Low Level Alarm]
    pointer1 --> op1[Operator Visual Reading]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams are shown as block-flow:** Mermaid cannot render true physical cross-sections of instruments (e.g., torque tube wall thickness, displacer welding detail). For KTU board answers, examiners award full marks to a *labelled schematic* drawn on paper — the diagrams above give you the correct signal-flow logic to label such a sketch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Archimedes' Principle. How is it applied in float-type level measurement devices?**

**Model Answer:**
Archimedes' Principle states that a body wholly or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the submerged portion of the body. In float-type level devices, a buoyant element (float or displacer) is placed on or suspended in the process liquid. As the level rises, the buoyant force changes, causing the float to rise or the torque tube of a displacer to twist. This mechanical motion is then converted by a transduction element (lever, LVDT, pneumatic nozzle) into a readable output proportional to the liquid level. (3 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a torque tube? Why is it preferred over packed-gland sealing in displacer level transmitters?**

**Model Answer:**
A torque tube is a sealed, torsionally flexible cylinder welded to the process vessel at one end and clamped to the displacer shaft at the other. It transmits angular motion from the wet displacer to the dry external sensor without breaking the pressure boundary.
