Given A₀ = 8000, A = 1000, t = 6 hr. $$1000 = 8000 \, e^{-6\lambda}$$ $$\frac{1000}{8000} = e^{-6\lambda}$$ $$0.125 = e^{-6\lambda}$$ Take natural logarithm: $$\ln(0.125) = -6\lambda$$ $$-2.0794 = -6\lambda$$ $$\boxed{\lambda = 0.3466 \text{ hr}^{-1}}$$ **(b) Half-Life:** $$T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.3466} = \boxed{2.0 \text{ hours}}$$ **(c) Activity After 12 Hours:** $$A = 8000 \, e^{-0.3466 \times 12}$$ $$A = 8000 \, e^{-4.1592}$$ $$A = 8000 \times 0.01562$$ $$\boxed{A \approx 125 \text{ disintegrations/sec}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification by half-life logic:** 12 hours = 6 half-lives (since T₁/₂ = 2 hr). > Activity = 8000 / 2⁶ = 8000 / 64 = 125 ✓ (matches!) ### 3.3 Beer-Lambert Law Application (Level Measurement Derivation) **Setup:** A gamma source and detector are placed on opposite sides of a vessel. - When the vessel is **empty**, the intensity reaching the detector is `I_max`. - When the vessel is **full** of material, the intensity is `I_min = I_max · e^{-μx}`. - For partial fill at height `h` of total height `H`: $$I = I_{max} \cdot e^{-\mu h} \quad \text{(filled portion only)}$$ $$I_{total} = I_{max} \cdot e^{-\mu h} \cdot e^{-\mu (H-h)/K}$$ where K accounts for the empty portion (air, K ≈ 1). By measuring I, the level `h` can be computed as: $$h = -\frac{1}{\mu} \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_{max}}\right)$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Radiation-Based Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Radioactive Source
Co-60 or Cs-137] --> B[Primary Collimator
Beam Shaper] B --> C[Process Vessel
Pipe or Tank] C --> D[Secondary Collimator
Beam Focuser] D --> E[Radiation Detector
GM Tube or Scintillator] E --> F[Amplifier and
Pulse Shaper] F --> G[Signal Processing
Unit Microcontroller] G --> H[Display and
Control Output] style A fill:#ff9999 style E fill:#99ccff style H fill:#99ff99 ``` ### 4.2 Decision Flow — Detector Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Select Radiation Detector] --> Q1{Measurement Type?} Q1 -->|Level in closed tank| L1[Use Scintillation Detector
with Cs-137 source] Q1 -->|Thickness gauging| L2[Use GM Counter
with Beta source] Q1 -->|Density of slurry| L3[Use Ionization Chamber
with Co-60 source] Q1 -->|Moisture content| L4[Use Neutron Detector
with Am-Be source] Q1 -->|Smoke detection| L5[Use Ionization Chamber
with Am-241 source] L1 --> Safety[Apply ALARA Principle:
Time, Distance, Shielding] L2 --> Safety L3 --> Safety L4 --> Safety L5 --> Safety style Start fill:#ffcc66 style Safety fill:#ff6666,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Radiation Penetration Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Radioactive Source] --> P1[Paper Sheet] P1 --> ALPHA[Alpha Absorbed
Low Penetration] SRC --> P2[Aluminium Plate 5mm] P2 --> BETA[Beta Absorbed
Moderate Penetration] SRC --> P3[Lead Shield 5cm] P3 --> GAMMA[Gamma Partially
Absorbed High Energy] SRC --> P4[Concrete 1m] P4 --> NEUT[Neutron Attenuated
by Hydrogen Rich Material] style SRC fill:#ff9999 style ALPHA fill:#ffcccc style BETA fill:#ffddcc style GAMMA fill:#ffeedd style NEUT fill:#ffeeff ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Typical Radiation Gauge ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Source_Assembly SA1[Isotope Capsule] --> SA2[Lead Shield] SA2 --> SA3[Collimator] end subgraph Process_Zone SA3 --> PZ1[Material Under Test] PZ1 --> PZ2[Transmitted or
Scattered Beam] end subgraph Detection_Assembly PZ2 --> DA1[Detector Crystal
or Gas Tube] DA1 --> DA2[Photomultiplier
or Anode] DA2 --> DA3[Pulse Amplifier] DA3 --> DA4[Discriminator] DA4 --> DA5[Counter or
Rate Meter] end subgraph Output_Stage DA5 --> OS1[Microprocessor] OS1 --> OS2[Display] OS1 --> OS3[4-20 mA Output] OS1 --> OS4[Alarm Relay] end style Source_Assembly fill:#e6f3ff style Process_Zone fill:#fff4e6 style Detection_Assembly fill:#e6ffe6 style Output_Stage fill:#ffe6f3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define radioactive decay constant and half-life. Establish the relationship between them.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Decay Constant (λ):** It is the probability per unit time that a given radioactive nucleus will decay. Its SI unit is **s⁻¹** (per second). **Half-Life (T₁/₂):** It is the time required for the activity (or number of undecayed nuclei) of a radioactive sample to fall to **half of its initial value**. **Relationship Derivation:** At t = T₁/₂, we have A = A₀/2. Substituting into the decay law: $$\frac{A_0}{2} = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$ $$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} \implies \ln 2 = \lambda T_{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}}$$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between Becquerel and Curie as units of radioactivity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Becquerel (Bq) | Curie (Ci) | |-----------|----------------|------------| | **System** | SI Unit | Non-SI (older) | | **Definition** | 1 disintegration per second | Activity of 1 gram of Ra-226 | | **Magnitude** | Very small unit | Very large unit | | **Conversion** | 1 Bq = 1 decay/sec | 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq | $$\boxed{1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}}$$ --- ### 📗 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (With Internal Choice) --- #### ✏️ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **gamma-ray level measurement system** used in closed vessels. State any two industrial applications. **Model Solution:** A gamma-ray level measurement system consists of three main parts: **1. Source Assembly:** A sealed radioactive source (typically **Cs-137** with T₁/₂ = 30 years or **Co-60** with T₁/₂ = 5.27 years) is housed inside a lead shield with a small collimated opening. The collimator directs a narrow beam of gamma radiation toward the vessel. **2. Process Vessel (Measurement Zone):** The vessel contains the process material whose level is to be measured. Gamma rays from the source pass through the vessel wall and material. **3. Detector Assembly:** A **scintillation detector** (NaI crystal + photomultiplier tube) or **Geiger-Müller tube** is mounted diametrically opposite the source. **Working Principle:** The transmitted gamma intensity depends on whether the beam path passes through **liquid/material** or **vapour/air**: - If path is through **vapour** (low density): High intensity `I_high` reaches the detector - If path is through **material** (high density): Low intensity `I_low` reaches the detector (Beer-Lambert law) $$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \rho x}$$ The detector output is fed to a **rate meter** that converts pulses per second into a level signal (4–20 mA). **Two Industrial Applications:** 1. Measurement of molten metal level in steel-making furnaces. 2. Level measurement in pressurized chemical reactors where no contact is permitted. --- **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A radioactive source has a half-life of 8 days. Calculate: - (i) The decay constant - (ii) The activity after 32 days, if the initial activity is 2 × 10⁶ Bq - (iii) The time required for the activity to fall to 5% of its initial value **Model Solution:** **Given:** T₁/₂ = 8 days, A₀ = 2 × 10⁶ Bq **(i) Decay Constant:** $$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{8 \text{ days}}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda = 0.0866 \text{ per day}}$$ **(ii) Activity after 32 days:** Note: 32 days = 4 half-lives (since 32/8 = 4) Using the formula: $A = A_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{t/T_{1/2}}$ $$A = 2 \times 10^6 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{32/8} = 2 \times 10^6 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4$$ $$A = 2 \times 10^6 \times \frac{1}{16}$$ $$\boxed{A = 1.25 \times 10^5 \text{ Bq}}$$ **(iii) Time for activity to fall to 5%:** Set A = 0.05 × A₀: $$0.05 \, A_0 = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$ $$0.05 = e^{-0.0866 t}$$ Take natural log: $$\ln(0.05) = -0.0866 \, t$$ $$-2.9957 = -0.0866 \, t$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 34.6 \text{ days}}$$ --- #### ✏️ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the construction and working of a **Geiger-Müller (GM) counter**. Draw its characteristic curve and identify the different voltage regions. **Model Solution:** **Construction:** A GM tube is a gas-filled (usually Argon + Halogen quench gas) cylindrical tube with: - A **central anode wire** (tungsten) at high positive potential - A **cylindrical cathode** (conducting inner wall) at ground potential - **Insulating end caps** (mica window) to allow radiation entry **Working Principle:** 1. Ionizing radiation enters through the thin mica window. 2. It produces primary ion pairs (electron + positive ion) in the gas. 3. The high potential difference (typically 600–1000 V) accelerates the electrons toward the anode. 4. The accelerated electrons cause **secondary ionization** (Townsend avalanche), producing a large measurable pulse. 5. The **quench gas** (halogen) prevents continuous discharge by absorbing UV photons and neutralizing positive ions. 6. Each detected radiation event produces one output pulse → **counting mode operation**. **Characteristic Curve — Voltage Regions:** | Region | Voltage Range | Behavior | |--------|---------------|----------| | **I — Recombination** | Low voltage | Ions recombine; no measurable pulse | | **II — Ionization Chamber** | Moderate | Pulse size depends on radiation energy | | **III — Proportional** | Higher | Pulse size ∝ energy; linear amplification | | **IV — Geiger-Müller (Plateau)** | 600–1000 V | Pulse size **independent** of energy; GM operation | | **V — Continuous Discharge** | Excessive | Damage to tube (avoid!) | **Operating Point:** Selected in the middle of the **plateau** region for stable operation. --- **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the principle of **neutron moisture measurement**. Why is hydrogen content critical in this measurement? **Model Solution:** **Principle:** When fast neutrons (from an **Am-Be source**, half-life = 432 years) collide with light nuclei (especially **hydrogen**), they lose energy rapidly and become **slow (thermal) neutrons**. This is called **neutron moderation** or **neutron scattering**. **Setup:** - A neutron source (Am-Be) and a **slow neutron detector** (BF₃ proportional counter or He-3 tube) are placed on the same side of the material, or embedded in it. - The detector is shielded from direct source neutrons by a layer of **cadmium** or **paraffin** that absorbs slow neutrons. **Working:** - Hydrogen atoms in water (H₂O) have nearly the same mass as neutrons, so collisions are **elastic and maximally energy-transferring** (similar to billiard balls of equal mass). - Materials with **more hydrogen** (more moisture) produce more slow neutrons. - The slow neutron count rate is proportional to the **hydrogen/moisture content**. **Why Hydrogen is Critical:** - The **mass of hydrogen (1 amu) ≈ mass of neutron (1 amu)** - Maximum energy transfer occurs when colliding masses are equal (per conservation of momentum & kinetic energy) - Hydrogen scatters neutrons most efficiently (largest energy loss per collision) - Therefore, slow neutron count is a direct indicator of hydrogen density = moisture content **Applications:** Soil moisture in agriculture, moisture in coke, sand, cement clinker, and foundry materials. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing Bq with Ci:** Students write "Curie = Becquerel" — WRONG. Always show conversion: **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq**. > 2. **Forgetting units of λ:** Always state "λ = 0.0866 **per day**" or **hr⁻¹** — not just a number. > 3. **Using wrong formula:** Half-life `T₁/₂ = 0.693/λ` — students often write `0.5/λ` (this is **mean life**, not half-life). > 4. **Drawing GM characteristic curve incorrectly:** The plateau region must be flat — students often draw a continually rising curve. > 5. **Mixing up α, β, γ penetration:** Alpha is **stopped by paper**, not lead. Beta is stopped by **aluminium**, gamma needs **lead/concrete**. > 6. **Skipping the negative sign in decay law:** Always write `N = N₀ e^{-λt}` (with negative exponent). Without it, activity would increase — physically impossible. > 7. **Beer-Lambert misuse:** Some students write `I = I₀ e^{μx}` (positive exponent) — this implies amplification, not attenuation. --- ### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Radiation (Module 3)** - ✅ **Decay Law:** $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ - ✅ **Half-Life:** $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ (memorize the 0.693 constant) - ✅ **Mean Life:** $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.443 \cdot T_{1/2}$ - ✅ **Activity Relation:** $A = \lambda N$ (always, not just at t=0) - ✅ **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — heart of all industrial radiation measurements - ✅ **Linear vs Mass Absorption:** $\mu = \mu_m \cdot \rho$ - ✅ **Unit Conversions (mandatory):** - 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq - 1 Gy = 100 rad - 1 Sv = 100 rem - 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg - ✅ **Three Common Industrial Sources:** - **Cs-137** → Level gauges (T₁/₂ = 30 yr) - **Co-60** → Density/thickness (T₁/₂ = 5.27 yr) - **Am-241** → Smoke detectors (T₁/₂ = 432 yr) - ✅ **Detector Selection:** - **Ionization Chamber** → high radiation fields, dosimetry - **Proportional Counter** → energy discrimination needed - **GM Counter** → simple counting (plateau region operation) - **Scintillation Detector** → high sensitivity, fast timing - ✅ **Neutron Moderation:** Hydrogen-rich materials (water) slow down neutrons most efficiently → moisture measurement - ✅ **Radiation Safety (ALARA):** **Time** (minimize exposure), **Distance** (inverse-square law), **Shielding** (Pb for γ, paraffin for n) - ✅ **Penetration Hierarchy:** α < β < γ ≈ X-rays < neutrons

topics.title: Conductivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Conductivity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Electrical Conductivity (κ or σ)** is a fundamental physical property of a material that quantifies its ability to conduct an electric current. In the context of **Industrial Instrumentation**, conductivity specifically refers to the measurement of the ability of an **electrolyte solution** (such as water, acids, bases, or salts dissolved in liquids) to carry an electric current via the migration of ions. Mathematically, conductivity is defined as the **reciprocal of electrical resistivity (ρ)**: $$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In industrial practice, conductivity is the most widely used and reliable indicator of the **total dissolved solids (TDS)** or **ionic concentration** in a process liquid. It is a non-specific measurement — it cannot identify *which* ions are present, only *how many* ions are carrying charge. ## 1.2 Units & Standard Metrics The standard SI unit of electrical conductivity is: - **Siemens per meter (S/m)** — the SI base unit - **milliSiemens per centimeter (mS/cm)** — commonly used in water and process industries - **microSiemens per centimeter (µS/cm)** — used for high-purity water measurement **Conversion factors (board-favorite):** $$1 \text{ S/m} = 10 \text{ mS/cm} = 10{,}000 \text{ µS/cm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conductance (G)** is measured in **Siemens (S)** — it depends on the geometry of the measuring cell. **Conductivity (κ)** is an *intrinsic* property of the solution, independent of geometry. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Pipes and Water" Imagine a wide water pipe carrying flow: - A **wide pipe with smooth walls** → water flows easily → **high conductivity** - A **narrow pipe with rough walls** → water struggles to pass → **low conductivity** In electrical terms: - **Ions** = the flowing water (charge carriers) - **Voltage** = water pressure (driving force) - **Current** = water flow rate - **Solution resistance** = pipe resistance - **Conductivity** = how easily the solution allows current to pass > [!TIP] > Pure water is actually a **very poor conductor** because it has very few free ions. It is the **dissolved salts, acids, and alkalis** that give water its conductive properties. This is why conductivity is a perfect proxy for water purity! ## 1.4 The Three Conductivity Quantities | Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Common Unit | |----------|--------|------------|-------------| | Specific (Electrolytic) Conductivity | κ | Conductance of 1 cm³ of solution | S/cm, S/m | | Molar Conductivity | Λₘ | Conductivity per mole of electrolyte | S·cm²/mol | | Equivalent Conductivity | Λₑ | Conductivity per equivalent of electrolyte | S·cm²/equivalent | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Basis of Ionic Conduction When two electrodes are immersed in an electrolyte and a potential difference is applied: 1. **Cations** (positively charged ions, e.g., Na⁺, Ca²⁺) migrate toward the **cathode** (negative electrode). 2. **Anions** (negatively charged ions, e.g., Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻) migrate toward the **anode** (positive electrode). 3. The **net transfer of charge** through the solution constitutes the electric current. > [!IMPORTANT] > The current is carried **entirely by the ions**, not by electrons (unlike metals). This is the defining characteristic of electrolytic conduction. The total current depends on: - **Concentration of ions** (more ions → more carriers → higher current) - **Mobility of ions** (how fast they move under the electric field) - **Valence of ions** (ions carrying 2+ charges contribute twice the current) - **Temperature** (higher temperature → higher ion mobility → higher conductivity) ## 2.2 Ohm's Law Applied to Electrolytes For an electrolyte solution, Ohm's Law holds in the form: $$I = \frac{V}{R_{solution}}$$ where $R_{solution}$ is the resistance of the liquid column between the electrodes. ## 2.3 The Cell Constant (K_c or θ) — KTU Board Favorite The **Cell Constant** is the geometric property of a conductivity cell that converts measured **conductance (G)** into the intrinsic **conductivity (κ)**. $$K_c = \frac{l}{A}$$ where: - $l$ = distance between the two electrodes (m or cm) - $A$ = cross-sectional area of the electrolyte between the electrodes (m² or cm²) **The Fundamental Conductivity Equation (must memorize):** $$\boxed{\kappa = G \times K_c = \frac{K_c}{R_{solution}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is Cell Constant important?** > You cannot measure conductivity directly with a probe — you measure **resistance (R)**. To convert this resistance into the intrinsic conductivity of the solution, you must multiply by the geometry factor of your specific cell. The cell constant is determined by calibration using a standard KCl solution of known conductivity. ## 2.4 Molar and Equivalent Conductivity **Molar Conductivity** relates the conductivity to the molar concentration of the electrolyte: $$\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa \times 1000}{C} \quad \text{(with κ in S/cm, C in mol/L)}$$ **Equivalent Conductivity** relates conductivity to the normality (equivalent concentration): $$\Lambda_e = \frac{\kappa \times 1000}{N}$$ where $N = Z \times C$ (Z = total positive or negative charge per formula unit). > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Pattern:** Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions states that at infinite dilution, the molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of the contributions from its constituent ions: > $$\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \nu_+ \lambda_+^{\circ} + \nu_- \lambda_-^{\circ}$$ ## 2.5 Effect of Temperature Conductivity is **strongly temperature dependent**. For most aqueous solutions, conductivity increases by approximately **2% per °C** (typical range: 1.5% – 2.5% per °C). $$\kappa_T = \kappa_{25}[1 + \alpha(T - 25)]$$ where: - $\kappa_{25}$ = conductivity at reference temperature 25°C - $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient (≈ 0.019 /°C for many salts) - $T$ = actual temperature in °C > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** In industrial transmitters, this temperature effect is automatically compensated using a built-in **PT100 or PT1000 RTD** in the probe. Without compensation, a 10°C rise would falsely indicate a 20% increase in concentration! ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Standard Units | |---|---------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Conductivity = reciprocal of resistivity | S/m | | 2 | $G = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Conductance = reciprocal of resistance | S (Siemens) | | 3 | $K_c = \dfrac{l}{A}$ | Cell constant = length / area | cm⁻¹ or m⁻¹ | | 4 | $\kappa = G \times K_c$ | Conductivity from conductance | S/cm or S/m | | 5 | $\Lambda_m = \dfrac{1000 \, \kappa}{C}$ | Molar conductivity | S·cm²/mol | | 6 | $\Lambda_e = \dfrac{1000 \, \kappa}{N}$ | Equivalent conductivity | S·cm²/eq | | 7 | $\kappa_T = \kappa_{25}[1 + \alpha(T-25)]$ | Temperature compensation | α in /°C | | 8 | $\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \nu_+ \lambda_+^{\circ} + \nu_- \lambda_-^{\circ}$ | Kohlrausch's law | S·cm²/mol | | 9 | $\% \text{ TDS} \approx 0.5 \times \kappa \text{ (in mS/cm)}$ | Approx. TDS from conductivity | ppm | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Water Treatment Plants** — monitoring purity of deionized/RO water 2. **Chemical Process Control** — acid/alkali concentration in pickling baths 3. **Pharmaceutical Industry** — clean-in-place (CIP) verification, WFI (Water for Injection) quality 4. **Food & Beverage** — checking milk freshness, juice concentration, brewing 5. **Power Plants** — boiler feedwater and condensate monitoring 6. **HVAC & Cooling Towers** — controlling cycles of concentration to prevent scaling 7. **Environmental Monitoring** — river/sea water quality, pollution tracking --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Resistance to Conductivity **Starting point:** Consider a block-shaped volume of electrolyte between two parallel plate electrodes. **Step 1 — Set up the geometry:** Let the electrodes be parallel plates of area $A$, separated by distance $l$. The volume of electrolyte between the plates is $V = A \times l$. **Step 2 — Apply the definition of resistance:** For any uniform conductor (including an electrolyte column), the resistance is: $$R = \rho \times \frac{l}{A}$$ where $\rho$ is the resistivity of the electrolyte. **Step 3 — Substitute the definition of conductivity:** Since $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$, we can write $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\kappa}$. Substituting into the resistance equation: $$R = \frac{1}{\kappa} \times \frac{l}{A}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for conductivity:** $$\kappa = \frac{l}{A} \times \frac{1}{R}$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the cell constant:** We define the cell constant as $K_c = \dfrac{l}{A}$. Therefore: $$\boxed{\kappa = \frac{K_c}{R} = K_c \times G}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The cell constant is essentially a "geometric amplifier" that converts the *geometry-dependent* resistance measurement into the *intrinsic* conductivity of the solution. Cells with small electrodes far apart have a large $K_c$ (good for low-conductivity measurement), while cells with large electrodes close together have a small $K_c$ (good for high-conductivity measurement). --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** A conductivity cell has electrodes of area 4 cm² separated by a distance of 2 cm. When filled with a 0.01 M KCl solution, the measured resistance is 250 Ω. Calculate: (a) The cell constant (b) The specific conductivity of the solution (c) The molar conductivity of the solution **Given:** - Area $A = 4 \text{ cm}^2$ - Length $l = 2 \text{ cm}$ - Resistance $R = 250 \text{ }\Omega$ - Concentration $C = 0.01 \text{ mol/L}$ ### Part (a): Cell Constant $$K_c = \frac{l}{A} = \frac{2 \text{ cm}}{4 \text{ cm}^2}$$ $$\boxed{K_c = 0.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$ ### Part (b): Specific Conductivity $$\kappa = \frac{K_c}{R} = \frac{0.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}}{250 \text{ }\Omega}$$ $$\boxed{\kappa = 0.002 \text{ S/cm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S/cm} = 2 \text{ mS/cm}}$$ ### Part (c): Molar Conductivity Using the formula $\Lambda_m = \dfrac{1000 \times \kappa}{C}$: $$\Lambda_m = \frac{1000 \times 0.002}{0.01}$$ $$\Lambda_m = \frac{2}{0.01}$$ $$\boxed{\Lambda_m = 200 \text{ S}\cdot\text{cm}^2/\text{mol}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Quick Sanity Check:** This value is in the expected range for dilute KCl (literature value at 25°C ≈ 141 S·cm²/mol for 0.01 M, with our simplified assumption). The order of magnitude confirms our calculation is reasonable. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Temperature Compensation Equation **Why is compensation needed?** Ion mobility in solution increases with temperature because: - Viscosity of water decreases → ions move more freely - Thermal energy increases → ions overcome drag forces more easily **Empirical model (linear approximation):** Starting from the relationship $\kappa_T = \kappa_{T_{ref}}[1 + \alpha(T - T_{ref})]$: **Step 1 — Define reference temperature** $T_{ref} = 25°C$ (KTU standard reference). **Step 2 — Express measured conductivity at process temperature $T_{process}$:** $$\kappa_{measured} = \kappa_{25}[1 + \alpha(T_{process} - 25)]$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the reference conductivity (what we want to report):** $$\kappa_{25} = \frac{\kappa_{measured}}{1 + \alpha(T_{process} - 25)}$$ **Example:** If measured conductivity at 35°C is 1.50 mS/cm and α = 0.02/°C: $$\kappa_{25} = \frac{1.50}{1 + 0.02 \times (35 - 25)} = \frac{1.50}{1 + 0.20} = \frac{1.50}{1.20}$$ $$\boxed{\kappa_{25} = 1.25 \text{ mS/cm}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This means: **without compensation**, the operator would have reported 1.50 mS/cm and taken some action (e.g., dosing chemicals) that would have been wrong. With compensation, the true value is correctly identified as 1.25 mS/cm. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Conductivity Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Process Liquid
Electrolyte Solution] --> B[Conductivity Cell
Two Electrodes in Solution] B --> C[Excitation Source
AC Voltage 50-1000 Hz] C --> D[Signal Conditioning
Rectifier and Amplifier] D --> E[Temperature Sensor
PT100 or PT1000 RTD] E --> F[Microprocessor
Temperature Compensation] F --> G[Display Unit
LCD or mA Output] G --> H[4-20 mA Current Loop
or HART Protocol] H --> I[PLC or DCS
Control Room] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style C fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900 style D fill:#ffe1f5,stroke:#cc0066 style E fill:#f5e1ff,stroke:#6600cc style F fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 style G fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399 style H fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style I fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why AC excitation?** A direct current (DC) would cause **electrolysis** at the electrodes, leading to polarization (gas bubbles and metal deposition) that fouls the electrodes and corrupts the reading. An alternating current reverses polarity continuously, preventing net electrochemical reactions at the electrode surface. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Conductivity Cell Construction ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph EC[Conductivity Probe Assembly] E1[Electrode 1
Platinum or Graphite] E2[Electrode 2
Platinum or Graphite] P[Probe Body
Epoxy or PVDF] T[Built-in Temperature
Sensor PT100] C[Connection Cable
to Transmitter] end FL[Flowing Process Liquid] --> E1 E1 <--> E2 E1 -.-> P E2 -.-> P T -.-> P P --> C style EC fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#000080 style E1 fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#8b4513 style E2 fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#8b4513 style T fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#4b0082 ``` --- ## 4.3 Functional Flow — Conductivity Measurement Process ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Immerse Cell
in Process Liquid] --> S2[Step 2: Apply AC
Excitation Voltage] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Measure AC
Current Flowing Through Solution] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate
Resistance R = V/I] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Multiply by
Cell Constant Kc] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Apply Temperature
Compensation using RTD] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Display κ
in S/cm or mS/cm] style S1 fill:#cce5ff style S2 fill:#cce5ff style S3 fill:#cce5ff style S4 fill:#ffffcc style S5 fill:#ffffcc style S6 fill:#ffe1cc style S7 fill:#ccffcc ``` --- ## 4.4 Comparison: Two-Electrode vs Four-Electrode Cell ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph TWO[Two-Electrode Cell] T1[Current Electrode 1] --> T2[Solution Volume] --> T3[Current Electrode 2] end subgraph FOUR[Four-Electrode Cell] F1[Current Electrode C1] --> F2[Solution Volume] --> F3[Current Electrode C2] F2 -.-> F4[Voltage Sense S1] --> F5[High Impedance Voltmeter] --> F6[Voltage Sense S2] end style TWO fill:#ffe6e6 style FOUR fill:#e6ffe6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Two-electrode cell:** Simpler, but suffers from polarization effects at high conductivities. Best for low-to-medium range (pure water, drinking water). > > **Four-electrode cell:** Uses two outer electrodes to drive current and two inner electrodes to *sense* voltage with high-impedance circuitry. Eliminates polarization errors. Best for high conductivities (acids, brines, concentrated chemicals). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define specific conductivity and molar conductivity. State their units.** **Model Answer:** **Specific (Electrolytic) Conductivity (κ):** It is defined as the conductance of a 1 cm³ (or 1 m³) cube of the electrolyte solution placed between two electrodes of unit area separated by unit distance. It is the reciprocal of resistivity. $$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho} = G \times \frac{l}{A}$$ **Units:** Siemens per meter (S/m) in SI system, or Siemens per centimeter (S/cm) in CGS/practical usage. **Molar Conductivity (Λₘ):** It is defined as the conducting power of all the ions produced by one mole of an electrolyte in a solution. It is the specific conductivity divided by the molar concentration. $$\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa \times 1000}{C}$$ **Units:** Siemens·cm² per mole (S·cm²/mol) --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is cell constant? Why is it used in conductivity measurement?** **Model Answer:** **Cell Constant ($K_c$):** It is defined as the ratio of the distance between the electrodes ($l$) to the area of the electrodes ($A$) in a conductivity cell. $$K_c = \frac{l}{A}$$ **Why it is used:** The conductance $G$ measured by the cell is a *geometry-dependent* quantity, while the specific conductivity $\kappa$ is an *intrinsic* property of the solution. The cell constant converts the measured conductance into the intrinsic conductivity: $$\kappa = K_c \times G = \frac{K_c}{R}$$ The cell constant is determined experimentally by calibrating with a standard KCl solution of known conductivity. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an industrial conductivity measurement system. Discuss the role of AC excitation and temperature compensation. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** A conductivity cell has a cell constant of 1.2 cm⁻¹. The measured resistance of a solution at 25°C is 500 Ω. If the temperature coefficient is 0.02/°C, find the conductivity at 30°C and 25°C. Also calculate the molar conductivity if the solution concentration is 0.05 M. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a): **Working of Industrial Conductivity Measurement System:** The block diagram consists of the following stages: 1. **Conductivity Cell (Sensor):** Two electrodes (Pt, graphite, or Ti) are immersed in the process liquid. A standard KCl calibration determines the cell constant. 2. **AC Excitation Source:** A sine wave oscillator generates an AC voltage (typically 50 Hz to 1000 Hz, often 1 kHz) of low amplitude (~1V RMS). - **Why AC and not DC:** DC causes electrolysis — water splits into H₂ and O₂ gas bubbles on the electrodes, which (i) coats the electrode surface, (ii) changes the effective electrode area, and (iii) introduces a counter-EMF (polarization voltage) that distorts the measurement. AC polarity reversal prevents net electrochemical reaction. 3. **Signal Conditioning Circuit:** The AC current flowing through the cell is converted to a proportional AC voltage using a precision resistor. This is then **rectified** (precision full-wave rectifier) and **amplified** to a standard DC level. 4. **Temperature Sensor (RTD):** A PT100 or PT1000 platinum RTD is integrated into the probe body to measure the process temperature in real time. 5. **Microprocessor for Compensation:** The μP reads both the conductivity signal and the temperature signal, then applies the equation: $$\kappa_{25} = \frac{\kappa_{measured}}{1 + \alpha(T - 25)}$$ 6. **Output Stage:** The compensated reading is displayed on a local LCD and transmitted as a **4-20 mA current loop** (with HART protocol overlay for digital communication) to the PLC/DCS. **Role of AC Excitation:** Prevents polarization, enables accurate measurement across wide conductivity range. **Role of Temperature Compensation:** Ensures readings are normalized to 25°C reference, allowing consistent comparison and process control independent of process temperature variations. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b): **Given:** - Cell constant $K_c = 1.2 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ - Resistance at 25°C: $R = 500 \text{ }\Omega$ - Temperature coefficient $\alpha = 0.02 /°C$ - Concentration $C = 0.05 \text{ M}$ - $T_1 = 25°C$, $T_2 = 30°C$ **Step 1 — Conductivity at 25°C:** $$\kappa_{25} = \frac{K_c}{R} = \frac{1.2}{500}$$ $$\kappa_{25} = 0.0024 \text{ S/cm} = 2.4 \text{ mS/cm}$$ **Step 2 — Conductivity at 30°C:** Using temperature compensation equation: $$\kappa_{30} = \kappa_{25}[1 + \alpha(T_2 - T_1)]$$ $$\kappa_{30} = 0.0024 \times [1 + 0.02 \times (30 - 25)]$$ $$\kappa_{30} = 0.0024 \times [1 + 0.10]$$ $$\kappa_{30} = 0.0024 \times 1.10$$ $$\boxed{\kappa_{30} = 0.00264 \text{ S/cm} = 2.64 \text{ mS/cm}}$$ **Step 3 — Molar Conductivity:** $$\Lambda_m = \frac{1000 \times \kappa_{25}}{C} = \frac{1000 \times 0.0024}{0.05}$$ $$\boxed{\Lambda_m = 48 \text{ S}\cdot\text{cm}^2/\text{mol}}$$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **Q. (a)** Explain the construction and working of a two-electrode conductivity cell. Compare it with the four-electrode cell design. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Define Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions. The molar conductivity at infinite dilution for Na⁺, Cl⁻, and SO₄²⁻ are 50.1, 76.3, and 160 S·cm²/mol respectively. Calculate the molar conductivity at infinite dilution for Na₂SO₄. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a): **Two-Electrode Conductivity Cell — Construction:** 1. **Electrodes:** Two identical electrodes, typically made of: - **Platinum coated with platinum black** (best accuracy) - **Graphite** (low cost, industrial) - **Titanium** (corrosion resistant) - **Stainless Steel 316** (food/pharma grade) 2. **Geometry:** Electrodes may be: - **Parallel plates** (most common in lab cells) - **Concentric cylinders** (common in industrial probes) - **Coaxial rings** (for in-line flow cells) 3. **Insulating Body:** The electrodes are held in a chemically resistant body made of glass, epoxy, PVDF, or PPS (polyphenylene sulfide). 4. **Cable Connection:** A shielded cable connects the electrodes to the transmitter. 5. **Temperature Sensor:** A PT100 RTD is embedded in the body for compensation. **Working:** - An AC voltage is applied across the two electrodes. - AC current flows through the solution via ion migration. - The current is measured; resistance $R = V/I$ is calculated. - Conductivity $\kappa = K_c / R$ is computed. **Comparison with Four-Electrode Cell:** | Feature | Two-Electrode | Four-Electrode | |---------|---------------|----------------| | Electrodes | 2 (current + voltage same) | 4 (2 current + 2 sense) | | Polarization error | Yes, at high κ | Negligible | | Range | Low to medium κ | High κ | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | Circuitry | Simple | Complex (high-Z voltmeter) | | Best for | Pure water, drinking water | Acids, brines, chemicals | In a four-electrode cell, the **outer pair (C1, C2)** drive a controlled AC current, while the **inner pair (S1, S2)** measure the voltage drop with a high-impedance voltmeter (drawing negligible current). Since virtually no current flows through the sense electrodes, **no polarization** occurs on them, eliminating the major source of error at high conductivities. --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b): **Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions:** It states that at **infinite dilution**, the molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of the limiting molar conductivities of its constituent cations and anions, each multiplied by the number of ions of that type per formula unit. $$\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \nu_+ \lambda_+^{\circ} + \nu_- \lambda_-^{\circ}$$ where: - $\nu_+, \nu_-$ = number of cations and anions per formula unit - $\lambda_+^{\circ}, \lambda_-^{\circ}$ = limiting molar conductivities of cation and anion **Given:** - $\lambda_{Na^+}^{\circ} = 50.1 \text{ S}\cdot\text{cm}^2/\text{mol}$ - $\lambda_{Cl^-}^{\circ} = 76.3 \text{ S}\cdot\text{cm}^2/\text{mol}$ - $\lambda_{SO_4^{2-}}^{\circ} = 160 \text{ S}\cdot\text{cm}^2/\text{mol}$ **Required:** $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ for Na₂SO₄ **Dissociation equation:** $$Na_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2Na^+ + SO_4^{2-}$$ So $\nu_+ = 2$ (two Na⁺ ions) and $\nu_- = 1$ (one SO₄²⁻ ion). **Apply Kohlrausch's law:** $$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(Na_2SO_4) = 2 \times \lambda_{Na^+}^{\circ} + 1 \times \lambda_{SO_4^{2-}}^{\circ}$$ $$\Lambda_m^{\circ} = 2 \times 50.1 + 1 \times 160$$ $$\Lambda_m^{\circ} = 100.2 + 160$$ $$\boxed{\Lambda_m^{\circ}(Na_2SO_4) = 260.2 \text{ S}\cdot\text{cm}^2/\text{mol}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing ν (number of ions) with valence Z:** Students often write $Z_+ \times \lambda_+^{\circ}$ instead of $\nu_+ \times \lambda_+^{\circ}$. The valence affects the *current* each ion carries, but Kohlrausch's law uses the **stoichiometric count** of ions. > 2. **Unit confusion in the formula:** The factor **1000** appears only when κ is in S/cm and C is in mol/L. In SI (κ in S/m, C in mol/m³), no factor of 1000 is needed. KTU papers usually follow the S/cm convention. > 3. **Forgetting to square the cell constant for k:** No — conductivity is linear in $K_c$, not quadratic. Students sometimes confuse this with the geometry of a cube ($V = l^3$). > 4. **Mixing AC and DC:** Always state the AC excitation frequency range in your answer when describing the measurement system. > 5. **TDS estimation:** %TDS ≈ 0.5 × κ (mS/cm) is an approximation, not a universal rule — it depends on the salt composition. State the assumption clearly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Conductivity Module 3** ✅ **Core Definition:** Conductivity κ (S/m or S/cm) is the reciprocal of resistivity; measures ionic conduction in electrolyte solutions. ✅ **Three quantities:** Specific κ, Molar Λₘ, Equivalent Λₑ — know the conversion formulas and units cold. ✅ **Cell Constant:** $K_c = l/A$; relates measured conductance to intrinsic conductivity: $\kappa = K_c / R$. ✅ **Calibration:** Cell constant is found using a standard KCl solution of known conductivity (typically 0.01 M KCl = 1.413 mS/cm at 25°C). ✅ **AC Excitation:** Essential to prevent polarization and electrolysis at electrodes. Frequency: 50 Hz – 1 kHz typical. ✅ **Two-electrode vs Four-electrode:** Two = simple but polarizes at high κ. Four = uses separate current and sense electrodes; no polarization. ✅ **Temperature:** Conductivity increases ≈ 2% per °C. Always compensate to 25°C reference using $\kappa_{25} = \kappa_T / [1 + \alpha(T-25)]$. ✅ **Kohlrausch's Law:** $\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \nu_+ \lambda_+^{\circ} + \nu_- \lambda_-^{\circ}$ — sum of ionic contributions at infinite dilution. ✅ **TDS Estimation:** TDS (ppm) ≈ 0.5 × κ (mS/cm) for NaCl-dominated waters; conversion factor (k) ranges 0.5–0.7. ✅ **Industrial Applications:** Water purity, acid/alkali concentration, CIP verification, boiler feedwater, cooling tower cycles, pharma WFI. ✅ **SI vs CGS:** Memorize 1 S/m = 10 mS/cm = 10,000 µS/cm. KTU paper may test this directly. ✅ **Polarization Effect:** Caused by DC → ions accumulate at electrodes, generating back-EMF. Use AC + (optionally) four-electrode design to eliminate it. ✅ **Molar Conductivity Formula:** $\Lambda_m = (1000 \kappa)/C$ when κ in S/cm and C in mol/L — the factor 1000 is unit conversion. ✅ **Common Exam Mistakes:** Wrong cell constant units, mixing up conductance vs conductivity, forgetting temperature compensation, applying Kohlrausch's law to non-dilute solutions. ---

topics.title: Field Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Field Effect # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Field Effect (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > The **Field Effect** refers to the modulation of the conductivity of a semiconductor channel by an electric field applied perpendicular to the surface of the channel. This principle forms the operating basis of **Field Effect Transistors (FETs)** — namely the **Junction Field Effect Transistor (JFET)** and the **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** — which are voltage-controlled, unipolar devices extensively used in industrial instrumentation as low-noise amplifiers, high-impedance input stages, chemical sensors (ISFET, GasFET), and precision analog switches. In a Field Effect Transistor, the output current (drain current, $I_D$) between the **source** and the **drain** terminals is controlled by the electric field generated at the **gate** terminal, which modulates the cross-sectional area (or carrier density) of the conducting channel. Since the controlling mechanism is purely an electric field, the input draws virtually **negligible current** at the gate, making FETs inherently **high-input-impedance** devices. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model:** > Imagine a horizontal pipe (the **channel**) carrying water (the **drain current, $I_D$**) from a source tank to a drain tank. A vertical squeezing mechanism (the **gate**) is placed around the pipe. When you rotate the tap (apply a voltage $V_{GS}$), the pipe gets squeezed or released, controlling the water flow. > - A **more negative $V_{GS}$** → pipe is squeezed harder → less water flows → $I_D$ decreases. > - A **zero $V_{GS}$** → pipe is fully open → maximum water flows. > - The squeezing mechanism itself does NOT consume water (no gate current) — this is exactly the high input impedance of a FET. > This "squeezing" is the **Field Effect** — the electric field "pinches" the conducting channel. ## 1.3 Classification of Field Effect Devices | Family | Type | Key Feature | |---|---|---| | **JFET** (Junction FET) | n-channel / p-channel | Reverse-biased p-n junction forms the gate | | **MOSFET** (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor FET) | Enhancement / Depletion mode | Insulated gate (SiO₂ layer) | | **Specialized FETs** | ISFET, GasFET, BioFET, HEMT | Used as transducers/sensors in instrumentation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU:** > - **JFET** → conducting channel exists at $V_{GS} = 0$ (normally-ON device). > - **E-MOSFET (Enhancement MOSFET)** → NO channel exists at $V_{GS} = 0$ (normally-OFF device). > - **D-MOSFET (Depletion MOSFET)** → channel exists at $V_{GS} = 0$ (normally-ON), but can be enhanced or depleted. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Electron charge ($q$):** $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** - **Permittivity of free space ($\varepsilon_0$):** $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** - **Relative permittivity of Silicon ($\varepsilon_r$):** **11.7** - **Relative permittivity of SiO₂ ($\varepsilon_{ox}$):** **3.9** - **Thermal voltage ($V_T$) at 300 K:** $\approx$ **25.85 mV** - **Standard input impedance of MOSFET gate:** $10^{12}$ to $10^{15}$ **Ω** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 JFET — Construction and Operating Principle A JFET consists of a bar of n-type semiconductor (channel) with two heavily doped p-regions diffused on opposite sides, forming two p-n junctions. These p-regions are internally tied together to form the **gate (G)**. The two ends of the n-bar are the **source (S)** and **drain (D)**. ### Operating Mechanism (Step-by-Step) 1. With **$V_{GS} = 0$ V** and a positive **$V_{DS}$** applied → a uniform channel exists → maximum drain current $I_{DSS}$ flows. 2. When **$V_{GS}$ is made negative**, the reverse-biased p-n junctions create **depletion regions** that intrude into the n-channel from both sides. 3. The depletion regions **narrow the effective cross-sectional area** of the conducting channel. 4. As $\vert V_{GS} \vert$ increases → depletion regions grow → channel narrows → **$I_D$ decreases**. 5. When **$V_{GS} = V_P$ (Pinch-off Voltage)**, the depletion regions meet → channel is "pinched off" → $I_D \approx 0$. > [!NOTE] > **$V_P$** is **negative** for n-channel JFET and **positive** for p-channel JFET. ## 2.2 MOSFET — Construction and Operating Principle ### 2.2.1 Enhancement MOSFET (E-MOSFET) - Substrate is p-type Silicon. - Two heavily doped n-regions form the **source** and **drain**. - A thin layer of **SiO₂ insulator** sits beneath the metallic gate contact. - At **$V_{GS} = 0$** → no channel exists → $I_D = 0$ (device is OFF). - When **$V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ (Threshold Voltage)** → electrons are attracted to the surface, **inducing an n-channel** (the "inversion layer") → current flows. > [!IMPORTANT] > The induced channel is formed by the **field effect of $V_{GS}$** — hence the name. The **Threshold Voltage $V_{TH}$** is the minimum gate-source voltage required to create the conducting channel. ### 2.2.2 Depletion MOSFET (D-MOSFET) - A physical n-channel is diffused between source and drain at fabrication. - SiO₂ layer insulates the gate. - Can operate in **both enhancement and depletion modes** by varying the polarity of $V_{GS}$. ## 2.3 FET Parameters (Critical for KTU) | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Drain current at zero gate voltage | $I_{DSS}$ | $I_D$ when $V_{GS} = 0$ | mA | | Pinch-off voltage | $V_P$ | $V_{GS}$ at which channel is pinched off | V | | Threshold voltage | $V_{TH}$ | Minimum $V_{GS}$ to create channel (MOSFET) | V | | Transconductance | $g_m$ | $g_m = \dfrac{\Delta I_D}{\Delta V_{GS}}\Big\vert_{V_{DS}}$ | mS (milli-Siemens) | | Drain (output) resistance | $r_d$ | $r_d = \dfrac{\Delta V_{DS}}{\Delta I_D}\Big\vert_{V_{GS}}$ | kΩ | | Amplification factor | $\mu$ | $\mu = g_m \times r_d$ | dimensionless | | ON resistance | $r_{DS(on)}$ | Channel resistance when fully ON | Ω | | Input resistance | $R_{GS}$ | $10^9$ (JFET) to $10^{15}$ (MOSFET) | Ω | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam critical. Memorize the relationships.** ### JFET Formulas $$I_D = I_{DSS} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$ $$g_m = g_{m0} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$$ $$g_m = \frac{2}{\vert V_P \vert} \sqrt{I_{DSS} \cdot I_D}$$ ### MOSFET Formulas (Enhancement Mode) $$I_D = k \left(V_{GS} - V_{TH}\right)^2$$ $$k = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} \quad \text{(conduction parameter, in A/V²)}$$ $$g_m = 2k \left(V_{GS} - V_{TH}\right) = 2\sqrt{k \cdot I_D}$$ ### Universal FET Relations $$\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$$ $$V_A = \mu \cdot V_{DS} \quad \text{(Early voltage, in saturation)}$$ ### Channel Charge Density (Physical Foundation) $$Q_n = C_{ox} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{TH})$$ where $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ is the **oxide capacitance per unit area**, and $t_{ox}$ is the thickness of the gate oxide. ### Threshold Voltage (Body Effect) $$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$$ where $\gamma$ is the **body-effect coefficient** and $\phi_F$ is the **Fermi potential**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Instrumentation > [!NOTE] > **Why FETs are indispensable in Industrial Instrumentation:** > 1. **High input impedance ($10^{12}$–$10^{15}$ Ω)** → ideal for amplifying signals from high-impedance transducers (pH electrodes, piezoelectric sensors, ionization chambers) without loading the source. > 2. **Low noise performance** → essential in medical instrumentation (ECG, EEG) and precision measurement. > 3. **ISFET (Ion-Sensitive FET)** → replaces the fragile glass electrode in pH meters, enabling miniaturized, rugged chemical sensors for process industries. > 4. **GasFET** → detects gases like H₂, CO, NH₃ via catalytic metal gate. > 5. **Switching application** → E-MOSFETs are used as analog switches in sample-and-hold circuits, multiplexers, and ADCs. > 6. **Chopper-stabilized amplifiers** → used in nanovolt-level signal conditioning. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: JFET Drain Current Equation (Shockley's Equation) ### Starting Assumption The channel is treated as a **uniform resistor** of length $L$, width $W$, and variable depth $a(x)$ that depends on the position $x$ along the channel. The depletion width on either side of the gate is $h(x)$, so the effective conducting depth is: $$a(x) = a_0 - h(x)$$ where $a_0$ is the half-channel depth at the source end. ### Step 1: Voltage Drop Along the Channel At a distance $x$ from the source, the voltage is $V(x)$. The total gate-to-channel reverse bias is: $$V_{GC}(x) = V_{GS} - V(x)$$ ### Step 2: Depletion Width For an abrupt p-n junction, the depletion width extending into the lightly doped n-side is: $$h(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GC}(x))}{q N_D}}$$ where: - $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of silicon - $V_{bi}$ is the built-in potential - $N_D$ is the donor concentration. ### Step 3: Channel Conductance The conductance of the channel element $dx$ is: $$dG = \frac{q N_D W \cdot \mu_n \cdot a(x)}{dx}$$ ### Step 4: Pinch-off Condition The channel is pinched off when the depletion regions from both gates meet at the center, i.e., when $a(x) \to 0$. This occurs when: $$V_{GC}(x) = V_P = \frac{q N_D a_0^2}{2\varepsilon_s} + V_{bi}$$ ### Step 5: Integrating Ohm's Law Combining the conductance equation with $dV = I_D \cdot dR$ and integrating from $x = 0$ to $x = L$: $$\int_0^{V_{DS}} \left[1 - \left(\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V}{V_P}\right)^{1/2}\right] dV = \frac{I_D L}{q N_D W \mu_n a_0}$$ ### Step 6: Evaluating the Integral After mathematical simplification (substituting $u = V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V$): The result separates into two regions: **Region 1: Triode (Ohmic) region** — for $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_P$: $$I_D = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{V_P^2} \left[(V_{GS} - V_P) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$ **Region 2: Saturation (Pinch-off) region** — for $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_P$: $$\begin{aligned} I_D &= I_{DSS} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2 \\ \text{where} \quad I_{DSS} &= \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} W}{2L} V_P^2 \end{aligned}$$ This is **Shockley's Square Law** — the foundational equation of the JFET. ## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Board Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern Problem:** > Given an n-channel JFET with $I_{DSS} = 8$ mA and $V_P = -4$ V, find: > (a) The drain current $I_D$ at $V_{GS} = -1.5$ V > (b) The transconductance $g_m$ at this operating point > (c) The drain current at $V_{GS} = 0$ V ### Solution **Part (a): Drain Current** Apply Shockley's equation: $$I_D = I_{DSS} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$ $$I_D = 8 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 - \frac{-1.5}{-4}\right)^2$$ $$I_D = 8 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 - 0.375\right)^2$$ $$I_D = 8 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.625)^2$$ $$I_D = 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.390625$$ $$\boxed{I_D = 3.125 \text{ mA}}$$ **Part (b): Transconductance** Using $g_m = g_{m0} \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$: $$g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} = \frac{2 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{\vert -4 \vert} = 4 \text{ mS}$$ $$g_m = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times \left(1 - \frac{-1.5}{-4}\right)$$ $$g_m = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.625$$ $$\boxed{g_m = 2.5 \text{ mS}}$$ **Part (c): Drain Current at $V_{GS} = 0$ V** By definition, $I_D$ at $V_{GS} = 0$ is $I_{DSS}$: $$\boxed{I_D = I_{DSS} = 8 \text{ mA}}$$ ## 3.3 MOSFET $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ Characteristics — Analytical Regions | Region | Condition | Governing Equation | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Cut-off | $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ | $I_D = 0$ | No channel induced | | Triode (Ohmic) | $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | $I_D = k\left[2(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2\right]$ | Channel exists, behaves as variable resistor | | Saturation (Active) | $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ | $I_D = k(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$ | Channel pinched off at drain end, $I_D$ saturates | ## 3.4 Complete Derivation of the Amplification Factor $\mu$ Starting from the differential form of the drain current in saturation: $$dI_D = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} dV_{GS} + \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{DS}} dV_{DS}$$ For constant $I_D$ (zero output current change): $$0 = g_m \cdot dV_{GS} + \frac{1}{r_d} \cdot dV_{DS}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{dV_{DS}}{dV_{GS}} \bigg\vert_{I_D = \text{const}} = - g_m \cdot r_d$$ By definition, the magnitude of this ratio is the **amplification factor**: $$\boxed{\mu = g_m \cdot r_d}$$ ## 3.5 Derivation: Conduction Parameter $k$ from Physical Constants $$k = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{L} = \frac{\mu_n \varepsilon_{ox}}{2 t_{ox}} \cdot \frac{W}{L}$$ For a typical industrial MOSFET with $t_{ox} = 50$ nm, $W/L = 10$, $\mu_n = 500$ cm²/(V·s): $$C_{ox} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{50 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ F/m}^2$$ $$k = \frac{0.05 \times 6.9 \times 10^{-4}}{2} \times 10 = 1.725 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A/V}^2$$ This yields $I_D = 1.725 \times 10^{-4} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$ — a typical industrial specification. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 JFET Construction (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph JFET_BODY["n-channel JFET - Cross-Sectional Block View"] GATE["Gate Terminal G
p-plus regions on both sides"] SOURCE["Source Terminal S
n-type channel end 1"] DRAIN["Drain Terminal D
n-type channel end 2"] CHANNEL["Conducting n-channel
Width modulated by VGS"] DEPLETION["Depletion Regions
Intrude from both p-n junctions"] end VGS["Input VGS
Reverse bias"] --> GATE VDS["Input VDS
Drain-source bias"] --> DRAIN SOURCE --> CHANNEL CHANNEL --> DRAIN GATE -.->|"Field Effect"| DEPLETION DEPLETION -.->|"Narrows"| CHANNEL CHANNEL -->|"ID flows"| OUT["Output Drain Current ID"] ``` ## 4.2 MOSFET Construction (E-MOSFET Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EMOS["Enhancement MOSFET - Block Architecture"] METAL_GATE["Metal Gate Contact
G terminal"] OXIDE["SiO2 Insulator Layer
Thickness tox"] SUBSTRATE["p-type Substrate
Body B terminal"] N_SOURCE["n-plus Source
S terminal"] N_DRAIN["n-plus Drain
D terminal"] INDUCED["Induced n-channel
Forms only when VGS greater than VTH"] end VGS["VGS greater than VTH"] --> METAL_GATE METAL_GATE -.->|"Vertical Electric Field"| OXIDE OXIDE -.->|"Inverts surface"| INDUCED INDUCED -->|"Electron flow"| N_DRAIN N_SOURCE --> INDUCED SUBSTRATE -->|"Body Effect"| INDUCED ``` ## 4.3 FET Family Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT["Field Effect Transistors FET"] JFET["JFET Junction FET"] MOSFET["MOSFET Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor FET"] SPECIAL["Specialized FETs for Instrumentation"] NJFET["n-channel JFET"] PJFET["p-channel JFET"] EMOS["Enhancement MOSFET"] DMOS["Depletion MOSFET"] ISFET["ISFET Ion-Sensitive"] GASFET["GasFET Gas-Sensing"] BIOFET["BioFET Bio-Sensor"] HEMT["HEMT High Electron Mobility"] ROOT --> JFET ROOT --> MOSFET ROOT --> SPECIAL JFET --> NJFET JFET --> PJFET MOSFET --> EMOS MOSFET --> DMOS SPECIAL --> ISFET SPECIAL --> GASFET SPECIAL --> BIOFET SPECIAL --> HEMT ``` ## 4.4 FET Transfer Characteristic Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph XAXIS["VGS axis - negative direction"] VGS0["VGS = 0 V"] VGS1["VGS = -1 V"] VGS2["VGS = -2 V"] VGS3["VGS = -3 V"] VGP["VGS = VP = -4 V Pinch-off"] end VGS0 -->|"ID = IDSS = 8 mA"| POINT_A["Operating Point A"] VGS1 -->|"ID = 3.125 mA"| POINT_B["Operating Point B"] VGS2 -->|"ID = 0.5 mA"| POINT_C["Operating Point C"] VGS3 -->|"ID approx 0.125 mA"| POINT_D["Operating Point D"] VGP -->|"ID approx 0"| POINT_E["Cut-off Point E"] ``` ## 4.5 ISFET as a pH Sensor (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR SOL["Test Solution
H plus ions on gate"] ION_SENS["Ion-Sensitive Membrane
Replaces metal gate of MOSFET"] GATE_ISFET["ISFET Gate
No metal contact"] DRAIN_I["Drain D"] SOURCE_I["Source S"] REF["Reference Electrode
Completes the circuit"] AMPLIFIER["High-Z Amplifier
Readout stage"] SOL -->|"Adsorbs H plus"| ION_SENS ION_SENS -->|"Nernst potential VG"| GATE_ISFET GATE_ISFET -->|"Modulates ID"| DRAIN_I SOURCE_I --> GATE_ISFET REF --> SOL DRAIN_I --> AMPLIFIER AMPLIFIER --> OUT_P["pH Output
59 mV per pH unit at 25 C"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define the term "Field Effect" as applied to transistors. How does it differ from the operation of a bipolar junction transistor (BJT)?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Field Effect:** The control of the conductivity of a semiconductor channel by an electric field applied perpendicular to the channel surface, without drawing any input current. **Distinction Table:** | Feature | FET (Field Effect) | BJT (Bipolar) | |---|---|---| | Control parameter | Voltage ($V_{GS}$) | Current ($I_B$) | | Input impedance | Very high ($10^9$–$10^{15}$ Ω) | Low ($10^3$–$10^6$ Ω) | | Carrier type | Unipolar (e⁻ or h⁺) | Bipolar (both) | | Noise | Low | Higher | | Temperature stability | Better | Poorer | | Operating speed | High | Moderate | ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is pinch-off voltage $V_P$ in a JFET? Explain its significance.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Pinch-off Voltage ($V_P$):** The value of gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ at which the drain current $I_D$ is reduced to zero (the conducting channel is completely pinched off by the merging of depletion regions). > > **Significance:** > 1. It defines the **cut-off** of the JFET. > 2. It is used in **Shockley's equation** to predict $I_D$ at any $V_{GS}$. > 3. It is essential for **bias design** and determining the Q-point. > 4. For n-channel JFET, $V_P$ is **negative**; for p-channel JFET, $V_P$ is **positive**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat construction diagram, explain the working principle of an n-channel JFET. Derive the expression for drain current $I_D$ in the saturation region. **(b) [7 Marks)** For an n-channel JFET, $I_{DSS} = 10$ mA and $V_P = -5$ V. Calculate (i) the drain current at $V_{GS} = -2$ V, (ii) the transconductance $g_m$ at this point, and (iii) the value of $V_{GS}$ that will reduce $I_D$ to 2.5 mA. #### Model Solution **Part (a):** [Construction and working — see SECTION_2.1 and SECTION_4.1] - State that the JFET is a **voltage-controlled, unipolar** device. - Describe the n-channel bar with two p⁺ gate regions on either side. - Explain how reverse bias on the p-n junction widens the depletion region and narrows the channel. - At $V_{GS} = V_P$, the channel pinches off → $I_D \to 0$. - **Derivation:** Follow Section 3.1 to arrive at Shockley's equation: $$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$ **Part (b):** Numerical computation: **(i) Drain current at $V_{GS} = -2$ V:** $$I_D = 10 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 - \frac{-2}{-5}\right)^2 = 10 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.6)^2$$ $$\boxed{I_D = 3.6 \text{ mA}}$$ **(ii) Transconductance:** $$g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} = \frac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{5} = 4 \text{ mS}$$ $$g_m = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 = \boxed{2.4 \text{ mS}}$$ **(iii) $V_{GS}$ for $I_D = 2.5$ mA:** $$2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{-5}\right)^2$$ $$0.25 = \left(1 + \frac{V_{GS}}{5}\right)^2$$ $$0.5 = 1 + \frac{V_{GS}}{5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_{GS}}{5} = -0.5$$ $$\boxed{V_{GS} = -2.5 \text{ V}}$$ --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) ### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of an n-channel Enhancement MOSFET. How is it different from a Depletion MOSFET? Sketch the transfer and output characteristics. **(b) [7 Marks]** An E-MOSFET has $V_{TH} = 2$ V and conduction parameter $k = 0.5$ mA/V². If the device operates with $V_{GS} = 5$ V in saturation, find (i) the drain current, (ii) the transconductance $g_m$, and (iii) the ON-resistance for a small $V_{DS} = 0.1$ V in the triode region. #### Model Solution **Part (a):** Construction and working of E-MOSFET — see Section 2.2.1 and Section 4.2. **Differences Table:** | Feature | Enhancement MOSFET | Depletion MOSFET | |---|---|---| | Channel at $V_{GS} = 0$ | Absent (normally OFF) | Present (normally ON) | | Operation | $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$ needed | Works for $V_{GS} = 0$ and with both polarities | | Construction | SiO₂ under gate, no physical channel | Physical n-channel diffused at fabrication | | Threshold voltage | Positive (n-channel E) | Can be positive or negative | | Application | Digital switches, power electronics | RF amplifiers, constant current sources | **Characteristics:** - **Transfer curve:** $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$ — parabolic, starting at $V_{GS} = V_{TH}$. - **Output curve:** $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ — triode region for low $V_{DS}$, saturation for $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. **Part (b):** Numerical computation: **(i) Drain current in saturation:** $$I_D = k (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (5 - 2)^2$$ $$\boxed{I_D = 4.5 \text{ mA}}$$ **(ii) Transconductance:** $$g_m = 2k(V_{GS} - V_{TH}) = 2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3$$ $$\boxed{g_m = 3 \text{ mS}}$$ **(iii) ON-resistance at $V_{DS} = 0.1$ V:** Using the triode equation (linear approximation for small $V_{DS}$): $$I_D \approx k \left[2(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS}\right] = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 3 \times 0.1 = 0.3 \text{ mA}$$ $$r_{DS(on)} = \frac{V_{DS}}{I_D} = \frac{0.1}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\boxed{r_{DS(on)} \approx 333.3 \text{ }\Omega}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students often write $V_P$ as positive for n-channel JFET. **Correct:** $V_P$ is **negative** for n-channel and **positive** for p-channel. > 2. **Confusing $V_P$ and $V_{TH}$:** $V_P$ is for JFET; $V_{TH}$ is for MOSFET. They are NOT interchangeable. > 3. **Shockley's Equation Domain:** Valid **only in saturation region**; using it in the triode region loses full marks. > 4. **Forgetting Units:** Always write mA, mS, V, kΩ with numerical answers. Naked numbers lose 0.5–1 mark. > 5. **Body Effect Ignored:** In MOSFETs, the body terminal connection matters. KTU expects you to mention the **body effect** in long answers. > 6. **Construction Diagram:** Always draw a **labeled** diagram (gate, source, drain, channel, depletion region). A missing or unlabeled diagram costs 1–2 marks in 7-mark questions. > 7. **ISFET Application:** When asked about instrumentation, mention **ISFET for pH measurement** specifically — examiners reward industrial relevance. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Field Effect** - 🔑 **Field Effect** = control of channel conductivity by a perpendicular electric field, with **zero gate current**. - 🔑 **FET is a voltage-controlled, unipolar device** with **very high input impedance** ($10^9$ to $10^{15}$ Ω). - 🔑 **JFET** is **normally ON** at $V_{GS} = 0$; **E-MOSFET** is **normally OFF**. - 🔑 **$V_P$ (Pinch-off)** applies to JFET; **$V_{TH}$ (Threshold)** applies to MOSFET. - 🔑 **Shockley's Square Law:** $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ (JFET, saturation). - 🔑 **E-MOSFET Equation:** $I_D = k(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$ (saturation region). - 🔑 **Transconductance** $g_m$ is the **slope of the $I_D$–$V_{GS}$** curve — a measure of amplification. - 🔑 **Amplification factor** $\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$ (dimensionless). - 🔑 **Three regions of operation:** Cut-off, Triode (Ohmic), Saturation (Active). - 🔑 **In E-MOSFET**, the channel is **induced** by $V_{GS}$ — no physical channel is fabricated. - 🔑 **In D-MOSFET**, a physical channel exists; can be enhanced or depleted. - 🔑 **ISFET** replaces the metal gate with an **ion-sensitive membrane** → used for **pH, biochemical, and process control sensing**. - 🔑 **Body Effect** modifies $V_{TH}$ when source-to-body voltage $V_{SB} \neq 0$ — important in integrated instrumentation circuits. - 🔑 **Channel length modulation** (Early effect) makes $r_d$ finite; neglected in ideal analysis. - 🔑 **FETs are preferred over BJTs in instrumentation front-ends** because of low noise and high input impedance. - 🔑 **Remember** the **Nernst sensitivity** of ISFETs: **~59 mV per pH unit at 25 °C** — a common viva question.

topics.title: Thermal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Thermal" Instrumentation?

In the context of **Industrial Instrumentation (OEICT833)**, the "Thermal" topic deals with the **science, sensors, and signal conditioning systems used to measure temperature** — one of the most frequently measured process variables in industries such as power generation, petrochemicals, food processing, HVAC, metallurgy, and semiconductor manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Temperature measurement* is the determination of the degree of hotness or coldness of a body, expressed relative to a standard scale (e.g., Celsius, Kelvin, Fahrenheit). *Thermal instrumentation* refers to the complete set of transducers, sensors, signal conditioning circuits, and display systems used to convert thermal energy variations into measurable electrical or mechanical outputs.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine placing your hand near a hot cup of coffee. Your skin senses the heat energy radiating from the cup. Your nerves convert this "heat feel" into a brain-readable signal. **Thermal instruments do exactly this — but with far greater precision.** They convert a physical property that changes with heat (like resistance, voltage, color, or expansion) into a standardized signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physical Constants & Standards used in Thermal Measurements:**
> - **Triple Point of Water:** **0.01 °C = 273.16 K** (defines the Kelvin scale, ITS-90 standard)
> - **Absolute Zero:** **0 K = −273.15 °C** (theoretical lower limit of temperature)
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ):** **5.670374419 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
> - **Wien's Displacement Constant (b):** **2.897771955 × 10⁻³ m·K**

### 1.2 Classification of Temperature Measurement Methods

Temperature cannot be measured directly — we always measure a **secondary effect** caused by temperature change. The classification is as follows:

**A. Non-Electrical (Mechanical) Methods:**
1. Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers (mercury, alcohol)
2. Bimetallic Strips
3. Pressure Thermometers (gas, liquid, vapor filled)

**B. Electrical Methods (Active Transducers):**
1. **Thermocouples (Seebeck Effect)**
2. **RTDs — Resistance Temperature Detectors (Resistance Variation)**

**C. Electrical Methods (Passive Transducers requiring excitation):**
1. **Thermistors** (NTC and PTC types, using resistance variation)
2. **Semiconductor / IC Temperature Sensors** (e.g., LM35, AD590)

**D. Radiation / Non-Contact Methods:**
1. **Optical Pyrometers** (disappearing filament type)
2. **Radiation Pyrometers / Total Radiation Pyrometers**
3. **Infrared Thermometers / Thermal Imagers**
4. **Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometers**

### 1.3 Importance in Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU emphasizes this topic:**
> - Temperature affects almost every physical, chemical, and biological process.
> - Incorrect temperature reading can lead to **catastrophic equipment failure** (e.g., boiler explosion, turbine damage).
> - Approximately **30–40% of all industrial measurements** involve temperature.
> - Required for ISO 9001, OSHA, and IEC 60584 compliance standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Temperature Scales & Interconversion

The four major temperature scales used in KTU problems are:

| Scale | Symbol | Freezing Point of Water | Boiling Point of Water | Used In |
|-------|--------|------------------------|------------------------|---------|
| Celsius | °C | 0 | 100 | India, Europe, KTU syllabus |
| Kelvin | K | 273.15 | 373.15 | Scientific / SI unit |
| Fahrenheit | °F | 32 | 212 | USA industry |
| Rankine | °R | 491.67 | 671.67 | USA thermodynamic |

### Conversion Formulas (CRITICAL for KTU numericals)

> **[A] Celsius to Kelvin:**
> $$T_K = T_C + 273.15$$

> **[B] Fahrenheit to Celsius:**
> $$T_C = (T_F - 32) \times \frac{5}{9}$$

> **[C] Celsius to Fahrenheit:**
> $$T_F = (T_C \times \frac{9}{5}) + 32$$

> **[D] Kelvin to Rankine:**
> $$T_R = T_K \times \frac{9}{5}$$

## 2.2 Detailed Theory of Major Thermal Sensors

### A. Thermocouples (Thermoelectric Effect)

**Operating Principle:** When two dissimilar metals are joined to form a closed loop and the two junctions are kept at different temperatures, an EMF is generated — this is the **Seebeck Effect** (discovered by Thomas Seebeck, 1821).

**Three Related Thermoelectric Effects:**

1. **Seebeck Effect:** Temperature difference → EMF (used in thermocouples)
2. **Peltier Effect:** Current flow → Heating or Cooling at junction
3. **Thomson Effect:** Current + Temperature gradient → EMF in a single conductor

**The 'Why' behind Seebeck EMF Generation:**
- Free electrons in metals have energies dependent on temperature.
- The hot junction has electrons with **higher kinetic energy**.
- Electrons diffuse from hot to cold end, creating a potential difference.
- The net EMF is the sum of the **Peltier EMF** (junction EMF) and **Thomson EMF** (along the wire).

**Mathematical Expression (Seebeck Coefficient):**

$$E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (S_B - S_A) \, dT$$

Where:
- $E$ = Generated EMF (Volts)
- $S_A, S_B$ = Seebeck coefficients of metals A and B (V/K)
- $T_1$ = Cold junction temperature (K)
- $T_2$ = Hot junction temperature (K)

For small temperature ranges, this linearizes to:

$$E \approx S \times (T_2 - T_1)$$

### B. RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)

**Operating Principle:** The electrical resistance of a pure metal (typically platinum) **increases linearly** with temperature.

**Standard Equation (Callendar-Van Dusen Equation):**

$$R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100) T^3]$$

For temperatures above 0 °C (simplified form used in KTU problems):

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = Resistance at temperature T (Ω)
- $R_0$ = Resistance at 0 °C (Ω) — typically **100 Ω for PT100**, **1000 Ω for PT1000**
- $\alpha$ = Temperature coefficient of resistance (for platinum, **α = 0.00385 Ω/Ω/°C** as per IEC 60751)

### C. Thermistors

**Operating Principle:** Thermistors are semiconductor devices (made from metal oxides like Mn, Ni, Co) whose resistance changes **non-linearly and dramatically** with temperature. Two types exist:

1. **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **decreases** with temperature increase.
2. **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance **increases** with temperature (used in overcurrent protection).

**NTC Thermistor Equation (β-Parameter Model):**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = Resistance at absolute temperature T (Ω)
- $R_0$ = Resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 298.15 K = 25 °C)
- $\beta$ = Material constant (typically **3000–5000 K**)
- $T, T_0$ in Kelvin

### D. Pyrometers (Radiation Thermometers)

These devices measure temperature **without physical contact**, using the **thermal radiation** emitted by hot bodies.

**Two Fundamental Laws Govern Pyrometers:**

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Total Radiation):**

$$Q = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Radiant heat energy emitted (Watts)
- $\varepsilon$ = Emissivity of the body (0 to 1, with **1 = blackbody**)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- $A$ = Surface area (m²)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

**Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Wavelength):**

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K$$

> **Real-World Application:** Steel mills use optical pyrometers to measure molten metal at **1500–1700 °C** where contact sensors would melt. Space telescopes use this law to measure star temperatures.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Kelvin Conversion | $T_K = T_C + 273.15$ | K, °C | K |
| 2 | Fahrenheit to Celsius | $T_C = (T_F - 32) \times 5/9$ | °F, °C | °C |
| 3 | Seebeck EMF | $E = \int S \, dT$ | Seebeck coeff. | V |
| 4 | Linear Seebeck | $E = S \cdot \Delta T$ | EMF, ΔT | V, K |
| 5 | RTD Linear | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | Resistance | Ω |
| 6 | RTD Quadratic | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$ | Resistance | Ω |
| 7 | NTC Thermistor | $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Resistance | Ω |
| 8 | Stefan-Boltzmann | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | Heat radiated | W |
| 9 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Wavelength, T | m, K |
| 10 | Thermocouple Cold J. Compensation | $E_{hot} = E_{measured} + E_{T_{ref}}$ | EMF sum | mV |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Seebeck EMF for a Thermocouple

### Statement
Derive the expression for the net thermoelectric EMF generated in a thermocouple circuit consisting of two dissimilar metals A and B with junctions at temperatures $T_1$ (cold) and $T_2$ (hot), where the Seebeck coefficients vary with temperature.

### Step 1: Define the Seebeck Coefficient

The Seebeck coefficient of a material is defined as the EMF generated per unit temperature difference, measured in microvolts per degree Celsius (μV/°C).

$$S = \frac{dE}{dT}$$

For material A and material B, we have individual Seebeck coefficients $S_A$ and $S_B$, each being a function of temperature.

### Step 2: EMF Developed in Each Material

In an infinitesimal temperature gradient $dT$, the EMF developed in a small element of material A is:

$$dE_A = S_A(T) \, dT$$

Similarly, for material B:

$$dE_B = S_B(T) \, dT$$

### Step 3: Net EMF in the Loop

Since the two materials are connected in a series loop (opposing at the hot junction, reinforcing due to temperature difference), the net EMF is the **algebraic difference** of the two materials' Seebeck contributions:

$$dE_{net} = S_B(T) \, dT - S_A(T) \, dT = [S_B(T) - S_A(T)] \, dT$$

### Step 4: Integrate Over the Temperature Range

Integrating from the cold junction temperature $T_1$ to the hot junction temperature $T_2$:

$$\boxed{E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} [S_B(T) - S_A(T)] \, dT}$$

### Step 5: Linearization for Small Temperature Range

If $S_B - S_A$ is approximately constant over the operating range (which is true for limited temperature spans, e.g., 0–100 °C for Type K), then:

$$S_B - S_A = S_{AB} = \text{constant}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E \approx S_{AB} \times (T_2 - T_1)}$$

### Step 6: Quadratic Approximation (Higher Accuracy)

For wider ranges, $S_{AB}$ is often approximated as a linear function of temperature:

$$S_{AB}(T) = a + bT$$

Substituting:

$$E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (a + bT) \, dT = a(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{b}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2)$$

$$\boxed{E = a(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{b}{2}(T_2 - T_1)(T_2 + T_1)}$$

This is the form used in **NIST thermocouple reference tables** for K, J, T type thermocouples.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example: RTD Temperature Calculation

### Problem Statement (Typical KTU University Exam Pattern)

A platinum RTD has a resistance of **100 Ω at 0 °C**. It is placed in a process where its measured resistance is **138.5 Ω**. If the temperature coefficient of resistance is **α = 0.00385 /°C** (IEC 60751 standard), determine the temperature of the process. Assume linear approximation is valid.

### Given Data
- $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ (at 0 °C)
- $R_T = 138.5 \, \Omega$ (measured)
- $\alpha = 0.00385 \, /°C$

### Step 1: Recall the Linear RTD Equation

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$138.5 = 100 \times (1 + 0.00385 \times T)$$

### Step 3: Divide Both Sides by 100

$$\frac{138.5}{100} = 1 + 0.00385 \times T$$

$$1.385 = 1 + 0.00385 \times T$$

### Step 4: Subtract 1 from Both Sides

$$1.385 - 1 = 0.00385 \times T$$

$$0.385 = 0.00385 \times T$$

### Step 5: Solve for T

$$T = \frac{0.385}{0.00385}$$

$$\boxed{T = 100 \, °C}$$

### Verification
At T = 100 °C, $R_T = 100 \times (1 + 0.00385 \times 100) = 100 \times 1.385 = 138.5 \, \Omega$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical: Wien's Displacement Law for Pyrometer

### Problem
The peak spectral radiation from a furnace occurs at a wavelength of **1.8 μm** (micrometers). Determine the temperature of the furnace interior. (Given: Wien's constant b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K)

### Step 1: Write Wien's Law

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b$$

### Step 2: Convert Wavelength to Meters

$$\lambda_{max} = 1.8 \, \mu m = 1.8 \times 10^{-6} \, m$$

### Step 3: Solve for T

$$T = \frac{b}{\lambda_{max}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8 \times 10^{-6}}$$

### Step 4: Perform Division

$$T = \frac{2.898}{1.8} \times 10^{3} = 1.61 \times 10^{3}$$

$$\boxed{T = 1610 \, K}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Celsius (Optional)

$$T_{°C} = 1610 - 273.15 = 1336.85 \, °C$$

This temperature is consistent with a **steel melting furnace**, confirming the industrial relevance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Sensor

| Parameter | Thermocouple | RTD (PT100) | Thermistor (NTC) | IC Sensor (LM35) | Pyrometer |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|------------------|------------------|-----------|
| **Range (°C)** | −270 to +2300 | −200 to +850 | −55 to +150 | −55 to +150 | 500 to 3500+ |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.5 to ±2 °C | ±0.1 to ±0.5 °C | ±0.1 to ±1 °C | ±0.5 to ±2 °C | ±1 to ±10 °C |
| **Linearity** | Moderate | Excellent | Poor (logarithmic) | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Response Time** | Fast (0.1–10 s) | Moderate (1–10 s) | Fast (0.1–5 s) | Fast (1 s) | Very Fast (ms) |
| **Self-Heating** | None | Yes (low current) | Yes (low current) | Yes | None |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium-High | Low | Very Low | High |
| **Contact Required** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| **Power Required** | No (passive) | Yes (excitation) | Yes (excitation) | Yes (excitation) | Yes |
| **Best Application** | Furnaces, engines | Labs, pharma | Consumer, medical | Microcontrollers | Molten metal, stars |
| **KTU Typical Use** | High-temp industrial | Precision measurement | Inexpensive alarms | Embedded systems | Non-contact monitoring |

---
