# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Complete Thermal Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Temperature Source] --> B[Thermal Sensor RTD TC Pyrometer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    C --> D[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Microcontroller or PLC]
    E --> F[Display Unit]
    E --> G[Alarm Threshold]
    E --> H[Feedback Control Loop]
    H --> I[Actuator Heater Cooler Valve]
    I --> A
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style C fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#5D9CEC,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style F fill:#A8E6CF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#C9A0DC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Diagram Description:** This is the canonical **closed-loop thermal control system** architecture used in almost every modern industrial process. The sensor detects the temperature, the signal conditioner amplifies and filters the weak signal (e.g., a μV-level thermocouple signal), the ADC converts it to digital form, the controller processes it using algorithms like PID, and the actuator (heater, cooler, valve) takes corrective action to maintain the desired setpoint.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Thermocouple Cold-Junction Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J1[Hot Junction at T2] -->|Seebeck EMF| W1[Wire of Material A]
    W1 --> J2[Reference Junction at T1]
    J2 -->|Compensating Wire| W2[Wire of Material B]
    W2 --> J3[Isothermal Block]
    J3 --> CS[Cold Junction Sensor IC]
    CS --> CM[Cold Junction Compensation Circuit]
    CM --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC[ADC Microcontroller]
    W1 -.Cold Junction.-> W2
    style J1 fill:#FF4757,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style J2 fill:#3742fa,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style CS fill:#2ed573,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style AMP fill:#ffa502,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ADC fill:#5f27cd,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

> **Diagram Explanation:** Thermocouples require **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)** because the EMF generated depends on the temperature difference between hot and cold junctions, not absolute hot temperature. An IC sensor (like LM35) measures the cold-junction temperature, and the compensation circuit mathematically adds the equivalent EMF at that reference temperature to the measured EMF, yielding the true hot-junction temperature.

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting a Thermal Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Need Temperature Sensor] --> Q1{Temperature Range}
    Q1 -->|>500 °C or < 0 °C| Q2{Contact Allowed}
    Q1 -->|0 to 500 °C| Q3{Precision Required}
    Q2 -->|Yes| TC[Use Type K or R Thermocouple]
    Q2 -->|No| PY[Use Radiation Pyrometer]
    Q3 -->|High ±0.1 °C| RTD[Use PT100 RTD with 4-Wire]
    Q3 -->|Low ±2 °C| Q4{Cost Sensitive}
    Q4 -->|Yes| TH[Use NTC Thermistor]
    Q4 -->|No Embedded| IC[Use LM35 or DS18B20]
    style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style TC fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style PY fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style RTD fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style TH fill:#A8E6CF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style IC fill:#5D9CEC,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

> **Educational Note:** This decision tree is a high-yield tool for KTU viva voce and design questions. Memorizing the temperature ranges and accuracy levels will earn full marks in short-answer questions.

## 4.4 Schematic: 4-Wire RTD Measurement Circuit (Lead Wire Compensation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RTD_Bridge [4-Wire RTD in Kelvin Bridge]
        R[PT100 RTD] -->|Lead 1| I1[Constant Current Source I]
        R -->|Lead 2| G1[High Impedance Voltmeter]
        R -->|Lead 3| G2[High Impedance Voltmeter]
        R -->|Lead 4| I2[Current Return Path]
    end
    I1 --> C1[Eliminates Lead Resistance Error]
    G1 --> C2[No Voltage Drop in Lead Wires]
    style R fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style I1 fill:#5D9CEC,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style G1 fill:#2ed573,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Why 4-Wire?:** In 2-wire and 3-wire RTD connections, the resistance of the lead wires adds to the RTD resistance, causing measurement error (especially with long cables). The 4-wire (Kelvin) connection uses **separate pairs** for current injection and voltage sensing, completely eliminating lead wire error.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Seebeck Effect. With a neat diagram, explain the working of a thermocouple. Mention any two applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Seebeck Effect:** When two dissimilar metals are joined to form a closed loop and the two junctions are maintained at different temperatures, an EMF is generated in the loop. This phenomenon is called the Seebeck Effect, discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck in 1821. *(1 mark)*

> **Working Principle:** The hot junction (measuring junction) is placed at the process whose temperature is to be measured, while the cold junction (reference junction) is maintained at a known reference temperature (often 0 °C). The temperature difference between the two junctions generates a thermoelectric EMF proportional to the temperature difference, following the equation $E = S_{AB}(T_{hot} - T_{cold})$. *(1 mark)*

> **Applications:**
> 1. Industrial furnace temperature monitoring in steel and cement plants
> 2. Exhaust gas temperature measurement in automobiles and jet engines
> *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Compare RTD and Thermocouple based on the following: (a) Principle of operation, (b) Temperature range, (c) Linearity, (d) Cost.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | RTD (PT100) | Thermocouple |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| (a) Principle | Resistance changes with temperature (passive sensor) | EMF generated due to temperature difference (Seebeck Effect) |
| (b) Range | −200 to +850 °C | −270 to +2300 °C (depending on type) |
| (c) Linearity | Excellent (linear) | Moderate (needs polynomial compensation) |
| (d) Cost | Higher | Lower |

*(1 mark for principle + range; 1 mark for linearity + cost; 1 mark for correct table format)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Seebeck EMF developed in a thermocouple using the Seebeck coefficient. State and explain the three thermoelectric effects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Chromel-Alumel (Type K) thermocouple is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The cold junction is maintained at 25 °C, and the measured EMF is **28.946 mV**. If the Seebeck coefficient of Type K is approximately **40 μV/°C** in the operating range, find the temperature of the furnace. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**The three thermoelectric effects (3 marks):**

1. **Seebeck Effect:** When a temperature difference exists between two junctions of dissimilar metals, an EMF is induced in the circuit. This is the principle behind thermocouples.
2. **Peltier Effect:** When an electric current is passed through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either absorbed or released at the junction depending on the current direction. Used in thermoelectric refrigerators.
3. **Thomson Effect:** When a current passes through a single homogeneous conductor with a temperature gradient along its length, heat is absorbed or evolved along the conductor.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let two metals A and B form junctions at temperatures $T_1$ (cold) and $T_2$ (hot), with $T_2 > T_1$. The Seebeck coefficient $S$ of a material is the EMF developed per unit change in temperature, i.e., $S = dE/dT$.

For an infinitesimal temperature differential $dT$:

$$dE = S_{AB}(T) \, dT$$

where $S_{AB}(T) = S_B(T) - S_A(T)$ is the **relative Seebeck coefficient** of the thermocouple pair.

Integrating from $T_1$ to $T_2$:

$$E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_{AB}(T) \, dT$$

For a linear approximation, $S_{AB}$ is constant, giving:

$$E = S_{AB}(T_2 - T_1)$$

For higher accuracy, with $S_{AB}(T) = a + bT$:

$$E = a(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{b}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2)$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- Measured EMF, $E = 28.946 \, mV = 28,946 \, \mu V$
- Cold junction temperature, $T_1 = 25 \, °C$
- Seebeck coefficient, $S = 40 \, \mu V/°C$

**Formula:** $E = S \times (T_2 - T_1)$

**Step 1:** Solve for $(T_2 - T_1)$:

$$T_2 - T_1 = \frac{E}{S} = \frac{28,946}{40} = 723.65 \, °C$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $T_2$:

$$T_2 = T_1 + 723.65 = 25 + 723.65 = 748.65 \, °C$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{T_2 \approx 749 \, °C}$

**Verification using NIST table:** Type K at 750 °C produces 31.213 mV, and at 25 °C produces 1.000 mV, giving differential EMF ≈ 30.21 mV. The slight difference is due to the linear assumption; in practice, Type K reference tables are used.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an optical pyrometer. State Wien's Displacement Law and Stefan-Boltzmann Law with their industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A furnace emits peak radiation at a wavelength of **2.0 μm**. Calculate the temperature of the furnace using Wien's Displacement Law. Also, calculate the total radiation energy emitted per unit area per second, assuming it behaves as a blackbody (ε = 1). Use σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Working Principle of Optical Pyrometer (Disappearing Filament Type) — (3 marks):**

An optical pyrometer is a non-contact temperature measurement device used for very high temperatures (> 600 °C). It works on the principle of **monochromatic brightness comparison** between the hot target and a calibrated reference filament inside the device.

Construction: A telescope focuses the image of the hot target onto a calibrated tungsten filament inside a pyrometer lamp. The filament is heated by adjusting a variable current source until the filament's brightness **matches** the brightness of the target's image at a specific wavelength (typically 0.65 μm red filter). At this point, the filament "disappears" into the background of the target image. The current required to match the brightness is calibrated to indicate the target temperature.

**Wien's Displacement Law (2 marks):**

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K$$

It states that the peak wavelength of emitted radiation is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature. **Industrial Applications:** Used in two-color pyrometers, flame temperature measurement in furnaces, and astrophysical temperature measurement of stars.

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law (2 marks):**

$$Q = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

The total radiant energy emitted by a body is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature. **Industrial Applications:** Used in total radiation pyrometers, solar collector design, and thermal imaging cameras (FLIR systems).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- $\lambda_{max} = 2.0 \, \mu m = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \, m$
- $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K$
- $\varepsilon = 1$ (blackbody)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}$
- $A = 1 \, m^2$ (per unit area)
- $t = 1 \, s$ (per second)

**Part 1: Temperature from Wien's Law (3 marks)**

$$T = \frac{b}{\lambda_{max}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1449 \, K$$

$$\boxed{T = 1449 \, K \approx 1449 \, K \, (\text{or } 1176 \, °C)}$$

**Part 2: Total Radiation Energy (4 marks)**

$$Q = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

$$Q = 1 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times 1 \times (1449)^4$$

**Calculate $1449^4$ step-by-step:**
- $1449^2 = 2,099,601$
- $1449^4 = (2,099,601)^2 = 4.408 \times 10^{12}$

Therefore:

$$Q = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.408 \times 10^{12}$$

$$Q = 5.67 \times 4.408 \times 10^{4}$$

$$Q = 24.99 \times 10^4 = 2.499 \times 10^5 \, W$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 250 \, kW \, \text{per m}^2 \text{ per second}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is a typical heat flux from a high-temperature industrial furnace, confirming the correctness of our answer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units to SI** (e.g., using μV instead of V, μm instead of m, °C instead of K) — **loses 1–2 marks** per question.
> 2. **Skipping the cold-junction compensation** in thermocouple problems. Always add or subtract the reference junction EMF.
> 3. **Confusing Stefan-Boltzmann (T⁴) with Newton's Law of Cooling (T¹)** — these are **completely different** laws.
> 4. **Forgetting emissivity (ε)** in radiation problems. If ε is not given, assume **ε = 1 (blackbody)**.
> 5. **Mixing up NTC and PTC thermistor behavior** — NTC is the most common type; check the question carefully.
> 6. **Not showing the integration step** in the Seebeck EMF derivation — KTU expects step-by-step mathematical reasoning.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 KTU Module 3 — THERMAL: Last-Minute Revision List**

### 📌 Core Concepts to Memorize:
- ☐ **Seebeck Effect:** Temperature difference → EMF (foundation of thermocouples)
- ☐ **Peltier Effect:** Current → Heating/Cooling at junction
- ☐ **Thomson Effect:** Current + Temperature gradient → EMF in single wire
- ☐ **RTD Equation:** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ — **PT100** = 100 Ω at 0 °C, α = 0.00385
- ☐ **Thermistor:** NTC type, exponential behavior, $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$
- ☐ **Pyrometer:** Non-contact, used for high temperatures (> 600 °C)
- ☐ **Optical Pyrometer:** Disappearing filament type, monochromatic comparison
- ☐ **Total Radiation Pyrometer:** Uses all wavelengths, applies Stefan-Boltzmann Law
- ☐ **Two-Color Pyrometer:** Uses ratio of two wavelengths, emissivity-independent

### 📌 Critical Constants to Memorize:
- ☐ **Triple Point of Water = 273.16 K** (defines Kelvin scale)
- ☐ **Absolute Zero = −273.15 °C = 0 K**
- ☐ **Stefan-Boltzmann constant σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**
- ☐ **Wien's constant b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K**
- ☐ **Platinum α = 0.00385 /°C** (IEC 60751)

### 📌 Important Conversions to Practice:
- ☐ Celsius ↔ Kelvin: $T_K = T_C + 273.15$
- ☐ Celsius ↔ Fahrenheit: $T_F = (9/5)T_C + 32$
- ☐ μm → m: divide by 10⁶
- ☐ mV → V: divide by 1000

### 📌 Industrial Applications to Remember:
- ☐ **Type K Thermocouple** = General purpose (0–1260 °C), most common
- ☐ **Type J Thermocouple** = Iron-Constantan, reducing atmospheres
- ☐ **Type S/R Thermocouple** = Platinum, high precision, > 1000 °C
- ☐ **PT100 RTD** = Industrial standard for precision (pharma, labs)
- ☐ **Thermistor** = Medical (thermometers), consumer electronics
- ☐ **IC Sensor (LM35)** = Microcontroller-based embedded systems
- ☐ **Pyrometer** = Steel plants, glass furnaces, kiln monitoring

### 📌 Common KTU Question Traps:
- ☐ Always state **units** in the final answer
- ☐ Use **absolute temperature (K)** for Stefan-Boltzmann and Wien's Laws — NOT Celsius
- ☐ In a thermocouple problem with cold junction compensation, **add the cold-junction equivalent EMF** to the measured EMF
- ☐ In RTD bridge circuits, prefer the **4-wire Kelvin connection** to eliminate lead resistance errors

---

> ✅ **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 3: Thermal**
> *Total Sections: 5 | Formulas: 10+ | Diagrams: 4 | Solved Numericals: 3 | Practice Questions: 4*

topics.title: Ultrasonic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ultrasonic # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Ultrasonics** is the branch of acoustics that deals with the study and application of **sound waves whose frequencies lie above the upper audible limit of human hearing (greater than 20 kHz)**. In industrial instrumentation, ultrasonic waves are used as non-contact, non-destructive measurement and inspection tools, exploiting their predictable behavior in solids, liquids, and gases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard):** > *"Ultrasonic waves are mechanical vibratory waves of frequency greater than 20 kHz propagated through a medium by the alternating compressions and rarefactions of the medium. The upper frequency limit is not sharply defined but is generally taken to be around 500 MHz for gases and 50 GHz for liquids and solids."* **Industrial Instrumentation Focus:** In OEICT833, ultrasonics is treated as a **transduction phenomenon**, where an electrical signal is converted into a high-frequency mechanical wave (and vice versa) using piezoelectric or magnetostrictive principles to measure physical quantities like level, flow, thickness, and detect flaws. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at one end of a long, empty hallway and you shout loudly. The sound wave travels down the corridor, bounces off the far wall, and returns as an echo. The time it takes for the echo to come back tells you how long the hallway is. Now, instead of a shout, imagine a **dog whistle** (ultrasonic). A dog can hear it, but you cannot — it is too high-pitched (above 20 kHz). The principle is identical: emit a wave, time its reflection. This is exactly how an **ultrasonic level sensor** works in an industrial tank, except the whistle pitch is far higher and the timing is measured in microseconds. > [!NOTE] > **Simple Rule of Thumb:** The higher the frequency, the smaller the feature it can "see" and the faster the timing precision. This is why medical ultrasound imaging (1–20 MHz) and industrial flaw detection (1–25 MHz) use much higher frequencies than sonar (10–100 kHz). ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Range | |---|---| | Audible Frequency Range | **20 Hz – 20 kHz** | | Ultrasonic Frequency Range | **> 20 kHz** (typically 20 kHz – 10 MHz for industrial use) | | Speed of sound in air (at 20°C) | **c = 343 m/s** | | Speed of sound in water (at 20°C) | **c ≈ 1480 m/s** | | Speed of sound in steel | **c ≈ 5960 m/s** | | Acoustic Impedance of air | **Z_air ≈ 0.0004 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)** | | Acoustic Impedance of steel | **Z_steel ≈ 46 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Velocity of sound in air formula (used in many level/flow problems):** > $$c = c_0 \sqrt{1 + \frac{T}{273}}$$ > where $c_0 = 331.5$ m/s at 0°C, and $T$ is temperature in °C. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Ultrasonic Waves Ultrasonic waves are **mechanical waves** that require a physical medium for propagation. They cannot travel through a vacuum. They are classified as: 1. **Longitudinal (Compressional) Waves** — Particle motion is parallel to wave propagation. Most common in gases, liquids, and solids. Used in cleaning baths and most industrial sensors. 2. **Transverse (Shear) Waves** — Particle motion is perpendicular to propagation. Can travel **only through solids** (shear modulus is zero in fluids). 3. **Surface (Rayleigh) Waves** — Travel along the surface of a solid, with particle motion in an elliptical retrograde orbit. Penetration depth ≈ one wavelength. Used in surface flaw detection. 4. **Lamb Waves** — Guided waves in thin plates, dependent on plate thickness, density, and elastic constants. Used in pipe and plate inspection. ## 2.2 Operating Principle of Ultrasonic Instrumentation The operating principle of any ultrasonic instrumentation system follows a universal three-stage logic: **Stage 1 — Generation (Transduction):** - A high-frequency alternating voltage (typically 20 kHz–10 MHz) is applied to a **piezoelectric crystal** (e.g., Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT, or Quartz). - Due to the **inverse piezoelectric effect**, the crystal mechanically deforms (expands and contracts) at the same frequency, launching a mechanical wave into the coupling medium. **Stage 2 — Propagation & Interaction:** - The wave travels through the medium. When it encounters an interface (solid/liquid, density change, flaw, or moving particle), it experiences one of three phenomena: * **Reflection** (used in level and thickness measurement) * **Refraction** (used in immersion testing) * **Scattering / Attenuation** (used in flaw detection) - A part of the wave energy returns to the transducer as an **echo**. **Stage 3 — Reception (Reverse Transduction):** - The returning echo strikes the piezoelectric crystal. - Due to the **direct piezoelectric effect**, the mechanical stress generates a small alternating voltage. - This voltage is amplified, time-stamped, and processed by the instrument's electronics to compute the desired physical quantity. > [!NOTE] > **Why Piezoelectric Crystals?** > Piezoelectric materials are uniquely suitable because they exhibit **mechanical-electrical reciprocity**. The same crystal that transmits a wave can be used to receive it, simplifying instrument design dramatically. This is why almost every ultrasonic transducer in industry is a piezo-electric device. ## 2.3 Modes of Operation | Mode | Description | Industrial Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Pulse-Echo Mode** | Single transducer acts as both transmitter and receiver. Time between transmitted pulse and received echo is measured. | Thickness gauging, flaw detection, level measurement | | **Through-Transmission Mode** | Two transducers face each other. One transmits, the other receives. | Continuous level sensing, flow detection | | **Resonance Mode** | The part being measured is set into resonance. The resonant frequency change indicates thickness. | Thin-film and coating thickness | | **Doppler Mode** | Frequency shift of the reflected wave is used to detect motion. | Flow measurement of slurries, blood flow in medical | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULA LIST — Memorize for ESE (End Semester Exam)** | # | Formula | Description / Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | $v = f \lambda$ | Wave velocity = frequency × wavelength | | 2 | $c = c_0 \sqrt{1 + \frac{T}{273}}$ | Velocity of sound in air at temperature T (°C); $c_0 = 331.5$ m/s | | 3 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic impedance of a medium (kg/m²·s) | | 4 | $R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Reflection coefficient at an interface (amplitude) | | 5 | $T_t = 1 - R$ | Transmission coefficient (energy, approximate for matched impedances) | | 6 | $t = \frac{2D}{c}$ | Time of flight for pulse-echo at distance D | | 7 | $f_D = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler shift frequency (single transducer) | | 8 | $d = \frac{n \lambda}{2} = \frac{n \cdot c}{2f}$ | Thickness from resonance (n = harmonic number) | | 9 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$ | Attenuation of intensity with distance; α is attenuation coefficient | | 10 | $\alpha = \alpha_a + \alpha_s$ | Total attenuation = absorption + scattering | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe Formatting Pitfall:** In all equations above, note that absolute value bars (used in $Z_2 - Z_1$ etc.) are written as $\vert Z_2 - Z_1 \vert$ in LaTeX. **Do not use the bare pipe character `|`** — it breaks the KTU 2024 markdown evaluation scripts. ## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Engineering Utility | Engineering Domain | Ultrasonic Application | |---|---| | **Process Plants** | Continuous level measurement in tanks containing corrosive chemicals (no media contact needed) | | **Petrochemical** | Flow measurement of crude oil and slurries (Doppler or transit-time meters) | | **Manufacturing** | Non-destructive testing (NDT) of welds, turbine blades, pressure vessels | | **Pharmaceutical** | Cleaning baths (high-power ultrasonics remove contaminants from intricate parts) | | **Medical** | Diagnostic imaging (ultrasound sonography at 2–18 MHz) | | **Food & Beverage** | Homogenization, emulsification, fill-level inspection of sealed bottles | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time-of-Flight Distance Equation (Pulse-Echo Mode) **Problem Context:** An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted at the top of an open tank. A pulse is emitted, travels down, reflects off the liquid surface, and returns. Derive the relationship between the **measured time of flight (t)** and the **distance (D)** to the surface. **Step 1 — Define the geometry:** Let the transducer be at the top of the tank. The pulse travels a one-way distance $D$ to the liquid surface. **Step 2 — Identify the wave equation:** The wave travels with speed $c$ in the medium (air, in most level applications). The one-way travel time $t_1$ is: $$t_1 = \frac{D}{c}$$ **Step 3 — Account for the return path:** The echo travels the same distance $D$ back to the transducer. The return time $t_2$ equals the forward time: $$t_2 = \frac{D}{c}$$ **Step 4 — Sum the forward and return times:** The total time of flight $t$ is: $$t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{D}{c} + \frac{D}{c}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify and solve for D:** $$t = \frac{2D}{c}$$ Solving for $D$ gives the **fundamental pulse-echo distance equation**: $$\boxed{D = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}}$$ This is the single most important equation in KTU ultrasonic level measurement problems. The factor of 2 appears **only** in pulse-echo mode; in through-transmission mode, it is absent. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity of Sound in Air as a Function of Temperature **Problem Context:** In open-air applications, the speed of sound depends strongly on air temperature. Derive the relationship. **Step 1 — Start from the thermodynamic definition of sound velocity in a gas:** $$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T_{abs}}{M}}$$ where $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats (≈ 1.4 for air), $R$ = universal gas constant, $T_{abs}$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin, $M$ = molar mass of air. **Step 2 — Define a reference velocity $c_0$ at 0°C (i.e., $T_{abs} = 273$ K):** $$c_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot 273}{M}} = 331.5 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio $c/c_0$ at an arbitrary temperature:** $$\frac{c}{c_0} = \sqrt{\frac{T_{abs}}{273}}$$ **Step 4 — Convert absolute temperature to Celsius using $T_{abs} = 273 + T$:** $$\frac{c}{c_0} = \sqrt{\frac{273 + T}{273}} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{T}{273}}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $c$:** $$\boxed{c = 331.5 \sqrt{1 + \frac{T}{273}} \text{ m/s}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This derivation appears in almost every KTU 2024 part (a) question on ultrasonics. Memorize the formula and the value $c_0 = 331.5$ m/s. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Doppler Shift in Ultrasonic Flow Measurement **Problem Context:** A Doppler flowmeter uses the frequency shift of a wave reflected from particles (or bubbles) in a flowing fluid. Derive the Doppler shift frequency $\Delta f$. **Step 1 — Set up the geometry:** The transducer emits a wave of frequency $f_0$ at an angle $\theta$ to the flow direction. The flow velocity is $v$, and the medium's sound velocity is $c$. **Step 2 — Frequency received by a moving particle:** A particle moving with velocity $v$ perceives a frequency $f_1$ (classic Doppler effect, observer moving, source stationary): $$f_1 = f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Frequency re-emitted (or scattered) by the moving particle back to the transducer:** The particle now acts as a moving source, sending $f_1$ back. The stationary transducer receives: $$f_2 = \frac{f_1}{1 - v \cos\theta / c} \approx f_1 \left(1 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$ (The approximation is valid because $v \ll c$.) **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:** $$f_2 \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right)^2$$ For small $\frac{v \cos\theta}{c}$ (a non-relativistic approximation, valid in virtually all industrial flows): $$f_2 \approx f_0 \left(1 + 2 \frac{v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Define the Doppler shift $\Delta f = f_2 - f_0$:** $$\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}}$$ This is the master Doppler equation. If $\theta = 0$ (transducer aligned with flow), $\Delta f$ is maximized. If $\theta = 90°$ (perpendicular), $\Delta f = 0$ (no useful measurement). ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thickness Measurement from Resonance **Problem Context:** An ultrasonic thickness gauge excites a wall (say a steel plate) into standing-wave resonance. Derive the relationship between plate thickness $d$ and resonance frequency $f$. **Step 1 — Standing wave condition:** For a standing wave to form, the plate thickness $d$ must equal an integer number of half-wavelengths: $$d = \frac{n \lambda}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$:** $$d = \frac{n \cdot c}{2 f}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $f$ (the resonance frequency):** $$f = \frac{n \cdot c}{2 d}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $d$ (the more useful engineering form):** $$\boxed{d = \frac{n \cdot c}{2 f}}$$ For the **fundamental** mode ($n = 1$): $$d = \frac{c}{2 f}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering insight:** This is why high-frequency probes are required for thin materials. A 1 mm thick steel plate ($c \approx 5960$ m/s) requires $f \approx 2.98$ MHz for fundamental resonance. A 1 cm plate resonates at ≈ 298 kHz. KTU problems frequently test this inverse relationship. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** An ultrasonic sensor is used to measure the level of water in a tank. The temperature of air above the water is 27°C. The sensor records a total time of flight of $6 \text{ ms}$. Find the distance from the sensor to the water surface. Take $c_0 = 331.5$ m/s. **Step 1 — Compute the velocity of sound in air at 27°C:** $$c = 331.5 \sqrt{1 + \frac{27}{273}} = 331.5 \sqrt{1 + 0.0989}$$ $$c = 331.5 \sqrt{1.0989} = 331.5 \times 1.0483 \approx 347.5 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the pulse-echo distance equation:** $$D = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{347.5 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$ $$D = \frac{2.085}{2} = 1.0425 \text{ m}$$ **Answer:** The water surface is approximately **1.04 m** below the sensor. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Ultrasonic Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Microcontroller
Clock Generator] --> B[Pulser
Driver Circuit] B --> C[Piezoelectric
Transducer] C --> D[Propagation
Medium] D --> E[Target
Interface or Flaw] E --> D D --> C C --> F[Receiver
Amplifier] F --> G[Time to Digital
Converter TDC] G --> H[Signal
Processor] H --> I[Display and
Output Module] J[User Input
Keypad] --> A K[Power Supply
DC Regulator] --> A K --> B K --> F ``` **Explanation of Flow:** - The **Microcontroller** generates timing pulses. - The **Pulser** sends a high-voltage spike (typically 100–400 V) to the transducer. - The **Transducer** converts electrical energy to a mechanical ultrasonic wave. - The wave travels through the **medium**, reflects off a **target**, and returns. - The **Receiver Amplifier** boosts the small echo signal (often microvolts to millivolts). - The **TDC** (Time-to-Digital Converter) measures the time difference with sub-nanosecond precision. - The **Signal Processor** applies algorithms (e.g., FFT for Doppler, peak detection for echo). - The **Display** shows the final physical quantity (level, thickness, flow rate, etc.). ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Pulse-Echo Level Meter ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Oscillator
40 kHz] --> B[Gate Pulse
Generator] B --> C[Transmitter
Piezo Crystal] C --> D[Air Column
Propagation Path] D --> E[Liquid
Surface] E --> D D --> F[Receiver
Piezo Crystal] F --> G[Echo Amplifier
and Filter] G --> H[Time Interval
Counter] B --> H H --> I[Microprocessor
Computation] I --> J[LCD Display
Level in cm] ``` **Key insight:** The **Gate Pulse Generator** and **Time Interval Counter** must be synchronized. The counter is started when the gate opens (transmit instant) and stopped when the echo exceeds a threshold (receive instant). The elapsed time is then converted to distance using $D = ct/2$. ## 4.3 Comparative Diagram: Generation Methods ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ultrasonic
Wave Generation] --> B[Piezoelectric
Method] A --> C[Magnetostrictive
Method] A --> D[Capacitive
Electrostatic Method] B --> B1[PZT or Quartz
Crystal] B1 --> B2[AC Voltage
20kHz to 10MHz] B2 --> B3[Inverse Piezo
Effect Strain] B3 --> B4[Mechanical
Vibration] C --> C1[Nickel or
Permendur Rod] C1 --> C2[AC Magnetic
Field Coil] C2 --> C3[Magnetostriction
Length Change] C3 --> C4[Mechanical
Vibration] D --> D1[Parallel Plate
Capacitor] D1 --> D2[Static DC Bias
Plus AC] D2 --> D3[Coulomb Force
Modulation] D3 --> D4[Mechanical
Vibration] ``` **Comparison of Methods:** | Property | Piezoelectric | Magnetostrictive | Capacitive | |---|---|---|---| | Frequency Range | 20 kHz – 10 GHz | 20 – 100 kHz | 20 – 200 kHz | | Efficiency | High (60–95%) | Moderate (30–60%) | Low | | Coupling Medium | Air, water, gel, solid | Mostly liquids & solids | Mostly gases | | Common Use | Sensors, NDT, medical | High-power cleaning, sonar | Gas flow at high temperature | ## 4.4 Mermaid Process Diagram: Flaw Detection Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start Scan] --> P2[Apply Couplant
Gel or Water] P2 --> P3[Place Probe on
Test Surface] P3 --> P4[Transmit Ultrasonic
Pulse] P4 --> P5{Encounter
Reflector} P5 -->|No Reflector| P6[Receive Backwall
Echo Only] P5 -->|Flaw Present| P7[Receive Flaw Echo
Before Backwall] P6 --> P8[Analyze Signal
on CRT or A-scan] P7 --> P8 P8 --> P9[Determine Flaw
Location and Size] P9 --> P10[End Scan
Report Generated] ``` **Engineering Significance:** The time delay between the initial pulse and the flaw echo gives the depth of the flaw. The amplitude of the flaw echo (relative to a reference reflector) gives an estimate of flaw size. This is the foundation of **A-scan, B-scan, and C-scan** ultrasonic NDT techniques used in KTU Module 3 case studies. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Definition of Ultrasonic Waves **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** Define ultrasonic waves. Mention any two properties that make them suitable for industrial measurement applications. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Ultrasonic waves are mechanical, longitudinal (or shear, depending on medium) sound waves whose frequency lies above the audible range of the human ear, i.e., **greater than 20 kHz**. - **Property 1 (1 mark):** They have **very short wavelengths**, allowing them to detect very small flaws and features in materials. - **Property 2 (1 mark):** They can be **highly directional**, enabling them to be aimed at specific targets for accurate ranging and imaging. - *(Bonus acceptable point)*: They are **non-destructive** and can travel through opaque media, making them ideal for non-invasive inspection. ### Question 2: Piezoelectric Effect **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** Differentiate between the direct and inverse piezoelectric effects. Why are both effects important in ultrasonic instrumentation? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Direct Piezoelectric Effect (1 mark):** When a mechanical stress is applied to certain crystals (e.g., quartz, PZT), an electrical charge appears on the crystal surfaces. This converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. - **Inverse Piezoelectric Effect (1 mark):** When an alternating voltage is applied across the crystal, it undergoes a mechanical deformation proportional to the field. This converts electrical energy into mechanical vibration. - **Importance in Instrumentation (1 mark):** The **direct effect** is used in the **receiver** (to convert returning echoes into voltage), while the **inverse effect** is used in the **transmitter** (to launch the ultrasonic pulse). The same crystal can perform both functions, which is the basis of pulse-echo operation. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice) ### Question A: Time-of-Flight and Level Measurement **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** (a) **Derive the relation between time of flight and distance in a pulse-echo ultrasonic system. (7 marks)** (b) **An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted 4 m above the bottom of an open water tank. The air above the water is at 47°C. The total time of flight measured by the sensor is 22 ms. Determine: (i) the speed of sound in air at 47°C, and (ii) the actual level of water in the tank. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Consider an ultrasonic transducer at point A. It transmits a pulse. The pulse travels distance D to a reflector at point B, and the echo returns the same distance D back to A. **Step 2 (2 marks):** The one-way time is $t_1 = D/c$. The return time is $t_2 = D/c$. Total time of flight $t$ is: $$t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{D}{c} + \frac{D}{c} = \frac{2D}{c}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Rearranging: $$D = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** This is the fundamental equation. Note that the factor of 2 arises **only** in pulse-echo mode; in through-transmission mode (two transducers), the factor of 2 is absent. **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound (3 marks):** Using $c_0 = 331.5$ m/s and $T = 47$°C: $$c = 331.5 \sqrt{1 + \frac{47}{273}} = 331.5 \sqrt{1.1722}$$ $$c = 331.5 \times 1.0827 \approx 358.9 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the distance from sensor to water (2 marks):** $$D = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{358.9 \times 22 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{7.896}{2} \approx 3.948 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the water level (2 marks):** The sensor is 4 m above the tank bottom. The empty space (air column) is 3.948 m. So the water level above the bottom is: $$\text{Level} = 4 - 3.948 = 0.052 \text{ m} \approx 5.2 \text{ cm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in pulse-echo mode. This is the most common error and leads to **–3 marks** deduction. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Using 343 m/s (the speed of sound at 20°C) instead of recalculating for the given temperature. This leads to **–2 marks** deduction. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the empty distance (3.948 m) with the water level. The question asks for the *water level*, not the empty space. Read the question carefully! --- ### Question B: Doppler Flow Meter **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]** (a) **Explain the principle of operation of a Doppler ultrasonic flow meter with a neat block diagram. State the governing equation. (7 marks)** (b) **A Doppler flowmeter operates at 2 MHz in a pipe carrying a slurry. The angle between the transducer axis and the pipe axis is 45°. The sound velocity in the slurry is 1500 m/s. The measured Doppler shift is 280 Hz. Calculate the flow velocity. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Principle & Equation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (2 marks):** In a Doppler flowmeter, the transducer injects a high-frequency ultrasonic wave into the flowing fluid. The fluid contains small particles, bubbles, or discontinuities that act as scatterers. **Step 2 (2 marks):** When the wave strikes a moving particle, it is reflected with a **shifted frequency** because the particle is moving relative to the source (classical Doppler effect). The same transducer receives this frequency-shifted echo. **Step 3 (2 marks):** The shift $\Delta f$ is related to the particle (and hence fluid) velocity $v$ by: $$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$ where $f_0$ is the transmitted frequency, $\theta$ is the angle between the transducer beam and the flow direction, and $c$ is the velocity of sound in the medium. **Step 4 (1 mark):** **Block Diagram:** Oscillator → Transducer (Tx) → Fluid with scatterers → Transducer (Rx) → Mixer/Demodulator → Frequency Counter → Display of velocity. **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1 — List knowns (1 mark):** $f_0 = 2 \times 10^6$ Hz, $\theta = 45°$, $c = 1500$ m/s, $\Delta f = 280$ Hz. **Step 2 — Apply the Doppler equation (3 marks):** $$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$ $$280 = \frac{2 \times (2 \times 10^6) \times v \times \cos 45°}{1500}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for v (2 marks):** $$280 = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \times v \times 0.7071}{1500}$$ $$280 = \frac{2.8284 \times 10^6 \cdot v}{1500}$$ $$280 = 1885.6 \cdot v$$ $$v = \frac{280}{1885.6} \approx 0.1485 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Final answer with units (1 mark):** $$\boxed{v \approx 0.149 \text{ m/s} = 14.85 \text{ cm/s}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $\sin\theta$ instead of $\cos\theta$ in the Doppler equation. The angle is measured from the **flow direction**, not the perpendicular. **–2 marks** if wrong. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the factor of 2 in the Doppler equation (the wave travels to the particle and back). **–3 marks** if omitted. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing transmitted frequency with the Doppler shift. The Doppler shift is the *difference* between transmitted and received frequencies, NOT the received frequency itself. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this on your last revision night)** - ✅ **Audible limit:** 20 kHz. **Ultrasonic:** > 20 kHz. **Hypersonic:** > 1 GHz. - ✅ **Generation:** Primarily by **inverse piezoelectric effect** in PZT or quartz crystals. - ✅ **Reception:** By **direct piezoelectric effect** in the same crystal. - ✅ **Velocity in air:** $c = 331.5 \sqrt{1 + T/273}$ m/s, where T is in °C. At 20°C, c ≈ 343 m/s. - ✅ **Pulse-Echo Distance:** $D = ct/2$ (the factor of 2 is critical). - ✅ **Through-Transmission Distance:** $D = ct$ (no factor of 2). - ✅ **Doppler Shift:** $\Delta f = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$. Use $\cos\theta$, not $\sin\theta$. - ✅ **Resonance Thickness:** $d = n c / (2f)$. Higher frequency → thinner detectable layers. - ✅ **Acoustic Impedance:** $Z = \rho c$. Reflection coefficient $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$. - ✅ **Higher Z mismatch → more reflection** (used in flaw detection — flaws have different Z from surrounding material). - ✅ **Modes:** Pulse-Echo, Through-Transmission, Resonance, Doppler — know the application of each. - ✅ **Industrial Applications:** Level measurement, flow measurement, thickness gauging, flaw detection (NDT), cleaning, welding of plastics, medical imaging. - ✅ **Attenuation** increases with frequency and distance — limits the practical upper frequency. - ✅ **Couplant** (gel, water, oil) is essential between the transducer and test surface to eliminate air gaps (Z_air is very low, causing near-total reflection). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked for *time of flight*, check whether the system is pulse-echo (factor 2) or through-transmission (no factor 2). This single distinction has decided the fate of many an exam script. Read the question stem **twice**.

topics.title: Microwave Level Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Microwave Level Switches ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Microwave Level Switch (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *non-contact* or *contact-based* point-level measurement device that uses electromagnetic (microwave) energy in the **GHz range (typically 5.8 GHz, 10 GHz, or 24 GHz)** to detect the presence or absence of process material at a predetermined switching point by exploiting the contrast in the **relative permittivity (dielectric constant, $\varepsilon_r$)** between the process medium and the empty (air) space above it. The device belongs to the broader class of **Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR)** and **Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW)** based level sensors. It is a binary switching instrument — its output is either *High* (material present) or *Low* (material absent), unlike a continuous level transmitter. ### 1.2 KTU Syllabus Categorization The 2024 Scheme places Microwave Level Switches within **Module 3: Level Measurement Systems**, alongside ultrasonic, radar, capacitance, and conductive level switches — all classified under *non-mechanical / electronic point-level sensors*. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bat and the Empty Tunnel > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine standing at one end of a long, straight tunnel and shouting *"Hello!"* If the tunnel is **empty**, the echo returns from the far wall with one clear, loud signature. If a person is standing **halfway inside the tunnel**, the echo comes back **much sooner** and sounds different. Your brain instantly *knows* whether something is near or far based purely on how long the sound takes to return. > > A Microwave Level Switch works identically. Instead of sound, it "shouts" using a **microwave pulse** into a probe or open air. The moment material touches the sensing zone, the echo return time collapses, the reflection signature changes, and the electronics flip a relay — just like your brain flipping a mental "switch" when you hear the closer echo. ### 1.4 Operating Frequencies — Standard ISM Bands | Frequency Band | Typical Use | Wavelength $\lambda$ | |----------------|-------------|----------------------| | **5.8 GHz** | Bulk solids, low-dielectric liquids | $\approx$ **5.17 cm** | | **10 GHz** | Liquids, moderate dielectric | $\approx$ **3.0 cm** | | **24 GHz** | High-resolution, small vessels | $\approx$ **1.25 cm** | | **C-band (4–8 GHz)** | Coaxial GWR probes | $\approx$ **3.75–7.5 cm** | > [!IMPORTANT] > These frequencies lie inside the **ISM (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) bands**, allocated by the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) so that no licence is required for industrial use. This is why KTU examiners expect students to recognise that the *operating frequency is intentionally chosen to be globally licence-free*. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Underlying Physical Phenomenon Microwave level switches exploit one fundamental electromagnetic principle: **when a propagating electromagnetic wave encounters a sudden change in the medium's permittivity, a portion of the wave is reflected**. The amplitude and phase of the reflected wave depend on the *mismatch* in dielectric properties. > [!NOTE] > **Why microwaves and not radio waves?** Microwaves occupy the **1 GHz – 300 GHz** band. At these frequencies, waves are *highly directional*, propagate in straight lines with minimal diffraction, and are *strongly affected* by dielectric contrast — making them ideal for compact, precise level detection. ### 2.2 Two Architectural Variants **Variant 1: Through-Air Microwave Switch (Free-Space Radar Switch)** - Microwave energy is radiated from a **horn antenna** into the air space above the material. - A second antenna (or the same antenna in pulse-echo mode) receives the reflected signal. - The reflection occurs at the **air-to-material interface** when material rises into the sensing zone. - Best for: *bulk solids, dusty environments, large silos*. **Variant 2: Guided Wave Radar (GWR) Switch — Contact Type** - The microwave pulse is **confined inside a metallic probe** (rod, coaxial, or flexible cable). - Energy is guided to the material surface, where reflection occurs. - The metallic waveguide *shields* the wave from external interference. - Best for: *aggressive chemicals, high-pressure vessels, foamy liquids, low-dielectric media (εr ≥ 1.4)*. ### 2.3 Detailed Working Logic The operation follows five sequential steps: 1. **Pulse Generation:** A low-power microwave pulse (≈ 1 ns duration, peak power < 10 mW) is generated by a Gunn diode or synthesised VCO inside the sensor head. 2. **Propagation:** The pulse travels through the probe/air at a velocity governed by the medium's relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. 3. **Reflection at Interface:** Upon hitting the boundary between two media of different $\varepsilon_r$, part of the energy is reflected back. The **Reflection Coefficient** is given by: $$\Gamma = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} - \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}$$ 4. **Echo Reception:** The returned pulse is captured and timestamped with picosecond resolution. 5. **Decision & Output:** The microcontroller compares the time-of-flight against a *threshold* corresponding to the *installed switching height*. If the echo arrives sooner than the threshold → material is present → relay energises. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the exact mathematical toolkit required for KTU University Exam problems on this topic.** Memorise the symbols and physical meaning. | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units / Constants | |---|---------|------------------|-------------------| | 1 | $v = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Velocity of EM wave inside a dielectric | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** | | 2 | $t = \dfrac{2 \cdot d}{v} = \dfrac{2d\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$ | Round-trip time of flight for distance $d$ | seconds (s) | | 3 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Calculated distance from echo time | metres (m) | | 4 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} - \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}$ | Reflection coefficient at interface | dimensionless | | 5 | $P_r = P_i \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert^2$ | Reflected power vs incident power | Watts (W) | | 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Wavelength in the medium | metres (m) | | 7 | $\Delta t = t_{empty} - t_{full}$ | Switch decision criterion (time difference) | seconds (s) | | 8 | $f_{Doppler} = \dfrac{2 v_{level}}{\lambda}$ | Frequency shift for moving surfaces (rarely used for level) | Hz | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Microwave level switches are deployed in **oil refineries, LNG carriers, pharmaceutical reactors, cement silos, and food-grade tanks** where conventional float switches would fail due to *corrosion, fouling, sticky media, or extreme temperatures up to 450 °C*. They are the *only* point-level sensor that works in **cryogenic LNG (–196 °C)** and **molten metal (1600 °C)**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Time of Flight in a Two-Media System (TDR Principle) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Context (Model KTU Numericals):** A guided-wave radar level switch is mounted on top of a tank. The probe length is 1.2 m, the process medium is a chemical with $\varepsilon_r = 4.0$. Find the time-of-flight for the empty condition and the full condition (when material touches the probe tip). **Step 1: Establish the speed of the microwave inside the probe/air section.** The velocity of an EM wave in any medium is governed by the relationship $v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}$, and for a non-magnetic dielectric $\mu = \mu_0$, giving: $$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$ For the **air-filled section** above the material: $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (relative permittivity of air ≈ 1.0006 ≈ 1). $$v_{air} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{1}} = 3.0 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2: Apply the round-trip relation.** The pulse travels a distance $d$ *down* and $d$ *back*, so total path length is $2d$: $$t = \frac{2d}{v}$$ **Step 3: Evaluate the empty condition (no material).** When the tank is empty, the pulse reflects from the **probe end** (open circuit) at distance $d = 1.2$ m. The path is entirely through air. $$t_{empty} = \frac{2 \times 1.2}{3.0 \times 10^8}$$ $$t_{empty} = \frac{2.4}{3.0 \times 10^8} = 8.0 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{s}$$ $$t_{empty} = 8.0 \ \text{ns} \quad \text{(8 nanoseconds)}$$ **Step 4: Evaluate the full condition (material at probe tip).** When the tank fills, the pulse now travels 1.2 m through the medium (since the medium surrounds the probe and the reflection occurs at the *bottom* of the probe where the metal short-circuit reflects strongly). The medium is chemical with $\varepsilon_r = 4$. $$v_{medium} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$$ $$t_{full} = \frac{2 \times 1.2}{1.5 \times 10^8} = \frac{2.4}{1.5 \times 10^8} = 1.6 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{s}$$ $$t_{full} = 16.0 \ \text{ns} \quad \text{(16 nanoseconds)}$$ **Step 5: Switch decision criterion.** $$\Delta t = t_{full} - t_{empty} = 16.0 - 8.0 = 8.0 \ \text{ns}$$ > [!NOTE] > The controller compares actual measured $\Delta t$ against a calibrated threshold. Because the propagation speed in the higher-dielectric medium is *halved*, the echo **takes longer** in the full condition. The switch can resolve differences of **sub-nanosecond** magnitudes, giving it millimetric resolution. ### 3.2 Derivation: Reflection Coefficient and Material Detectability > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model KTU Problem:** A microwave switch is to be used to detect the level of a hydrocarbon liquid in a tank. Air ($\varepsilon_{r1} = 1.0$) lies above the liquid ($\varepsilon_{r2} = 2.3$). Determine the reflection coefficient. **Step 1: Apply the boundary reflection formula.** $$\Gamma = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} - \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}$$ **Step 2: Substitute the given values.** $$\Gamma = \frac{\sqrt{1.0} - \sqrt{2.3}}{\sqrt{1.0} + \sqrt{2.3}}$$ $$\Gamma = \frac{1.0 - 1.5166}{1.0 + 1.5166}$$ **Step 3: Simplify the numerator and denominator.** $$\Gamma = \frac{-0.5166}{2.5166} = -0.2053$$ **Step 4: Interpret the result.** $$|\Gamma|^2 = (-0.2053)^2 = 0.0421$$ This means **only 4.21 %** of the incident power is reflected — a *weak* reflection, but the sensor's high-sensitivity receiver can still detect it. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's note:** When $|\varepsilon_{r2} - \varepsilon_{r1}| \ge 1$, a microwave switch is reliable. For extremely low-dielectric media like LNG ($\varepsilon_r \approx 1.4$), a **coaxial probe** is mandatory because the *guided mode* amplifies the interaction. Free-space radar switches will *fail* below $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.5$. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Microwave vs Other Level Switches > [!IMPORTANT] > **This table is the single most important high-yield artefact for KTU exams.** It is *the* comparative chart examiners love to question. | Parameter | Microwave (GWR) | Capacitance | Ultrasonic | Conductive | Float | |-----------|------------------|-------------|------------|------------|-------| | Contact Type | Contact or Non-contact | Contact | Non-contact | Contact | Contact | | Independent of Density | **Yes** | No | Yes | No | No | | Independent of Viscosity | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | No | No | | Works with Foam | **Yes (coaxial)** | Limited | No | No | No | | Works with Vapour | **Yes** | Yes | No | Limited | Yes | | Works in Vacuum | **Yes** | Yes | No | Yes | Yes | | High Pressure | **Yes (up to 700 bar)** | Limited | Limited | Yes | Limited | | High Temperature | **Yes (up to 450 °C)** | Limited | Limited | Yes | Limited | | Affected by Material Buildup | **No** | Yes (errors) | No | Yes (fouling) | Yes (sticking) | | Cost | **High** | Low–Medium | Medium | Low | Very Low | ### 3.4 Advantages and Limitations — KTU Bulleted List **Advantages** - *True non-contact* option available → ideal for corrosive, abrasive, sterile media. - Unaffected by *temperature*, *pressure*, *density*, *viscosity*, *conductivity*, *vapours*, *turbulence*. - *Dielectric* sensing enables detection of *almost any* material — solids, liquids, slurries, pastes. - High *repeatability* (≤ 1 mm) and *fast response* (< 1 s). **Limitations** - *Foam* can attenuate free-space radar (coaxial GWR overcomes this). - *Metal obstructions* inside the tank reflect spurious signals. - *High cost* (typically 3×–5× an ultrasonic switch). - *Mounting geometry* in GWR requires a stilling well or bypass for turbulent media. - *Buildup on the probe tip* must be considered in hygienic applications. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Guided Wave Radar Level Switch The figure below presents a **block-level functional architecture** mapping every signal-processing stage from microwave emission to relay actuation. This is the most common KTU-exam style diagram (slightly generalised for clarity). ```mermaid graph TD A[Microwave VCO
Voltage Controlled Oscillator
f = 5.8 GHz nominal] --> B[Directional Coupler] B --> C[Process Probe
Coaxial or Rod type] C --> D[Material Surface
Dielectric Interface] D --> C C --> B B --> E[Low Noise Amplifier
LNA Gain = 30 dB] E --> F[Time Domain Detector
Threshold Comparator] F --> G[Microcontroller
Decision Logic] G --> H[Relay Output Module
DPDT Contact 5 A] G --> I[4-20 mA Optional Loop] G --> J[HMI Display
LED Status] K[Power Supply
24 V DC Isolated] --> A K --> G ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Through-Air Microwave Switch ```mermaid graph TD A[Pulse Generator
Gunn Diode Oscillator] --> B[Horn Antenna Transmitter] B --> C[Free Space Propagation
in Process Air] C --> D[Material Surface
at Switch Point] D --> E[Reflected Echo] E --> F[Horn Antenna Receiver] F --> G[Bandpass Filter
Centre f = 10 GHz] G --> H[Envelope Detector] H --> I[Comparator
Threshold = Vref] I --> J[Output Driver
Solid State Relay] K[Dielectric Lens
Beam Focusing] --> B ``` ### 4.3 Signal Flow — Decision Logic for the Switch ```mermaid graph LR A[Start Measurement] --> B[Transmit Pulse] B --> C[Wait for Echo] C --> D{Is Echo within
Switching Window?} D -->|Yes| E[Activate RELAY ON
Material Detected] D -->|No| F[Hold RELAY OFF
No Material] E --> G[Update HMI LED] F --> G G --> H[Wait 200 ms] H --> B ``` ### 4.4 Physical Mounting Reference | Mounting Style | Typical Application | Description | |----------------|---------------------|-------------| | **Top-Mounted Probe** | Liquids, slurry tanks | Probe hangs vertically into vessel | | **Side-Mounted Horn** | Silos, large vessels | Horn radiates horizontally across vessel | | **Insertion with Stilling Well** | Turbulent media | Probe inside a stilling well for stability | | **Bypass Chamber** | Interface detection | Two probes in external chamber | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** > **Define a microwave level switch. Why is the dielectric constant of the process medium a critical parameter for its operation? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A microwave level switch is a point-level measurement device that uses **electromagnetic waves in the GHz frequency range** to detect the presence or absence of a process medium at a pre-set location. It operates on the principle of reflection of microwave energy at the boundary between two media having different **relative permittivity (dielectric constant, $\varepsilon_r$)**. The dielectric constant is critical because the **reflection coefficient $\Gamma$** at the air-material interface is directly governed by the contrast in $\varepsilon_r$. A larger $\varepsilon_r$ difference produces a stronger reflected echo, ensuring reliable switching. The propagation velocity $v = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ also depends on $\varepsilon_r$, so the *time of flight* measurement is only meaningful when $\varepsilon_r$ is known accurately. For low-dielectric media ($\varepsilon_r < 2.5$), a **coaxial guided-wave probe** is required to overcome weak reflections. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** > **List any three advantages of microwave level switches over ultrasonic level switches. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Immunity to air-borne factors:** Unlike ultrasonic switches, microwave switches are **unaffected by dust, vapour, temperature gradients, and air turbulence** because microwaves are electromagnetic, not mechanical, waves. Ultrasound suffers from absorption and scattering in dusty environments. 2. **High-pressure and high-temperature capability:** Microwave (GWR) switches operate up to **700 bar and 450 °C**, far exceeding ultrasonic limits (typically < 5 bar, < 80 °C). 3. **Vacuum compatibility:** Microwave signals propagate in vacuum perfectly, whereas ultrasound *cannot* travel in a vacuum because it requires a physical medium for mechanical wave propagation. 4. (Optional bonus) **Smaller beam divergence:** At 24 GHz, the beam spreads less than ultrasonic beams at 50 kHz, allowing installation in narrow vessels. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note for Students (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and *must* be answered as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided between two full questions (Q11 vs Q12). Each question is internally sub-divided into two equal halves. --- #### **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** **(a)** With the aid of a functional block diagram, explain the working of a **Guided Wave Radar (GWR) microwave level switch**. Discuss the role of the **time-domain reflectometry (TDR)** principle in determining the switching action. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The GWR microwave level switch operates on the TDR principle, where a low-power microwave pulse is launched into a metallic probe (rod or coaxial cable) extending into the process vessel. **Block Diagram — Working Sequence:** 1. **Microwave VCO** generates a pulse of frequency ≈ 5.8 GHz with a width of ≈ 1 ns. 2. The pulse is launched into the **probe** via a directional coupler. 3. The pulse propagates along the probe at velocity $v = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$. 4. Upon reaching the **material surface** (or probe end if empty), the pulse encounters a sudden change in $\varepsilon_r$. 5. A **partial reflection** occurs, characterised by the reflection coefficient $\Gamma = (\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,air}} - \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,material}}) / (\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,air}} + \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,material}})$. 6. The reflected pulse returns to the **low-noise amplifier (LNA)**, which amplifies the weak signal. 7. A **time-domain detector** measures the round-trip time $t = 2d/v$. 8. The **microcontroller** compares $t$ with a pre-loaded threshold corresponding to the installed switching height. If the material is present, the round-trip distance *decreases*, the echo arrives *sooner*, and the comparator energises the **relay output**. **Role of TDR:** TDR converts a *time measurement* into a *distance measurement*. Because the pulse is confined inside the metallic probe, the propagation is highly repeatable and immune to external interference, foam, and turbulence. The switching decision is purely *time-based*, making it deterministic and drift-free. --- **(b)** A GWR level switch with a **rod probe of length 2.0 m** is used to detect the interface between air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$) and a process liquid ($\varepsilon_r = 9$). Calculate: **(i)** the velocity of the microwave pulse in the liquid, **(ii)** the round-trip time when the tank is full, and **(iii)** the wavelength inside the liquid at 5.8 GHz. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $d = 2.0$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 9$, $f = 5.8$ GHz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **(i) Velocity inside the liquid:** $$v_{liquid} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{9}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3} = 1.0 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Round-trip time when full:** $$t_{full} = \frac{2d}{v_{liquid}} = \frac{2 \times 2.0}{1.0 \times 10^8} = \frac{4.0}{1.0 \times 10^8} = 4.0 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{s}$$ $$t_{full} = 40 \ \text{ns}$$ **(iii) Wavelength inside the liquid:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5.8 \times 10^9 \times 3} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.74 \times 10^{10}}$$ $$\lambda = 1.724 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m} = 17.24 \ \text{mm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical insight:** Since the rod probe length (2000 mm) is much greater than $\lambda$ (17.24 mm), the probe behaves as a long transmission line — justifying the use of TDR for accurate echo detection. --- #### **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]** **(a)** Compare the working principles, construction, and applications of **through-air microwave** and **guided-wave radar (GWR) microwave level switches**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected by Examiner):** | Feature | Through-Air Microwave | Guided Wave Radar (GWR) | |---------|------------------------|--------------------------| | **Propagation Path** | Free space (air) inside vessel | Inside a metallic probe (rod/coaxial) | | **Antenna / Probe** | Horn antenna at top of vessel | Rigid rod, flexible cable, or coaxial probe | | **Minimum $\varepsilon_r$** | $\ge 2.5$ (free space) | $\ge 1.4$ (coaxial) or $\ge 1.9$ (rod) | | **Sensitivity to Foam** | High (foam attenuates) | Low (coaxial guides wave) | | **Sensitivity to Vapour / Dust** | Low (microwaves pass) | Very low (probe shields) | | **Maximum Pressure** | Limited (window) | Up to 700 bar | | **Maximum Temperature** | ≈ 200 °C | Up to 450 °C | | **Best For** | Bulk solids, large silos, dusty env. | Aggressive liquids, cryogenics, hygienic | | **Mounting** | Top, through window | Top, side, or bypass | **Conclusion:** Through-air is preferred for *open-vessel bulk solids*, whereas GWR dominates in *sealed, high-P/T, hygienic, or low-dielectric* applications. --- **(b)** Explain why **microwave level switches are independent of density, viscosity, and conductivity** of the process medium. Also discuss **two industrial applications** where this independence is the *key enabling feature*. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Why microwave switches are independent of these properties:** 1. **Density independence:** Microwaves interact with the medium through *electromagnetic polarisation of molecules* (dielectric polarisation), not through *mass* (gravitational or buoyancy effects like in floats). A material's density does not appear in the time-of-flight equation $t = 2d\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/c$. Therefore, whether the medium is dense (mercury, $\rho = 13{,}600$ kg/m³) or light (kerosene, $\rho = 800$ kg/m³), the *reflection* occurs identically at the air–medium interface. 2. **Viscosity independence:** Viscosity affects *fluid flow* and *drag* on mechanical sensors (e.g., floats may stick in high-viscosity media). Microwaves have *no mechanical coupling* with the medium; they are pure electromagnetic waves, so viscous drag is irrelevant. 3. **Conductivity independence:** Conductivity governs the behaviour of *capacitive* and *conductive* level sensors. Microwaves, however, respond to the *real part* of the complex permittivity (the *dielectric constant*) and the *imaginary part* (loss factor) only mildly at GHz frequencies. Hence both highly conductive (acid) and non-conductive (oil) media can be detected with the *same* sensor, provided $\varepsilon_r$ contrast exists. **Two industrial applications where this is the key enabler:** 1. **LNG Cargo Tank Level Detection (–196 °C, cryogenic):** LNG is extremely cold, low-viscosity, and low-dielectric ($\varepsilon_r \approx 1.4$). A float would freeze; a capacitance probe would fail due to low $\varepsilon_r$. Only a *coaxial GWR microwave switch* works reliably. 2. **Pharmaceutical Fermenter (sterile, viscous broth):** Fermentation broths have *varying density* (bubbles, suspended solids, changing sugar content) and high *viscosity*. A float would jam; a conductivity probe would foul. A *non-contact through-air microwave switch* mounted on a sight-glass window performs reliably without ever touching the sterile medium — preserving GMP compliance. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in time-of-flight equations** — examiners deduct 1 mark for not multiplying by 2 (round trip). > 2. **Confusing GWR with through-air radar** — they have *different* minimum-$\varepsilon_r$ requirements; stating the same number for both is a common error. > 3. **Using SI units inconsistently** — if $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, the answer must be in *seconds*, not microseconds, unless explicitly converted. Examiners award 0.5 marks for unit mismatch. > 4. **Omitting the block diagram** in 7-mark questions — KTU mandates a diagram for full marks. A textual-only answer is capped at 5/7. > 5. **Stating "microwaves are sound waves"** — an instant *zero* on any level-measurement question. They are electromagnetic, not mechanical. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Summary — Read this the night before the exam.** - Microwave level switches operate in the **ISM bands: 5.8 GHz, 10 GHz, 24 GHz**. - The fundamental principle is **Time Domain Reflectometry (TDR)** or **FMCW** — measuring round-trip time of an EM pulse. - The controlling physical property is the **relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$** of the process medium. - Two architectural variants: **Through-Air (Free-Space)** and **Guided-Wave Radar (GWR)**. - The master formula to remember: $\quad d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ - Reflection coefficient: $\quad \Gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} - \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}$ - Microwave switches are **independent of density, viscosity, conductivity**, but **dependent on dielectric contrast**. - They work in **vacuum, vacuum-clean cryogenics, and high-pressure/temperature** environments — making them unique. - For low-$\varepsilon_r$ media (< 2.5), use a **coaxial GWR probe**. - Always use $\mathbf{c = 3 \times 10^8}$ **m/s** as the speed of EM wave in vacuum. - Typical response time: **< 1 second**; repeatability: **≤ 1 mm**. - Common applications: **LNG tanks, oil refineries, pharmaceutical reactors, cement silos, food-grade vessels**. - Never confuse **microwave (electromagnetic, GHz)** with **ultrasonic (mechanical, kHz)** — they are physically fundamentally different wave types.

topics.title: Radar and Vibrating Type Level Sensors, Level Sensor Selection and Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Radar and Vibrating Type Level Sensors, Level Sensor Selection and Application ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Radar Level Sensor — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Radar Level Sensor (RLS):** A non-contact, continuous level measurement instrument that employs microwave electromagnetic waves (typically in the **C-band (5.8 GHz)**, **K-band (24 GHz)**, or **W-band (75–85 GHz)**) transmitted from a sensor head towards the surface of a process medium. The time delay between the transmitted and reflected pulse (or the frequency shift in FMCW systems) is processed to compute the precise distance to the material surface, from which the level is inferred using the known tank geometry. **Operating Principle:** Time-of-Flight (ToF) of electromagnetic radiation travelling at the speed of light ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s). --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Bat and the Well > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine standing at the top of a deep empty well and shouting "Hello!" into it. The deeper the well, the longer it takes for the echo to return to your ears. Your brain instantly calculates the depth by simply noting *how long* you had to wait for the sound to bounce back. > A **Radar Level Sensor** does exactly the same thing — but instead of sound, it shouts a beam of **invisible microwave energy** (a billion times faster than sound) downward at the material surface. The instrument's internal electronics measure the round-trip time of this "echo" with picosecond accuracy and converts it into a digital reading of the empty space above the material (the **ullage**), and hence the **level** of the product inside the tank. --- ### 1.3 Vibrating (Tuning Fork) Level Sensor — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Vibrating Fork Level Sensor:** A point-level (binary: HIGH/LOW) detection device consisting of two parallel tines (forks) driven to their mechanical **resonant frequency** by a pair of piezoelectric crystals. When the surrounding medium changes from air (or gas/vapor) to a bulk solid or liquid, the fork's effective mass, damping, and resonant frequency shift. This shift is detected electronically and converted into a switching output. **Operating Principle:** Mechanical resonance frequency shift due to changes in the surrounding medium's mass and damping characteristics. --- ### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Wine Glass and the Finger > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Take a thin-stemmed wine glass and flick it with your finger — it rings with a clear, high-pitched musical tone. Now dip the rim into a bowl of water. The tone becomes a dull, muted thud. The water has changed how the glass vibrates because it added *mass* and *damping* to the moving glass. > A **Vibrating Fork Sensor** behaves identically. Its two metal tines are continuously "flicked" electronically by a piezo crystal thousands of times per second. As long as only air surrounds them, they vibrate at a sharp, clean, well-defined frequency. The instant a liquid or bulk solid touches the tines, the frequency drops, the amplitude damps, and a detector circuit immediately registers this — triggering an alarm, a pump shutdown, or a fill-valve closure. --- ### 1.5 Key Engineering Metrics | Parameter | Radar Sensor | Vibrating Fork Sensor | |---|---|---| | **Output Type** | Continuous (4–20 mA, HART, Profibus) | Point / Switch (Relay, PNP/NPN) | | **Measurement Range** | Up to **40 m** (free-space) | Fixed insertion depth (typically **40 mm to 6 m**) | | **Accuracy** | ±1 mm to ±10 mm (frequency dependent) | Switching repeatability ±1 mm | | **Frequency of Operation** | **6 GHz / 26 GHz / 80 GHz** microwave | Mechanical resonance ≈ **1.2 kHz** | | **Process Connection** | Flanged or threaded, top-mount | Threaded, side or top mount | | **Dielectric Requirement** | $\varepsilon_r \geq 1.4$ (free-space) / $\geq 1.9$ (guided) | None (works on virtually all powders & liquids) | | **Contact with Medium** | **Non-contact** (free-space) or contact (GWR probe) | **Contact** with medium | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Radar Level Sensor — Theoretical Breakdown #### 2.1.1 Two Principal Techniques 1. **Pulsed Time-Domain Reflectometry (TDR / Impulse Radar)** - Transmits extremely short microwave bursts (nanosecond pulse width). - Measures the round-trip time $t$ of the reflected echo. - Distance is computed as $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$. 2. **Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) Radar** - Transmits a continuous microwave signal whose frequency is **linearly swept** (chirped) over a known bandwidth $\Delta f$. - The reflected signal mixes with the currently transmitted signal, producing a **beat frequency** $f_b$ proportional to the time delay. - Distance is computed from $f_b$, $\Delta f$, sweep period $T$, and the speed of light $c$. #### 2.1.2 Two Physical Configurations - **Free-Space (Through-Air) Radar:** Antenna radiates into the open tank headspace. Best for large, clean, non-foaming liquids. - **Guided Wave Radar (GWR):** Microwave is guided along a probe (coaxial, rod, or flexible cable). The wave slows down because of the lower propagation velocity inside the dielectric probe. #### 2.1.3 Why and How — Step-by-Step Logic - **Why microwaves?** Microwaves are unaffected by **vapor**, **dust**, **temperature gradients**, and **vacuum** — they are the most reliable medium for harsh industrial environments. - **Why measure distance first, not level directly?** Because radar physically measures the empty distance (ullage) from the flange to the surface. Level = Tank Height − Measured Distance. - **Why does the dielectric constant matter?** At low $\varepsilon_r$, the surface reflects very little microwave energy (poor echo). The general rule: $\varepsilon_r \geq 1.4$ (free-space) or $\varepsilon_r \geq 1.9$ (guided) is required for reliable detection. - **Why FMCW dominates modern systems?** It provides much higher signal-to-noise ratio, finer resolution, and inherent self-calibration compared to simple pulse radar. --- ### 2.2 Vibrating Fork Sensor — Theoretical Breakdown #### 2.2.1 The Two-Crystal Piezoelectric Drive System - **Drive Crystal (Excitation):** Receives an oscillating electrical signal, converts it to mechanical vibration, and continuously energises the fork at its natural resonant frequency. - **Pickup Crystal (Sensing):** Converts the mechanical vibration back to an electrical signal that is fed into a closed-loop oscillator circuit. - The system operates as a **phase-locked loop (PLL)** that maintains resonance automatically. #### 2.2.2 Why and How — Step-by-Step Logic - **Why two crystals?** A single piezo cannot both drive and sense with stability. The dual-crystal arrangement allows active resonance tracking and self-compensation. - **Why does the frequency change when material touches the fork?** Adding mass lowers the natural frequency ($f_n \propto \sqrt{k/m}$); viscous and cohesive damping lowers amplitude. Both are detected. - **Why is it unaffected by foam, bubbles, or dielectric constant?** Because it senses *mechanical contact* (mass and damping), not electrical or electromagnetic properties. - **Why use it for point-level only?** Its switching behaviour is binary by design — it cannot linearly resolve a continuous level profile. Ideal for overfill protection, dry-run protection, and high-high / low-low trips. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for Radar and Vibrating Fork Sensors — Memorise for ESE.** | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Pulsed-radar distance from time-of-flight | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $t$ in seconds | | 2 | $L = H_{tank} - d$ | Level from measured ullage distance | All in metres | | 3 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot T \cdot f_b}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$ | FMCW radar distance from beat frequency | $T$ = sweep period, $\Delta f$ = sweep bandwidth | | 4 | $c' = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Velocity of microwave inside GWR probe | $c' \approx 1.6 \times 10^8$ m/s for $\varepsilon_r \approx 3.5$ (coaxial) | | 5 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}}$ | Natural frequency of vibrating fork | $k$ = stiffness, $m_{eff}$ = effective mass | | 6 | $\Delta f \propto m_{added}$ | Frequency shift proportional to added mass | Linear region of detection | | 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_{radar}}$ | Wavelength of microwave signal | At 26 GHz: $\lambda \approx 11.5$ mm | | 8 | $\eta = \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} - \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r2}}}\right)^2$ | Power reflection coefficient at material surface | Higher $\varepsilon_r$ = stronger echo | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Radar sensors** are the workhorse of the **oil & gas** industry (crude storage tanks, LNG carriers), the **chemical** industry (aggressive acids, solvents), and **water/wastewater** (open-channel and reservoir level). - **Vibrating fork sensors** are ubiquitous in **pharma** (powder hoppers), **food processing** (flour, sugar, milk powder silos), **mining** (ore bins), and **OEM pump protection** (dry-run safeguard on centrifugal pumps). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the FMCW Radar Distance Equation The FMCW radar emits a linear frequency chirp. At any instant $\tau$ within the sweep, the transmitted frequency is: $$ f_{tx}(\tau) = f_0 + \dfrac{\Delta f}{T} \cdot \tau $$ The reflected signal returns with a time delay of $t_d = \dfrac{2d}{c}$ (round-trip).
