# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Cross-Correlation Function

### Step 1: Define the Two Sensor Signals

Let $x(t)$ be the upstream sensor signal and $y(t)$ be the downstream sensor signal. Both are assumed to be **ergodic, stationary random processes** with zero mean. The fluid disturbance travels with mean velocity $v$, so a pattern observed at $x$ at time $t$ reaches $y$ at time $t + \tau_d$, where $\tau_d = L/v$.

Therefore, ignoring attenuation and shape distortion:

$$y(t) = \alpha \, x(t - \tau_d) + n(t)$$

where $\alpha$ is an amplitude scaling factor and $n(t)$ is uncorrelated measurement noise.

### Step 2: Define the Cross-Correlation Function

The cross-correlation function between $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ is defined as the time-average of their product, with a deliberate time shift $\tau$:

$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, y(t + \tau) \, dt$$

**Reasoning:** We slide $y(t)$ past $x(t)$ by a time shift $\tau$. If $\tau$ matches the true physical delay $\tau_d$, the two patterns will align perfectly, and the integrated product will be large. For all other $\tau$, the signals will be misaligned and (because they are random) the product will average out to nearly zero.

### Step 3: Substitute the Signal Model

Substitute $y(t+\tau) = \alpha \, x(t + \tau - \tau_d) + n(t+\tau)$ into the definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{xy}(\tau) &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \left[ \alpha \, x(t + \tau - \tau_d) + n(t+\tau) \right] dt \\
&= \alpha \, \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, x(t + \tau - \tau_d) \, dt + \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, n(t+\tau) \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Statistical Properties

- The first integral is the **auto-correlation** of $x(t)$ at lag $\tau - \tau_d$:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, x(t + \tau - \tau_d) \, dt = R_{xx}(\tau - \tau_d)$$

- The signal $x(t)$ and the noise $n(t)$ are **statistically independent** (uncorrelated), so their cross-average is zero:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, n(t+\tau) \, dt = R_{xn}(\tau) = 0$$

### Step 5: Final Cross-Correlation Expression

Combining both results:

$$
\boxed{R_{xy}(\tau) = \alpha \, R_{xx}(\tau - \tau_d)}
$$

**Interpretation:** The cross-correlation function between the upstream and downstream signals is simply a **scaled, time-shifted replica of the upstream auto-correlation function**. The peak of $R_{xy}(\tau)$ occurs at $\tau = \tau_d$, because the auto-correlation $R_{xx}$ is always maximum at zero lag (i.e., $R_{xx}(0) \ge R_{xx}(\tau)$ for any $\tau$).

### Step 6: Recovery of Velocity and Flow Rate

The peak detection yields $\tau_d$:

$$v = \frac{L}{\tau_d}$$

$$Q = A \cdot v = \frac{A \cdot L}{\tau_d}$$

## 3.3 KTU-Style Numerical Solved Example

> **Problem:** A cross-correlation flow meter has two capacitance sensors separated by $L = 0.15$ m on a 75 mm ID pipe carrying a paper pulp slurry. The cross-correlation peak occurs at a time delay of $\tau_d = 60$ ms. Calculate the (a) mean flow velocity, (b) volumetric flow rate, and (c) mass flow rate if slurry density is $1050 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.

### Solution

**Part (a):** Velocity

$$v = \frac{L}{\tau_d} = \frac{0.15 \, \text{m}}{0.060 \, \text{s}} = 2.5 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Part (b):** Volumetric Flow Rate

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$Q = A \cdot v = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.5 = 1.104 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 39.76 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Part (c):** Mass Flow Rate

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 1050 \times 1.104 \times 10^{-2} = 11.6 \, \text{kg/s}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define cross-correlation flow meter. State the principle of operation.**

> **Model Answer:**
> A cross-correlation flow meter is a transit-time flow measurement instrument that uses two identical non-intrusive sensors separated by a known axial distance $L$ along the pipe.
> **Principle:** Naturally occurring random flow disturbances (turbulence eddies, density fluctuations, particle clusters) are detected first by the upstream sensor and, after a transit time delay $\tau_d$, by the downstream sensor. The cross-correlation function $R_{xy}(\tau)$ is computed continuously; the shift $\tau$ at which it peaks equals $\tau_d$. The flow velocity is computed as $v = L / \tau_d$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four advantages of cross-correlation flow meters over conventional flow meters.**

> **Model Answer (any four):**
> 1. Suitable for **two-phase, slurry, and opaque flows** where other meters fail.
> 2. **Non-intrusive** — no moving parts, no pressure drop.
> 3. **No calibration fluid required** — can be calibrated in-situ.
> 4. Independent of fluid's **electrical conductivity, optical clarity, or acoustic homogeneity**.
> 5. Excellent **long-term stability** with no drift.
> 6. Bidirectional flow measurement capability. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Set A]`

**(a)** Derive the cross-correlation function for a cross-correlation flow meter and show that its peak occurs at the true transit time $\tau_d$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a cross-correlation flow meter for measuring two-phase flow in a slurry pipeline. List the sensors commonly used. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

Let the upstream and downstream sensor signals be $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ respectively. For a disturbance convected at mean velocity $v$ over axial separation $L$, the true transit time is $\tau_d = L/v$. The downstream signal is the delayed upstream signal, possibly with amplitude scaling $\alpha$ and additive noise $n(t)$:

$$y(t) = \alpha \, x(t - \tau_d) + n(t)$$

The cross-correlation function is defined as:

$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, y(t + \tau) \, dt$$

Substituting $y(t+\tau)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{xy}(\tau) &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \left[ \alpha \, x(t + \tau - \tau_d) + n(t+\tau) \right] dt \\
&= \alpha \, \underbrace{\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, x(t + \tau - \tau_d) \, dt}_{R_{xx}(\tau - \tau_d)} + \underbrace{\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, n(t+\tau) \, dt}_{= 0 \text{ (independent)}} \\
\therefore R_{xy}(\tau) &= \alpha \, R_{xx}(\tau - \tau_d)
\end{aligned}
$$

The auto-correlation function $R_{xx}(\cdot)$ attains its global maximum at zero lag, i.e., $R_{xx}(0) \ge R_{xx}(\tau)$ for all $\tau$. Therefore $R_{xy}(\tau)$ attains its maximum at $\tau = \tau_d$.

> **Step-by-step marks allocation:** Signal model (2 marks), Definition of $R_{xy}$ (1 mark), Substitution and statistical independence argument (3 marks), Peak-shift conclusion (1 mark). **(7 marks total)**

#### Part (b) — Block Diagram & Operation (7 marks)

> **Block Diagram Description** (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block): The slurry pipeline carries solid-laden liquid. Two **capacitive** or **conductivity** sensors (preferred for conductive slurries) or **optical** sensors (for non-conductive slurries) are mounted flush on the pipe wall, separated by distance $L$. The pre-amplified, band-limited, and digitized signals are fed to a DSP that performs cross-correlation in the time domain (or via FFT in the frequency domain for speed). The peak lag $\tau_d$ is detected using polynomial interpolation around the maximum. The mean velocity $v = L / \tau_d$ and flow rate $Q = A \cdot v$ are then displayed and output as 4–20 mA / HART.

**Commonly Used Sensor Pairs:**
1. **Capacitive sensors** — for non-conductive liquids and slurries.
2. **Conductivity / Electrical resistance probes** — for conductive slurries (e.g., paper pulp, mining slurries).
3. **Optical (photoelectric) sensors** — for clear or semi-opaque liquids with refractive index fluctuations.
4. **Ultrasonic Doppler sensors** — for bubble-laden or particle-laden flows.
5. **Strain-gauge / piezoelectric wall sensors** — to detect pressure fluctuations transmitted through the pipe wall.

> **Marks allocation:** Block diagram (3 marks), Working explanation (2 marks), Sensor types (2 marks). **(7 marks total)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Set B]`

**(a)** Explain the mathematical basis of the cross-correlation technique. Define the cross-correlation function and discuss its properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cross-correlation flow meter has two sensors spaced $L = 0.20$ m apart. The cross-correlation peak is observed at $\tau_d = 40$ ms. The pipe inner diameter is 100 mm. Calculate the (i) mean flow velocity, (ii) volumetric flow rate in $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$, and (iii) mass flow rate in kg/hr if the fluid density is $1200 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Mathematical Basis (7 marks)

The **mathematical basis** of the cross-correlation technique lies in the statistical measure of similarity between two signals as a function of time shift $\tau$.

**Definition:**
For two jointly stationary random processes $x(t)$ and $y(t)$, the cross-correlation function is:

$$R_{xy}(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t) \, y(t + \tau) \, dt$$

**Key Properties:**
1. **Not commutative in general:** $R_{xy}(\tau) = R_{yx}(-\tau)$.
2. **Boundedness:** $\vert R_{xy}(\tau) \vert \le \sqrt{R_{xx}(0) \cdot R_{yy}(0)}$.
3. **Symmetry of auto-correlation:** $R_{xx}(\tau) = R_{xx}(-\tau)$; maximum at $\tau = 0$.
4. **Peak location reveals delay:** If $y(t) = \alpha \, x(t - \tau_d)$, then $R_{xy}(\tau)$ peaks at $\tau = \tau_d$.
5. **Noise rejection:** Integration over $T$ averages out uncorrelated noise, improving SNR by $\sqrt{BT}$.
6. **Normalized form:** $\rho_{xy}(\tau) = R_{xy}(\tau) / (\sigma_x \sigma_y) \in [-1, 1]$.

> **Marks allocation:** Definition (2 marks), Five properties (3 marks), Peak location principle (2 marks). **(7 marks total)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $L = 0.20 \, \text{m}$, $\tau_d = 40 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s}$, $D = 100 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$, $\rho = 1200 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Mean flow velocity:**

$$v = \frac{L}{\tau_d} = \frac{0.20}{0.040} = 5.0 \, \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$Q = A \cdot v = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.0 = 3.927 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$:

$$Q = 3.927 \times 10^{-2} \times 3600 = 141.4 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 1200 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-2} = 47.12 \, \text{kg/s}$$

Converting to kg/hr:

$$\dot{m} = 47.12 \times 3600 = 1.696 \times 10^{5} \, \text{kg/hr}$$

> **Marks allocation:** Velocity (2 marks), Volumetric flow rate (3 marks), Mass flow rate (2 marks). **(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the time-delay peak argument:** Many students write the cross-correlation formula but **omit the crucial step** showing that $R_{xx}$ peaks at zero lag and therefore $R_{xy}$ peaks at $\tau = \tau_d$. This alone costs **2–3 marks** in 7-mark derivation questions.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** $\tau_d$ must be in **seconds** (not ms), and flow rates must be in requested units ($\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ or litres/min). KTU examiners strictly penalize missing conversions.
> 3. **Confusing $R_{xy}$ with $R_{yx}$:** Always state the convention — for an **upstream-first** measurement, the peak of $R_{xy}$ is **positive** at $\tau = +\tau_d$, not negative.
> 4. **Omitting sensor types in descriptive questions:** Part (b) of descriptive questions **always** asks for sensor types — forgetting to list at least 3 sensor types costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Neglecting the noise term:** In the derivation, failing to mention that $R_{xn}(\tau) = 0$ due to statistical independence is a frequent deduction point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Final-Minute KTU Prep:**

- **Core equation (memorize):** $v = L / \tau_d$ and $Q = A \cdot L / \tau_d$.
- **Cross-correlation definition:** $R_{xy}(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} (1/T) \int_0^T x(t) y(t+\tau) \, dt$.
- **Key result:** $R_{xy}(\tau) = \alpha \, R_{xx}(\tau - \tau_d)$ — peak at $\tau = \tau_d$.
- **Sensors used:** Capacitive, Conductivity, Optical, Ultrasonic Doppler, Strain-gauge.
- **Best application:** Two-phase, slurry, opaque, and non-conductive flows.
- **Major advantage:** No calibration with the actual fluid; works with random natural disturbances.
- **Limitation:** Requires sufficient turbulence to generate a measurable signal; unsuitable for highly laminar flows.
- **Implementation:** Real-time DSP using either direct time-domain multiplication-integration or FFT-based fast correlation.
- **Normalization:** $\rho_{xy} \in [-1, 1]$ — a value close to **+1** indicates a strong, reliable correlation peak.
- **Noise rejection:** SNR improves as $\sqrt{BT}$ where $B$ is bandwidth and $T$ is integration window.
- **Units to remember:** $L$ in m, $\tau_d$ in s, $v$ in m/s, $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s.
- **Flow direction detection:** If $R_{xy}$ peak is at $\tau > 0$, flow is in the assumed direction; if $\tau < 0$, flow is **reversed** (bidirectional capability).
- **Pipe diameter dependency:** Larger diameter → larger $A$ → higher $Q$ for the same $v$ — but correlation signal strength may decrease.
- **KTU 2024 Bloom's L4–L5 focus:** Expect derivation questions on $R_{xy}(\tau) = \alpha R_{xx}(\tau - \tau_d)$ and 14-mark comparison questions with sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vortex Flow Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT833 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Vortex Flow Meters # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Vortex Flow Meter** is a volumetric flow measurement instrument whose operating principle is based on the natural phenomenon of **von Kármán vortex street** formation. When a fluid (liquid, gas, or steam) flows past a non-streamlined (bluff) body placed perpendicular to the flow direction, a regular, alternating series of low-pressure vortices is shed from the downstream side of the bluff body. The frequency of vortex shedding is linearly proportional to the volumetric flow velocity, enabling accurate, obstruction-type flow measurement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Flow Measurement):** > The KTU 2024 Scheme (OEICT833 – Industrial Instrumentation) categorizes Vortex Flow Meters under *Obstruction-Type Flow Meters*. It is grouped alongside orifice plates, venturi tubes, and pitot-static tubes due to its insertion of a primary element (bluff body) into the flow stream. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine standing beside a slow-moving river where a thick, sturdy tree trunk lies across the current. As water rushes past the trunk, you can hear and see a rhythmic "swoosh… swoosh… swoosh" pattern of swirling eddies forming on alternating sides of the trunk and being carried downstream. The faster the river flows, the more rapidly these swirls form and detach. A **Vortex Flow Meter** does exactly the same thing — but engineered with surgical precision: - The **tree trunk** = the **bluff body** (a precisely shaped obstruction). - The **river** = the **process fluid** (water, steam, oil, gas, chemicals). - The **swoosh frequency** = the **vortex shedding frequency** (measured by a piezoelectric sensor). - The **ruler measuring the swirls** = the **electronic signal conditioner**, which converts frequency → flow rate. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constant — Strouhal Number (St):** > A dimensionless coefficient that remains nearly constant (**St ≈ 0.2**) over an enormous flow range. This makes the vortex meter remarkably linear and calibration-free across a wide span. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Strouhal Number (St) | **0.17 – 0.21** (≈ 0.2) | dimensionless | | Operating Reynolds Number | **10,000 – 1,000,000** | dimensionless | | Accuracy Class | **±0.5 % to ±1.0 %** of reading | % | | Turndown Ratio (Rangeability) | **10:1 to 20:1** | ratio | | Output Signal | **4–20 mA** / pulse / HART | mA / Hz | | Minimum Velocity (Liquid) | **≈ 0.3 m/s** | m·s⁻¹ | | Minimum Velocity (Gas/Steam) | **≈ 6 m/s** | m·s⁻¹ | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physics — The von Kármán Vortex Street When a bluff body (of characteristic width **d**) is immersed in a uniform flow of velocity **V**, two distinct flow regimes emerge: 1. **Upstream side:** Smooth, attached laminar/turbulent flow. 2. **Downstream side:** Alternating low-pressure vortices peel off from the upper and lower edges of the bluff body in a regular, periodic pattern. This periodic pattern is termed the **Kármán Vortex Street** (named after Hungarian-American engineer **Theodore von Kármán**, 1911). The shedding is governed by an equilibrium between: - **Inertial forces** of the moving fluid (driving the swirl). - **Viscous forces** that dissipate the vortex energy. The ratio of these forces is the **Reynolds Number (Re)**: $$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot d}{\mu}$$ where **ρ** = fluid density, **V** = flow velocity, **d** = bluff body width, **μ** = dynamic viscosity. ## 2.2 The Strouhal Equation — The Heart of the Vortex Meter The fundamental governing equation of a vortex flow meter is the **Strouhal relationship**: $$f = St \cdot \frac{V}{d}$$ Rearranged for velocity: $$V = \frac{f \cdot d}{St}$$ And for volumetric flow rate (**Q**): $$Q = V \cdot A = \frac{f \cdot d \cdot A}{St}$$ where: - **f** = vortex shedding frequency (Hz) - **St** = Strouhal number (dimensionless, ≈ 0.2) - **V** = average fluid velocity (m/s) - **d** = width of the bluff body (m) - **A** = cross-sectional area of the pipe (m²) - **Q** = volumetric flow rate (m³/s) ## 2.3 Why Does the Vortex Meter Work as a Flow Sensor? - The bluff body has a **fixed width (d)** — a mechanical constant. - The Strouhal number **St** is **constant** in the operating Re range. - Therefore, **V ∝ f** (frequency is directly proportional to velocity). - The piezoelectric / capacitive sensor detects the pressure oscillations caused by vortex shedding and converts them into a **pulse train** whose frequency is measured electronically. > [!NOTE] > This is why vortex meters are sometimes called **"frequency-output flowmeters"** — the output is intrinsically digital (pulses), making them immune to analog drift and noise. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Expression | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | Strouhal Equation (Velocity) | $V = \dfrac{f \cdot d}{St}$ | Fluid velocity from vortex frequency | | Strouhal Equation (Flow) | $Q = \dfrac{f \cdot d \cdot A}{St}$ | Volumetric flow rate | | Strouhal Number | $St = \dfrac{f \cdot d}{V}$ | Dimensionless shedding coefficient | | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V \cdot d}{\mu}$ | Defines operating flow regime | | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Required for steam/gas applications | | Pipe Cross-Section | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2}$ | Area for internal diameter **D** | | Vortex Shedding Frequency | $f = St \cdot \dfrac{V}{d}$ | Raw sensor output | | K-Factor (Pulses per m³) | $K = \dfrac{f}{Q} = \dfrac{St}{d \cdot A}$ | Calibration constant of the meter | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Industry | Application Sector | Typical Use Case | |---|---| | **Power Plants** | Steam flow measurement in boiler lines | | **Oil & Gas** | Custody transfer of natural gas, LNG | | **Chemical Plants** | Corrosive liquid and gas flow | | **HVAC & District Cooling** | Chilled water, hot water circuits | | **Pulp & Paper** | Black liquor, stock flow | | **Food & Pharma** | Clean-in-place (CIP) fluids, deionized water | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Strouhal Relationship from Dimensional Analysis The vortex shedding frequency **f** must depend on the bluff body width **d**, the fluid velocity **V**, the fluid density **ρ**, and the dynamic viscosity **μ**. So we can write: $$f = \phi(d, V, \rho, \mu)$$ There are 5 variables and 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), so by **Buckingham's π-theorem**, there are **5 − 3 = 2** dimensionless groups. **Step 1:** Choose repeating variables: **d, V, ρ** (these contain all three dimensions). **Step 2:** Form the first π group using **f**: $$\pi_1 = f \cdot d^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot \rho^{c}$$ Imposing dimensional homogeneity $[M^{0} L^{0} T^{0}]$: $$T^{-1} \cdot L^{a} \cdot (LT^{-1})^{b} \cdot (ML^{-3})^{c} = M^{0} L^{0} T^{0}$$ For T: $-1 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -1$ For M: $c = 0$ For L: $a + b - 3c = 0 \Rightarrow a - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1$ Therefore: $$\pi_1 = \frac{f \cdot d}{V}$$ **Step 3:** Form the second π group using **μ**: $$\pi_2 = \mu \cdot d^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot \rho^{c}$$ Imposing dimensional homogeneity: $$(ML^{-1}T^{-1}) \cdot L^{a} \cdot (LT^{-1})^{b} \cdot (ML^{-3})^{c} = M^{0} L^{0} T^{0}$$ For M: $1 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -1$ For T: $-1 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -1$ For L: $-1 + a + b - 3c = 0 \Rightarrow -1 + a - 1 + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$ Therefore: $$\pi_2 = \frac{\mu}{\rho \cdot V \cdot d} = \frac{1}{Re}$$ **Step 4:** Combine both π groups: $$\frac{f \cdot d}{V} = \phi\!\left(\frac{1}{Re}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Define the **Strouhal Number** as the functional form of the left-hand side: $$St = \frac{f \cdot d}{V}$$ The complete functional relationship becomes: $$\boxed{St = \phi(Re)}$$ In the operating Reynolds number range of a vortex flow meter (10⁴ to 10⁶), the function **φ(Re) is essentially constant ≈ 0.2**, which is why: $$f = St \cdot \frac{V}{d} \quad \text{(linear, calibration-free)}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A vortex flow meter with a bluff body width of **d = 25 mm** is installed in a pipe of internal diameter **D = 100 mm** carrying water. The piezoelectric sensor outputs a vortex shedding frequency of **f = 120 Hz**. The Strouhal number for the bluff body shape is **St = 0.20**. Calculate: > (a) The average fluid velocity. > (b) The volumetric flow rate. > (c) The K-factor of the meter in pulses/m³. **Given Data:** $$d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$$ $$f = 120 \text{ Hz}, \quad St = 0.20$$ **(a) Average Fluid Velocity:** $$V = \frac{f \cdot d}{St}$$ $$V = \frac{120 \times 0.025}{0.20}$$ $$V = \frac{3.0}{0.20} = 15.0 \text{ m/s}$$ **(b) Volumetric Flow Rate:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.100)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$Q = V \cdot A = 15.0 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$Q = 0.1178 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = 117.8 \text{ L/s}$$ **(c) K-Factor (Pulses per m³):** $$K = \frac{f}{Q} = \frac{120}{0.1178}$$ $$K \approx 1018.7 \text{ pulses/m}^{3}$$ **Verification (cross-check using the K-factor definition):** $$K = \frac{St}{d \cdot A} = \frac{0.20}{0.025 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.20}{1.9635 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$K \approx 1018.7 \text{ pulses/m}^{3} \quad \checkmark$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Vortex vs. Other Obstruction Meters | Parameter | Vortex Meter | Orifice Plate | Venturi Tube | Pitot-Static | |---|---|---|---|---| | Primary Element | Bluff body | Concentric plate | Convergent-divergent tube | Pitot probe | | Output Signal | Frequency (pulses) | Differential pressure (ΔP) | Differential pressure (ΔP) | Differential pressure (ΔP) | | Accuracy | ±0.5–1.0 % | ±1.0–2.0 % | ±0.5–1.5 % | ±1.0–3.0 % | | Permanent Pressure Loss | Moderate | High | Low | Very Low | | Turndown Ratio | 10:1 to 20:1 | 3:1 to 5:1 | 5:1 to 10:1 | 4:1 to 6:1 | | Moving Parts | **None** | None | None | None | | Sensitivity to Density | None (frequency-based) | High (ΔP ∝ ρ) | High (ΔP ∝ ρ) | High (ΔP ∝ ρ) | | Best Suited For | Steam, clean liquids | Clean liquids/gases | High flow, low loss | Spot measurement | | Cost | Medium-High | Low | High | Low | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Vortex Flow Meter ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Process Fluid
Inlet Stream] --> B[Bluff Body
Shedding Element] B --> C[Vortex Generation Zone
Alternating Low Pressure Pockets] C --> D[Piezoelectric Sensor
Pressure Fluctuation to Charge] D --> E[Charge Amplifier
Impedance Conversion Stage] E --> F[Signal Conditioner
Filtering and Amplification] F --> G[Frequency to Analog Converter
f to 4 to 20 mA] F --> H[Pulse Output Stage
Cumulative and Frequency] G --> I[Flow Controller or DCS
Distributed Control System] H --> I I --> J[Display and Totalizer
Engineering Units] I --> K[Control Valve Actuator
Process Regulation] ``` ## 4.2 Vortex Shedding Mechanism (Schematic Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Inlet Pipe Section] --> P2[Flow Conditioner
if Required] P2 --> P3[Upstream Straight Length
10 to 20 D] P3 --> P4[Bluff Body Insertion
width d perpendicular to flow] P4 --> P5{Vortex Shedding Region
von Karman Street} P5 --> P6[Sensor Element
Piezoelectric or Capacitive] P6 --> P7[Downstream Pipe Section] P6 --> S1[Signal Cable to Transmitter] S1 --> S2[Microprocessor
Frequency to Flow Conversion] S2 --> S3[Output
4 to 20 mA plus HART] ``` ## 4.3 Frequency-Velocity Linear Relationship (Operating Logic) ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[Low Flow Velocity] --> R1[Low Vortex Frequency f] Q2[Medium Flow Velocity] --> R2[Medium Vortex Frequency f] Q3[High Flow Velocity] --> R3[High Vortex Frequency f] R1 --> O1[Linear Mapping via St constant] R2 --> O2[Linear Mapping via St constant] R3 --> O3[Linear Mapping via St constant] O1 --> Z[Microprocessor Computation of V and Q] O2 --> Z O3 --> Z Z --> OUT[Final Output
Flow Rate in Engineering Units] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]** Define the **Strouhal Number** and state its significance in the operation of a vortex flow meter. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Strouhal Number (St)** is a dimensionless quantity that relates the vortex shedding frequency to the bluff body width and the free-stream fluid velocity: $$St = \frac{f \cdot d}{V}$$ **Significance (3 key points for full marks):** 1. It is the **calibration constant** of the vortex flow meter; once the bluff body shape is fixed, St remains constant across the operating Reynolds range (10⁴–10⁶). 2. Since St is constant, the relationship **f = St·V/d** is **linear** in velocity, eliminating the need for in-situ calibration. 3. A higher St value (closer to 0.2) gives a stronger, more easily detectable vortex signal, improving low-flow sensitivity. The universal value for most bluff bodies is **St ≈ 0.2**. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]** List **any three advantages** and **any three limitations** of a vortex flow meter. **Model Answer (3 Marks — concise tabular form expected):** **Advantages:** 1. **No moving parts** → excellent long-term reliability and zero mechanical wear. 2. **Frequency output** → inherently digital, immune to drift, noise, and analog signal degradation. 3. **Wide rangeability** (10:1 to 20:1) and good accuracy (±0.5 % to ±1.0 %). **Limitations:** 1. **Not suitable for very low flow rates** (vortices become too weak to detect). 2. **Sensitive to pipeline vibrations** that can corrupt the vortex signal. 3. **Incompatible with highly viscous, slurry, or multiphase fluids** — the vortex street becomes irregular or collapses. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Slot]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle** of a vortex shedding flow meter. Label all major components. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Strouhal relationship** using dimensional analysis, clearly stating the boundary conditions for which the relationship is valid. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A ### Part (a) — Construction and Working Principle (7 Marks) **Construction (4 Marks):** A vortex flow meter consists of the following components: 1. **Bluff Body (Shedding Bar):** A precisely machined obstruction of width **d**, inserted perpendicular to the flow. Common shapes: rectangular, trapezoidal, T-shaped, delta-shaped. The shape is optimized to give a stable Strouhal number across a wide Re range. 2. **Sensor:** A **piezoelectric crystal** (most common) or **capacitive sensor** mounted inside or behind the bluff body. It detects the alternating low-pressure pulses as vortices are shed. 3. **Meter Body / Flanges:** Integrally machined or flanged body matching the line size, ensuring the bluff body is centered in the pipe. 4. **Signal Conditioner & Transmitter (Electronics Module):** Converts the raw sensor charge into a standardized output (4–20 mA + HART, pulse output, or fieldbus). 5. **Pipe Section:** Includes recommended **upstream straight length of 10D to 20D** and **downstream length of 5D** to ensure a fully developed flow profile. **Working Principle (3 Marks):** - The process fluid enters the meter body and impinges on the bluff body. - As the fluid flows past the bluff body, **alternating vortices** are shed from the two edges of the bluff body at a regular frequency **f**. - Each shed vortex creates a **local low-pressure pocket** that the piezoelectric sensor detects as a small pressure pulse. - The sensor generates a **sinusoidal voltage waveform** whose frequency matches the vortex shedding rate. - The electronics module counts the pulses (frequency) and computes flow rate using the **Strouhal equation**, then outputs a 4–20 mA signal proportional to flow. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Process Fluid] --> B[Bluff Body] B --> C[Von Karman Vortex Street] C --> D[Piezo Sensor] D --> E[Charge Amplifier] E --> F[Filter and Amplifier] F --> G[Microprocessor] G --> H[4 to 20 mA Output plus HART] G --> I[Pulse Output] ``` ### Part (b) — Derivation of Strouhal Relationship (7 Marks) *(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.)* **Key Steps to Present:** 1. Identify all relevant variables: **f, d, V, ρ, μ** (5 variables, 3 dimensions → 2 π groups). 2. Choose **d, V, ρ** as repeating variables. 3. Form **π₁ = f·d/V** → defines the Strouhal number. 4. Form **π₂ = μ/(ρ·V·d) = 1/Re** → defines the Reynolds number. 5. Final relationship: **St = φ(Re)**. 6. **Boundary Condition (Critical for full marks):** For the operating Re range of **10,000 to 1,000,000**, φ(Re) ≈ constant ≈ 0.2, so the meter is **linear and calibration-free**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Slot]** **(a)** Explain the **sensing mechanisms** used in vortex flow meters. Compare piezoelectric and capacitive sensing methods. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A vortex flow meter with bluff body width **d = 20 mm** is installed in a **DN 80** pipe. The vortex shedding frequency observed is **f = 85 Hz**, and the Strouhal number is **St = 0.21**. Determine: (i) The average fluid velocity. (ii) The volumetric flow rate in m³/hr. (iii) The K-factor of the meter in pulses/litre. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B ### Part (a) — Sensing Mechanisms (7 Marks) The vortex shedding creates **oscillating pressure fluctuations** near the bluff body. The sensor must convert these mechanical oscillations into electrical signals. **(i) Piezoelectric Sensing (4 Marks):** - A piezoelectric crystal (typically PZT — lead zirconate titanate) is mounted inside the bluff body. - As a vortex is shed, the localized pressure variation mechanically stresses the crystal, producing a small **charge (Q)** proportional to the applied force. - The charge is fed into a **charge amplifier** (which converts high-impedance charge to a low-impedance voltage). - Advantages: rugged, high sensitivity, excellent high-frequency response, no external power needed for the sensor itself. - Limitations: sensitive to cable noise, temperature drift requires compensation. **(ii) Capacitive Sensing (3 Marks):** - A small **diaphragm** in the bluff body forms one plate of a capacitor; the bluff body body itself forms the other plate. - Vortex pressure variations deflect the diaphragm, changing the **capacitance (C)**. - This change is detected by an **AC bridge** or oscillator circuit whose frequency or amplitude varies with C. - Advantages: lower temperature sensitivity, suitable for some low-temperature applications. - Limitations: lower signal-to-noise ratio at low flow rates compared to piezoelectric; more complex electronics. ### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** $$d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}, \quad D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$$ $$f = 85 \text{ Hz}, \quad St = 0.21$$ **(i) Average Fluid Velocity (2 Marks):** $$V = \frac{f \cdot d}{St} = \frac{85 \times 0.020}{0.21}$$ $$V = \frac{1.70}{0.21} \approx 8.095 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Volumetric Flow Rate (3 Marks):** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.080)^{2} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$Q = V \cdot A = 8.095 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$Q = 0.04070 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$ Converting to m³/hr: $$Q = 0.04070 \times 3600 = 146.5 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{hr}$$ **(iii) K-Factor in Pulses/Litre (2 Marks):** $$K = \frac{f}{Q} = \frac{85}{0.04070} = 2088.5 \text{ pulses/m}^{3}$$ Convert to pulses/litre (1 m³ = 1000 L): $$K = \frac{2088.5}{1000} \approx 2.09 \text{ pulses/litre}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Vortex Flow Meter Questions:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Forgetting to convert mm → m before substitution in the Strouhal equation. This single mistake can cascade and cause wrong answers in 2–3 sub-parts. > 2. **Confusing St with Re** — Students sometimes invert the Strouhal number formula. Remember: **St = f·d/V**, so **V = f·d/St** (NOT V = St·d/f). > 3. **Forgetting to mention the St = constant assumption** — In derivation questions, simply stating the final equation without the Reynolds range boundary condition loses 2 marks. > 4. **Ignoring K-factor units** — K-factors are quoted in pulses/m³ or pulses/litre; mixing units is a frequent error. > 5. **Skipping the bluff body shape in construction answers** — Examiners specifically look for the **shape of the bluff body** (rectangular, T-shape, etc.) as a 1-mark point. > 6. **No mention of "no moving parts"** — This is the single most-valued advantage of the vortex meter in theory questions. > 7. **Confusing vortex meter with turbine meter** — Turbine meters DO have moving parts (the rotor); vortex meters do NOT. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vortex Flow Meters** - **Operating Principle:** Alternating low-pressure vortices shed by a bluff body in a flow stream — the **von Kármán vortex street**. - **Governing Equation:** **f = St · V / d** → the cornerstone formula. - **Strouhal Number:** **St ≈ 0.2** (dimensionless, constant in the Re range 10⁴ to 10⁶). - **Output Nature:** **Frequency / Pulses** — inherently digital, drift-free. - **Primary Element:** **Bluff body** of width **d** (shedding bar) — fixed, non-moving. - **Sensor:** **Piezoelectric** (most common) or capacitive. - **Cross-Sectional Flow Equation:** **Q = (f · d · A) / St**. - **K-Factor:** **K = f / Q = St / (d · A)** — pulses per unit volume. - **Reynolds Number:** **Re = ρVd / μ** — must stay in the operating range for linearity. - **Mass Flow Rate:** **ṁ = ρ · Q** — for steam/gas applications. - **Accuracy Class:** **±0.5 % to ±1.0 %** of reading. - **Turndown Ratio:** **10:1 to 20:1** — wide rangeability. - **Advantages:** No moving parts, digital output, good accuracy, low maintenance, wide rangeability, immunity to fluid density/viscosity changes. - **Limitations:** Unsuitable for low flow rates, sensitive to vibrations, incompatible with slurries/dirty fluids, requires 10D–20D upstream straight pipe. - **Industrial Uses:** Steam, natural gas, chemicals, chilled water, district heating. - **Comparison Anchor:** Vortex meters combine the **no-moving-parts reliability of orifice plates** with the **digital noise-immunity of turbine meters** — the best of both worlds for clean fluids. - **Key Shapes of Bluff Bodies:** Rectangular, T-shaped, trapezoidal, delta (triangular) — all designed to maximize St stability. - **Always state the Re range** when defining the validity of the Strouhal equation.
